## Conceptual models of space colonization 

###  By [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

I'm thinking morose thoughts about the practical prospects for space colonization (ahem: stripped of the colonialist rhetoric, manifest destiny bullshit, "the Earth's too fragile and vulnerable to keep all our eggs in one basket", and the other post-hoc attempts at justification) and trying to sort them out in case I ever feel inclined to go back to writing the sort of medium term SF epic that Kim Stanley Robinson nailed in his Mars trilogy in the 1980s.

And what I'm nibbling on is, to paraphrase Oliver Cromwell, the big question of what if all our models or paradigms for how to structure a colony effort are wrong?

While the strong form of the [Sapir-Whorf hypothesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linguistic_relativity) is evidently invalid, a weaker version—that language influences thought—is much harder to argue against. When we talk about a spaceship, a portmanteau word derived from "[outer] space" and "ship", we bring along certain unstated assumptions about shipping that are at odds with the physical parameters of a human-friendly life support environment for traversing interplanetary distances. _Ships_ , in the vernacular, have _captains_ and a _crew_ who obey the captain via a _chain of command_ , they carry _cargo_ or _passengers_ , they travel between _ports_ or to a well-defined _destination_ , they may have a _mission_ whether it be _scientific research_ or _military_. And of these aspects, only the scientific research angle is _remotely_ applicable to any actually existing interplanetary vehicle, be it a robot probe like [Psyche](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psyche_\(spacecraft\)) or one of the [Apollo program](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apollo_program) flights.

(Pedant's footnote: while the Apollo crews had a nominal commander, actual direction came from Mission Control back on Earth and the astronauts operated as a team, along lines very similar to those later formalized as [Crew Resource Management](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crew_resource_management) in commercial aviation.)

Anyway, a point I've already chewed over on this blog is that [a spaceship is not like a sea-going vessel](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2009/11/the_myth_of_the_starship.html), can't be operated like a sea-going vessel, and the word "ship" in its name feeds into various cognitive biases that may be actively harmful to understanding what it is.

Which leads me to the similar term "space colony": the word _colony_ drags in all sorts of historical baggage, and indeed invokes several models of how an off-Earth outpost might operate, all of which invoke very dangerous cognitive biases!

First cognitive bias on the chopping blog: the word "colony" _usually_ implies a self-sufficient settlement, that can "live off the land" and reproduce almost anything the humans living there need, including more human workers raised from infancy. We don't, for example, talk of [McMurdo Station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McMurdo_Station) in Antarctica as a colony even though it has a population of up to 1500 people at different times of year because it's entirely resupplied from other land masses and nobody is born there (and hopefully nobody dies there either). McMurdo Station doesn't have kindergartens, schools, universities, retirement homes, farms, factories, and a resident political apparatus to set policy.

Dilbert Stark's bloviation about a Mars colony aside, I don't expect we'll see an off-Earth colony that meets the self-sufficiency criterion any time in the next century—at least not without major technical breakthroughs in the life sciences and in automated manufacturing. Unless you can run a space colony with a 17th century level of education and specialization (not to mention infant mortality) you can't make it self-sufficient with less than millions of people because _no environment in the solar system is compatible with human life in the absence of high technology life support_. 

The American model of colonization—a cognitive bias that underpins both the American _and_ Russian space programs' associated ideological drive towards human expansion—is biased towards an unpopulated or underpopulated terrestrial biome with breathable air, plentiful sources of water and minerals, a biosphere that naturally turns sunlight into biomass that can be directly eaten or fed to food animals, and so on. To use a simile, it's like pitching a tent on the roof of a fifty story high skyscraper (with working electricity and water supplies) and announcing you've constructed a dwelling. To extend this into a metaphor, for _space_ colonization you're going to build a cement factory and get a degree in structural engineering first.

In addition to the "camping in the garden is the new Lewis and Clarke expedition" cognitive bias, the word "colony" comes with a number of other associations:

**The military model**

When we talk about colonies it's a hop, skip, and a jump to colonial _ism_ , with its model of military outposts on the edge of the known/civilized world, and a mission to bring civilisation to the unruly indigenes at gunpoint. It's in _all_ our cultural tropes relating to colonies, especially in those nations that used to run overseas empires (or evolved from such empires). This in turn implies a _military chain of command_ with enforcement via _military discipline_ , and a _military mission_ , which almost invariably requires an adversary and some sort of exit strategy (even if the exit strategy is "make a wasteland, call it peace, come home").

I have no idea how _Star Trek_ has evolved since I last watched it (I hit my throw-book-at-wall point halfway through the pilot episode of TNG and _refuse_ to give it a second chance) but ST at least superficially started out from the military model, in a universe with copious adversaries so no shortage of possible military missions.

It is difficult for me to conceive of how a military colonization model might apply unless we succeed in taking our dysfunctional social structures into deep space and well beyond Low Earth Orbit. (In LEO, all you need are ground-launched weapons platforms: not a colony.)

**The homesteader model**

In which, like our marcher empire colonists from the US frontier and the Siberian expansion, our colonists are given title to forty acres and a mule, or a bunk in a penal colony, and go forth to play live-action Stardew Valley. Heinlein wrote this a _lot_ , for example in _Farmer in the Sky_ and in the background of _Space Family Stone_ , and in one of the novella-sized chunks of _Time Enough for Love_. _Star Trek_ by some accounts started as "wagon train in space", riffing off the Western genre TV series of that name. 

As noted above: the homesteader novel approximates to erecting a tent on top of a skyscraper and declaring victory. More to the point, an awful lot of homesteader settlers died, and that was with a very low bar to success at self-sufficiency because they were settling inside a biosphere, the spacegoing equivalent of colonizing the downstairs bathroom.

Homesteading tended to be a family sized enterprise, and a patriarchal family culture at that with a high rate of population growth (kids are livestock who can herd other livestock, not dangerous liabilities who sometimes fiddle with the airlock controls when they get bored). They are, at most, tribal enterprises where the colony approximates Dunbar's Number, on which note, a quick review of the history of the [Donner Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donner_Party) is instructive. (The Donner Party had the misfortune to get stuck high in the Sierra Nevada mountains by an early snowfall without having laid down enough supplies to last through winter: if I was going to write a sequel to Tom Godwin's [The Cold Equations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Cold_Equations) I can think of few better topics to choose than a bunch of starry-eyed settlers on the high frontier getting trapped in the coffin corner of a three axis constraint diagram with axes representing delta vee, energy, and consumables.)

**The corporate model**

Do I really need to explain why [Company Towns](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Company_town) are bad places to live, and a company town in spaaaaace is pretty much the ultimate dystopia?

Yes, yes I do! In a nutshell: companies are artificial social constructs that _offload all their externalities onto the state they are embedded in_. If the company is the entire habitat, then it can only offload "useless eaters" via the airlock. Babies are useless eaters—they don't change their own diapers! So.

(For a less grotesque critique of capitalism in space, read just about any American or British SF set on a space colony written in the past 30 years: [Luna](https://www.amazon.com/Luna-New-Moon-Ian-McDonald-ebook/dp/B00V34YJE0/) by Ian Macdonald is a good starting point (although his premise—a mining colony grinding up the Lunar regolith to extract 3He for fusion reactors on Earth—is based on junk science). In particular, two words should strike fear into your heart: _oxygen tax_.)

**The Pilgrim Fathers model**

Otherwise known as theocracy in space. Worryingly, religious belief rather than economics seems the most plausible incentive for space colonization. (I consider the argument that "the Earth's too fragile to keep all our eggs in one basket" to be part of this category: it's based on the "be fruitful and multiply" command from _Genesis_ , and its explicitly religious roots stem from Russian Cosmism and the writings of the 19th century theologian [Nikolai Fyodorovich Fyodorov](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nikolai_Fyodorov_\(philosopher\)).)

Religious colonies can in fact get shit done in harsh environments, as witness the Plymouth Colony. The things they get done can include witch hunts and mass executions of unbelievers, poisoning the neighbours with smallpox infested blankets, going [full Jonestown](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jonestown#Deaths_in_Jonestown), and experimentally verifying the feasibility of building [The Republic of Gilead](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Handmaid%27s_Tale#Setting) in orbit around Jupiter, only now with 100% less escape routes to Canada.

**The Polynesian model**

Build small boats, move to the next island just on the horizon, fish, farm, then chop down trees to build more boats, and move on in the unexplored direction. It worked for the Easter Islanders, it got the Maori to Aotearoa, why couldn't it work?

In a nutshell, the Polynesian model suffers from a combination of the failure modes of the homesteader model and (pick any combination of) all the others—the Religious retreat, the company town, the military expedition: living off the land is _really hard_ when there's not actually any land, nor a supply chain able to manufacture spacecraft, nor a biosphere to overrun. It _might_ work, in the far future, if the unit of settlement isn't an outrigger trimaran (or group thereof) but a self-propelled city state with enough millions of people to sustain a technology base (including educating the educators for the next generation of niche specialties). But that begs the question of the management or political governance of such a city-state. The people in such a colony _live_ there: it's no more a colony than this island Earth we all live on today is a colony—it's a closed cycle world, just a lot smaller than this one.

To go back to sarcastic similes, it's as if England circa 1606 decided to colonize North America by physically packing up London and the surrounding hundred kilometre radius of villages and farms and plonking them down on Long Island. At which point, yeah, you've got enough human capital in place to stick around and enough food supplies to make it work, assuming they survive the first harsh continental winter (the origins of the annual US [Turkey holocaust day](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thanksgiving_\(United_States\)) are instructive in this respect). Congratulations! James Blish ran this riff in his [Cities in Flight](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cities_in_Flight) tetralogy, and it looks superficially plausible at first glance, although it eerily parallels the aerospace engineering nostrum that if you strap enough thrust to a concrete block you can make anything fly.

Anyway, this leads up to my question for this blog entry: **what do all these models fail to account for?** Are there other, _better_ models for how to build and run a space colony? If not, why not—what are the universal points of failure (other than "human beings")?

**PS/Administrative Note** : All comments referring by his given name to a certain multi-billionaire of South African origins who owns his own space program will be deleted by the moderators (I don't want the world's richest man to sue me for libel, or to be dogpiled by his glassy-eyed fans doing a google search). If you can't think of your own nickname for him, feel free to call him "Space Karen" or "Dilbert Stark".

**2:**

Given the degree to which prisons today are used as a substitute for providing actual psychiatric care, I don't see prison planets being viable. The nearest they might come is if we posit a neo-Stalinist regime intent on imprisoning and deporting _all their most diligent workers_ as political dissidents, which seems unlikely (although if Donald Trump wins the election on November 4th, watch out) ...

It might look like a subtype of the corporate model in practice, but a pure autocratic model may look slightly different. The top-down command structure of reporting to the CEO might be less extreme than reporting to the King of Mars. Without any prior claims of sovereignty on the planet's surface, it could, in effect, be the default model.

**4:**

I'd suggest AppleTV's "For All Mankind" with especial attention to season four whenever the blackmarket on Mars comes up... never mind that such tonnage of smuggled goods would be caught (no matter the bribery)... 

just looking at the stuff the staff ensuring harsh conditions on the Mars base were in dire need of but the Powers That Be refused to provide: cotton underwear; booze; Preparation H; toothpaste (other than pre-approved brand); et al

what initially looked like a sweet gig paying high wages turned out to be a scam due to everything _other than oxygen_ was pay before you consume...including food...

so... "company store" with labor unions illegal and any attempt at a tools down sit downs leading to thugs shipped up to crack heads

slavery? 

nope... you gotta feed your slaves... employees who refuse to buy food from the monopolistic base cafeteria and gouger blackmarket are ignored as they starve

we can dream of the High Frontier but until there's 3D printers turning sand and carbon monoxide into soup and steaks by the ton per day, forget about anything larger on Mars than ten monkeys in a fart trapping tin can

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7949)

[](https://elainegallagherwriter.wordpress.com/) [Elaine](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7949) | [October 13, 2024 16:46](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199036)

**5:**

I guess the political-rivalry model that got humans to the moon would be a subset of the military model, and it would come with its own set of adversaries built in to keep the game going. The jingoism evident at the moment in various right-wing regimes, not only the one having an election right now - remember which president instituted the "space force" - has a religious quality that might not allow the rivalry to lapse once it's established. Given the military-industrial complex and its small regard for human life, the company town model might then use that competition as a bootstrap to provide "camp followers" and the rest of the civilian infrastructure that the military rests on. And civilization might follow after the number and population of installations "out there" and the technical capability both reach levels that make a revolution potentially survivable. From there is how Banks's Culture got established.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5004)

[](http://egypt.urnash.com/rita/) [Margaret Trauth](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5004) | [October 13, 2024 16:55](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199037)

**6:**

The Polynesian model made me think of Blish's _Cities in Flight_ and then you mentioned them. I finally read them recently, they were _strange_ books full of a lot of direct reference to a phase of American history that was fresh in the minds of Blish and his original readers but just a little footnote of "I don't remember a goddamn thing about Grapes of Wrath besides being made to read it in high school English" to this Bill & Ted's Excellent Generation reader.

One thing you don't touch on is the idea of radical alteration of humanity to make it more spaceworthy. The "Lobsters" in Sterling's _Schismatrix_ come to mind: living in contraptions that straddle the line between "spacesuit" and "spaceship", but mostly get thought of as "the body of a creature that swims in space". Realistically it's probably still pretty likely to be like putting on water wings and splashing around the pool on top of your metaphorical skyscraper of infrastructure and technology, mining the raw elemental building blocks of life out of random asteroids and turning them into nutrient sludge that your belly will accept is a ways off, never mind shielding your mushy human form and some kind of terrarium to deal with recycling your pee and poo. It sure makes for some pretty fantasy images if you handwave "shielding" and "transmutation" though.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7949)

[](https://elainegallagherwriter.wordpress.com/) [Elaine](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7949) | [October 13, 2024 16:55](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199038)

**7:**

As to why the rivalry might establish itself, weapons manufacture and military adventurism are the #1 way for plutocrats to soak the taxpayer. If for some reason, say dwindling oil stocks in the Middle East, Earth-bound shoving matches become less profitable, the kleptocracies of various polities could arrange among themselves to take the game into orbit and out from there. This is how our least favourite South African is already operating.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6014)

[Tom](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6014) | [October 13, 2024 17:13](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199040)

**8:**

Technologies that might make this possible: \- A selection of additive manufacturing and "making" machines allowing close to full self sufficiency (given the raw materials) \- Advances in material sciences (leveraging carbon and silicon as primary materials) \- Savagely good recycling tech \- Source of top up materials \- Really efficient super thin solar panels \- Flexible precision fermentation to produce food without plants \- (ideally some kind of better-than-chemical drive)

All of the above a progressions from what we have now, no "magic" breakthroughs required.

With those you could posit a self-sufficient rotating asteroid settlement. In my world-building they are anarco-syndicalist descendents of abandoned corporate miners. I agree and I cannot think of a deep space colony model that would be planned but extended trips migt be a thing, followed by accidents and smart people trying to survive.

I don't think planets make sense. If we have to be self sufficient being at the bottom of a gravity well is only so usefull. Also, we don't know if gestating in low gravity is even possible yet. On a space habitat you can spin it to 1g.

In my world-building there is a Mars colony - a hellscape oligarcy vanity project lorded over by the male descendents of the founder (its not subtle). Women of child bearing age are confined to the "gyno-train" which travels in a fast circuit giving a sufficient gravity for safe reproduction.

Well, it makes sense in my head.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5609)

[](http://kevinmarks.com) [Kevin Marks](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5609) | [October 13, 2024 17:22](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199041)

**9:**

Deb Chachra's book on Infrastructure set the terms for a paradigm shift that could extend to Space eventually. The key point is that we have been treating matter as abundant and energy as rare for the Industrial cycle, and we are now moving to a domain where energy can be abundant due to solar, wind, hydropower and storage. Conversely, reusing materials in a closed cycle now makes more and more sense. If, and it is a big if, we succeed in this transition, and learn how to survive in the newly hostile landscapes of the climate crisis era, we may end up with an approach that could work in space. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8783)

[Retiring](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8783) | [October 13, 2024 17:23](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199042)

**10:**

Hard to imagine any of these actually succeeding. The overhead supply costs are just too high, without something like Star Trek's transmutation system, aka replicator. But maybe orbital or lunar outposts, with regular resupply and staff rotations. The McMurdo Station model. Need to be a reason to be there, of course. Maybe "Because the Chinese are already there" would be enough to fund the effort.

With room-temperature transmutation, the possibilities are much broader. And the religious impetus does seem powerful. Apparently there's nothing a true believer won't try to do.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7342)

[MCarreira](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7342) | [October 13, 2024 17:26](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199043)

"A City On Mars" (Kelly and Zach Weinersmith) focuses on the kind issues normal SF leaves out also - have you read it?

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8725)

[TheBrett](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8725) | [October 13, 2024 17:44](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199044)

**12:**

Combination of **Corporate** and **Science Station**. The first people to fill out a Mars Bio-Colony* will fall into two categories:

* Operations staff and personnel - the people it takes to set up a habitation area on Mars or elsewhere for a population numbering < 50,000 people. 

* Science staff and researchers - NASA probably as one client of this, but if it's cheap enough then you'd see university- and foundation-backed researchers like with the big ground telescopes. 

Not so much a Company Town as a University Town, with the branch campus being the keystone around which the other stuff will center. At least while it's still small, since once you get to groups larger than tens of thousands you can start having a population that's large enough that it generates its own demand for goods and services sufficient that folks will immigrate to do the work. 

I also don't think it will take millions to be self-sufficient. That's if you wanted to build the entire industrial stack on Mars with the level of specialization and mass-manufacturing you get on Earth, but you don't really need that for your space bio-colony - you just need to be able to repair and replace the components of your habitat, and you can do that at a pretty small scale with machine shops/etc even if it would be way more expensive in labor and time per unit than it would cost on Earth. 

  * I use the term **Bio-Colony** because I think it has two useful aspects:

  * It separates it from plain "colonization", which has clearly become tainted with connotations because of recent history. This isn't Europeans conquering native land - it's the equivalent of plants expanding into lifeless area left behind by retreating glaciers. 

  * It reinforces that it's not just people going to the colony - it's a slice of Earth's biosphere, including bacteria and whatever other forms of plant and animal life we bring with us. 




[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7913)

[SFReader](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7913) | [October 13, 2024 17:45](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199045)

**13:**

Since you already mentioned religions, how about abbeys and convents? Not sure how they run in the 21st century but for hundreds of years these institutions were for relatively well-off folks who didn't fit into to the rest of society - some definitely had some sort of major psychiatric issue. Regardless, these folks were also more educated than ordinary folk, wanted to be left (mostly) alone, provided for themselves, implicitly and explicitly agreed to follow orders, and worked on generations-long projects. Like an army minus the ray guns. (Maybe get some of the Asian monks/nuns to figure out a space version of karate or judo.)

BTW - the Jesuits formed specifically because the founders wanted to go to Asia to learn about Asian politics, religions, lifestyles and economics. Some of these Jesuits returned and even strongly urged the Pope to adopt some of the Asian religious beliefs/customs.

Historically, nuns were mostly the social services workers with quite a few convents founded for the purpose of providing basic education and health care to their local communities.

Both types of institutions usually did their own farming and assorted jobs related to survival.

So there is historical precedent for sending friars/priests/nuns as expeditionaries and habitat start-ups. FYI - one of the founders of the Jesuits, Ignatius of Loyola who's got a few colleges/universities named after him - was Jewish. None of the articles I read about him say that he was forced to convert suggesting that specific sect/religion was less important to this group and its existence than willingness to get info. Because we're fairly comfortable these days with globalism, we could probably get Buddist, Sikh, Hindu, etc. priests/nuns to participate.

For life ... most if not all of the various orders of priests/brothers/nuns usually take vows for life, i.e., poverty, chastity, obedience, service to others, etc. IVF would probably be an okay workaround for ensuring species/crew/colony continuity, i.e. they'd still be able to keep their vows. (You can go Dune or Mazarin's France from here.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6800)

[Remy Porter](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6800) | [October 13, 2024 17:48](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199046)

**14:**

There is the Dandelion Model. Instead of sending canned monkeys anywhere, you send some mix of automation and biological reactors which can bootstrap a biome. Or, related, a colonial organism model, where you bootstrap a biome and then follow on with canned monkeys following sny combination of your models. These tend to not be popular with colonial folks because these are operations that happen on something more like geological time. 

Then theres the Vectored Solar System Model, where you vector the solar wind as a source of thrust and steer the solar system around to make jaunts to neighboring stars easier. Not quite the same, as thats close to Dyson Sphere type thinking. 

Honestly though, I’d be real interested in a story of space colonization which happens across geological time. Not the typical “we made a generation ship” stuff (if you have a ship capable of supporting a stable biosphere across thousands of years it raises the question of what you actually need a planet for).

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) | [October 13, 2024 18:13](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199050)

**18:**

About the Polynesian model…

It is the only one where we’ve got good documentary evidence of people establishing independent, permanent colonies in places without humans already present, so by default it’s the only one we know that works. That said, the Māori reached Antarctica and couldn’t settle it, so it is limited. 

It’s worth realizing that all the Polynesian and Micronesian islands were settled by birth rank societies, which may or may not be relevant. The highest ranking was the first born child of the first born child going back. The lowest ranking was the lowest born child of the lowest born child, going back…. And, unsurprisingly, they cared so much about memorized genealogy that they hooked their entire mnemonic system into it. Anyway, the society was indelibly ranked, in theory, and in practice they had all sorts of work arounds for letting skilled lower ranked people do their thing. That, from the evidence, works as a colonial model. But if you want to steal it, read up a lot on how it works in practice.

Would this work in a space colony without FTL or some other way to get to a habitable planet? No, but I don’t think any system would. We’re no more suited for colonizing space than we’re suited for colonizing the midwater abyssopelagic zone of our oceans, three klicks down and no land in sonar range. 

If we’re given magic to get us to the surfaces of life-bearing alien worlds, then I’d suggest that the Polynesian model works extremely well. If, that is, you only colonize remote volcanic islands. This isn’t as stupid as it sounds. What you’re looking for in a colony site is a place with shirt-sleeve weather and the simplest ecosystem available, so that you and your immune system have a chance to adapt. That’s a remote island. Once people stop dying there and the crops and domestic vertebrates, insects, and fungi are producing a reliable surplus nicely, then you try exploring the mainland. Of course, building everything out of biomass and basalt derivatives is a chore, and if you can’t fish your life will suck, but if you’re stupid enough to abandon a habitable planet for the great unknown, what do you expect? 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6884)

**21:**

It's more of a subset to other forms of settlement, but the remittance man should not be forgotten! This was the habit the British upper/middle classes had of sending their failsons to Canada, Australia, etc. and giving them an allowance, hoping they would make something of themselves or die quietly far away.

Assuming all the tech for Mars/Jovian moon/asteroid settlements etc. is in place and reasonably reliable, BUT it's still considered adventurous to a certain degree, getting rid of your most embarrassing family member by shipping him off to manage setting up a SCOP factory or materials processing facility that's already 99.9% automated (or run by skilled contract workers he can "manage") could become attractive, depending on the social order of the day.

Remittance men were mostly a late-Victorian/early Edwardian phenomenon, but the more recent version was FILTH: Failed in London, Try Hong Kong.

Re: '... these folks were also more educated than ordinary folk, wanted to be left (mostly) alone, provided for themselves, implicitly and explicitly agreed to follow orders, and worked on generations-long projects.'

SF/F is often loaded with interesting tech but is decades behind real-life with respect to psychology. Any new tech will have a psych consequence. And crew/colonist members' psych - probably as much as their high-tech gizmos - determine mission success. 

Yeah - I know that keeping psychology mysterious and unfathomable is a cheap and convenient way to plot stories but it's been so overdone that the characters are boring and the plots/stories forgettable. 

Below is a possible starting point and it doesn't even include info on neuroscience. 

<https://www.apa.org/education-career/guide/science>

To SF/F authors: Time to move past the B-movie Freudian crap!

**24:**

I think the "why" question is more important than the "how" question.

Absent credible intelligence on the long term bio-compatibility of the destination, there will be no voluntary migration for normal people who wants kids etc.

With speed of light, we're never going to receive such detailed intelligence from our own sensors, and caveat emptor applies if we receive time-share offers from somebody else.

Forced migration as a matter of law could be a possibility.

In terms of quality of life, working in the He3 mines on the Moon with no possibility of return, is is probably 50/50 with life-time without parole in a US jail, but I cannot imagine any scarce resource which would justify the cost.

The final possible why is "Because we need you there", for some idealistic reason or other.

I know astronomers who would kill to staff the first observatory on the moon, even if their packed lunch would be their last meal.

And that will be my best guess: Scientific installations, possibly even quite large, where the option of coming home "will depend on future technological advances", and there will still be no shortage of volunteers.

**26:**

I'm struck in all your examples but a lack of seeing how true robust automation can vastly change some of these systems of organization. We've already proven with our design techniques of less many decades that we can build machines that last decades in these foreign environments we now have ample evidence that we can make automated tools that can dynamically interact with unpredictable environments. Some of the training tools for robotics that are available today that did not exist. Four years ago can create credibly adaptive automation. There are levels of civilization and robustness of that civilization that you can now that were impossible simply a few years ago.

Education, environmental upkeep, food supplies, maintenance, and transport can all be automated today on another planet if you put the resources into it. And there is no moratorium on supplying these outposts from the home planet and anywhere else. Refined ingots of titanium can be shipped up and then turned into 1000 tools and recycled into 10,000 more. Self-sustaining even on earth barely means self sustaining. Even the Polynesians traded from island to island, across thousands of miles of ocean over the course of centuries. 

I think your cognitive assumptions about what qualifies as a colony might be influencing you to think it's a very different thing than what would end up out there on 100's of different heavenly bodies. All of them supporting each other, all of them interacting, one of them can fail, and yet most of the people survive heading off to other places.

Maybe a colony isn't one big thing. Maybe colonizing the solar system is one big interactive, crazy mess, but somehow it works.

All the examples Charlie quoted are underpinned by positive population pressure. In other words, there is a demand for more resources, more space, or more conformity. However, current demographic trends show a negative population pressure taking over mid-century (Foreign Affairs Nov/Dec 2024 edition has a great article about it). So my question would be, what would drive space-side settlement at a time of reducing global population?

**33:**

That’s better than Leon Stunk.

Well, let’s blend DJT into the story of interstellar space colonization.

Assume friendly aliens land the day after Trump gets re-elected and say that they’ve got room for 10,000, political refugees preferred….

I’m being silly of course, but I’m willing to bet that a major driver of colonization into the modern Neolithic (Polynesian) wasn’t population pressure, it was socio-political pressure, getting away from people you hate or who may want to kill you. 

In Polynesia, remember that the far islands were mostly settled in a wave over a few centuries, well before any of them hit peak population or even reorganized as a state. Rivalry among chiefs is probably what drove it. I should point out the codicil to this: there are few if any examples of a state successfully colonizing a never-inhabited island., and most of those are in the South Altlantic or South Indian Ocean. Or Antarctica. All the other places were colonized by bands or tribes. 

This brings up the important question, that we don’t actually know if a state-derived system can actually colonize a previously uninhabited place. Ascension Island and the associated islands are about as close as we can to answering this question, and they’re no more isolated than are the Antarctic stations.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8725)

[TheBrett](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8725) replied to [this comment from Greg Tingey](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199062) | [October 13, 2024 20:55](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199068)

**34:**

@28 Greg Tingey

I wasn't really thinking printers, although you probably would use those in parts. More just that there's a lot of stuff where you could make small batches of it while maintaining the equipment you have really well, and so you don't necessarily need the level of specialization and scale you'd need on Earth to make it cost-effective here. 

The real challenge to Martian autonomy would be that it might be cheaper to just ship anything durable that isn't too bulky there versus making it on Mars. That might even include food when you remember that they'll need a lot of habitat volume for agriculture (you can save some space with hydroponics, but it tends to be labor-intensive and finicky versus regular agriculture - bad combination for a Mars bio-colony). But not all food, since I think they'd want fresh food frequently rather than living off of freeze-dried rations. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from foxforcefive](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199034) | [October 13, 2024 20:55](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199069)

**35:**

_The top-down command structure of reporting to the CEO might be less extreme than reporting to the King of Mars. Without any prior claims of sovereignty on the planet's surface, it could, in effect, be the default model._

Politely: bullshit.

The overriding benefit of democracy is that it provides a mechanism for the **peaceful** transfer of power when a government loses the minimum base of support it requires in order to persist.

Autocracies ... don't have that.

In practice over time most monarchies have softened about the edges enough to become constitutional monarchies -- ones with norms that the non-royals are comfortable with: the royals are figureheads, or at their most powerful presidential figures (the POTUS normally exercises roughly the authority of a late 18th century British monarch). Monarchies generally give their heirs the best possible education that can be crammed into their skulls (however short on natural aptitude they may be) and teach them a whole bunch of stuff about Duty and Responsibility and LAwful Action to keep them from acting out. Even so, about one time in five you end up with a Kaiser Wilhelm II or a Tsar Nicholas II or King Edward VIII who is manifestly _less than_ competent: if you're lucky you then get an abdication scandal and the crown passes to a younger and less incompetent sibling.

We don't have many examples of unhampered monarchies right now: the most noteworthy is North Korea, and I'm still expecting the Kim dynasty to go down in flames.

Now, on Mars there's no easy way for the Shit King to go into exile. Nor is it obvious that a Martian dynasty would be any better than a terrestrial one at throwing up competent leaders. The damage, if an inept monarch craters a Mars colony, would be far worse than on Earth -- think about where the air comes from. So it seems to me that monarchy on Mars would be prone to _very deadly_ succession crises, until a term-limited or lower jeopardy form emerged. If nothing else, consider the way the USSR's Central Committee functioned under Kruschev and then Brezhnev after they settled the fallout from Stalin's demise: even de-facto dictators want to die in bed surrounded by their grieving friends and family rather than in front of a firing squad or in an airlock.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=9119)

[Matthew2](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=9119) replied to [this comment from Wyvernsridge](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199063) | [October 13, 2024 20:58](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199070)

**36:**

The follow up to this is, assuming space settlement is possible, and there's some economic/political/religious rationale, how do you keep the settlement sustainable if the total fertility rate is 1.5? Keep shipping in people from Earth?

Whatever kind of society you have there, it has to operate in a way that allows for and encourages a TFR of at least 2.1 to continue, and a bit more than that if you want/need population growth.

We don't actually know how to have natural population increase without either 1) subsistence farming, which makes kids assets or 2) Gilead.

_One thing you don't touch on is the idea of radical alteration of humanity to make it more spaceworthy._

For the sake of these essays, I group that sort of thing in with the Singularity, FTL travel, anti-gravity, and so on under the heading "magic wands". Yes, if we had a magic wand we could cast spells -- but in the absence of magic wands, what can we plausibly expect to achieve?

[Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796) | [October 13, 2024 21:15](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199074)

**40:**

2 separate issues: How to go is the first. Why and what to do on arrival is the other.

The only really pressing impetus for migration away from Earth is the eventual expansion of the sun, at which point this planet will be subsumed. Other options might include early detection of a pending supernova that will vaporize us all, or a migrant black hole on its way. Insert various plausible non-human created world ending catastrophes here.

If we had say 200 years notice of the end of the world, it's an open question whether 'let's get some of us out of here' overcomes 'it's all a hoax burn the scientists and click the link to buy my supplements'. If we get 5 years notice we're toast. 

In the first instance, the expansion of our sun is still eons in the future. We'll either be extinct or will have long since advanced our technology to make such an escape trivial. Or we've developed a cultural hostility to science and die.

I've more or less accepted that canned monkeys will never travel the stars, at least as currently imagined. The most likely path will be a widespread seeding of van Neumann machines with some form of uploaded consciousness, possibly with the ability to recreate sapient monkeys (or cetaceans, or corvids, or arachnids) on discovery and adaptation of a likely spot. All happening on geologic time, where the furthest seeds are still travelling long after the home planet is subsumed into the expanding sun.

**44:**

_The only really pressing impetus for migration away from Earth is the eventual expansion of the sun, at which point this planet will be subsumed._

That lies about 4000My in the future. Reminder: vertebrate life is only about 518My old!

Of more pressing concern: as the sun brightens over time, it'll kick out more short-wavelength UV light, which in turn splits water molecules in the upper stratosphere into OH- and H+ ions; the H+ then recombines into H2 in the ionosphere and gets blown out of the solar system by the solar wind.

This leads to the dehydrogenation of the hydrosphere and then the upper mantle at which point Earth loses its oceans and tectonic subduction (which relies on hydrated rock layers for lubrication) grinds to a halt. When the plate tectonic system stops, we lose _all_ replacement of the lost hydrogen and the Earth gradually turns into a cooler version of Venus (i.e. baking hot rock with a blanket of CO2 and nitrogen). 

This happens within 1-2GY.

Worse: we exist in an atmosphere which is roughly 16% O2 by volume (that is, the partial pressure of oxygen, or p)2, is 16%). It supports combustion but wildfires are thankfully rare.

Below about 12% O2 you don't get sustainable combustion (I've visited libraries which artificially maintain a 12% pO2 specifically as a fire suppression system). But _above 30%_ even waterlogged biomass -- like your body -- will burn like a candle.

The pO2 in the atmosphere is gradually rising: at the start of the pre-Cambrian it was down around 1%. While there have been epochs when it was higher than it is today, today is close to peak pO2. If you extrapolate the curve into the future (a very iffy proposition, to be sure) we hit 25% or higher in about 500My, at which point lightning strikes will be triggering runaway wildfires everywhere on land where there's vegetation to burn.

So we can point to land becoming uninhabitable at T plus 500My, plate tectonics ceasing around 1000My, and Earth turning into Venus 2.0 by about 2000My.

None of which is _our_ problem because mammals have only had their run for the past 66My or so and will be long gone before then, but that's the sort of time scale we're talking about.

(There are ways to stretch the habitable duration of Earth, especially if you have the right kind of magic wand -- I tackled some of the trip hazards ahead in "Palimpsst" -- but we're veering wildly off-topic now.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7114)

[](http://www.illuminated.co.uk/) [Sam Kington](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7114) | [October 13, 2024 21:40](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199079)

**45:**

Among my mental notes for games I could run, is what I called "Social Democracy IIIIIIIINNN SPAAAAACE". It starts with the One Big Lie you're allowed in space opera, being wormholes that can take you from one solar system to another, and then asks: how would you decide who gets to colonise each planet, and how? (Let's assume you have a choice of so many that there are a good number of potential viable candidates.)

With a premise that people would have learned from experience (this might be the forbidden second big lie), what I ended up with was a tender process, backed by physical volunteers and a months- or years-long VR experiment.

So, for instance, a bunch of people might get together and say "we want to live in a solarpunk world, where we live side-by-side with nature, and we never killed the passenger pigeons". Some others might say "let's have a rugged individualistic world where there are still dinosaurs, dammit, and any politician has to be a _proper_ representative, not a member of an organised political party, and they'll wear the logos of their political backers on their suit like a goddamned NASCAR driver".

If your proposal gets enough support, you then take a bunch of the backers and put them into a reasonably-accurate VR simulation of the world they'd proposed (third big lie?), and see how they cope. Only if enough of them come out of it sane and say "I'd totally do that" do they get a go on a planet.

The players would be envoys from the equivalent of the UN, who go over and see how well it's going.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

This puts me in mind of Rawls' theory of justice, in which a system is only considered reasonably equitable if the inventor would be willing to experience the whole justice system from start to finish after being accused of a crime they didn't commit. (If they're willing to submit to it then there's some hope that it won't lead to wrongful convictions, excessive punishments, or other sub-optimal outcomes.)

**47:**

It's worth reading. They cite this blog!

The book reinforced my existing opinion: we are _so far_ from being able to construct any kind of self-sustaining settlement off Earth that all other questions are irrelevant. I mean, we have never successfully run a self-sustaining isolated environment that will support humans _on Earth_ (including Biosphere 2, which the book talks about at length), let alone in an actually hostile environment. We have no idea if humans can reproduce off Earth and getting to the point where that's an experiment anyone might want to try is many many decades away. The people saying 'oh we could just automate it all' do not have the slightest idea of how hard it is.

And the whole 'lifeboat' thing is just bullshit (as you imply). It would almost certainly be cheaper to build things that would enable humans to survive anything up to and including a Chicxulub-level event _on Earth_ than on Mars.

So I think what these models fail to account for is 'it doesn't matter because everybody dies'. While everybody is on the process of dying some kind of _Lord of The Flies_ horror.

**49:**

Lets see: New modes of space habitation: 

1: "Fun and exciting job openings in the lunar remote operator sector"

Very, very few humans on an industrial site that employs absurd numbers of teleworkers spread across timezones to provide 24/7 hands on the controls.

Economic justification: Useful chemical and industrial processes nobody wants to be anywhere near. Product delivered to earth by aero capture-> single use glider lander. 

2: We all go to heaven when we die: Uploads are, of course, prohibited from competing with the biological in earths job market. 

This rule does not, however, apply in space.

Any talk of independence from earth government is somewhat constrained by the fact that the only chip fab that can build the hardware an upload needs to exist... is still in Taiwan. Also the fact that the workload is reasonable with 90 % of cpu cycles spent fucking about in VR.

2a: Grimdark version "We all go to heaven to die": With the collapse of pension systems for Reasons that would piss anyone off that understood them, pensioners are shuffled off to mine the deep dark while hopped up on.. just all the drugs.. to either strike it rich or, you know.. Not. 

3: Space is Red: "Syndicalist mining coops in outer space.": Being utterly sick of US workers who really want to cosplay the american revolution 2.0... for the 4th time.. the international corporations running resource extraction ops in the asteroids instead hires a bunch of Red as hell union labor to run syndicalist subcontractors. Because at least the syndicalists understand that the point is to Make Money.

**51:**

I think the Antarctic model of a permanent station supplied from Earth is the most plausible. OK it’s expensive but space travel is getting cheaper and rivalry between the USA and China looks like it could provide political fuel in the same way that the moon landing depended on the US/CCCP rivalry. Arthur Clarke The Sands of Mars is one of the first SF books I read and looks a lot like the Antarctic model. No competing nations in the book though. 3D printing should make replacing worn out equipment easier. Fabricating electronics is more difficult but I suspect this will become practical in a couple of decades. Crops can be grown in surface domes while the habitats are underground or in lava tubes. Or vertical farms underground with artificial lighting. But I can’t see any economic or military incentive so the Antarctic model is the only one left. But I expect some economic invent would eventually develop from a scientific outpost if it survives for long enough. .

**53:**

"what do all these models fail to account for? "

Immortality, or at least cheap effective order of magnitude lifespan increase. This could be the tech innovation to kick off an urgent need to make self sustaining off- world habitats, if for no other reason to avoid lethal overcrowding on Earth. Once the miles long floaty terrariums in space get the kinks worked out, a project taking centuries or millennia, then a gradual drift outward from the solar system would just sort of happen by itself. Eventually a few would reach livable planets elsewhere, but if not, nobody would be too disappointed. I see it as a coral reef model of dispersal, with habitats alternating between motile and sessile forms.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5657)

[](http://dubiousprospects.blogspot.ca) [Graydon](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5657) | [October 14, 2024 00:08](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199088)

**54:**

An organism is a mechanism that turns food into shit.

An ecology is a mechanism that turns shit into food.

We don't know how this works.

The immensity of our ignorance is presently poorly quantified in this regard; it's not "there's a problem to solve" (like re-entry with aerobraking via non-ablative means) but "we haven't got names for the fields of study we don't know exist".

Unless and until we can build an ecology we can understand, the only possible spaceflight model is a stored-victuals sort of model where you can go out and maybe get simple things (gases, water) but your ability to do anything is constrained by your food stores. You could handwave destinations other than Earth (not very many of them; Cherryh's Union Space setting does this) where there's already a usable ecology on that planet, but everything in space must have a planetary ecology supplying it with the necessities of life. Call it the Ocean Liner model; quite large, quite fast, interesting cultural consequences, but no ability to create new destinations or otherwise expand the network.

Also, there's this common tendency to think of mass extinctions as "many organisms die". This is not correct; a mass extinction does involve lots of organisms dying, but the deaths are a side effect; the actual mass extinction is "the carrying capacity of the ecology drops substantially for much more than generational time".

Earth isn't going to recover Holocene carrying capacity for a good long while (> 1 Myears on the paleo record) and even if we're not extinct we're not necessarily going to have "big silly project" levels of surplus.

There's a parallel to a small, fragile (because small) ecology that's trying to convey people, too; the minimum size for reliability could be too large to undertake.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6734)

[Mickdarling](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6734) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199075) | [October 14, 2024 01:01](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199091)

**55:**

_RE: Education, environmental upkeep, food supplies, maintenance, and transport can all be automated today on another planet if you put the resources into it. Bullshit._

_If you think I'm wrong, prove it by pointing to examples of this happening here on Earth, where if things go wrong we can walk over and fix them in person._

Answering the last point first, isn't the whole point of these 'colonies' to have people there? If they are there, they can walk over and fix things. The goal of automation isn't to build a civilization from dirt to skyhook unattended, it's to allow the production of some large multiple of X (food, education, material production, maintenance, healthcare, etc.) per human needed to kick with a lunar boot when things go haywire.

As to the need for examples, and why you don't have them ready at hand here on earth, I should not have to be the one to explain to you that the economics here on Earth are different than they would be out there. Human labor is incredibly cheap here, as are plants, and air, and bugs, and full 1G gravity, and a host of other things.

**So lets tackle earth examples:**

**Education** : Primary thru Graduate level: Home schooling, with remote learning, along with correspondence courses. e.g. Edison was nearly entirely homeschooled. Spread ten kids out across mars and you can still hook em up to a remote learning setup, not far off what millions dealt with for years under Covid. Ramanujan famously learned mostly in isolation and through correspondence. Newton and Maxwell both did some of there most famous work in isolation. Data storage is cheap, and guided education through any reading material is not a hard problem when the cost of not being able to find the right manual will get you killed. 

**Environmental Upkeep and Food** : Check out <https://farm.bot/> for something that is highly automated. There are some large scale automated pot growing systems that put this to shame, and there are no unhurdlable obstacles to making these with easily plug and play designs that could be shipped off to any of a thousand orbiting rocks. Hydro and Airponics are also proven tech and highly automate-able. Bioreactors for Algae have been tried for O2 production and mostly failed, but again, the economics haven't incentivized the investment. I've worked on 3D printed tissue scaffolds that grew complex living tissue in bioreactors. The economics favored putting it on a shelf and millions more for research rather than productize anything. That is here on Earth, not if you are somewhere out there. The hardest parts might simply be dropping a large enough volume of environmentally controlled tin cans on whatever rock you are aiming at.

**Production and Maintenance** : Additive manufacturing for is obviously a lot easier than shipping up a full Ford Motor manufacturing plant prototyping machine shop. check out the video of a 3D printed rocket engine. [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aOFyRBsP-YY&t=24s](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aOFyRBsP-YY&t=24s) and the article here [https://builtin.com/articles/3d-printed-rocket#:~:text=3D%2DPrinted%20Rocket%20Definition&text=At%20this%20time%2C%203D%2Dprinted,travel%20and%20Mars%2Dbound%20missions.](https://builtin.com/articles/3d-printed-rocket#:~:text=3D%2DPrinted%20Rocket%20Definition&text=At%20this%20time%2C%203D%2Dprinted,travel%20and%20Mars%2Dbound%20missions.)

Maintenance doesn't need to be robots rebuilding robots. It just needs to be efficient enough and the urgency low enough for a few people to manage large infrastructure production and repairs. Remote Waldos are used today for nuclear power plants where the economics and safety trump doing things by hand. <https://www.jsm.or.jp/ejam/Vol.5No.1/NT/NT54/NT54.html#:~:text=The%20manipulator%20developed%20by%20MHI,This%20produces%20the%20following%20merits.> and <https://www.ans.org/news/article-6355/savannah-river-turns-to-drones-for-inspecting-waste-tanks/>

Oh, and check out just a few cool automation tools in the mining industry here: <https://design1st.com/portfolio/neptec-3d-lidar-sensor/> and literally tons of modern mining equipment has been automated to the point of rarely needing people in the driver's seat while in production. Onsite dynamic LIDAR doubles for **Transport** of goods and personnel too. Or, just drive really slow and remote from light minutes away. What's the hurry?

You asked for examples, so there you go. I suspect this is a game of No True Scotsman and no examples will ever be perfect to suffice in your present morose mood. Living in these different environments is an **process challenge**. The processes we have here only vaguely match what they will out there, but they are not inconceivable, or unmanageable. 

Maybe try to find insoluble problems here on Earth. Not ones that haven't been solved here, but ones that CANNOT be solved here. That might be the only problem you'll fail to solve.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5955)

[Troutwaxer](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229) | [October 14, 2024 01:32](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199093)

**57:**

I'm going to leave the social issues (colony vs. research station or whatever) aside because those are secondary issues. To have a SpacePlace where people can survive you need three things.

REALLY CHEAP DELTA-VEE: As in 'cheap-enough to ship a factory and it's workers to Mars for no more than you'd spend moving that factory from say... Michigan to Hong Kong.' This is obvious-enough. Various space-oriented billionaires are working on it and I think we'll get there eventually.

But once you've got really-cheap Delta Vee all the other problems get solved, because then it does become profitable/useful to ship factories/farms/people/cities to Mars (or wherever) _if you've got a reason to send them there._ Which brings us to...

SOMEPLACE WORTH GOING: For something other than tourism and/or scientific exploration? Where does the average person want/need to go in space that's actually worth going? What, if anything, does any place in space have that we don't have on Earth? Sure, I'd love to skip around Mars with a rock hammer, but at the cost of doing some version of astronaut training and spending 2-3 years on a spaceship? Nah. I think I'll head to Nepal 'cause I'm really curious about that place too...

Certain rare metals are probably worth leaving earth for. Maybe the product of some yet-to-be discovered unearthly life - weird alien hormones or Martian ivory or something.

But really. Why leave home?

LOW COMPLEXITY: How many people are going to go to space _without a guarantee_ if it requires five-ten years of education? Asteroid miner? Can you fix/reprogram every bit of your spaceship/suit? Do you have enough replacement parts? Do we really want to send out Ph.D-equivalents to dig the unobtanium mines?

Low complexity is difficult. AI might get us there eventually, but that means building a spacesuit that can solve all it's own problems itself without ever involving the human, who's got better things to do than fix the actuator. Same for the ship. And the tools. One of the big problems is how to solve the issue of complexity. Or perhaps a better phrasing is 'how to translate between what someone living in a biosphere with gravity does naturally to what someone living in no gravity with no biosphere must do automatically?' How does the monkey who's adapted to gravity/easy food deal with spaaaace?

Going back to Charlie's original question, how does the worldbuilding and background of a mid-term space epic get laid out...

We're eventually going to have cheap delta vee. Might take a few centuries, but we'll have it.

The complexity problem has to be solved somehow.

The real question any mundane science-fiction-epic writer needs to answer is _motivation._ Given what we know about spaaaace what would make anyone other than a scientist or asteroid miner leave earth? What makes rebuilding our paradigm of Space+Ship or HowToHaveSociety even worth considering?

Sharp, poisonous dirt? Liquid methane? The proper answer to such questions is "Libertarian space-scum fuck off."

Once motivation is established the rest of the questions probably answer themselves. The new version of Space+Ship is 'whatever scratches the motivation itch.' The new version of HowToHaveSociety also probably relates to motivation... and so on. It probably comes down to questions like "What form of politics works for both humans and trans-plutonian space-squids."

I don't have any good answers, but them's ma questions.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5955)

**58:**

Don't need low complexity if you can train the workers fast. 

Was a relevant DARPA project a while back: 

<https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/AD1002362.pdf>

If you have a technical specialty you want to cram into peoples heads it is possible to do _much_ better than our current educational system. Still probably looking at a year spent in simulated workstations and getting the initial tutors to train the computer on is going to be a.. bit of a challenge, but a space mining org could quite credibly grab bodies off the street and have them in space next year if the market for platinum metals or whatever justified it.

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Graydon](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199088) | [October 14, 2024 01:46](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199095)

**59:**

Words matter. Ecology is a science, ecosystem is a system of nutrient flows, which you're talking about. It's not mysterious, it freaking complicated.

The _easy_ way to think about life is that it produces local order by increasing universal entropy. Basically, a living planet burns faster than a non-living planet does, but it's more orderly while it burns.

Earth has a simple advantage, it's freaking huge, with huge stores of water, carbon, oxygen and so forth locked in the rocks, far more than life needs. Life can screw up rather badly, and the slow crustal recycling processes (volcanoes, erosion, subduction, and such) will eventually take the surplus crap out away from the surface, or put more necessary stuff back on.

This will keep going for another 1,400,000,000 to 1,500,000,000 years (in theory) before plate tectonics shuts down and we're stuck with living on the surface. The sun will make this place uninhabitable before then.

The whole point of Canned Monkeys Don't Ship well is that a spaceship has to be as small as possible to travel effectively, and this means there's little surplus. There's no mystery about this, only the near-impossible task of keeping screwups from happening that cause the just-in-time nutrient cycling of a starship from screwing up to the point of becoming uninhabitable for humans.

As for planets with biospheres...

A really simple way to think of this is the Buddhist point that YOU are an illusion. In ecosystem terms, you're not an isolated individual, but a part of an exceedingly intricate processing web with many inputs and outputs. We can simplify that chain to some degree (say on a space station) and keep people alive for a limited time, but in order to support humans indefinitely, we have to find ways to link all our necessary outputs and inputs into the bigger system we're supposed to be a part of, whether it's an alien planet or a spaceship. And if that bigger system isn't stable, neither is our life within it. I'm phrasing it this way to make it clear that shipping an isolated human to another planet is basically really expensive specimen delivery, not introducing a new species. Without being a part of a functioning ecosystem, that human will die. 

And the ultimate purpose of all this is to maximize entropy. So, basically, terrestrial life is Earth's way of chanting Hail Eris, All Hail Discordia. And space colonization is Earth's way of proselytizing other planets and getting them to join the cult. And I'm neither joking nor not joking in this.

**60:**

Reading this underlines what a good series the KSR Mars books are - he has to handwave life extension to get his characters to live through the social developmental processes and has magic industrial automation + space elevators to do a lot of the heavy lifting in the background, but he gets into the questions you are asking. There's a McMurdo modelled beginning, then company towns, homesteaders, polynesian model etc. All of these fail in different ways as the series progresses and only once they get to a large polity (aided by the aforementioned magic industrial systems and cheap-flight-enabling space elevators) do you see a real long term space colony emerge.

[levity leaven](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8829) | [October 14, 2024 04:05](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199099)

**63:**

Without a magic wand, the cost of lifting bodies out of gravity wells has somehow to be made worthwhile. If a "colony" means a livable joint for meatfolk, there's a minimum number of meatfolk you need ship there. If you hope to coerce them while they are in their joint then you will need either to be willing to ice the whole spot from orbit (in which case you will presumably need to be able to afford another one) or be willing to ship Space Maureens (like a Karen, but more heavily armed) to enforce your will upon them. The bigger and badder these enforcers are, the more it costs to get them back. Granted there are "softer" methods of enforcing compliance, but: what is the colony for? Is it just a stunt to see if you can raise baby humans (sorry, Terran organisms, humans were never actually specified) on another planet? 

Other objectives might be better served by a different idea of "colony". An ancient race, wealthy in resouces and lore (like the Hainish) might send robots falling about their local volume looking for interesting things to look at closer. If such a probe finds signs of "someone to talk to", maybe they attempt to send a message. Of course they are all "phoning home" to the best of their ability, albeit probably not with the magic-wand "ansible". If they find someone to talk to, they try talking. If they can talk, they have a conversation. The conversation might include an offer for "us", with our small fleet of robots in orbit around "their" sun, to build them a space elevator and ploop it down in a likely spot. This is a big lift for any imaginable non-magical robot, so would likely involve lots of interplanetary / interstellar comms, so don't expect anything to happen quickly- but if "they" accept the space elevator, we can send them more gifts from the inventory of the robots' memory and the mass of whatever rocks might be in nearby orbits. Also they can now send us (or rather, our robots) things that they make on or in their planet. If they decide to do that, and the things are intended for "us" and not for "our" robots (which are now their robots, effectively, if that's what they want) then the things will have to be brought back to us somehow. Since there's a flight going, some of "them" might want to visit us. If so, it's to be hoped we have a space elevator of our own, else the visit might be longer than intended. This assumes that "they" are capable of surviving such a trip, of course, but if all goes well you could develop an automated trade mission between two or more planets, more or less by accident, in this way. That's how you Ekoumene!

This all assumes _planetary_ colonies, and that a "space elevator" is something other than an extra-long, stretchy magic wand.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) | [October 14, 2024 04:06](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199100)

**64:**

Getting back to the question:

_What do all these models fail to account for? Are there other, better models for how to build and run a space colony? If not, why not—what are the universal points of failure (other than "human beings")?_

The question to me is: what's the point of the space colony? If it's to get away from Earth and not come back, then one of the islander models works best. The colony is home, keep people living there as long as possible. Something akin to an Australian aboriginal model might work...everybody in the colony has a role to play, and generally everybody knows at least some of the other roles really well, because family members, friends, etc. play those roles. So if a role goes unfilled, someone else steps in, and then they train up the successor when one becomes available. Given current birth rates, I think that keeping a constant population is a good enough goal. And of course roles can evolve over time.

If the purpose of the space colony is something else, then there is a mission, and the strategy to fulfill that mission needs to be the driving force in the structure of the colony. Shipping out so that your children twelve generations removed can kill the bug eyed monsters seems pointless, but whatever. The underlying mission is likely to be some form of entropy maximization by spreading terrestrial-style dissipative structures off Earth. Yay! The question then becomes whether the colony is aimed at maximizing entropy as fast as possible (go berserk, disrupter bros!) or maximizing entropy over the extremely long term (starward ho, you eco-freaks!Moar kiddies wherever possible!)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=9123)

[](http://norightturn.blogspot.com/) [IdiotSavant](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=9123) | [October 14, 2024 04:18](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199101)

**65:**

A lot of the people talking about "automation" and "AI" seem to be ignoring the requirement for self-sufficiency. This means not just food and water and air, but "reproduc[ing] almost anything the humans living there need, including more human workers raised from infancy". And this does in fact require replicating not just the entire required industrial stack (to supply spare parts and manufacture new things), but also the _educational_ one (to train the people to work that stack, and the people who will train those people into the future on an ongoing basis). And if you want a magic wand to solve the problems, then it means your society must be able to train the people to design, operate, troubleshoot, build and fix it - because a society which imports magic wands or relies on them being reproduced by magic is not self-sufficient. If everyone dies when your magic wands fail, then you are not a self-sufficient colony.

"Were historical colonies ever actually self-sufficient?" is an interesting question. Because the successful ones never actually needed to be, and even when they'd reproduced their (painfully short) tech and educational stack, they still imported vast quantities of _people_ , to make up for losses and provide skills and population growth. And on that front, I think Matthew2's [comment upthread](https://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199070) about fertility rates may be relevant; it turns out that modern global society, the only way we currently know to sustain anything like the technological base needed for a space settlement, is _not_ self-sufficient in people. Fortunately we've got a lot of them (for now...), but a space settlement won't.

So, I think "Antarctic science base" or "remote resource-extraction site with rotating workforce and few permanent inhabitants" or maybe even "settlement permanently supported by migration" are workable models, depending on technological assumptions about transport. Actual self-sufficiency requires different social models (fully automated luxury space communism?) 

(Meanwhile, I am feeling a sudden need to read my copy of Ed Wright's _Ghost Colonies_ , about places that failed. It lists "ignorance, naivety, disease, internal fighting [and] attacks from outside" as reasons for failure. The first four seem like they could still be an issue in space)

what everyone sees as a chip...isn't

the exterior shell is various combo of basic plastics and straightforward metal alloys for the prongs... locally sourced feedstocks no matter how expensive still cheaper than lifting to LEO...

the actual circuits is about 2%(?) of the mass

so... sheets of circuit chips shipped up 'n out... locally sliced and encased... then robot spotwelds completed chips onto motherboard

yeah... Earth still holds their leash... but costs are reduced... 

big, big plus?

sheets of chips being much lower in bulk can be properly stored against cosmic rays and thoroughly locked up... 

uh, maybe not so good from colonist perspective {ooooopsie}

[Howard NYC](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8141) replied to [this comment from Graydon](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199088) | [October 14, 2024 05:10](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199104)

**68:**

Basics of modern economics:

** At various moments, without warning, new insights into science lead to new ideas.

** Factories are built to leverage new ideas into finished products.

** A factory is a mechanism that turns raw materials into finished products.

** A consumer is a mechanism that turns finished products into damaged bits too worn out to function.

** Some portion of garbage could be melted down and used as raw materials.

** A significant portion of garbage cannot be recycled.

** Given enough time, there's an occasional insight into how to exploit previous abandoned garbage.

** We don't know how this works, how all the various bits 'n pieces fit together.

in space, there is plenty of room for garbage dumps but demands for useable raw materials will always exceed supply... not enough tools because there's not enough factories building tools
