[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) | [October 14, 2024 05:21](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199105)

**69:**

I just realized one thing that's missing from the original question: _What do all these models fail to account for? Are there other, better models for how to build and run a space colony? If not, why not—what are the universal points of failure (other than "human beings")?_

There's a problem with STL colony ships that we're mostly not thinking about: the technology that makes STL interstellar travel possible gets developed and deployed on Earth first. 

So, basically, we're talking about skyscrapers that can support their occupants for years at a time, reasonably lightweight shielding that can resist the explosion of a decent-sized nuke, being able to hurl megatonnes at a consequential fraction of the speed of light, and so forth.

Can you imagine what a trillionaire like, oh, Leo Husk would do with his inviolable Fortress of Desolation? Literally nothing can touch him and his minions in there for decades at a time. Or we could control both global warming and the spread of pest species by having regular nuclear wars between arcologies that hate each other. All that dust lofted would keep the Earth cool, and making the weapons would keep the military industrial complex going...

So that's the feedback part of the loop: technologies that are absolutely essential to make a STL ~~transmissible~~ viable would be rather unpleasant on the surface of a planet, but they have to be developed on a planet before they can be flown.

The feed-forward loop is, of course, that the cultures that developed within these bombproof arcologies are the ones going to the stars. 

We're in Torment Nexus Land again, aren't we?

May I suggest that we pray that FTL works and we live in a non-causal universe? Then, the answer for how a FTL ship's culture is designed for a particular mission might be: "we're doing the mission to find out."

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8394)

[AndrewMck](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8394) | [October 14, 2024 05:47](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199106)

**70:**

My problem with all these models is you can’t go from here to there. You need a transitional stage or stages:

Start with near earth bases, supported in same way as Antarctic bases. Imagine a reason for them to be established and then grow. Imagine new technologies to support this growth. Imagine reasons to continue expansion towards Luna, asteroid belt, Mars etc. Imagine new technologies that allow for increasing levels of self sustainability/reasons for continued growth. When big enough, imagine reasons to move whole lot to new location.

Given the ‘imagines’ in this it is a science fiction plot, not a realisable plan.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8394)

[AndrewMck](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8394) | [October 14, 2024 05:56](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199107)

**71:**

Or, to put it another way, it can’t happen as a mission or a Manhattan project. It could only happen as the contingent outcome of long term investments for other reasons that just happened to creat sufficiently large and flexible sustainable societies that then had a reason to move away from Earth

**72:**

»Other options might include early detection of a pending supernova that will vaporize us all«

The bad news: By the time we might have an actionable probability, it is far too late to go anywhere that will offer better protection than this particular rock. (Hint: Radiation from supernova travel at c, we do not.)

The good news: Being 25-40 meter under the surface of this rock (or it's water) is sufficient shielding to survive any supernova which does not strip the atmosphere from the planet or boil the oceans.

The not so good news is that there is a high probability that the surface of the planet will be sterilized to a depth of a meter or more, so /any/ land biology you want afterwards, must be dragged to safety underground or underwater.

[Mikko](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4631) | [October 14, 2024 08:04](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199109)

**73:**

This was a fun read, and thought-provoking. I'm currently re-reading Robinson's Mars trilogy, and it feels... quaint. I read it maybe twenty years ago, and then it wasn't as much an alternate history (of course it wasn't!). But I'm not seeing a joint US-Russian Mars mission launching in 2026.

I don't remember that much about it, just the big picture, though. It's nice to re-read it.

I also have the Weinersmiths' 'City on Mars' book reserved from the library, I'll pick it up later this week. I expect it'll be a nice contrast to the over thirty-year old scifi.

And as a third thing, I continued playing the computer game 'Per Aspera', which is about building a Mars base, first using robots and then building settlements for humans. The goal seems to be to terraform Mars and build on it. I played it a year ago for maybe twenty hours, just beginning to see how it works, and now I continued my game. 

However, I have conflicting feelings about the game. It's a nice little builder game, you extract resources and use them to build stuff, and then extract more resources (and maybe crash Deimos on the planet...). There is obviously the constraint of how much stuff there _is_ to extract. I like the gameplay, but I can't help thinking should I be doing this. One plays an AI controlling the colony system and the player has a lot of control over the building mechanics, but the politics of the people living on Mars can't be even seen. 

There is some politics involved, and I haven't seen the complete game, so I don't know the whole plot. Still, I feel like it's the worst of the Mars projects: there's a planet, let's go and dig it up to build more stuff, and irreversibly destroy what was there. (I seem to side with Ann from the Mars trilogy even more than I did earlier.)

I'll keep going on playing it, however, if only to see what the plot is. My Utopia Planitia base was already flooded, so I have made big changes to Mars already.

I think whatever method you choose for your bases outsidce of Earth, they will be on the very edge of failure for a long, long time, maybe for ever. As long as they don't have the complete ecosystem with them, they are dependent on Earth, and in most cases help for any problem is months or years away, if it ever arrives. Any disruption in the deliveries from Earth will mean possible failure (and deaths), even if nothing goes wrong in the base in the first place.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4672)

**74:**

On the notion of a "closed cycle system" I'm afraid... they don't exist. For Earth it is first and foremost the output of the Sun. On that system-level time-scale other matter coming from space is also significant (as the lack of neighborhood T. Rexes attests).

I'm sure we can always disregard factors, but the very nice premise of the article here is pointing out biases. I'm still of the strong opinion we should first ~~colonize~~ inhabit the vast oceans... Not holding my breath for that one though (pun intended).

**76:**

What do those models fail to account for. Mostly, I think you're already being too generous with the models you have. Making the assumption that any colony will last long enough to complete a single pregnancy is not something I would put money on. If the colony is on a planet or moon - Luna or Mars are most likely - then some dust will get in. We don't know what this will do to a large group of humans, or the various mechanical and biological systems keeping them alive.

Are there better models. Without significant technical advances, the only model I can see working is the current LEO or polar base model. A small crew, hugely inefficient use of resources because nearly everything has to be shipped a long way, and any local production is just a bonus. This would be a more sensible way to approach the problem. Instead of trying to create a self-sustaining colony, gradually develop a slightly less dependant colony. Success would be locally producing 10% of the required food, water or oxygen.

**79:**

You are of course right, but that wont stop us going to space and having semi permanent residence there I think.

Military Think of what Space can deliver that we CANT get here, For the military its pretty simple. Space is the ultimate high ground. I would envision the start to be just nation states "claiming" or using the high ground which will eventually grow to "forward bases". The problem is this idea has a economy of heat death feel about it. The further "out" you go, the more "high your ground" but the more expensive it is to maintain and the less value the high ground is. The Earth facsimile is a forward base in some hell hole on earth (thinking Afghanistan), Humans get cycled in and out along with the supplies needed.

Tourism Then there is tourism and the ever expanding need to get more "unique" or "Extreme" experiences (meaning further out and more isolated). These would be like a cross between cruise ships and hyper isolated cabins in the woods on earth. Except I think the economics of this mean the only humans using it are going to be the customers. I doubt there would be many human "staff", so dependent on the state of robotics and automation.

Lastly, "Industry". This I suspect would be the biggest. Making medicines and technology in space because space has zero G or near Zero G... you cant replicate that on earth. In fact I think its the only thing that space can offer that we cant find an example of a facsimile situation here. We can go anywhere and do anything on earth... so I'm not sure what sort of model this would follow. Probably something similar to the military model but with "space university" thrown in (because knowing what you can industrialize in space will require humans to research and discover in space).

**81:**

I want to dig into the self-sufficiency question, not in terms of whether it's possible, but if it's actually desirable from the perspective of the Terrestrial organisation putting forth its resources to found an extra-Terrestrial colony.

I think that a highly capable and structured Terrestrial organisation is always required by a project of this scale, and that self-sufficiency necessarily runs counter to the interests of that kind of organisation. Whatever their reasons for wanting to have such a colony, setting up high-value supply chains aren't at the heart of it, and remaining the provider of (chips, thorium, vaccines) keeps that relationship the way they want it.

Looking at historical colonies on Earth, they also aren't self-sufficient for these things either (then steel, gunpowder; now pharmaceuticals, aircraft) until much later in their lifecycle.

So my question is; how much, and what kind, of self-sufficiencies must the parent organisation set up to achieve its objectives (military, religious, economic (hah!))? What are they motivated to keep back?

[tfb](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5897) replied to [this comment from Poul-Henning Kamp](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199108) | [October 14, 2024 14:59](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199119)

**82:**

I think this gives a good example of how hard things are off the Earth and how deeply silly the lifeboat idea is.

If there's a supernova which sterilises the surface (but does not strip the atmosphere / boil the oceans) then (a) there's probably lots of stuff left in the oceans, and (b) there's an awful lot of what was previously living stuff on the surface. So if you take with you stuff that will grow in the remains, it will likely grow.

(I have ignored what happens to the climate / composition of the atmosphere, but the very worst is almost certainly not worse than Mars.)

So a 'lifeboat' on Earth is likely more survivable even in the case of something that sterilises the planet, if I'm right (I may not be). Not to mention a supernova which sterilises Earth is going to be equally bad for any crops being grown using sunlight on Mars.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Pete Wildsmith](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199118) | [October 14, 2024 15:17](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199120)

**83:**

_Looking at historical colonies on Earth, they also aren't self-sufficient for these things either (then steel, gunpowder; now pharmaceuticals, aircraft) until much later in their lifecycle._

Don't be so sure about that. Cortes brought a blacksmith with him (Hernando Serrano), and they went looking for the materials they needed to make their own gunpowder.

The other thing to remember is that the vast majority of hominid colonists were mostly/entirely self-sufficient. Here I'm talking about the people who first settled Eurasia, Australia, the Americas, most islands. The Oceanians did in fact ship stone from good quarries to nearby islands that weren't so lucky, so they weren't entirely self sufficient, just mostly.

This brings up the basic point, that most of what we think of as colonization from the last thousand years or so (starting with Spain in the Canary Islands) is better seen as a hostile takeover, not settling where no humans have ever been. Very few people have ever seen a place where no humans have ever been (deep sea biologists, people working in some parts of New Guinea), and I'm not sure that we understand what that means for imagining a colony culture that can deal with such things.

**84:**

_By the time we might have an actionable probability, it is far too late to go anywhere that will offer better protection than this particular rock. (Hint: Radiation from supernova travel at c, we do not.)_

Unless our astrophysics is completely wrong, we will be aware of an impeding supernova for tens of millennia before it happens. Supergiant stars within 50 light years are hard to miss.

Of all stars visible with naked eye, Betelgeuse is the most likely candidate for blowing up next, and at 640 light years, it will be just a light show. If it were ten times closer, it would be a great impetus to develop relevant technologies.

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) | [October 14, 2024 15:44](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199122)

**85:**

Different cultural issue: Navy versus Air Force.

The point is that our default model for long duration space travel is an oceangoing vessel, because historically those have been the systems that were isolated for long durations (cf all the Brits operating in the Pacific until the Mid-20th Century). I'm not knocking the naval model of the captain as god's representative on the ship, authority-wise.

No, the issue is that space colonization (by satellites and now military systems) has been run primarily through various Air Forces, which have a very different, almost corporate culture. 

The model we're used to for this is, of course, commercial air travel, where the pilot is in charge of the plane. Can you imagine a pilot in charge of a generation ship? Ship captain feels more natural, but that's not where the culture went.

This leads to a couple of ideas. One is that, for a long-term manned space mission, the ship(s) might actually bring Mission Control along with them. They'd effectively be the board of directors, telling the pilot/captain what to do. The captain of the ship would serve a term, then rotate out, following the astronaut model of NASA. Indeed, NASA and the USSF's model of astronaut training might be used to create a cadre of officers capable of running the ship, taking the helm in turn, rather than by time in rank or other means, and if they serve long enough, they become part of mission control. There actually is a model for this: Republican Rome, and the cursus honorum. Replace the latter with sets of skill tests rather than voting, and it might work in a ship.

The other thing to be concerned about is that the USAF/USSF has been historically tied to the NRO (National Reconnaissance Organization) for a very long time. This is especially true with the USSF now, as it is primarily an information-based force now, not apparently capable (yet?) of putting boots in the sky. I'm quite sure this tie to military intelligence would work its way into any manned deep space missions undertaken by the USSF or similar organizations. I'm just not sure how it would work in practice. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8752)

[rocconteur](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8752) | [October 14, 2024 16:00](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199123)

**86:**

The only way I could imagine a reason to do the offworld colonies would be one of two reasons - an emergency or going into space for an impossibly damn good reason.

In the first case, I could imagine something like a seveneves, or something like "solar flair predicted to sterilize earth in 50 years so there's a movement to get us up and out." I think in that case, you'd be assuming that emergency ingenuity/gun-to-the-head scenario would lead us to invent what we needed to in order to survive. It really just boils down to "if we don't leave we all die and I'm a far-off observer ergo we all left" which isn't the best logic, I know. 

The only other reason I could see it would be immortality. Some scientist determines that there is a way to engineer the human body to be functionally immortal - body can endlessly repair itself, body can keep the brain fairly young and plastic, body can survive for long stretches on tiny amounts of sunlight and trace water/minerals. Obviously can't survive being hurled into a sun or shot into space for 100,000 years or ground up in a mulcher, but "daily wear and tear" becomes a thing of the past. 

The drawback would be that it doesn't work in a gravity well of any magnitude. At this point I'm worldbuilding, I know. Maybe it can live in Moon gravity? Maybe it can "hang out" in gravity for short stints, for fun, like going swimming. But the bioprocess that repairs/rejuves shuts down in any gravity. 

It brings up the sad start that the first few people who get the treatment are the rich, which sucks.

Still: outposts in the asteroid belt make more sense. Nearing 90, have millions in your government health fund, don't want to die? Government gets your money, you get the injection and care until "extremis style" your genes get re-worked and the biochanges are all done. Off you go to space, shot to the belt, to one of the communities getting set up there. You're in many ways taking a tech step backwards - yes, your new body can survive off of a sip of water a day and a few hours in a light room, but life under those conditions suck. There's the 'net - maybe everyone has the VR stuff built into their bodies now, with a lump on their back supporting a built in CPU so again self-contained. Some kind of algae exists that functions as a wifi repeater and it's spread around the asteroids. Maybe you have to live and work for a few generations before there's 3d-printed matter to make hamburgers. 

But it's better than dying, maybe. I like to think if we had VR, games/entertainment, drugs, some sort of sex, and immortality, we'd figure it out.

**87:**

»not apparently capable (yet?) of putting boots in the sky.«

USAF had plans in that direction back in the early days of spaceflight, some years ago NASA found some of the kit they developed for USAF astronauts.

After WWII real-time weather reports from space was a BIG military dream, no more flying bombers to a clouded city etc. Absent any other technology, "boots in the sky" were the plan.

The program was overtaken by the feasibility of primitive imaging weather satellites and computerized spy-satellites, and was silently dropped.

I do not think anything about that calculus has changed in any meaningful way, unless one is very subscribed to the "Lone Ranger" mentality.

Reagan's "Starwars" was also planned as uncrewed.

**89:**

_I thought this a lot when watching "The Expanse". The ships in this setting look pretty complicated, yet every Belter can do at least their routine maintenance, if not necessarily major repairs._

This has sort of come up on this blog at times.

Many of us like our current auto tech level. But we tend to think repairs are about the same level of difficulty as 1960s and earlier.

And they are NOT.

SciFi tends to do the same.

My father would tell about the 1930s Plymouth he was driving just after WWII while attending the local community college. The main bearings were shot. So he would take newspaper, fold it into dense narrow strips and put this between the crank and block. He said he'd do this most Fridays end of day. And got so good at it that he could still make his date that night.

That's just now how space ship repair will work in the somewhat near future. There would need to be something between 3D printing tech and magic wand replicator tech to make such things workable.

**90:**

While I agree with you that there’s little use in putting combat astronauts in orbit, I’m quite sure they’re thinking about it.

The reason is that the US Air Force had its roots in the US Civil War with a contractor putting up a spotter balloon with a telegraph cable to the ground. By 1905, there was an air regiment in the Signal Corps, flying lifting kites and balloons for artillery spotting and aerial photography, and they incorporated airplanes early on. Then in WW1, they took on a direct role in combat.

I’m quite sure the USSF is thinking about how to recapitulate that development with combat astronauts, if they can ever find a role for them. And no, I’m not sure there is a roll for them, because orbital speeds are so fast that they’d need computers to aim and fire anyway.

In the meantime, we have drone-on-drone warfare becoming important in Ukraine. I’m guessing that kind of warfare is more achievable for the USSF. 

Anyway, these are our space dudes, so if we’re talking about space culture, they’re going to be one of its predecessors. “Mission Control on Mars” may well be how a Mars colony is run.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4869)

[](http://scrivenerserror.blogspot.com) [Jaws](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4869) | [October 14, 2024 17:06](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199128)

**91:**

Any "military model" colony will collapse rather rapidly; it's inherently not viable.

• This is no longer Napoleonic navies, in which the 90% lower ranks are filled with uneducated lower-class boors incapable of thinking beyond the end of the next work period. Fictional versions tend to founder on the "generalist versus specialist" problem, with incompatible means of promotion and of exercise of authority; as a really obvious example, prioritizing the space guns over the environmental plant is going to have consequences in space even more dire than in earthbound submarines.

• Nobody ever thinks about things like "Where is the next generation of lieutenants/ensigns coming from, how do I train them, how do I weed out the unsuitables?" or "What do I do when there's nothing beyond the Peter Principal?" or "Who's in charge of the Fletcher Memorial Home for Incurable Tyrants... and Kings... and Retired Generals?" This way lies "inherited"/"purchased" leadership roles, even in purportedly nonaristocratic societies, and senior leadership holding on for decades after they've passed their sell-by dates.

• Long-term military organizations depend on substantial rotation-in and rotation-out of both resources and personnel over periods far shorter than those of truly isolated-by-space posts ("space is big"). Even in those instances of someone working from "bottom to top," acquiring the distinct skillsets applicable to different ranks (even more than the specialized-knowledge problem) is virtually impossible when stuck among the same relatively-small subpopulation for everything.

• Putting this as gently as possible, military organizations don't cope well with rapidly changing perceptions of circumstances. Recent specific example: "Europe" from early October 1987 through August 1991 — approximately "fall of Berlin Wall +/- two years." The consequences (repeatedly) within the next decade are appalling examples of dysfunction that have remained outside the awareness of even fiction-writers.

Just not going to make it past the first six to eight years. But planning for transition out of such a model assumes a level of foresight on the challenges that will actually be experienced that will epitomize a "failure of the imagination." Think Apollo One... without being able to go home at night to a comfortable suburban home in Houston or Orlando (because, if nothing else, there's no Houston or Orlando). 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8691)

[Martin Rodgers](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8691) | [October 14, 2024 17:10](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199129)

**92:**

The complexity problem space is hard, and we've barely identified the size of it, nevermind the solutions.

One example: city transport complexity. Every "colony", whatever form it takes, will have some equivalent. As the LA joke puts it, "Where ever you want to go, it'll take you an hour." I.e. the cost will always be higher than expected, until you adjust your expectations.

All the "colonies in space" stories we have, whether in SF or from spacebros, handwave problems like this or ignore them entirely. Delany attempted to address this in _The Stars in My Pocket like Grains of Sand"_ , with his three examples of "cultural fugue", all wildly different. (OTOH FTL, aliens etc.) Bruce Sterling made some small attempt in Schismatrix, refering to "Prigogenic levels of complexity", but that was just setting up the final part of the novel. I do like how he portrayed the instability of polities, and the precariousness their citizens. (OTOH aliens, the implication of FTL, etc.)

So, who is addressing this problem in the real world discussion of, say, colonies on Mars? Maybe someone has, but I've yet to find any examples.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from peter w](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199125) | [October 14, 2024 17:20](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199130)

**93:**

_The NRO is presumably focussed on looking down to earth. I assume that until there is deep space activity by other nations they wouldn't find much of interest in Martian orbit. The chances of something hostile coming from there are a million to one..._

Hmmm. I wasn’t thinking about the tech, so much as the culture of the agency. 

Obviously I know very little about how the Nitwits, Rubes, and Oafs (their joke) work. What I can see from the outside of “Black NASA” is that they’re closely intertwined with the USSF and with military contractors, so there’s presumably a lot of crossover among the three cultures. They’re techs, not into wet work like the CIA (again AFAIK. The USAF does have spec ops people. Do they sheep dip them through the NRO?).

Trevor Paglen’s old book on “badge int”, studying secret military and intelligence programs from the badges they generated had one retiree calling the badges “gang signs,” which might give an idea of how workers in the different NRO programs relate to each other. Kind of like The Laundry? Imagine that kind of infighting on a deep space voyage.

That’s why I suggested that a “Mission Control on board” scenario might resemble the Roman Republican model (see ACOUP for details, he ran a. series on how it worked). Basically, Roman culture was strongly hierarchical and based on patron-client relationships (see gang badges, above). Every male citizen was liable for conscription (universal service aboard ship), and those who went into politics started low in the army and worked their way up via election (switch this for training). Once they hit a certain level, they were inducted into the Senate (Mission Control) for life. The Senate chose two highly qualified senators to be Consuls to sun things, with a term lasting for a year (ship's captains). 

There are a few nice things about this model, at least for SFF: it superficially makes sense, it might even work, a writer can learn the Roman system from online sources like ACOUP, it can scale up to the size of an entire off world colony, as Rome scaled up, and it has some spectacular failure modes (ave Caesar). 

Oh, and the secretive and possibly corrupt public-private partnership thing hints at another reason for offworld colonies: they make for the ultimate in protected offshore financial institutions. Imagine an offshore financial center run by civilian contractors at the USSF military colony on Mars? Notionally they are there to coordinate the inter world finances of the crew, but that’s not how they make the money that keeps the base supported by Earth.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8691)

[Martin Rodgers](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8691) replied to [this comment from Martin Rodgers](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199129) | [October 14, 2024 17:23](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199131)

**94:**

Yes, apologies for misspelling his name. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ilya_Prigogine>

We haven't even solved these problems on this planet, never mind any other. As you've pointed out, the failure modes anywhere else are brutal. Here at least we can recover. Here we can pretend complexity problems won't (and don't) bite us. We, as a species, have been ignoring the evidence.

Perhaps these ideas are still too knew to enter the cultural memespace. That will certainly bite us. Some have argued that it already has. I'm inclined to agree.

I've long believed that if we can't solve these problems _here_ , we'll never solve them anywhere else. Now I suspect it's far worse. Too many people believe only their nation state can do it. This is the NOBUS delusion - "Nobody but us can do this." I fear this could get ugly at the nation state level. Whoever feels they've lost this race may do anything to undermine everyone else. How bad could that get?

Ok, add that one to the pile of complexity problems.

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from Mickdarling](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199091) | [October 14, 2024 17:23](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199132)

**95:**

_Education: Primary thru Graduate level: Home schooling, with remote learning, along with correspondence courses. e.g. Edison was nearly entirely homeschooled._

Right.

Please explain to me how home schooling works for _any_ course that requires hands-on experience in a laboratory or workshop. Bear in mind that education is _not_ just learning from texts. And bear in mind that Edison was born 177 years ago: his schoolroom would have reflected the requirements of 160-180 years ago, and if you've ever glanced at a century-old syllabus you'd know most of it is hopelessly inapplicable today, never mind to a kid on a space colony.

Farmbot: this is bullshit promotional puffery from a techbro startup, not a working farm in a box, much less a closed-loop biosphere in a box. We don't know how to do that. Previous attempts haven't worked very well (cf. Biosphere 2.)

Automation and 3D printing is nice: but it's not self-maintaining and self-callibrating, and there's some question over how much human intervention is going to be needed in any semi-automated factory. Put it another way: let's see if Amazon can run a lights-out factory (no humans) on Earth for more than 24 hours without some schmuck with a spanner having to run it to tighten something before we propose doing something even harder ten light-minutes away.

You ask for insoluble problems on Earth. Well, the challenge I'd like to see us meet is: build a Biosphere 3, like Biosphere 2 only sized to hold 100 humans and feed/aerate/nourish them for 12 months with no inputs except energy. If we can hit _that_ goal then we might be ready to ship a radiation-hardened version sized for 20 people over 5 years to Mars. Until then ... better have a return ticket and a year's worth of MREs close to hand.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

[JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093) | [October 14, 2024 17:44](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199133)

**96:**

You mention the Pilgrims, but what about Jamestown? 

What I know of the history suggest it was founded as an _outpost_ based on the exploitation of natives for **gold**. Those who initially came to Jamestown expected to get rich & return to England with their riches. They didn't expect to stay there.

It began morphing into a colony when the natives proved to have insufficient gold to exploit (and insufficient surplus food to feed themselves and the "colonists"- leading to the first conflicts). 

The initial "colonization" effort was meant only to establish self sufficiency for the outpost because supply from the mother country was so difficult & unreliable ... and continued to rely on exploitation of the natives for some time after it became a _"colony"_.

Where are our space colonists going to find Martian natives to exploit? 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199132) | [October 14, 2024 17:48](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199134)

**97:**

OTOH, the US military routinely does do distance learning. It’s required for moving up in ranks. The current VP candidate Tim Walz retired a rank lower than his acting rank, in part because he retired before he could complete the distance learning coursework required to hold his brevet rank.

And my wife, like many professionals, is constantly doing continuing education to maintain her certification, all online.

On the flip side, I agree that initially learning a subject is hard if you only see it online, people do it, but the success rate is lower than in person group classes and far lower than tutoring.

My guess is that, for children on a spaceship, they’ll probably get taught via a combination of tutoring, mixed age learning, apprenticeships where passing a skills exam is important, and distance learning for “book subjects.” Thing is, it would have to be a really big ship for age-segregated classes to be necessary to handle the number of children, and educators know anyway that mixed age and one on one education work massively better anyway. 

Regardless, everyone in space would have to be a lifelong learner.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229)

**99:**

_This is no longer Napoleonic navies, in which the 90% lower ranks are filled with uneducated lower-class boors incapable of thinking beyond the end of the next work period. Fictional versions tend to founder on the "generalist versus specialist" problem, with incompatible means of promotion and of exercise of authority_

Niven and Pournelle tried to address this in CoDominium/Mote stories: they tailored their fictional physics (FTL and force fields) so as to make Galactic Empire consciously modeled on 19th Century British Empire, and Imperial Navy complete with capital and support ships, broadsides, teenage midshipmen, barely literate recruits, and all but "rum, sodomy and the lash" (actually, lash is mentioned) _to make sense._

Yet, even given their tailor-made physics AND abundance of human-compatible planets throughout Milky Way, I found the results implausible.

[Retiring](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8783) | [October 14, 2024 18:29](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199138)

**101:**

Here are a couple of things to think about.

First off, in a human colony, what's a human? Pohl in the later Gateway novels had people uploaded to computational media. Seems much simpler logistics if energy is the only requirement. So do away with biological bodies. Ship copies of uploaded interesting people, living in simulated virtualities, in all directions.

Secondly, isn't Clarke's Law something on the order of "Sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic"? Lots of SF with space colonies basically rely on this; aliens provide "magic wands" based on principles humans don't understand. And who's to say they're wrong? Perhaps the human mind is simply unable to grasp certain concepts that exist in the universe. Perhaps other intelligences can see things differently.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796)

[Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796) | [October 14, 2024 18:34](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199139)

**102:**

On a couple of occasions I've spent some months on a ship at sea. Complex, involving constant (CONSTANT) ongoing maintenance work by many people, with entropy a constant and active participant in everything. 

Even in modern ships there are enough shifting variables that 'if something unexpected goes wrong quickly we all might die today' is a real thing that is on your mind, most days. When I worked the offshore fishing fleet we would hear about it happening on the radio to people we knew, regularly enough to trigger some deep reflection and a career change for me. Usually starting a distress call or just 'Has anyone heard from the guys on the Hope Bay lately?' and ending with a futile search.

I admit I struggle to imagine how to structure such a precarious social exercise as anything other than 'captain in charge, crew with well-defined and mutually redundant skillsets'. There have been various attempts at other forms of decision making and control at sea, but the ocean is nothing if not ruthless at pruning out nonviable modes of survival. Space would be more brutal, not less.

That said, a ship could do that for a year, even five. Not for decades without some form of deliberative renewal of power structures. Power corrupts, and the failure modes are horrible. Examples abound.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8792)

[alantyson](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8792) | [October 14, 2024 18:35](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199140)

**103:**

I'm also on the side of we need to figure out why we're doing this before we figure out how.

I'm a romantic when it comes to a human presence in space, but if I set that aside, the only practical reason I can possibly think to send people to other worlds (the Moon included) is if 1a) there's something there that we can't get on Earth, or 1b) there's something there in far greater quantities and concentrations than there is on Earth, and 2) we cannot use automated technology to get it for us.

Reasons 1A and 1B keep cropping up every few years, only to be revealed as impractical or just plain wrong not too long after (see the whole Helium-3 thing). But, let's say we did find an economically-significant source of something we want, say on Mars or an asteroid. There might be an argument for processing the stuff on site, and then sending a mostly-finished product back to Earth: pollution and other unwanted by-products. There's those who argue that a shift from internal combustion cars fueled by fossil fuels to electric cars powered by lithium batteries is no great improvement, because of the ecological cost of lithium mining (full disclosure, I own an electric car and have squared that ethical circle for myself, but it is something I think about from time to time). Ditto copper, iron, molybdenum, heck even just rock and sand quarries produce some truly awful byproducts.

So let's go with something we _know_ is up there: lunar regolith. Let's say -- and I don't think this is much of a stretch, but I'm ready to be convinced otherwise by those in the know -- that we can make high-grade concrete out of the stuff. Concrete production has a huge carbon footprint, but there's really no superior alternative if we want to build a lot of housing, storage, and seawalls quickly... and we're gonna have to, heck we _already_ have to. So imagine if we put a concrete factory on the moon, all the carbon and other byproducts get pumped into the dry cough of the moon's atmosphere where it won't harm anyone, and periodically Earth gets a ready-to-build chunk of concrete fired at it to land in a convenient body of water near a major site of construction.

Okay, cool: Why can't robots do all this for us?

If I _really_ want a reason to put human regolith miners and mixers on the moon, the argument I'd make is that although humans are fallible, machines are also fallible, especially when it comes to very complex tasks. Humans and machines working together can compensate for each other's failings, and enhance each other's strengths. This is true for just about any human endeavor, so why would it not be true for a lunar concrete plant? 

Of course, this is no more a "colony" than a rock quarry in a rural area, or an offshore oil rig, is a "colony." It's just a long-term worksite. And maybe that's alright, if we never have perpetual habitation on any of these worlds. If all this is just a tool to help us try and repair or ease the damage we've done to the only place any of us will ever really call home. I could live with that.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1357)

**104:**

I suspect sea is rather worse than space in terms of entropy constantly working on human-built machines. Salt water is horribly corrosive and a great electric conductor of electricity; vacuum is neither. Also all physical stresses on a spacecraft are self-inflicted, intentional, and predictable; physical stresses on a ship in storm is anything but.

**106:**

_I'm also on the side of we need to figure out why we're doing this before we figure out how._

Perhaps a two-legged answer for this might help, because I'm not sure where you're going with this.

One leg is reality: to be blunt, I don't think there is currently any economic case for establishing an offworld colony. The best I've come up with is using it as an offshore financial haven that's more secure than the ones on Earth, due to distance and whoever is controlling transport to and from the colony. And all the US has to do is change a few laws, and the reason for such a colony to exist goes away. So yes, I agree with you on that.

The other leg is SFF. Charlie can correct me, but here I assume that the reason for creating an offworld scenario is that they sell well enough to be worth writing, and having something new and different while being familiar is a good idea. Not that I'm giving him new ideas he's going to appropriate (NOTE: I DON'T MIND IF ANYONE APPROPRIATES WHAT I WRITE HERE. ANYTHING I'M ACTUALLY WORKING ON ISN'T HERE). 

In this leg, I read your question as more about plausibility than reality. 

Plausibly, the reason for things like lunar bases is the superpower equivalent of two stags measuring each others' antlers to see if they're equally enough matched that a challenger can overthrow a dominant bull. In other words, it's a display of missileering, engineering, and logistics, not an economic exercise. 

Going from this or an economic rationale to a real system can be done. Because I enjoy worldbuilding and I'm killing a few minutes, I'll post an option next.

[whitroth](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3431) | [October 14, 2024 19:36](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199144)

**107:**

Charlie, thanks for saying _most_ sf. I thought about a lot of what you're arguing in building my future timeline. I have life _extension_ , not immortaility I also have genengineering... and add in personal advisors that run simulations, and tell you what the results will probably be if you do or say this or that. The point of that is less conflict... and more thinking. 

I have FTL being developed in the later 2080s. Colonization, yes - global warming will take centuries to mitigate. But how it's done: find a habitable planet in the goldilocks zone. Chose a large island or part of a continent that's between the ocean and the costal range. Genengineered prepper plants, followed by airweed to adjust the atmosphere IN THAT AREA. Both, along with later biofeedstock for the food printers (prototype tech _now_). Oh, and all of these plants are genengeneered with kudzu, but with built-in limits. 

Then mostly-robot-built arcology (remember, in much of the world, people lived in the village, and went out to the fields around, not like in the US, lone farmer in the middle). 

_Then_ first canned monkies shipped. Resources to do this 50 year plan need to be spent on Earth, of course. 

Side note: I just realized yesterday or today what Dilbert Stark's desire actually is: he wants to be Rhodes of Rhodesia.

[Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796) replied to [this comment from ilya187](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199141) | [October 14, 2024 19:59](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199145)

**108:**

"I suspect sea is rather worse than space in terms of entropy constantly working on human-built machines."

Counterpoint - time and speed. Harmless at 2kph is deadly at 200kph and cataclysmic at 2000kph. And a trivial entropic flaw in a system that might threaten a ship at sea over a five-year voyage is going to grow into a cataclysm in a 500-year voyage, at some point.

A ship in a storm is an extreme case, but the more we extend the mental model into a spacefaring vehicle the more 'other' threats might become a reality. Specks of dust are deadly at high velocities, not to mention all the stuff we just don't know about yet.

The only way space works as a sustainable habitat for apes is if we get to the point where the dangers are trivially managed. A tight crew of highly trained and focused astronauts is very different from a huge group of diverse people with diverse jobs, ages and experiences. 

There would never 'not' be a need for someone maintaining the vessel, but we won't be going anywhere far until it is on the level of a public works department (maintenance, well planned improvements based on changing needs, small part of overall activity in a polity) rather than a constant risk of catastrophe mitigated only by highly focused specialists in high-risk jobs.

As for 'why' explore the galaxy, I don't know. Individually I can see people wanting to go 'because its there', but societally it too costly before it becomes trivial.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199143) | [October 14, 2024 20:01](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199146)

**109:**

How to boot people to space for SFF, as an example.

Since I'm on a pseudoRoman kick this morning, I'll continue with that. 

To reiterate, I'm using US Air Force Culture, as translated into what little I know about US Space Force Culture (the latter is a recent offshoot). What they do right now is that they fly drones, missiles, rockets, and satellites. AFAIK, the USSF is a NON-COMBAT commmand, so we're talking something a little like Star Trek with a USAF culture, not Stargate (which was combat USAF, if you remember). 

It can be made more like ancient Republican Rome by fiddling. For example, replace slaves with robots and drones. Replace a hereditary patrician aristocracy with a meritocratic officer promotion system with Astronauts on top (Astronaut is the second hardest MOS to get into in the US Armed Forces). Rank in the meritocracy is largely based on skill in wrangling and repairing drones and robots, then at higher levels, managing systems of robots and logistics in general. Logistics is actually what Roman military officers did, as much as fight, and it's the reason there had no separation between the politicians and the military leadership. Both were about logistics. The point here is not to put togas in space, but to show how a current military system can morph into something like a historical system that an author can then use to assemble a familiar society. 

Spaceships of course have Mission Commands on Earth, and I've noted that a Mission Command staffed by Astronauts helping a captain run a ship is akin to a Roman Senate helping a Consul run the country or an army. But how do you get Mission Command aboard ship or on another planet?

(Hauling out my magic wand): The Story Universe is an Einsteinian Brickverse. There is no free will, time's an illusion, so is causality. Therefore FTL can exist. Therefore, also, that institution of Roman times--Oracles--can also exist, just to make it a bit more Roman and also to generate plots around chosen ones and such.

FTL: An AI system generates a design for a working FTL warp drive that can cover up to 100 light-years per internal durational year. Why this much? Most of the interesting stars are over 20 light years away, and if the ship is like a submarine, it can go most of a year on internal rations, so 50 light years out, 50 light years back, in a voyage lasting a year.

Suddenly, long distance exploration is very possible, but ground-based mission control isn't. So they move Mission Control aboard ship and we get to the ship-board culture I've discussed.

Next bit of magic wand waving: habitable planets turn out to be common, if you can tolerate red dwarf tide-locked worlds. Here the point is to let the author do their worldbuilding thing on an alien world that's not Mars.

Finally, we assume that climate change bites down on Earth a few decades after the starships start flying, so that staying connected to Earth becomes increasingly problematic. Time to move on.

Then we assume that the reason for colonizing new worlds isn't population pressure, it's interpersonal relationships among the top brass. Some may find that taking their clients on a ship and founding a new colony is safer and saner than staying and fighting with their worst enemy. Looking at Polynesia, this seems to be the reason for chiefs setting off for new islands. Things like population pressure tended to happen after they lost the ability to sail away, because they'd used all the trees suitable for building long-distance boats. I'd suggest something similar, that it would be easier for a new colony with untapped resources to build more starships, rather than one that's struggling with resource depletion and climate change.

Is there room for aliens, or for colonies with cultures derived fromChinese/Indian/EU/Muskiam oworld? Of course! This is just scene setting, not a plot or anything.

That's how I'd go about worldbuilding, going from now to humans becoming a spreading interstellar nuisance, with astronauts running legions of drones in work or warfare.

Thoughts?

**112:**

_There was a short-lived live-action series on British TV a while back, "Star Cops"._

I liked that series, despite the horrible cultural stereotypes that made it painful at times even in the 80s. 

Given the topic, I'm reminded of Devis' lovely line: "I looked up at the stars once. Got something in me eye. Think it was batshit."

**114:**

Yeah, this. My late wife grew up in a small town in central TX, pop around 2k, and was stuck with the same kids all the way through school. Who disliked her, to say the least - a) she read, a lot, all the time, and b) hit a growth spurt in her early teens, and was taller than most of the guys. 

You _really_ want to be able to get out of a small community if you don't fit. I will point out that we've got a community here... and have to have people from around the world to have that community.

**125:**

_You really want to be able to get out of a small community if you don't fit._

Friend of mine moved from Prince Edward Island to Toronto in the 90s. He's gay, and PEI was no place to be gay if you wanted to live without the constant risk of being hassled or beaten up if someone found out.

For him, the financial cost of living in Toronto was worth it, because here he didn't have to hide.

PEI's apparently better now — you're less likely to be beaten up. Which is to say "not as bad" is not the same as "good".

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

**126:**

_So, what's the hardest MOS to get?_

Guarding the Tomb of the Unknowns at Arlington. Can’t find the article now, but it was a veteran of the Old Guard sounding off about Trump’s creepy photo op at Arlington. 

As you probably know but I didn’t, the Old Guard conducts military funerals. They demand perfect dress and drill for the ceremony. He thought the job of the civilian who got attacked by Trump’s flunkies was more difficult than what he’d done, because his role was just ceremonial, while she had to interact with the families. And Trump.

In passing, he mentioned that the guy with the smartest gear in his unit had tried to become a Tomb Guard and washed out. Their standards of perfection is so stringent purportedly that it’s easier to become a military astronaut than to join them.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199145) | [October 14, 2024 21:00](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199164)

**127:**

_And a trivial entropic flaw in a system that might threaten a ship at sea over a five-year voyage is going to grow into a cataclysm in a 500-year voyage, at some point._

Or less. A Russian module of the ISS has a leak. For a while. And it is slowly getting worse. And no one is sure exactly where the assumed crack is. Russian have try to patch with sealant all over but still it keeps slowly getting worse. It is a cargo docking module so no one has to be in it very much. But the leak has caused negotiations (back room yelling?) that the hatch to this module will remain closed unless access is required. And the staff is currently expected to do their duties with a clear path to their return capsules in case of an "emergency".

And I doubt Granger has a spare module they can ship up on the next cargo flight.

<https://www.grainger.com/>

Currently everyone in the decision tree is hoping things can last 5 or 6 more years. I'm beginning to have doubts.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from whitroth](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199156) | [October 14, 2024 21:06](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199165)

**128:**

_Homeschooling? You mean not even a one-room schoolhouse? Really?_

In Edison's time in the US unless you were in a city or small town you were home schooled. You were likely 5 or more house walk or ride from a school. Each way. If lucky your parents taught you more than how to take care of the crops and animals. If taught to read and write it was from the KJV that was likely the only book in the home.

My grandfather, born in 1885, walked to school about an hour or less away. So he DID get to go to a real school.

The relationship to Edison's time and home schooling now is more in the title than in what happened.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199132) | [October 14, 2024 21:15](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199168)

**131:**

_Please explain to me how home schooling works for any course that requires hands-on experience in a laboratory or workshop. Bear in mind that education is not just learning from texts. And bear in mind that Edison was born 177 years ago: his schoolroom would have reflected the requirements of 160-180 years ago, and if you've ever glanced at a century-old syllabus you'd know most of it is hopelessly inapplicable today, never mind to a kid on a space colony._

Yes.

Edison is a terrible example of how home schooling "works".

His mother was an ex-school teacher. Apparently his parents were somewhat well off and he got to buy wires, batteries, and such for experiments. And as an early teen got a job riding trains selling stuff to the passengers. And after saving the life of a 3 year old the parent got him a job in a railroad telegraph office at 15. So his education involved a lot more than sitting at home at the kitchen table.

Plus he was off the charts smart and driven.

Extrapolating him to the average kid popping out of a female womb or even an artificial one is a bit of a stretch. At least until we figure out how to organize zygote DNA and the early years of childhood to turn out repeats of Edison. And it is my understanding that Edison did not play well with others. Throughout his life.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

**135:**

Greenland was and is inhabited by Inuit. And….Irish monks got to Iceland before the Norse did, and left when the latter arrived. ( <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Papar?wprov=sfti1> )

The closest example is Madeira, which was uninhabited when the Portuguese landed in 1418. There’s some evidence that people visited the archipelago prior to that date, but it wasn’t settled. ( <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Madeira?wprov=sfti1> ). Otherwise you have to get well into the subantarctic region to find uninhabited islands, and for the most part they’re still not permanently inhabited.

And if someone brings up the Falklands, we can have fun with the history of the Falklands Island Wolves.

**139:**

_Greenland was and is inhabited by Inuit_

And they appear to have arrived there relatively recently, too — around 1200 CE. After the Norse, actually.

Before them there were the Saqqaq and Dorset. I don't recall if the Dorset replaced the Saqqaq after the latter died out or if they took over by force. There was definitely conflict between Inuit and Dorset in the Arctic as the Dorset were replaced, although I don't know if that happened in Greenland or not.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from whitroth](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199175) | [October 14, 2024 22:05](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199177)

**140:**

Again, I'm with tfb on the chemistry bit. They are very picky about what gasses are released into a semi-closed loop system with a new batch of air filters being $40 million and a few months away.

They recently had a pump or similar go bad on the urine recycling system. So they stored it in bags till a new pump was brought up. As they didn't want to toss water out the hatch and have to replace it from earth later.

But this leak has been going on for 2 years. (I think.) And I'm betting that more than "Bubba the Tire Guy" has been working on finding it. Just stepping outside and watching for "smoke" is a really really big deal. Assuming the leakage rate is such that the "smoke" will not disperse so fast you can't even see it if looking right at it.

See my earlier comments on complexity of repairing such things.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1312)

[Vulch](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1312) replied to [this comment from whitroth](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199167) | [October 14, 2024 22:21](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199178)

**141:**

Very little of the hull of any part of the ISS is actually accessible. There's equipment and storage on every available surface which doesn't help in finding a leak or getting at it to fix it. The leak has peaked at 1.7kg a day which is about 1.3 m^3, there's not going to be much of a draught to shift any kind of tracer, even if it is possible to turn off all the air circulation equipment in the module for long enough for it not to dominate any kind of drift.

**145:**

Oh come on. A leak in a tire is just not the same or even anywhere near the same as a leak in the ISS. Not even close. Remotely.

I do all kinds of repairs on "stuff". Autos, plumbing, carpentry, electrical wiring. Way more than most. But I have my limits. And try and realize them. And aside from me messing up a brake job, I'm not putting my life and others on the line.

A leak in the ISS is just not the same as any kind of leak on earth. Except maybe in a sub. But even then for most slow leaks the sub can surface. The Thresher sinking exposed a design problem with a value where PV=NRT caused it to freeze and keep them from surfacing. The Scorpion, well, they don't know why. But the vast majority of situations, a sub can surface and open a hatch.

Not so with the ISS.

Yell if you want. I'm done with this one.

David L @ 128:

_My grandfather, born in 1885, walked to school about an hour or less away. So he DID get to go to a real school._

FWIW, I walked home from school every day while growing up; about 30 minutes if I didn't get side-tracked along the way. 

I had a ride to school in the mornings because it was on my Dad's way to work (and also because I had a sister one grade behind me and of course my parents weren't going to make her walk ... through the "Mom's" network she always had a ride home as well). Would have been too obvious if I'd had to walk both ways while she got a ride.

It was more like an hour walking once I got to High School, but by then I had an after-school job and was old enough to hitch-hike.

**149:**

"The USAF does have spec ops people. Do they sheep dip them through the NRO?"

Rather the opposite. Leaving aside the distinct skillsets and mindsets, the organizational missions are fundamentally incompatible and the learning curve between is far too steep for actual _people_. Plus, that sort of "crosstraining" tends to drive out anyone who actually is competent in both areas (who don't suffer from/develop psychiatric disorders so serious that even that organization in McLean won't take them).

Then, too, there are personnel-policy and assignment restrictions involved, parallel to "if you've been in the Peace Corps in nation X and later do any other government service, you cannot be assigned any role however minor involving 'intelligence' related to X or any neighboring nation." Some of those restrictions are no doubt themselves classified, or at least not talked about.

Which leaves the side issue of where all those novel-friendaly space-based spies/other covert ops/analysts are going to come from, and vice versa (not just Flandry!). Not to mention what damage they're going to cause later on...

**153:**

every 'online' or 'remote book-based' or 'correspondence course' that I've ever heard about had a fail rate above 50%

nobody who failed to finish the material would admit to it so anyone thinking about signing up would assume it worked as advertised

the recent experience of millions of kids enduring 'remote learning' was at best flawed and at worst an epic fail

those monkeys in the tin can are there to obey orders as to which screw get loosened prior to swap out of fiddly bits

effectively, crude bio-based robotic remotes obedient to far distant wisdom

everyone ought read up on Apollo 13 since that blind compliance with orders from engineering nerds was exactly what saved those three astronauts from sucking vacuum

now do that for ten years and eighty-three hundred crisis moments 

BTW: it is still my dream of beaming up the Enterprise "any day now"... traveling to distant worlds and exploring exotic civilizations and initiating 'second contact' with blue-skinned alien babes 

(yeah, I am aware of left/right handed proteins-sugars-etc likely leading to massive allergic reactions... but leave my dream unchallenged, eh?)

**154:**

Yup. 

What I was wondering, which seems to be wrong, is whether the CIA/SEAL "sheep-dipping" had an equivalent in the NRO/USAF-USSF. The point of sheep-dipping a SEAL as a civilian CIA agent was that it made assassinations hits by civilians, not acts of war by warfighters. So far as I can tell, that doesn't apply to the NRO/USAF relationship.

What is interesting about the NRO (from Wikipedia) is that they're a DoD agency of about 3000 people, with most of their staff seconded from the armed services (including the USAF and USSF)...and "tens of thousands" of civilian contractors (security nightmare, we has it. I'm guessing). Anyway, they do satellite recon. LOTS of satellite recon.

Soooo, getting back to SciFi stories of Space Guardians colonizing alien worlds for fun and profit. My guess is that anyone sussing out planets for potential colonies is going to be doing orbital recon using the best tools at every bandwidth they can muster, before they even send in the landers and sample drones. To me that sure looks like NRO kinds of stuff.

I suspect there's a place for SPECOPs on a starship, mostly because small-scale tactical violence is about all the capacity you can cram into a starship (aside from, well, high speed kinetic strikes). So there's room for both the hyperspectral sensing boffins and the kill them with knives and IEDs crowd on a colonial starship.

[deater](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4860) | [October 15, 2024 04:59](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199193)

**156:**

I think what you're missing is the "art/hobbiest" model, where people do things because it looks/sound cool and not for any plausible logical or economic reason.

I'm part of a surprisingly large group that writes games/demos for 40 year old computer hardware. Why? It's an interesting challenge and fun. That's all. The kind of person who wrings their hands and asks "why would you do this if you don't get paid?" will probably never understand.

If payload to orbit costs get low enough, then people will try to go out into space because it sounds cool. Many will fail, but maybe some won't. It definitely won't be the people staying home doing cost/benefit analyses that will be remembered by history though.

The Weinersmith book is interesting, but definitely comes across like books from 1910 saying "according to all current science, there's no possible way airplane travel will be economical" or the tales from the 1950s where the world market for computers is like 3. I suppose it could go the other way like never-quite-getting-there commercial nuclear fusion or quantum computing, but it just takes one breakout engineering path to overthrow everything. And there has been some remarkable accomplishments in spaceflight happening just this week.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1322)

[Jean Lamb](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1322) | [October 15, 2024 05:25](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199194)

**157:**

Homeschooling? Sometimes a good way to hide how a kid is being abused. "The Fun They Had" by Asimov automatically depended on a woman staying home supervising (along with laundry, dishes, etc., because a woman's time was not worth anything in Asimov's childhood, apparently). Currently big with tradwife communities till the wife hits a wall, and then the kid is shuffled off to the public school in senior year to get a diploma. This does not always work out well. 

Also, lab classes. It is surprisingly easy to make chlorine gas in a sink trap (my husband's predecessor did that, and that's why there was an opening. Also, he was a losing football coach, which sin was worse we'll never know). 

I've seen a kid homeschooled by learning how to unplug sewers with his dad. I hope he also learned how to read and write. 

Homeschooling is a really good way to derail the careers of (mainly) women. There has to be some supervision, or the kid will be on Warcraft or Pornhub (depending on age). Or the homeschooling (most often for girls) will be in the Domestic Arts. 

In space, the smaller kids with good dexterity will be the ones cleaning the Jeffries Tubes unless you have robots for that (cf powder monkeys). 

And teaching small children to Not Touch That? A classroom is a lot safer, believe me! 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) | [October 15, 2024 06:00](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199195)

**158:**

Random thought: 

So you've got your spaceship full of colonists, and you've got your planet to land them on, and you've got a few hundred klicks and a lot of delta-V between the two.

Do you just drop the colonists off and leave them stranded? If you want to give the access to space, how do you go about doing that? Heck, for a place like Mars, how do you even get them down safely?

Presumably, if the spaceship is an interstellar warpship, you can make a warp shuttle to land and take off at will. Inquiring minds want to know how you land a warp drive without warping the landscape unduly, but I suspect the answer is "next question, please?" That's the easy way.

The weird way, and it is very weird, is John Powell's patented Airship to Orbit in reverse. I think it's cool and support him on Patreon. While I'm not sure it would work (unlike warpships. Ahem) he is making progress. Anyway, the Airship to Orbit system is tha you use an inflatable flying wing around the length of the Hindenburgh to carry about the weight of a Dragon capsule up to an inflatable station in the high stratosphere (you need a big, light airship to get any weight that high). You tranship your cargo and/or passengers at the station (oh yeah, for a station he's currently envisioning an inflated five-armed station with arms a mile long or so). From there you load the cargo to a really big orbital ascender (a MHD rocket-powered inflated flying wing with wings a mile long, made out of stuff not much thicker than clingwrap) to slowly accelerate to orbital velocity by getting lift in the near-vacuum of the mesosphere. The orbital ascender would get shredded on the ground, but since it's designed to get lift in something most of us would consider a vacuum, it theoretically can fly and accelerate up to the Karman line where flight no longer works. 

Anyway, if you want to get really silly for colonizing, here's a notion: carry the airships for airship to orbit rolled up in the starship. When you get to the planet you want to land on, start inflating the high altitude airships and getting them into the atmosphere. Presumably you can make a decent stratospheric station out of modified orbital flying wings, so you slowly decelerate these wings in the mesosphere, bring them to a relative stop in the stratosphere, and hook them together to form the basis for a station. Then you fly orbital ascenders down with balloon borne construction equipment and probably a human or two to make the stratospheric base habitable (or fly them down at the beginning. Whatever). Once the base is complete, you bring down the much sturdier lander airship, inflate it in the stratosphere, and use it as the shuttle to the landing zone. 

One advantage is that it's fairly low tech, and if it works at all, it will work in a lot of places. You could probably land an orbital ascender on Mars, for example. It also scales pretty rapidly (more ships, more stations, and more coverage are easier than constructing multiple rocket launching and landing facilities), especially if you find oil and start making plastic films. The big drawback is that hydrogen is the most likely lifting gas. Another disadvantage is that it's really hard to get any lift in the upper stratosphere and above (even U2s and SR71s fly in the middle stratosphere), so you need huge lifting bodies for relatively little weight lifted. But the same is true for those big ol' rockets. 

And finally, having a stratospheric base can be useful for communications, as well as for launching research balloons to monitor the planet and doing science directly from the crewed stratospheric base. 

Daft, yes. More daft than having a bunch of single-stage-to-orbit shuttles that can land on rough ground? I'm not so sure about that. How do you build an all terrain SSTO anyway? And how do you land a warpship again? 

So something goofy to think about.
