[levity leaven](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8829) | [October 15, 2024 07:07](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199197)

**160:**

What's missing from all the models is that shipping humans off Earth has a non-negotiable energy cost, as OGH points out (iirc) in the High Frontier Redux post. Even if you assume all their needs are already waiting for them in orbit, the cost of orbiting millions of apes at a time is disruptive.

Any "Colony" in the sense of "off-Earth settlement populated by Earth-borne apes" represents a planetary-scale investment in an option to undertake an extremely long risk on a venture which may yield negligible return, and will almost certainly not show any within a human lifetime of its launch. It's certainly something that could plausibly be narrated in the context of a fantasy story, but it doesn't make sense to actually do.

The good news about that is: if you have the resources to attempt such a colony, you don't have to exercise the option! There are many other things you can do with all that energy and the big fancy "spaceship" habitat knowledge. Let your imagination be your guide, but may I suggest that this planet might be better off if humans used it to house and amuse themselves in ways that mitigate or reverse the damage we've done getting there?

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from levity leaven](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199197) | [October 15, 2024 07:29](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199198)

**161:**

We can do a survey, but I’d hazard a guess that most of the people participating in these shenanigans think of an off world colony as an artistic exercise, not a realistic one. Long story short, yes, we agree with you. 

As a topic of creativity, it’s worth contemplating if there’s anything new or interesting that can be done in the genre, or not.

For what it’s worth, I think there’s been so much new science in the last decade that, for that reason alone, it’s worth poking at things again to see if something new can be made. Others may disagree. Charlie’s question about social structures is also worth contemplating. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

[Moz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298) | [October 15, 2024 07:44](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199199)

**162:**

Just playing bootstrap/MVP games in my head, humans are already at "we have one" on things other than bleeding edge chip fabs. Every time there's a hiccup we discover another few of them, like the factory that makes the extrusion machines for making N95 masks, or the people that make some part of the chain that gave us RNA vaccines (remember how for a while we could make tens of doses a week?) I foresee a great deal of 'oops, turns out we need..." for everything from judges to football referees.

Political structure to drive this I suspect will come back to the old model we know and hate. You enculturate children in ideas of the common good and abnegation using some higher purpose not necessarily the actual goal. Make them Quaker-style Christians or Cuban style Communists, for example. Then a generation or two in you have people whole could potentially survive in a space colony. This is to some extent how many Polynesian societies work. The bit about shunning people who don't give more than they get is notable.

So what we're missing is not the "executed for disobeying an order" brutality, it's the "executed/left to die for being unlikeable" part that was common in many frontier societies and more generally in fragile ones. The origin of the term 'scapegoat' is also relevant.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

[Moz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298) | [October 15, 2024 07:54](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199200)

**163:**

Worth noting that societies without a surplus die quickly and predictably the first time production drops. Which also means that you absolutely have to budget to have weirdo freaky people who ignore your rules, man. Or create art, challenge preconceptions, or just don't conform. Balancing that against necessary productivity is a challenge for just about every society.

I think where the corporate model colonies (and the dictator-for-life ones favoured by the Stark types especially) will collapse is from forgetting that the "just in time" model is fragile in the extreme. It's trivial to imagine someone unloading critical supplies from a transport in order to fit the required quantity of caviar in, because displeasing the Stark is a fatal error. But it's just as easy to imagine it being uneconomic to have more than one spare when two turn out to be necessary.

My bet is that not having art and recreation will absolutely fuck at least one expedition. The approved recreation will be corporate and boring, so people will invent fun things to do that are not approved. Which will lead to unforeseen/unforeseeable problems. Or completely foreseeable ones, like someone getting pregnant before the midwife is shipped up and now what do you do? This isn't Scott Base where everyone gets shipped home after six months. But more likely it will turn out that the mural you cant see from Earth affects albedo of the wall it's on, or the funky new sport someone invents causes osteoporosis, or the newly invented board game turns out to be far to addictive to a certain personality type that's over-represented in the crew... so there is an "excess mortality event".

Oooh, pets: someone domesticates something that arrived at a pest. Or an experiment. Turns out that purple cockroaches cause allergies that become life-threatening after a while.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1774)

[Poul-Henning Kamp](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1774) replied to [this comment from ilya187](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199121) | [October 15, 2024 08:21](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199201)

**164:**

»Unless our astrophysics is completely wrong, we will be aware of an impeding supernova for tens of millennia before it happens.«

We know astrophysics is wrong, we have two high precision incompatible values for the Hubble-constant and we can find neither dark matter nor dark energy anywhere. The only question is how wrong it is.

More relevant: The domain of "unknown unknowns" never ceases to surprise us.

The GAIA mission has detected all kinds of "huh?!" stuff, including a couple of wandering black holes at 1560 and 3800 ly. GAIA could spot those because they (happen to?) have detectable companion stars. (How many do not ?)

There are even skilled and credible astronomers who argue that there is a high-mass object on the outskirts of our own solar system, which might, or might not, be a chibi-BH.

Our best bet at early warning would be a surveillance network of a dozen GAIA-like satellites, and a constant real-time monitoring of their output, for things which move in unexpected ways.

The cost would be nothing compared to a proxy-war or a pandemic, and over time, it would probably also tell us what is wrong with astrophysics.

[tfb](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5897) replied to [this comment from whitroth](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199179) | [October 15, 2024 11:55](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199206)

**169:**

> _And you think that someone who works with leaks day in and day out needs to have several master's degrees, I assume._

I think you are confused about two and a half things here.

**The half a thing** is that spacesuits are probably rather high-tech things, and they're made in very small numbers so a lot of the technology is not nearly as sorted-out as the kind of technology we're used to in daily life. So my guess is that repairing them needs a lot more knowledge than you might think (but I don't know this).

**The first full thing** is: OK let's assume spacesuits end up being made in a standardised way in fairly large numbers with all the technology sorted out. Quite likely fixing them becomes something that you don't need a degree to do. So you could have a job description which is 'spacesuit maintenance technician'. And that might not be a degree-level qualification.

How many people do you need to have before that kind of job specialisation makes _any sense at all_? Thousands? Tens of thousands? I don't know, but a lot. Before that point you're going to have someone whose job description is 'can look after many of the systems in the environment, can, if needed, pilot the spaceship, can do the maths to work out what the trajectory should be if pushed'. And yes, that's a degree-level qualification and more than that: that's why astronaut selection is not something you or I would pass and we'd have to be plutocrats who could buy ourselves a flight like, well, plutocrats do.

**The second full thing** is, well, consider a surgeon, or a dentist. Doing mundane surgery doesn't need you to understand Lagrangian mechanics, and probably doing everyday dentistry is easier than that. But you know what: I _really_ want to know that the person I'm going to pay (sigh) to drill holes in my teeth knows _exactly_ what they're doing, has done the ethics training so they don't drill holes which are not needed, and _is personally liable_ if they fuck up badly. Because my teeth are important to me. I want my dentist properly trained, in other words.

Spacesuit leak-fixing is the same deal: when I get into the spacesuit you've fixed for me, then if you messed it up, I die. I want the person who fixes my spacesuit to be very, very good at fixing spacesuits. I also want them to be able to _prove_ to me that they are before they fly, because once we're on Mars and it turns out they're not so good at it after all, what do I do? Throw them out the airlock and wait for another spacesuit-repair technician to arrive from Earth by which time I'm dead? The way they prove they are is _by having a qualification_.

I mean it's like building rockets. It's just classical mechanics and some chemistry right? Any second-year undergraduate physics student should be able to derive the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation, after all. It's not like it's rocket science ... oh, wait.

So it turns out that really a lot of skills which are 'not degree-level' are, in fact, degree-level: _that's what a lot of people on Earth, today, do degrees for_.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5897)

[tfb](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5897) replied to [this comment from Poul-Henning Kamp](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199201) | [October 15, 2024 12:32](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199207)

**170:**

> _There are even skilled and credible astronomers who argue that there is a high-mass object on the outskirts of our own solar system, which might, or might not, be a chibi-BH_

If they were skilled enough to know where it is, they're skilled enough to point a telescope at it, and watch it lens things behind it. And also watch for the radiation as crap falls into it.

But, of course, they don't know where it is. They don't know if there _is_ a planet 9: it's just something people like to think about because hey, cool.

And yes, people are hoping to look for flares from things falling into potential primordial black holes in the outer solar system, but they're planning to do it, if they can do it, by using the Vera C. Rubin telescope which will be a survey instrument, because they have no idea where such a thing might be.

And they're doing it more in the spirit of 'this is a thing we should do, just in case' than because anyone seriously hopes to find anything. The same way people are measuring (have measured I think) how antimatter behaves under gravitation: we almost certainly know the answer, but if it doesn't do what we think we've just killed GR which would be an amazing discovery. This is the same deal: very low likelyhood, but nobel prizes pour down like rain if it's true.

(We also have already looked for primordial black holes by lensing, because they were a dark matter candidate. We didn't find enough (or, I think, any), so it would be doubly extraordinary if there was one really close at hand.)

Generally speaking the things we know we don't understand are not stellar evolution for anything near enough to be a problem. Of course, we might be wrong, but we have looked at an awful lot of stars by now: corrections in this area are more likely to be the kind of corrections you have to worry about from relativity for slow-moving objects: they're there, but they're small.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from Thomas Jørgensen](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199092) | [October 15, 2024 12:57](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199208)

**171:**

_Electronics fabrication is not going to be moved into space._

Semiconductor lithography requires _ridiculous_ clean room environments which are horrifically expensive to maintain on Earth (we have an annoying thing called "an atmosphere"). 

NASA experimented with a [wake shield facility](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wake_Shield_Facility) deployed from the Shuttle in the early 90s, but as usual, the horrendous cost of shuttle missions and the requirement to deploy at a low orbital altitude fucked with the results, which were inconclusive.

A _working_ wake shield (in much higher orbit) with a continuous 24x7 source of photovoltaic power would be a game-changer for lithography machines: bear in mind they cost on the order of $200M apiece and a fab line at TSMC uses dozens of ASML's EUV machines. So _it's possible_ that moving chunks of the semiconductor lithography process into high orbit might make economic sense if Starship hits its target launch cost of $2M-10M a flight. (Musk says $2M; I say, realistically multiply by 500%.)

Some chunks of the process (substrate crystal production, wafer slicing, chiplet slicing, packaging) _might_ benefit from taking place in a wake shield setting: then again, it will probably be cheaper to do them on Earth.

Manufacturing pharmaceuticals in orbit: I think they're very probably junk prospectuses designed to milk investors, insofar as the hot action this decade is all in synthetic antibodies and other biomolecules which you can grow in fermentation vessels full of very expensive GM yeast cells. 

_Certain rare metals are probably worth leaving earth for._

Only if you can refine them on site (that is, on orbit) and bring them home cheaply. Starship might make it possible to throw big metal refineries at the asteroid belt, for some value of "big", but it depends on imponderables -- all our smelting and fractionating processes presuppose gravity and thermal convection. New or even better processes may be possible, but developing them is a chicken-and-egg process. I am profoundly unconvinced by current asteroid mining proposals, although it's encouraging to see venture capitalists get so enthusiastic they're willing to throw hundreds of millions of dollars at sending a washing machine sized probe to a nearby rock in order to poke it. (Come back in a decade, with a fully reusable Starship system flying frequently and available for charter, and things might be different. But one thing's for sure: rugged asteroid-miner roughnecks wielding hammer drills are _not_ going to happen.)

NB: I am suddenly realizing that, anent my insistence on "no magic wand technologies", we are teetering dangerously close to actually having a _real world_ working magic wand. Insofar as SpaceX's Starship has now demonstrated booster recovery in flight and will soon (next six months) have to demonstrate ship-to-ship propellant transfer in orbit to keep NASA happy, we might, by 2030, actually have a working "space truck" that delivers what NASA optimistically promised US Congress back in 1968 with the Space Shuttle!

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199208) | [October 15, 2024 13:53](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199209)

**172:**

_and a fab line at TSMC uses dozens of ASML's EUV machines._

Which is why these plants are so freaking huge. Like major airport huge. Buildings acres in size. This comes from how it takes 30 to 60 or more days to fab a complicated chip. Lay down the first diagram, etch or deposit, rise off, repeat a 100 or more times. So you wind up with a LOT of WIP. (Work in Process) And I'm not an expert here but I think each of those EUV systems is tuned to the current chip STEP so the chips flow from one to the next. Not that one EUV machine programs itself for each step.

Anyway, building a modern or even a 10 year old design requires a LOT of floor space these days. And, as you said, said floor space has to be extremely clean.

So after you prove a prototype can work. How do you scale up to making full layered chips? In space?

Like you on some of these issues, I don't see it.

[Robert Prior](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537) replied to [this comment from Jean Lamb](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199194) | [October 15, 2024 14:36](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199211)

**174:**

_Homeschooling? Sometimes a good way to hide how a kid is being abused._

Depending on how you define abuse, I might bump "sometimes" up to "often".

We've had more than a few previously-homeschooled kids come through my school, often starting in grade 9 (roughly 14 year olds). They were all several grades behind expectations in most if not all the skills they would have learned in elementary school. (And if you remember me ranting about elementary schools passing along kids who didn't have skills, this isn't a contradiction — the home-schooled kids were behind _them_.)

Almost none of them were capable of writing more than a paragraph. Whatever their parents were doing for writing, it didn't seem to include long-form essays (where "long" is 250+ words). 

In terms of science (my subject), they had an astonishing variety of misconceptions, 'supported' by Youtube videos. But the most notable thing is they all had no concept of _research_ — of looking at evidence and drawing conclusions based on it. To them, research was what you did to gather material to convince others that your opinions were correct. (To be fair, this wasn't exclusive to homeschooled kids, but they were the ones who most tenaciously held that position.)

But the most obvious difference was that they lacked stamina, or grit, or whatever you call the ability to push through a difficult or boring task on your own. They wanted constant reassurance that they were on the right track, constant feedback and suggestions while completing work, etc. They needed almost constant short-term reassurance or they stopped working. (Other kids were like that too, but a higher proportion of homeschooled kids.)

Many also wanted to negotiate over assignments and tests. "I'm not interested in that, so I'm going to write my essay on this instead." "I'm not ready for the test, so I'll write it Friday." I think this is part of the stamina/grit thing — they felt empowered to refuse or postpone unpleasant tasks (which will no doubt serve them well in the workplace). 

(I'm all for customizing assignments to match students' passions, and I do it all the time (within limits). But when a student decides that cells are boring so they're going to write their report on fashion instead, and instead of writing they'll make a Tiktok video…)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

[Robert Prior](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537) replied to [this comment from tfb](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199206) | [October 15, 2024 14:57](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199212)

**175:**

_I really want to know that the person I'm going to pay (sigh) to drill holes in my teeth knows exactly what they're doing, has done the ethics training so they don't drill holes which are not needed, and is personally liable if they fuck up badly._

One of my many amazing nieces is a dental surgeon, and we were talking about something like this at Thanksgiving just a couple of days ago. There's a problem with dental practices when they get too big, which is when you get non-dentist managers who are very good at managing processes and money — because they don't have either the clinical knowledge nor the ethical training of dentists, they tend to push the practice in directions that maximize profit rather than dental health. So you really want a dentist in charge, but given how much time that takes that means you need a dentist who likes managing enough to be content doing it, but still likes dentistry enough that they don't forget the treatment/ethics side of things.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

_So that's the feedback part of the loop: technologies that are absolutely essential to make a STL transmissible viable would be rather unpleasant on the surface of a planet, but they have to be developed on a planet before they can be flown._

This is a variant on Niven's Law: "every reaction drive is a weapon of effectiveness proportionate to its efficiency at propulsion".

And ay plausible interstellar propulsion system requires the energy budget of a medium-scale ~~interstellar~~ nuclear war.

**178:**

Okay. So here's a reason to move off planet. Someone discovers a way to make an FTL drive. This particular FTL drive includes the need to accelerate to close to _c_ before turning on the hyperspace converters (or warp drive, or whatever.) This discovery includes, as a small bonus, the ability to detect the use of FTL drives up to X light-years away and it's discovered that at least one other species is using a similar drive.

Someone realizes that a bad navigational accident is a planet-killer. (Imagine the Enterprise hitting Earth at close to _c_ with the shields up) and considers all the ways that a bad navigational 'accident' could be made to happen... Or maybe they learn the hard way with Ceres or Europa or something.

So 'impulse drive'+FTL is the one fictional advancement you allow, then build in all the other obstacles we've discussed over the years as problems the characters must solve with realistic future tech.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199208) | [October 15, 2024 15:54](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199216)

**179:**

One problem with orbital industry is waste disposal. Space is very big and very empty, but it is also very bent, so the usual industrial practice of dumping it and letting someone else deal has a lot of consequences, most of them moving at orbital speeds through the job site, and some causing cascading consequences when they hit. 

As an example, I don’t think anyone would now seriously propose pushing a small asteroid into cislunar orbit for mining purposes, if the upshot of doing so is a Kessler Cascade that makes accessing geosynchronous orbit impossible. At this point in our history, we need functioning weather satellites far more than we need whatever’s in that rock. Even if it’s lots of platinum.

This is just an example. Manufacturing waste is just as problematic. On Earth, if you drop a screw, you can bend over and pick it up. In space, you drop a screw, and if you’re having a really bad day, it puts a hole through your spacesuit a few orbits later. Or, more likely, a spill makes it hard for any supply craft to reach your manufacturing facility, until enough of the debris is trapped in something, 

Turning to SFF, probably I’m not reading the right books, but orbital pollution is one of those problems that doesn’t get mentioned enough. Approaching a planet that’s recently had an orbital war or major accident in orbit is probably at least as dangerous as trying to land in the middle of a shooting war on the surface. Armoring up starships is essential. For exploration scenarios, one early sign of a dead civilization is likely to be lots of crap in orbit, unless something (stellar flares, whatever) cleaned the area, in which case the debris is bopping through the system as a whole.

It’s probably also worth exploring what disposing of space debris in the atmosphere means to the atmosphere. The problem is that stuff burns up in the mesosphere. That’s a region we know very little about, because it’s effectively impossible to fly in with current aircraft. A few balloons have managed to get into the bottom of it, otherwise it’s studied with sounding rockets. So there’s not much there, except for all those sprites and elves that people report as bright electrical phenomena above clouds. And the debris also settles into the stratosphere, which is very chemically active (ozone layer, etc). The upshot is, we assume atmospheric disposal is safe because until Musk showed up with his magic wands, we didn’t have a good way of dumping large amounts of ewaste into the upper atmosphere. We can do that now. 

It will be interesting to see what the consequences are. Based on a few centuries of entrepreneurs inventing new kinds of industrial wastes that they didn’t deal with, I’m willing to bet it will get messy in uncomfortable ways. Orbital garbage patches to match oceanic ones, possibly atmospheric garbage patches too if we’re really unlucky.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4676)

[Olivier Galibert](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4676) | [October 15, 2024 16:12](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199217)

**180:**

My personal hypothesis on how space "colonies" could happen:

Hypothesis 1: the cost of going to orbit goes down enough to be equivalent to, say, a transatlantic flight. Given SpaceX, I wouldn't call it magic wand, not entirely.

Hypothesis 2: Some economical activities are found that work better in orbit (some examples seen before, like finer semiconductors).

Then we could get space stations designed not for show and politics, but for actual industry in space. With people regularly going up and down on a monthly basis or whatever, and supplies moving as much. Eventually, local production is developped to reduce the amount of supplies to move around, and experience is gained into ecologic systems and recycling and all that stuff. Some people stay longer and longer on the stations, because they have no reason to go down. Births and deaths eventually happen, and after a while they routinely happen up there. In a natural progression, at some point, some stations end up almost not depending on earth anymore. Then it's just a matter of time before some group decides to upgrade the station engines to actually move around in the system.

The question then will me more on whether they'll be interested in going back down on a planetary or satellite surface. And, interestingly, it places no hypothesis on the political model of the ones deciding to move around.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199213) | [October 15, 2024 16:48](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199218)

**181:**

So, basically, flying a STL, reaction-powered starship that’s fast enough (0.1-0.2c or higher) to get to another star in a reasonable amount of time for a human means that the passengers are essentially living through a nuclear war every time the ship maneuvers , and every time it hits any substantial fragment of interstellar debris. And the key word is living. Ideally, they should be thriving through all this.

And how far away from Earth does a STL ship have to be before everyone is comfortable with it lighting off its main drives?

And, of course, stealthy approaches are not an option with this technology…

Oy vey.

**182:**

Not these days.

The chip has either a fairly small shell or is directly linked from pads on the edge of the chip to the circuit board. The days of a tiny chip in the middle of a big plastic shell with ten pins to the inch have gone. Take a look at the motherboard of a computer or, even better, your phone (once it's died and you can afford to disassemble it).

The wafers that get manufactured are 37 cm in diameter and about 1 mm thick, according to my colleages. That's just 100,000 cubic millimetres and, at a density of 2.33, about 250 grammes. That will give you between a hundred and a few thousand chips once you've sawn it up.

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from Poul-Henning Kamp](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199108) | [October 15, 2024 17:33](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199220)

**183:**

_The not so good news is that there is a high probability that the surface of the planet will be sterilized to a depth of a meter or more, so /any/ land biology you want afterwards, must be dragged to safety underground or underwater._

Eh, not unless it's REALLY close to us. If Sirius B decided to go Bang we'd be in big trouble. Luckily Sirius B isn't currently orbiting close enough to suck hydrogen off the surface of Sirius A, so that's not gonna happen.

Betelgeuse? Nope, too far away at 500ly. There'll be a very pretty light show -- if Betelgeuse goes bang tomorrow you won't need to drive with headlights at night for a few months -- but to get the ozone layer stripped by a supernova we'd really have to be closer than 100ly.

That's not to say the Earth's never been whacked by a supernova, but _right now_ there are no proximate bang-stars in our stellar neighbourhood: wait a few million years and that'll change, of course.

The other big whoopsie event is if we get hit by the polar beam from a gamma ray burster. That'd be rapidly lethal to anything on the exposed hemisphere at a range of thousands of light years. But GRBs are rare, and brief, so the other hemisphere would be safe-ish. Worst case would therefore be a GRB flashing us from 1000-3000ly away right over the Azores, so it gets all of the Americas, Africa, and Europe. China would be on the edge of the blast zone: Japan, Australia, and NZ in particular might very well survive, although there will be some not-insignificant supply chain disruption as 90% of the human population keels over from acute radiation poisoning ...

Problem is, we're nowhere close to being able to predict GRBs. So attempting to flee would be futile. And fleeing within the inner solar system probably wouldn't work either -- you'd need to get out to the Kuiper belt to have any hope of being safe from an Earth-sterilizing GRB radiation beam.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) | [October 15, 2024 17:39](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199221)

**184:**

Just to summarize, here is my take on five magic wands needed for headache free FTL-based science fiction. Plus an answer to the Fermi Paradox.

The five magic wands:

-A universe that permits FTL. The simplest version of this AFAIK is the Einsteinian brickverse. We’re in a four dimensional brick, free will is an illusion, as is chance, and time doesn’t flow. Paradoxes from FTL don’t happen, because events can’t be changed. If a ship ends up time traveling, it’s because the structure of the universe required that to happen.

For a story, this means that things like precognition, psychic or not, are real. Chosen One stories are legitimate. More interesting is how this affects things like behavior and philosophy, because fatalism is a real issue in a brickverse.

-FTL, probably with a descendant of the Alcubierre warp drive scenario. There are a lot of them, and there’s a real world proposal for an STL warp drive using only real matter. Anyway, this is the only realistic candidate we have for an FTL drive, so we might as well use it.

-gravity control. If you can make a warp drive, you can control gravity. Since an Alcubierre design has warp system components at front and back, there’s the interesting question of whether you can land a warp ship with both ends on, or whether the back, anti-gravity, end, can be used as an anti-gravity thruster for landing. Design-wise, it’s better to think of these warp ships as skyscrapers, where the ship falls downward in the direction of travel and the decks are perpendicular to the direction of movement. Jokes about flying ass first through the universe are entirely appropriate. Having artificial gravity that makes down perpendicular to the direction of travel is silly at best.

—force fields and shields, or something very similar. Stuff moving at interplanetary or interstellar speeds is really, really dangerous, so it’s probably better to assume that there’s a warp shield or something similarly light, durable, and replaceable, that can be used to not deal with it.

-power sources and heat disposal systems that can deal with these energy fluxes without steaming the monkeys using them. How these power towns and space stations gets interesting.

Now, to the Fermi Paradox: where is everybody? Here are two workable and non-exclusive answers.

The first is that a warp drive has an upper speed limit, and starships have a service lifetime before they need to be careened and repaired If that speed limit is below 100 light years per year and the service time before refit is a year, there actually aren’t that many useful stars we can reach from Earth, before the ship has to return to Earth. Most of our neighbors are red dwarfs. My quick check suggested 100c is a low edge, and 1000c is better. The Milky Way is about 106,000 light years in diameter, and sane sapients avoid the core. Warping from one side of the galaxy to the other takes a really fast ship.

It’s entirely possible that we could develop FTL, only to find that all the exoplanets we can reach are less habitable than the ones in our solar system. It could be that the aliens haven’t gotten here because we’re too far away for their drives, even if they know we’re here.

A second is that our planet is quarantined. This is not because we’re in a wildlife conservation area, but because we’re on a known developmental path that means that we’re about to make our near-planetary orbits too dangerous to occupy due to space debris. The big warp ship might have shields that can handle the orbital mess were predictably going to develop, but if they can’t land, due to lack of shielding on the shuttles and dangers from space debris (not to mention armed defense on the ground and in the atmosphere), they may just wait until we can fly out to meet them before they say hi. Otherwise, we’re not interesting enough to deal with right now.

**185:**

The obvious answer to this is "Bomb the Moon". 

You pick a crater on Luna to be "The Landfill" and when you have accumulated a container of crap you don't want on your orbital job site, you attach an ion drive and a computer to it and send it off to lithobreak in that crater. 

With good design, you will even get the drive and computer back.. in a couple of years. 

Which will inevitably lead to phd students doing their dissertation on seismic mapping of lunar interor using the scheduled... landfalls.. from the refineries.

[Poul-Henning Kamp](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1774) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199220) | [October 15, 2024 18:08](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199223)

**186:**

Yes, the stuff we know about and have studied seems mostly harmless.

But new and better observatories like GAIA (and to a lesser degree so far WEBB) keeps throwing up "Huh, we didn't know that was possible..." observations, which chip away at the Fermi Paradox.

If small, even tiny, roaming black holes are a thing, (which we were until a couple of years quite sure they were not, because we only spotted big ones so far), that is an entirely new class of hazards for any biosphere, because they are very hard to spot until it is too late.

Is that what GAIA BH1, BH2 and BH3 are ?

Certainly looks like it, but we need much more data to estimate the frequency of the phenomena, or to prove that the "wandering" part of the hypothesis is wrong.

Detecting three black holes at "neighborhood distance", with the first instrument we have that could, but which was not built and optimized for that purpose, is slightly worrying.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796)

[Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796) | [October 15, 2024 19:11](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199224)

**187:**

Another model for space 'expansion' would be the 'excruciatingly slow' model. The 'Bootes Void' hypothesis of a geologically slow but inexorable expansion of Matrioshka civilizations. No reason to go to the next star until the current one is completely maxed out in utility. But all the local technological barriers to overlapping Dyson spheres are solved problems.

Presumably such a civilization would be operating on very long time scales, and would be sending probes and exploratory parties thousands of years ahead of any actual expansion, and said expansions would be planned in detail down to the molecule.

This would provide an explanation for the Fermi paradox as well. Civilizations that reach a certain technological level don't let energy escape because they have a use for it. So the various galactic voids out there aren't actually empty, they are just dark because nothing escapes them except on purpose.

Wave an sf wand and that maybe provides some explanation for missing mass? I'm not a physicist. I have thought about a short story that involves the discovery or arrival of a very early probe from such a civilization, on the order of a notice that 'in 620M years your solar system will be absorbed into the prosperity sphere, please see to your affairs'.

[Guy Rixon](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7550) | [October 15, 2024 19:25](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199229)

**192:**

Seems to me that all off-world habitats that could get funded are colonies in the sense that they are designed and operated for the benefit of people who don't live there. All would start dystopian and the only ones that prosper are those that have some kind of revolution.

Which brings in a bunch of problems above those of simply surviving out of a biome.

* How do you have an armed revolution without wrecking the habitat?

* If you solve problem 1, how do you live with what you had to do?

* How does your outpost survive after you piss off the supporting organisation at home?

Clearly, problem 3 kills most revolting settlements, but if that one is somehow solved, I think problem 2 might be the hardest bit. 

All war is hell; but a civil war with improvised melee weapons in a tiny space where you know every opponent personally, and none of them can be allowed to survive ... is another level. And then, if you get over that, all those dead oligarchs are irreplaceable biomass that can't be wasted, so you can't even bury them outside, you have to use them. Being forced to use the remains of somebody you knew as a kind of growbag, then eat what grows out of them: leads quickly to too broken to function and not really wanting to go on.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) | [October 15, 2024 19:26](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199230)

**193:**

Here's another take on the Fermi Paradox. It's based on the USSF/PseudoRoman model above.

Basically, the U/P model is that AI gives us a design for a warpship, and a fleet get built before civilization collapses due to climate change and stuff, and they can't build any more starships.

The warpship people aren't idiots, so on seeing Earth no longer able to make or rebuild starships, they realize that, unless they make the goal of their colonization effort to make more starships, they won't survive as starfaring cultures. So this starts a colonization wave spreading across local space at, say, the speed of light or so. This assumes that a ship can travel 100 light years maximum, so the maximum exploration voyage is 50 light years, then return. Also, it takes 50 years from when a colony is founded until they finish building their first starship. These numbers are arbitrary. 

Next, assume that habitable planets are reasonably common, so that within the sphere that they can reach and return from, they're likely to find at least one habitable planet. Likely doesn't mean certain, because of course they'd eventually hit the edge of Orion's Arm and stop.

Also, assume that once a civilization has used up its local resources and crashed, it takes between 5 million and 300 million years to regenerate the petrochemical and mineral resources needed to support a civilization of similar scale. This doesn't mean that humans die off, but it does mean that, if a starship finds such a planet, they won't be able to find the resources they need to start a colony or even refit their starship, assuming they have the tools with them to do so. 

Assume also that after, say, 500-1,000 years, a colony is full up, resources are all in use, its climate is changing, and they end up crashing into a post-civilizational state that no longer supports star travel.

Finally, there's the fact that the Earth orbits the galactic center around once every 200 million years. This is relevant because every star orbits at a different speed, and the result is that distances between stars change over a period of tens of thousands of years. Also, the Milky Way apparently produces less than 10 new stars per year, but assuming habitable planets form, it takes billions of years for those planets to become habitable by humans. 

The result of all this is that an interstellar species expands at, say, the speed of light. However, the planets that are supporting the wave are newly settled. Behind them is a slower wave of planets crashing to the point where they can no longer support starships. Since there are only a few habitable planets within cruising range, finding and founding new colonies becomes paramount. If they can't expand, they're ultimately going to get stuck.

This whole expansion runs into trouble if it runs out of new habitable planets to expand into. One way to do this is through sheer bad luck (it's all hot Jupiters as far as they can reach). Another way is that they run planets already used up by alien civilizations. These are great for ruins, and the aliens may well still be there. But the planet does not have the resources to found a colony and build a starship, so they're dead ends.

Eventually, of course, new stars will enter the humans sphere, but probably too slowly to stop the decline of star flight within the Terran sphere, unless it's hundreds of thousands of years across. 

Thing is, the Milky Way has had habitable stars since before the Sun was born. If no one stopped by, I'd guess it's because enough civilizations have come and gone that most of the habitable planets are post civilizational, and it's really hard to find a new home within range.

**199:**

**200:**

Natural exponential increase. Hawai'i went from a few hundred at most at founding to half million in ca. 500 years, using Neolithic technology, and having population pressure when they were discovered by Cook.

Remember also, if this is the pseudo-Roman model, they don't have slaves, they have lots and lots of robots. Part of the constraint on population isn't just how fast women can survivably have children, it's also how fast they can find suitable rare earths and other materials, and turn them into the tech they need to be productive. This is where things like hyperspectral imaging from orbit or airborne surveys really matters.

**201:**

_The program was overtaken by the feasibility of primitive imaging weather satellites and computerized spy-satellites, and was silently dropped._

More specifically it was high bandwidth communications relayed by comsats, and spy satellites with CCD sensors instead of film.

Early American spysats (CORONA, then the early [KH-9 Hexagon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KH-9_Hexagon), aka Big Bird) had elaborate zero-gee cartridge fed film cameras with on-board fixing/developing tanks, that then spooled the exposed film into capsules for re-entry (which were captured in mid-air at first to stop them landing in very remote areas where the Soviets might conceivably snatch them off the permafrost/ice).

NASA's [Lunar Orbiter program](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lunar_Orbiter_program) (which mapped out landing sites for Apollo) pioneered the use of film cameras, on-board development labs, and slow-scan TV to relay the frames back to Earth -- it was a technical triumph in 1966/67.

But the NRO already wanted a better spysat than the KH-9, and they got it in the [KH-11 KENNEN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KH-11_KENNEN) (aka Key Hole), which used electro-optical imaging from 1971 onwards: no more film cannisters to parachute back to earth. It was the first modern spy satellite, and I strongly suspect its proximity led to the cancelation of both the USAF's [MOL program](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manned_Orbiting_Laboratory) \-- a small space station hanging off the arse of a Gemini capsule, with spy cameras on board -- in June 1969. (It followed the more ambitious X-20 Dynasoar into oblivion and ended the USAF's crewed space program until the Shuttle era.)

The USSR persisted with film for somewhat longer than the USA, and indeed the first Salyut space stations provided cover for the [Almaz spy station program](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almaz), a Soviet equivalent of MOL, which ran through the 1970s. 

Relics of the Almaz era hardware formed some of the modules of Mir, and the last bits to fly are still bolted to the ISS to this day.

But anyway: crewed military spaceships went out in the 1960s in the USA as their mission (change film cannisters!) was overtaken by digital photography, and a decade later in the USSR. _Some_ crewed military missions were resumed in the 1980s in the Shuttle Program, but those mostly consisted of Shuttle missions flown by military crews to launch classified payloads (which is now continued by the X-34B program -- an unmanned USAF spaceplane).

Humans in space are horrendously expensive to keep alive, and the military is even more cost-sensitive than a prestige scientific research organization.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

**202:**

_They’re techs, not into wet work like the CIA (again AFAIK. The USAF does have spec ops people. Do they sheep dip them through the NRO?_

The US government is a de facto Imperial government and functions as such, which means there's copious space for empire building around special missions within the bureaucracy.

Remember that the Army of the US Navy (the USMC) has its own Air Force and aircraft carriers, right?

The US National Intelligence Community is an agency that acts as a clearing house between intelligence agencies and has at least nineteen _declared_ intel agencies as members,

The NRO has (had? Bits of it may belong to USSF now) a bigger budget than NASA.

And so on.

(This is not unique to the USA: historically, all empires do it, and so do a lot of smaller great powers.)

Except that's not what I said. I suggested, as an intermediate step to a Biosphere III, that someone try growing vegetables in a submarine - like the kind already owned by the U.S. or U.K. Navy, _not an underwater habitat._

When you can successfully grow vegetables in a sub, which is essentially a sealed habitat, you're probably ready to grow them in a harder habitat, like space.

**208:**

One of the legitimate things Zeroth brought up above is that a major arm of the Air Force Spec Ops force are the Pararescuemen (aka PJs <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Air_Force_Pararescue> ). They do combat search and rescue, as well as non-combat search and rescue. They're the ones who jump out of helicopters in the middle of big storms to get families off sailboats in the Pacific, not the doorknockers who free hostages and kill kidnappers (i.e. SEALs).

Anyway, I'd joked about a colonizing starship needing soldiers who used knives and IEDs (Peter w called these "space marines.").

Thinking about it, in the situation of colonizing a new planet with a colony that's heavy on drones and other autonomous machinery, who is more needed, a squad of space marines (to do small scale warfare) or PJs (any environment combat medics and rescuers)?

How much of a role is there for human mobile infantry (powered or otherwise) in space warfare, let alone in space-based confrontations or spaceship boarding actions? Wouldn't a kill swarm work better than a dozen guys in powered exoskeletons? 

Conversely, I think there's a legitimate call for rescue workers who specialize in getting people out of godsawful situations, in wrecked spacecraft, lost on alien planets, etc.

Haven't read past your post yet, so apologies to folks if this has been mentioned already. Anyways - wondering whether you've also read this book and how it compares.

I just picked up this book today based on a recommendation by Alan Dove, one of the TWiV regulars. Looked it up - it's won a Hugo and was also nominated for several science communication awards (incl Royal Inst). When I scanned its index I was pleasantly surprised that it covered many topics incl. psych and (best yet!) includes 30 pages of Notes and References (mostly sci articles). Since it was also a 2023 NYT best seller, fairly good chance that you can find it at your public library. 

'A City on Mars - Can We Settle Space, Should We Settle Space, And Have We Really Thought This Through?' by Kelly and Zach Weinersmith

Anyways, after catching up on folks' comments here, I'm off to read this book. If it's what I hope it is, then it might become the unofficial reference guide for writing realistic SF based on knowledge to-date.

**213:**

_Florida is out of gas_

So you're saying my solar powered car is a good idea after all? I just have to find out where my solar panels went, fish them out of the ocean or whatever, then plug them all back together? :P

I kind of love how "society" goes into mad panics at the drop of a hat and decides that some random triviality is in fact a life-threatening essential that must be fought for. Petrol I can kind of see, because the roads still kind of work and most of the US is car dependent as fuck. But toilet paper? Pasta? Beanie Babies?

We've been through the whole "disaster preparedness" on this blog before and concluded that the average reader is _weird_ (Western, educated, industrialized, rich, and democratic). And oh boy should a certain political party be leaning on that right now.

But that does come back to how colonies fail, especially in the absence of local people to pillage for parts when they need them. The Roanoke failure in the USA matches some of the early Australian ones nicely, albeit some of the ones here we are still not sure exactly where they tried to set up, just that it obviously didn't work (Australia has lots of "looks like land" that is actually sandbars, and you can't farm sandbars even after you've got rid of the mangroves and reeds. Just because you're a days sail up the river doesn't mean you're past the sandbar)

I'm still 100% that the way to colonise a planet is to have a mostly self-sufficient, definitely self-reproducing spaceship and spawns out progeny as it passes through or near stars. That may well mean that the "space ship" is actually a moon-sized object orbiting a gas giant used as a fuel tank and the "engine" is a small black hole that spits a plume of dirty nasty bullshit out one side of the resulting structure. The moon has a billion or so people living their lives and occasionally shedding "you're not my real parent" teenage colonies.

**216:**

Re space suit repair... I don't want my damaged suit patched, over than temporarily. I want it seamlessly _repaired._ Flaws in woven layers need new material woven in. Flaws in polymer layers need new material put in and polymerization links between old and new up to spec. The people with the degrees will be doing the material science and sophisticated devices to make that feasible. The repair device should be operable by a trained ten-year-old.

**217:**

Anent my previous comments (and some of others') I propose another conceptual model for real-world space colonization:

The Mind Virus.

It's a very seductive, even addictive, idea. Many of us here want, or have wanted, to live off-planet, in some outer space of our imagination. I have dreamed of living in a rock-hopper in the Asteroid Belt, drilling caves and building fantastic scapes within them for fun. Others have immortalized their outer-space dreams in written fiction; I'm sure many of us here can think of examples even if we have not perpetrated them ourselves :^)

Many of us agree that it's not a practical thing to attempt, and that there are many better things to accomplish (and, IMO, better stories to be told) by using the energy, knowledge and materiel needed for space colonization for other, more useful and immediate things.

I didn't come to want to live in space because of Apollo. I wanted to live in space because of Star Wars. To tiny me, Star Wars looked REALLY COOL and definitely the way the future should be (never mind that it takes place "a long time ago").

Later in life and after long and arduous education, I understand that it's not a practical idea. I am not a billionaire, so I will not be starting my own space program. 

However, recent events show us that there are at least tens of millions of people out there willing to endorse, vote for, or even kill for all kind of impracticable, inhuman and nonsensical ideas. Not all of these ideas are as attractive as space colonization. Some are even less practical. When polities make decisions based on "votes" between small numbers of carefully-curated choices determined by political elites with keen understanding of human manipulation but poor understanding of "where does all the energy go", it's possible for large groups of folks to commit themselves to expensive and counterproductive boondoggles. Hell, the USA probably could have built a permanent Moon base (or better yet, a Top Step) not with what the Iraq Wars have cost, but merely with the money that was _lost there_ during operations.

For reasons already explained a viably-populous space colony probably entails wrecking the local biosphere building and flinging all the necessary infrastructure, consumables and personnel. Many, many people will die, possibly including everyone the project's leaders consider "non-essential" to the project. Most likely it will include anyone who objects.

The physical world is sufficiently hostile to life that even the most hippy-dippy social utopia should provide plenty of conflict-fodder for an infinite amount of stories. It's valuable to tell stories of human foibles and failure modes (e.g. Mind Viruses) but I feel that most stories concentrate on that - maybe because character-driven stories are good! There are relatively few stories in my ken where the humans have Shit Figured Out and are dealing with real, wicked, potentially-intractable problems, which would be intractable if they _didn't_ have that. I guess that means I should write some dang stories, but I can really only do non-fiction.

**219:**

Side comment on another barrier to an electronics industry **INNN SPAAAAAAACE** : Getting the product down to the surface for either further processing or direct incorporation.

Grown crystals are a lot more delicate than "but they're solid!" allows for, especially in relatively large sheets. The g-forces of reentry on anything very far short of a space elevator will not play nice with them, ranging from obvious shattering to microfractures and matrix realignment. "Better packaging" can limit gross structural failure (shattering and visible cracking), but g-forces will reach right through any conceivable packaging to futz with crystal structures. That will probably (not certainly) be exacerbated by any irregularities resulting from growing the crystals in micro- or zero-G in the first place (IIRC, an experiment nearly half a century ago on Skylab implied this problem when they couldn't bring the small-but-cool crystals they'd grown back intact).

It _might_ be possible to minimize gross structure failures by precutting the sheets into smaller units — perhaps even finished size — but now we've just added another set of machinery with even more moving parts requiring maintenance that has to be gotten to the facility in the first place. installed, calibrated, and above all lubricated and maintained. Not to mention the occasional software upgrades...

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

[Moz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298) | [October 16, 2024 02:56](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199257)

**220:**

I'm going to go with Ursala K Le Guin for a bit: the management model is an anarchy, but the sort of highly structured anarchy you get in volunteer organisations rather than the willfully stupid Libertarian type of "anarchy but with state violence".

This is similar to the military model in some ways, including the 'led by the relevant expert' stuff that utterly breaks more conventional management especially the mammonite "highest paid person's opinion" stuff. But rather than punishment coming via there "military is organised force, so we're using that to punish you" you get the other sort of discipline "now explain _why_ you did that, again, this time talking more about how you feel about what happened afterwards". In the extreme this becomes a bureaucratic nightmare (as with [Philosophy Tube's experience of the UK trans discouragement system](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v1eWIshUzr8)) but the general mode is much more compatible with good mental health than the alternatives.

The spokescouncil model or any of the more recent online distributed organisational setups would work quite well. This is how you get an event like Burning Man, or a bush doof, or even a protest in a desert, and those can scale to the point where there are formal rules and "you must agree to these in order to get site access" type restrictions. The key that makes those anarchic rather than fascistic is that the price of not agreeing is not getting to go into space rather than being imprisoned, exiled or killed.

You end up with a whole bunch of sub-groups, often including groups whose purpose is middle management ("the site coordination committee", "sanitation organisation officials" and so on) who run round in circles trying to find out what happened to the toilet building crew rather than actually building toilets. I've seen this scale disturbingly well, and be bizarrely flexible as well (what do you mean the protest organisers are now organising to defeat a government manhunt? Well, that's what happens when it turns out that the Woomera Concentration Camp fences were built by the lowest builder and now there's 50-odd refugees loose). And yes, ... I hear... that they had both a search and rescue group to help find people _and_ whatever the opposite is to help people who didn't want to be found. Someone, somewhere has a multinode fan-out diagram of how the core understands the overall structure, but also some guesses as to how the bits of structure that evidently exist but are not part of the structure are structured.

I think we could actually get this via a single stroke of magic involving a space elevator, warp drive, teleportation or some other gimmick that lowers the cost of transport. Anything that makes it possible for a few hundred to a few thousand people to go camping in space then slowly branch out into providing a better base for the next colony attempt to build on.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8995)

[Jonathan Burns](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8995) | [October 16, 2024 04:00](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199258)

**221:**

Gracious host, what you might be missing ...

Lightspeed communications (interplanetary). From Britain to the New World, a letter will take some weeks. The Colony will have to deal with disease outbreak, a cold snap, mutiny or native animal attacks, all on its own. It will be that long before the mother country even knows there's trouble.

But Earth to Mars take minutes, to the outer planets it's hours. A whole nerd culture is ready to lend advice and upload software. The Colony is only isolated physically. They're only a special offshoot of the mother culture.

With interstellar colonies it's different. But even then, a colony is only years away from the colonies before.

It all makes for a different kind of story.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4809)

**224:**

Correspondence school in Oz/NZ is pretty intensive though, and relies on a lot of adult supervision. It's quite distinct from what's known as 'homeschooling'. Often kids in correspondence school will have a nanny or parent with them for the whole school day, and that adult is not doing other tasks, they're basically a teacher.

And the pay is not bad, as a rule. Which means it's expensive for the parents (albeit often less expensive than boarding their kids in town, as well as better for the kids - sending a five year old away for the whole school year is basically fostering).

Current AI is fucking atrociously terrible at any kind of teaching task below about 15 years old as far as I can tell. It can be used for specific sub-tasks with expert supervision, but often it increasing the teaching load rather than decreasing it. Which kinda defeats the point for any kind of colony. Also, unless it's a big colony you still really, really want to have the full set of teachers involved just so you can get expert opinions on ~~how badly the AI~~ whether the AI is missing anything.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Moz](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199257) | [October 16, 2024 04:57](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199262)

**225:**

_the sort of highly structured anarchy you get in volunteer organisations rather than the willfully stupid Libertarian type of "anarchy but with state violence". This is similar to the military model in some ways, including the 'led by the relevant expert' stuff that utterly breaks more conventional management especially the mammonite "highest paid person's opinion" stuff._

Yay! Let's talk!

I'm going to disagree with some points. 

Part of where I'm coming from is about scale, and it's about the notion that any system can be hacked, so it has to evolve.

With regard to volunteer group-style adhocracy. You and I both like it, but...The two buts are that I'm not sure how well it scales, in group size and group duration. The Rainbow Family makes it work for hundreds/thousands of people for a few weeks, but the peak crowd is only there for a week, much like a convention. My educated guess (having seen this happen) is that crowd-sourcing governance in a way that gets nothing done and pisses everyone off often spawns demagogues and authoritarians, often quite vicious ones. There's nothing to oppose them, so it's an easy hack. Thus volunteer adhocracy breaks down, probably rapidly with big crowds (where it's vulnerable to organized subversion) and over longer times in smaller groups, where trust erodes (or is deliberately eroded) among the people doing the work. 

This isn't to say that a highly organized group works better, because anything can be hacked, especially if it's unchanging for a long time, and more especially if it involves a lot of people. Still, things like Robert's Rules of Order or Black's Law Dictionary (common law) help people organize things and make decisions, for good and bad.

I also disagree with strictly nonviolent punishment, because I do believe in the right to individual and group self defense, escalating to a lethal level if nothing else works. On a starship, someone who endangers, harms, or kills a bunch of people should be at risk of death if it wasn't an accident. As an example, someone who deliberately tries to kill everyone by tampering with life support on a spaceship should be free-dried in an air lock, and their cryomains given to the family for whatever ritual is appropriate. It's cruel, but you can't afford to give them a second chance, especially in a limited ecosystem. That's group self defense.

This is another scaling issue: justice and correct behavior in the limited ecosystem of a starship or a small offworld colony has to take consequences into account, and it has to accommodate incompetents (children, elders, intoxicated or sick people) as well as misbehaving competent people. For example, a child messing up a life support system has to be dealt with in a way that deals both with their level of education (if they did it out of ignorance) and their intent (if they did it out of malice, boredom, accident...). Still, the survival of the entire group overrides the rights of the individual, for what should be obvious reasons, in this situation. This kind of justice won't necessarily scale to a society of millions or billions of people. 

How to do this in a good way? That's the hard question, and part of the question is: at what scale, in area, number of people, and duration. Ship Law (small group, medium duration) will look very different than planetary law (millions of people across an entire planet for decades to centuries). Things like democratization of force (everyone can lawfully defend themselves violently) can work in smaller populations, while large state-scale populations seem to work better with monopolization of violence by a government, especially if that government is trusted to some degree. Otherwise, it breaks down into messy feuds and vendettas.

One thing that's actually related to it is the question of how to raise children off Earth. This is essential, of course. It intertwines with justice because, with a few children in a small group in a small colony or starship, they have to learn, and that necessarily involves making mistakes. How to make those mistakes learning experiences rather than lethal is something that actually impinges on a lot of areas of extraterrestrial justice. 

MY 0.02 cents. Great ideas!

**229:**

Part of it is the modern "right to disconnect" (from your job) which is similar to but distinct from the still contested right to quit your job. If you have a colony where people can't quit other than by dying that's inevitable, and also inevitably a problem for any justice system (but not for most legal systems. Hmm, maybe I should use that as an example next time I have that discussion).

I'm assuming lethal punishment as well as corporal, but I'm less keen on having it be the first resort (as it is far too often on Earth). Hence my focus on how do you maintain discipline/what punishments do you have. Not least because a colony that can't rehabilitate criminals has a whole lot of problems and they're only going to get worse. You might even end up like Australia is now. Or, bob forbid, the US. Speaking of which, armed slave-catchers are one of the failure modes of a lot of colonies.

The question of how you deal with "we only have one person left who can do this job, and they don't want to. Do we force them by whatever means we can dream up, or just accept that everyone here dies?" It's been a real question for other colonies, and I suspect the most common outcome is "everyone dies". Too many opportunities for the slave to sabotage the system.

Nunneries and monasteries are possibly better models than remote research stations because we've mostly gone off the idea of leaving the latter alone for a few years to see what happens. Overwintering in the Antarctic was probably the last effort in that direction: <https://oceanwide-expeditions.com/blog/the-first-winter-over-in-antarctica> "dirty, smelly and cramped" could well describe the ISS too :)

But the religious cloisters have traditionally been voluntary. And had the whole lot, from tool-makers to farmers to research scientists to a habit of killing intruders (and exiling/killing dissidents). And they sometimes last a decent number of centuries. Which kinda circles back to my starting comment about using religion as the core and colonising/exploring as the side quest.

so... less a 'space race' as a grimly unblinking 'treasure hunt' with the prize being one or more chunks of unpulverized 'alien tech which from a human perspective qualifies as some sort of 'future tech'

some interest in weapons or dual-use tech to be exploitable in making big ricks into small gravel... 

but those with the longer term perspective will be seeking out buried treasure in hopes of finding a 3D printer performing atom layering (3DPPAL)... Star Trek replicator in all but name... which would completely tip over humanity's current economic model

one safe guess?

in the interests of disaster recovery, designers of a 3DPPAL would embed on the chassis an obvious "PUSH ME" button... which when invoked would trigger emergency mode... whereupon the 3DPPAL produces two more 3DPPALs and those two will -- you guessed it -- each produce two more... and so on and so forth...

then each will automatically switch over to producing exactly one critical bit... foodstuffs, medicines, robotic doctors, comm box, shelter wedges, robotic welders, etc

all that before permitting selection of whatever next to be produced... survivors of disasters rarely know what's critical... assuming they are not bleeding out... 

figuring out the specifications for what goes into the 3DPPAL's blueprint files will take years, maybe decades

but if we are assuming there's a built in universal translator -- emergency mode -- which will learn English in a surprisingly short time...?

the built in AI gets a good look at the stars, maps out pulsars to determine a hundred million years having lapsed and while humans might be descended from an enemy species (unlikely), the AI decides to hold no grudges... 

...or sadly there's a deeply coded directive to take the long view and patiently set up humanity towards self-destruction?

Can be as gentle as you can make them with a parachute.

We dump the excess velocity in the atmosphere but only because of economics.

It will "only" cost you about as much delta-v as a launch…

**235:**

> I believe I've read that the current staffing of the ISS spends about 1/2 of their time doing plumbing repair and tuneups and similar.

You'd be surprised how much of modern research looks like that, albeit with less "if the life support system fails, everybody dies". Merely "if your two main laser diodes go bang in quick succession, have a fun three month death march to fix the consequences"

A typical day in the last third of my PhD (i.e. after we'd finished building the experiment from scratch) generally involved 3-8 hours of fixing everything that went wrong overnight before we could actually "do" science. And once you've gotten everything running smoothly, you run the sucker until you had to go home, because that remaining few hours is where all of the _actual science_ needs to be done. As more than one PI have commented to me, or in my hearing - (work-life balance) or (high-impact publications and _maybe_ an academic career), pick one of two.

I'm not joking about "fixing what went wrong overnight", either. Plenty of unusual events could, and did, make things even worse (1), but just the natural movement and vibration of the _ground floor_ of a room in a 1970s office building, converted into a kinda-clean-room optics laboratory on the cheap (2) was enough to require hours of tuning _every day_ to keep the equipment working at the sort of 95th-percentile-of-theoretical-optimalness that was needed to get decent science out of it.

Perhaps Durham had unusually poor laboratories leading to _more_ of that tuning grunt-workg - but I heard the same complaints from researchers at notably well funded research centres such as ETH Zurich and the Institut d'Optique, which had _much nicer_ laboratories than I did. If you want to do cutting edge science, you unavoidably have to spend a lot of time getting your equipment and systems working _at_ their cutting edge. 

(1) All of these things happened, some of them multiple times

  * Power failures or even voltage excursions. 
  * Unusually gusty weather (you might not feel the extra vibrations, but with an interferometer, you can watch them in real time, and the cumulative effect can be significant)
  * Unusually heavy goods vehicles on the main road 150m away
  * Pile-driving at the construction site to which said vehicles were delivering
  * A new cleaner who didn't realise that "no entry to unathorised personnel" meant her too



(2) _cough_ Sorry, per the university prospectus, "World-class scientific research facilities"

**237:**

Open University? Been there done that (PM951 Computing and Computers) around 1980. It’s not an easy option when you’re working full time and on call once a week. At that time on call usually involved working up to 24 hours one day every to weeks and Saturday or Sunday. The course was excellent, the tutor was good and practical work involved booking evening sessions on a remote mainframe terminal at the local further education college. The TV component was first class. Much better than most of my university lecturers. We were allowed to bring anything (textbooks, lecture notes etc) to the exams but there was no time to consult them if you wanted to pass the exam. At the end of this I was sufficiently confident to write curve fitting software for steroid immunoassays at work and code a planetarium program for a 16K ZX81. The modern Open University has intensive use of PCs and would be an ideal model for small colonies. The problem would be plagiarism. Careful design could mitigate this. In my exam one of the tasks was to write an algorithm for a craps game. Very rew people in Britain have ever played craps. Artificial intelligence could be the way to deal with this if all the students online activity was monitored.

**240:** 170 "They don't know if there is a planet 9: it's just something people like to think about because hey, cool." 

I thought it had been determined that there's no planet 9 within a ridiculously large distance because, if there were, the Pioneers and Voyagers would have been deflected. (Wherever I read this was talking about their positions being known to a small number of centimetres.)

172 (I work for the chip-making division of Samsung.) We're currently making 80 to 90 layer wafers and, to a first approximation, each layer takes about a day, so three months to make a wafer. 

The bottom layers tend to be more standardized transistors and gates while the upper layers are metal masks connecting the various terminals of the lower layers. So it's common to manufacture the bottom half, store the wafers, and then do the top half when you've got a detailed design. Or extract some wafers 10 or so layers from the end and store them, so that if there turns out to be a bug when you test the first batch of chips, you don't have to start from square one and can get a new design out in a fortnight instead of three months. But you're limited on the changes you can make at this point, of course.

The person I asked isn't sure whether wafers move from machine to machine for each layer, or whether the masks for the metal layers can be exchanged quickly. But, unless they can be exchanged much faster than you can remove one wafer and insert the next - something I doubt - it's probably much better to move the wafers than fiddle about with the masks. Remember it's a pipeline: once you've got the first wafer coming out of the end, it doesn't matter how long it takes to make a wafer, they come out one per day per pipeline. (If you want a million chips in a year, you need a lot of pipelines.)
