**243:**

My understanding of monasteries is that they’re generally aimed at limiting distractions, and they generally have a novitiate period wherein someone who wants to join figures out if that’s a life they can and want to lead. So in that sense a monastic life can fit with deep space exploration.

The first problem is that monasteries aren’t about reproduction, so they have to recruit new members. Again, with long exploratory missions, this isn’t necessarily a bad thing. With colonization, it is.

The funny thing is how often religious life ends up trying to replicate tribal life, of the go off by yourself to live a life free of whatever the authoritarian sleazeballs wanted to force you to do. Since those people manage to raise children in that life, they’re a better model for a colony than a monastery is, I think. So long as you don’t give them ideas that other lifestyles are possible or desirable, as advertisers do to us…

**244:**

_Homeschooling works if the curriculum is designed properly._

I think we're having one of those 'separated by a common language' moments.

In North America, "homeschooling" means that the parents keep their children at home and teach them themselves, with no involvement of the school system (beyond maybe a self-certification that they are teaching appropriate material). It's not the same thing as distance learning such as online school or correspondence school, which are run by an actual school that handles the curriculum and assessment. 

So here, a student taking some form of distance learning wouldn't be referred to as "homeschooled", even if they were at home while they were learning. "Homeschooled" means the parents chose what they learned (or didn't learn). 

It's _possible_ a homeschooled child can get an excellent education, if the parents are good at teaching and brought in extra resources to make up for what they couldn't do themselves. (And the self-awareness to recognize their weak points.)

It's not the way to bet, though. Often parents opt for homeschooling because there are large parts of public education they object to and they don't want their children exposed to. Very often the bits around recognizing that people not like themselves are still people and have the same rights they do, or the bits around informed consent and bodily autonomy, or trivia like the separation of church and state…

It's not uncommon to discover the 'curriculum' a female student followed at home was basically housework and childminding, with a side order of submissiveness to male authority, with her own schoolwork squeezed into her non-existent free time. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1804)

[](http://www.nancybuttons.com) [Nancy Lebovitz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1804) | [October 16, 2024 14:45](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199282)

**245:**

I haven't read all the comments, but I asked myself, "How is off-earth habitation different from living on earth?" and the snap answer is "It's a lot more work".

My tentative answer is that human capacity for work would need to be increased. This doesn't mean the result would need to be a leisureless dystopia. I think it would take greatly increased cognitive and sensory capacity and mental endurance, with possibly some increased physical capacity.

It would be an interesting spin if they actually need _more_ leisure because burnout is a serious risk.

This would at least be good enough for science fiction, with people running groups of robots and being able to see areas of the biome directly.

There would need to be an excellent tools for avoiding tyranny because they couldn't afford leaders who are seriously wrong.

**247:**

_Current AI is fucking atrociously terrible at any kind of teaching task below about 15 years old as far as I can tell._

The other problem with AI (and other forms of distance learning) is that it's not good at motivation/enforcement. As we discovered during the pandemic, it's very difficult to notice and grab a student's wandering attention from a screen (as opposed to being in the same room). Especially for younger children, you almost always need someone in the room with them keeping them on task.

This person doesn't need to be a teacher, necessarily. When my nieces were young popo used to sit with them while they did their homework. Her English wasn't good enough to help, but she encouraged them and kept them focused until they were finished. (And if they got stuck she kept asking questions until they figured it out themselves. Very Socratic.)

What techbros miss is something any elementary school teacher knows: the curriculum is only a part of the learning that happens in school.

**248:**

_the still contested right to quit your job_

I've learned in the last couple of years that many American teachers (and nurses) are bound by contract to not quit during the school year, even if the school totally changes the job on them. If they do, they can lose their license and even be billed for the cost of the replacement teacher.

(Which I find gobsmacking, because not only are they not getting paid for the rest of the year, but they are actually billed for it.)

I was reading yesterday of a case where a chap got divorced and lost his house, so had nowhere to live, and the school system basically said 'sucks, but you need to figure something out until June because it's not our problem'. Local rents were _over_ his salary and he had no money left (divorce). 

I'll give you two guesses as to whether the states that do that are left-leaning woke hellholes of government overreach, or right-leaning bastions of small government and individual rights.

[codepolice](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=9021) | [October 16, 2024 16:00](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199288)

**251:**

The model that makes sense to me is seeding. Kind of similar to some (marginal?) theories of how life arose on Earth - that it was brought by a comet.

Suppose we find a suitable planet - good gravity, magnetosphere, presence of water. Most likely we'll need to send a package of microbes, perhaps bioengineered to fit the environment, to create a breathable atmosphere. Later some plant seeds, fungus spores. Eventually we send another package of frozen eggs and sperm with machines ato reconstitute a human population. I am obviously handwaving away a whole bunch of technology, including artificial wombs.

An interesting question is would it be possible to raise a recognizably human being outside of Earth? We are learning more and more about the importance of bacteria and such to our functioning. It is suspected that there isn't enough information in our genome to encode for a human being, after all don't we share ~50% of the genome with a lentil? So the environment must provide some important context/input. What happens outside of that?

What kind of culture gets created when the babies are raised by machines, as they would have to be?

The plusses to this approach is that we don't have to transport fragile human bodies for several centuries. No need for complex self-sustaining technology on the other end. It only needs to last long enough to raise the first generation.

Apologies if this was already discussed I haven't caught up on the entire discussion yet.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4809)

**254:**

We are now onto "Why would someone want to colonize Earth"? '

Lets see: Reasons that are not just a horror story: 

1: Fertility Crisis induced labor shortage. Like all advanced societies, they have ended up with a very, very low fertility rate. Low enough that even out and out immortality isn't enough for much in the way of expansion. 

(species with strong enough reproductive instincts to not wind up with this problem.. don't make it for the long haul.)

The Zeta multi-species corporation looks at the discovery of earth as the find of the millennium. Billions of people acculturated to salary work, almost all of whom are currently working at prices that Zeta Corp can trivially vastly outbid. Ka-Ching! 

No hidden catches, even. Sure, maaybe you won't be.. super content to go back home at the end of your 5 year contract, once you have seen.. well, still the boondocks, but, you know, actual civilization...

but the paycheck is real, the jobs are good, and the job training is in fact considerably better in terms of knowledge gained than multiple phds. 

2: Post Scarcity Boredom. Some people don't do well with idleness. Not at all. The K'Ktah are a particularly severe case. Hence: The Nerf-Gun invasion. 99 million disposable droid bodies drop from the sky, scream FIGHT ME and proceed to Count Coup on as many people as they can. With nerf weapons. To motivate actually fighting back, said droid bodies come with goody packs for anyone that defeats one. (.. said goody packs are good.. but also in ways the K'Ktah find funny) Monthly invasions projected to keep going until K'Ktah run out of bored people. So forever.

**255:**

Nice idea, but it presumes that the new settlements inevitably crash in the same way as the homeworld. I'm not sure I believe that.

Resource exhaustion: what exactly are they running out of? What does a starship need so much of that a world of say 100,000,000 people can't manage a few starships per century? Also, is this particular Unobtanium refreshed if the planet is fallow for a few millenia? I'm thinking that things like rare earths are renewed slowly by volcanic and tectonic activity.

Ecological collapse due to over use of fossil fuels: my guess is that a civilisation that got to a world in a STL vessel/habitat knows how to do without fossil fuels, and is smart enough not to kill a new world. But human stupidity is vast and endless, so maybe this is optimistic.

I see a different reason for a post-starflight era. I think one natural end-state for a human world is a huge number of tiny countries that get on with each other just well enough to trade a bit and not to go to war. Imagine late-medieval Europe, but with 21st century or later philosophy, culture and tech. Getting enough nano-states to cooperate in building a starship just gets too hard. Without a military incentive, the big projects wither.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1357)

**257:**

_o reiterate, I'm using US Air Force Culture, as translated into what little I know about US Space Force Culture_

At risk of sounding contrarian, by the time we look far enough out for any military-model management structure space colony to be remotely plausible, the USA as it exists today will be dust. 

Taking USAF or USSF as a model is likely as plausible for the 22nd century as taking Admiral Lord Nelson's Royal Navy circa 1800 as a model; in fact, while they might speak a language they think of as "English", their culture won't be descended from today's anglosphere at all -- at least, not without a whole lot of cultural remixing with everyone else around today.

Demographically, the USA is only experiencing population growth or stability due to immigration: by 2200 it may well be majority Spanish-speaking and the descendants of the early WASP settlers, the majority of the 1950s period, will be profoundly outnumbered. The UK is also going in that direction (albeit not with hispanic immigration: it's currently driven by a mix of Chinese, Indian subcontinent, and African arrivals). China's going to tip over into negative population growth soon, Russia is already in demographic freefall (killing off or driving into exile a generation of young men by starting a land war in Asia hasn't helped), and so on.

I suspect by 2200 the main "great powers" in play will have emerged from India or sub-Saharan Africa. Possible scope for an Arab/Persian rennaisance too: lots of PV power in that part of the world. (Yes, regular black flag weather is going to be a horrendous problem there. But on the other hand: PV power is getting ridiculously cheap to install right now, and heat pumps/air conditioning is a thing: China went from third world to developed status in fifty years, and it's quite possible for large chunks of Africa to do likewise.

And looking further ahead, the main space-colonial power is likely to be whoever settles Antarctica (or, possibly, Northern Canada and Eastern Siberia) ...

So: possibly military-style management of space colonies, yes. But USAF/USSF style management? Probably not.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from ilya187](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199147) | [October 16, 2024 17:50](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199295)

**259:**

Re: 'The other problem with AI (and other forms of distance learning) is that it's not good at motivation/enforcement. As we discovered during the pandemic, it's very difficult to notice and grab a student's wandering attention from a screen (as opposed to being in the same room).'

This system from Carnie Learning is supposed to be quite good - it's a game that kids can play and when they get stuck they're offered hints. Haven't tried it myself yet. Still needs human teachers, so it's a support not a replacement. But it does provide feedback, so if a kid gets stuck, the human teachers can better identify/intervene. This could save teachers hours of work and also better spot which kids need help with what. Increasingly I think that integrative tech whether education, medicine, law, etc. rather than replacement tech is the way forward. Tech is a tool, not a substitute.

<https://www.carnegielearning.com/solutions/math/mathia-adventure/>

Re: Motivation/reinforcement

I was about to comment that how well an off-planet colony might thrive would also depend on reinforcement (reward/punishment). 

A military model* requires the ability to ascend in rank and with that more room to exercise certain behaviors. The financial model (most of the US) mostly uses tokens ($$/large mansions) and to a lesser extent name recognition (family fortune, media star/idol) and to an even lesser extent recognition of expertise in some areas (academic degree/Olympic medal, Super Bowl cup, etc.). These current models are all designed to be as restrictive as possible whereas in a colony that has only its inhabitants to rely on, I think inclusivity would be more important both short and long-term. 

Inclusivity depends on recognizing that differences are okay - it's what makes each individual unique and wanted/prized and enjoyed - as long as these differences are not injurious to anyone! 

Imagine a 200 member colony space ship where one member starts and keeps on teasing another member - and this persists for years. If the member being teased has not been assured that his/her difference is perfectly okay, and if the member doing the teasing hasn't been taught that non-stop picking on someone can be hurtful/is oblivious to signs of hurt on another person, what's to stop that person being teased from saying 'enough is enough - I can't take this hurt anymore, my life isn't worth living, no one else seems to think that what I'm experiencing matters (I don't matter)' and committing suicide. In a very small crew, chances are that any loss of personnel would be (at least) a mini disaster. If that crew member that suicided had any particular area of expertise that none of the other crew shared, it'd be a major disaster. OOC - and what would you do to that crew member who did all that teasing? And would there be any consequences for the crew/colony leadership?

*I have zero personal experience with any military - my perception of the military structure is entirely based on news (TV & 'print') plus the usual fiction: TV shows, films, books. And an SF/F blog. Reliable info/corrections are welcome!

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199294) | [October 16, 2024 18:02](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199297)

**260:**

_At risk of sounding contrarian, by the time we look far enough out for any military-model management structure space colony to be remotely plausible, the USA as it exists today will be dust._

Objection noted, but it should have been clear that I was looking at FTL popping up sooner, rather than latter, as a result of the current ferment in cosmology resulting in evidence that shows how it can work. Basically, you start with a Brickverse or some other cosmology that permits FTL, and let the cosmologists explode, excuse me, explore, from there, using AI (NOT LLMs) to help them write the proofs and design the experiments.

Don't forget, climate change has a lot of inertia, so if we don't get our acts under control 2200-2500, possibly longer, will see the most rapid temperature rises and peak heat. I really don't think this is optimal weather for building starships, whatever else is going on.

My personal take is that Earth's population is going to be much closer to one billion in 2200, absent serious efforts by the super-rich to break that human centipede they're forming and pull their heads out of each other's asses. 

If this is all the case, we have a fairly small window in which FTL could take off, and so I used the organization of the most likely group to get a shot at the stars in the next few decades: the USSF. 

If you like, you could put up Taikonauts, Cosmonauts, or Muskminions instead. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5955)

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from whitroth](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199174) | [October 16, 2024 18:19](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199299)

**262:**

_Why? That's how they do tires, well, unless you can spray the entire module down with water with dish soap, and look outwide for the bubbles._

_Rolls eyes_

You know the ISS is ventillated? It has fans running constantly to keep the air moving in order to avoid dead spots that are hypoxic, too cold, too hot, etc.

So for starters you'd have to isolate the leaky module and shut off the ventillation before you could fog/spray inside it.

For seconds ... ISS modules are not tires! Behind the inner walls they're stuffed full of plumbing and electronics and insulation, and beyond that you get to the outer wall (which is presumably where the leak is located). Your mist will _probably_ be drawn towards the correct wall for the leak, but there's air circulation behind the panels so then you need to strip out the lining of a module that was never designed to be stripped out.

(Your comparison is pulling out the interior of a submerged submarine -- not to find a leak, in a submerged sub that's going to be pretty obvious, but to find something hidden on the back side of the internal fittings and pipes.)

Note that if you get it wrong, oops, you just knocked a hole in a $100Bn space station.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4869)

[](http://scrivenerserror.blogspot.com) [Jaws](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4869) | [October 16, 2024 18:20](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199300)

**263:**

I can't project to every potential crystal structure, but nonaxial nonmomentary 2G is almost certainly enough to place large sheet structures in considerably jeopardy, and probably enough to cause microfracturing and potential structural realignment, of doped crystals. Known (and projected using chemical propulsion) launch technology and systems greatly exceed that. (back-of-envelope-calculation) Windshear-plus-recovery-of-control-maneuver can at least come close even at present-day civilian aircraft speeds, requiring a massive quality-inspection apparatus to be put in place on retrieval/landing.

These problems can probably be minimized — but at what cost and practicality? Not to mention at what assumptions that **everybody** will cooperate.

**267:**

Every SF club is an anarchy. The mostly work, unless a) too many people leave, or b) some clique gets in, which usually means a handful of people, partly because no one else wants to do all that work in the "free" time. Right now, the large club I'm in has issues, and I've been "punished" for what's basically their libel and that they don't like me. 

This is a problem with _small_ colonies, or generation starships without many thousands of people: how do you get away from those who've decided They Don't Like You?

**268:**

The gods, fallen or otherwise, have/left behind imcomprehensible stuff. Say, you're an ancient Sumerian, and find a door of metal, into a cave, and there's these sheets of something in a frame, and a pad with a symbols on it, that you can push down, but have no idea what it does. And there are things around that (the gods would call power switches)... 

This is assuming they didn't simply leave things to tell you to Go Away, or maybe here's a bone, when you're done worrying it, maybe we'll give you another, but in the meantime, don't bother us. (Semi-spoiler for my 11,000 Years).

[renke_](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7979) | [October 16, 2024 19:04](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199308)

**271:**

While I don't see a feasible colonisation story with (more or less) current technology some kind of "seal hunter" model may be reachable, in the scope of the 18th/19th century approach (sail for a month to some remote place, club seals until the freight hold is full, sail back [with bad timing not before the sea ice is molten again]).

Here they had the means (keeping a ship with its crew alive and afloat for half a year or so, not a lot of provisions could be gathered where they went) and a target (it was worth it to haul back a ship full of sealskins), what about the seal hunter in space?

More or less plausible means in the near future are a starship, with a couple of Bigelow station segments attached to it; a huge volume (in space terms) for a smallish price (.. in space terms ..), giving enough room for a team of seal hunters and all the stuff they may need when sailing away for months. Add a second skin to the Bigelow balloons and the 100 tons of water moved to LEO in another starship should give a quite nice radiation shield for the squishy humans in them. Tack on solar panels similar to ISS and an ion drive and the whole thing is mobile.

The target? Much harder, imo. One of the Arjuna asteroids could be an energetically interesting destination, where the contraption I outlined above has enough delta-v to reach it, stay there a some time and come back again. But what are the sealskins? What makes it worthwhile? Nothing, I guess. Even a metal-rich asteroid does - I guess - not justify the costs. And the crew cannot simply collect gold nuggets, but this requires quite a lot of ore processing in space, to bring back only the metals and not tons and tons of asteroid dust.

Outside of curiosity/scientific interest (sometimes hard to differentiate) I don't see a good reason to perform a "seal hunter in space" stunt. Nothing comes to mind that is valuable enough in 100 tons/1 year time packages to ramp up such an operation. And the contraption requires a lot of investment, no one will build such a thing for a single tour to an asteroid coincidentally close to earth, to be seen again 50 years later or so.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3431)

**273:**

"We must love one another, or die."

It might not be necessary to go that far, but getting out into space habitation probably means we'll need to get better at treating each other well.

It might be institutional, it might be genetic (you've got the genes for bullying, you can't be part of the starship crew), it might be how children are raised, it might be something we can't imagine, but maybe it's a problem which has to be solved.

**274:**

_My tentative answer is that human capacity for work would need to be increased. This doesn't mean the result would need to be a leisureless dystopia._

Recent history in North America says people suck it up or die. Settlers to the northern great plains of the US and into Canada basically had to work sun up to sun down (and more in the winter) just to stay alive. The amount of firewood to be chopped in the summer to survive the winter was flat out huge. And they did it.

Not that I'd volunteer for such a situation.

And growing up on a farm prior to 1900 in the US wasn't much better no matter where you lived. Unless your name was Cartwright.

Side discussion on how the plains natives did it. Mostly with a standard of living the new folks would not accept.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

_I work for the chip-making division of Samsung._

When the auto industry in the US had to stop production then start shipping cars without features people who did not get it kept saying why don't they just go order more chips now? 

I think you basically confirmed my thoughts. That to actually do more than lab experiments in space based chip making we'd need a facility 50 to 100 (or more) times the volume of the ISS. And all that implies.

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) | [October 16, 2024 20:08](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199313)

**276:**

_Side discussion on how the plains natives did it. Mostly with a standard of living the new folks would not accept._

Low population densities had a lot to do with it.

It’s worth considering that the golden era of the Plains Indians on horses lasted barely two centuries, while the golden era of homesteading lasted a century at most. I’m not sure either group knew how to live sustainably on the prairies. The people they displaced probably had a better idea of what worked, but for the most part we don’t know what they did.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

**277:**

_I’m not sure either group knew how to live sustainably on the prairies._

Yes. 

But my comment was more about how much work you can get people to do when needed.

Personally I'd LOVE to visit a colonial star ship. But I would NOT want to be a colonist. I suspect the Mayflower Pilgrims had a better life and better survival rates than such future folks might have. And for the Pilgrims it was brutal. Only half of them made it a year. And that was a better survival rate than most of the folks who tried to settle from Europe to New England around that time.

Anyway, I suspect that not too long into a years long voyage, most non sleeping and eating time would be spend keeping things working.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199313) | [October 16, 2024 20:27](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199315)

**278:**

_I’m not sure either group knew how to live sustainably on the prairies._

In the eastern half of the US we have the mound builders. Apparently they figured out how to live in large groups (100s) for centuries. But they vanished by the 1600s/1700s. Small pox and such is suspected. 

Out west in the US, Mesa Verdi is an interesting place to visit. Obviously a thriving civilization was there for 100s of years. But about 800 years ago they left. And so far no one has found a diary or log book. Lack of water due to climate change is suspected as the most likely cause. Or was when I last read up on it.

We get to see how they lived due to the extensive small villages cut into the cliffs.

Worth a day or two if anyone happens to be near there.

[https://www.google.com/search?client=firefox-b-1-e&q=mesa+verdi](https://www.google.com/search?client=firefox-b-1-e&q=mesa+verdi)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4093)

[](http://artworkbydaleallen.blogspot.com/) [Dale Allen](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4093) | [October 16, 2024 20:49](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199316)

**279:**

National Geographic put out a streaming TV series called MARS with two seasons, part fictional drama and part documentary. A Ron Howard production.

Season 1: In 2033, the International Mars Science Foundation launches an expedition to Mars to establish a permanent research colony called Olympus Town. After the initial base is established in a lava tube with an abundance of frozen water, the base grows into a small colony over several years. The colonists withstand many hardships on the harsh cold and dusty planet. 

Season 2: After a decade on Mars, Lukrum, a corporate mining company sets up base near the colony and proceeds to bully its way into a disastrous finale.

As for the documentary parts during the episodes there are interviews with all the usual suspects from NASA scientists, engineers, and celebrities, such as Neil deGrasse Tyson, Robert Zubrin, Andy Weir, Susan Wise Bauer, and Dilbert Stark (of course).

Mars: <https://www.nationalgeographic.com/tv/show/c46d0bc3-872f-41ec-8bdd-ee70ad8dbba3>

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

**283:**

_It’s easier to educate children properly at home in the uk._

I get the impression the motivations for doing so are also different than over here. A lot of the people opposed to public schools are also opposed to _any_ government-funded initiatives.

We have TVO and other resources available, and yet large chunks of our homeschooling community ignores them in favour of buying materials from Billy-Bob's Christian Curriculum or whatever is being touted by various mommy blogger influencers… (Yes, I'm cynical. I've met too many of that type of parent.)

If you're curious, here's an overview of what is required:

  * letter of intent delivered to your local school board



And here's a summary of what subjects you have to teach:

And here's a list of the standardized tests your children will have to pass:

(Yes, the last two lists are empty. That's because there are no requirements and no tests, although you can choose to have your child take the tests children in public school must pass if you want to.)

I've looked at some 'highly regarded' homeschooling resources, and was very underwhelmed by them. Lots of worksheets and low-level learning, but not much higher-order thinking required.

**285:**

_why don't they send up one or two people, as they do at Antartic stations, whose just is maintenance?_

Wild stab says what when they're only sending 2-4 people up at a time and the total population is normally those 2-4 people, they don't want to risk it. Everyone who goes up has to have a general aptitude for learning and being flexible. They've relaxed from "must have STEM PhD _and_ be an experienced test pilot" levels of learning and flexible, but not a huge amount. They definitely don't have enough people that a dishwasher who can also operate a toaster has any place (unlike the US Antarctic base).

My impression is that the "job description" for an ISS occupant is lengthy, and includes a bunch of special training so they can operate the various research toys on the ISS as well as all the basic stuff, from operating the taxi home to getting out of a spacesuit without wrecking it. Plus obviously a tolerance for everything from wearing nappies to sleeping next to the machine that goes GRRRRRRR BING!! GRRRRRRRR all 'night'.

There's also the point that lots of people wanna be a astronought, they really really wanna be one. So adding more filters doesn't reduce the pool of possibilities to an unreasonable number, and thus the selection committees can start by just throwing every single filter anyone comes up with into the pile, dropping the candidate list in the top and seeing what comes out.

**287:**

_Anyway, I suspect that not too long into a years long voyage, most non sleeping and eating time would be spend keeping things working._

Ya think? 

People tend to view starships as like hotels or submarines, but given the closed ecosystem constraint, I suspect that, to a first approximation, the crew section would be a greenhouse (more than 90% of the productive biomass has to be nonhuman, ideally 99% or more). It could be a pinkhouse (one of those indoor facilities where the LEDs are optimized to only produce light frequencies plants use) but I suspect that having daylight-colored LEDs and lots of green would save the crew's sanity, which is also important.

I'd even go so far as to say that, if someone's doing a video of life on a long-duration starship with artificial gravity (the latter to let it be shot in real life), that making a major set one or more high tech greenhouses would be a really good and different way to go.

I'd also expect that a colony on a new world, even if the surface was directly habitable without PPE, would look a lot like the life support system of the starship, scaled up and replicated as much as possible. In other words, each settlement would be a huge greenhouse complex of some appropriate sort.

In a video, someone could have fun contrasting the terrestrial daylight inside the colonial greenhouses with, say, the light of the red dwarf they're living under. The intensity (W/M2) would be about the same, but the spectrum would most definitely not be. Using the difference between inside and outside lighting would set the scene quite nicely, even if the outside shots were filmed at Vasquez Rocks or the Alabama Hills with the vegetation mocked up using cheap Plasticaceae trucked in from a prop warehouse ( <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vasquez_Rocks> <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alabama_Hills> )

As for the death rate... yeah. I'm wondering why some here think that the solution would be to ship hundreds of millions of random, non-reproducing people in from Earth, as opposed to getting the cost/benefit per surviving child as low as possible, encouraging the surviving adults to have as many children as possible, and to raise them cooperatively? Oh well.

**290:**

I have _some_ clue: freeze-drying, Teflon, Kapton, structural composites, and more I won't list right now- but whenever I actually look the list grows. I'm not against exploration of outer space, but we have already done "man in a can" and we should use cheap robots instead of valuable people (that's the only kind, y'all.)

I first saw Star Wars on a 40-foot screen, bright colors, beautiful people, literally overwhelming sound. I was small and impressionable. You were too, once. Do you remember?

At that time, Star Trek happened (for me) on a tiny black-and-white lozenge, blurry and with hokey music. I didn't understand its complex goings-on with my tiny undeveloped brain. I didn't understand Star Wars either, but: oooooh shiny!

Now that I'm more nearly capable of understanding what each is about, given a choice I'd pick Star Trek every time. Star Wars is still very shiny, and "the force" is quite useful in my daily life, but I don't actually need to live in a movie to access what works in this world. If I was to live in a world designed by storytellers, I will pick Gene Rodenberry over GLucas every time, twice on Sundays.

**291:**

Since it's been brought up, I fink it's time again to plug my favorite large-scale anarchism, the Debian Project: debian.org

If you haven't heard of it, the Debian Project is the volunteer project that maintains the Debian System, one of the oldest and probably the single most widely-used "linux distribution". I suspect that Debian's popularity as a VPS operating system means that there are more installations of Debian operating at any given time than there are physical computers. The Project consists of a core group of about 1500 volunteer "members" defined by their presence on various keyrings, and an unknown number of members of various "teams" working on various aspects of the project (Kernel Team, FTP-Masters team, i18n team, etc.) Teams are porous and informal, levels of formality vary by team. Volunteer maintainers have "ownership" over packages (meaning that they have sole ability to push patches or to delegate said ability). The Project is explicitly set up in such a way that no one can force anyone else to do anything, but the work still gets done. 

If you are on a Debian system, or any system that can pull packages from Debian main package archive, you can 'apt-get anarchism', which installs a version of the "Anarchism FAQ" maintained by a Debian team. This FAQ is voluminous, so if you are looking for an unreasonable amount of detail on some folks' opinions and information about anarchism, there is a lot of it in there. I read some of it and found it stultifyingly boring. YMMV.

IIRC not much explicit mention of anarchism is in the current versions of the Debian Manifesto or the Debian Constitution. Documents which support my claim that the Project was founded with anarchist principles in mind probably exist, but I don't remember where. There might be references to them in the aforementioned FAQ.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

**294:**

Heteromeles @ 276: _It’s worth considering that the golden era of the Plains Indians on horses lasted barely two centuries_

True, because -- unlike their previous primary pack animal species -- horses were large enough to actually carry an adult human at useful speeds and distances, as well as cargo on a travois or pack frame. 

Since that previous pack animal (_Canis familiaris_) had been domesticated for about as long as humans have been documented as living in the American continents, it had a rather longer run as a highly useful technological enhancement for those populations. (Dog-dawn travois were very much a thing, especially for the Plains Indians in particular, for a _very_ long time.)

**295:**

There is quite a bit of archaeology about how people lived on the Prairies, and much of the rest of the continent. Not so much in the popular imagination though.

If you are ever in the area, a visit to 'Head Smashed in Buffalo Jump' is a very well spent day. >10,000 years of intermittent usage of the same location for the (self-explanatory) function, with occasional gaps of as much as 1000 years. 

Someone on here has spoken at length about oral histories and how to do things being passed down through millenia.

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) | [October 17, 2024 04:58](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199333)

**296:**

_Someone on here has spoken at length about oral histories and how to do things being passed down through illenia._

IIRC, most of the tribes that dominated the Great Plains by the 1850s were nowhere near the Plains in 1500. 

I do completely agree that there's 10,000 years of archaeology out there, at least. Thing is, the way they used the Plains (stampeding bison into traps, especially) changed completely once horses started filtering out from Old Mexico (including Texas and California) and especially once large herds of mustangs ran wild and could be caught and tamed.

For example, the Comanche were a Shoshone (Great Basin) tribe. The Lakota started off in Minnesota in the woods. The Osage started by the Mississippi and Ohio Rivers. All of them got pushed towards the Plains by Americans and/or other tribes, until they got enough horses to get really mobile and adopted a nomadic lifestyle from those they encountered. The golden age of the Plains Indians really was quite short, and the actual history of it is pretty wild. 

I should note that I AM NOT saying that the Plains Indians were worse environmentally than the settlers! The homesteading population boom is dying away, and the human density on the Great Plains is returning down to where it was when the Plains Indians dominated. What they were doing was more sustainable than what white immigrants did (cf Dust Bowl, Ogallala aquifer depletion, etc). Could they have kept it up for 10,000 years? That's much harder to answer.

**300:**

»As for the death rate...«

Will be 100% soon enough.

On any interstellar ship, microbial passengers will outnumber the rest by a factor well north of millions, they will not be individually ticketed or subject to security checks before boarding - and they will mutate.

Even taking state-of-the-art medical technologies such as "call a friend back home" and 3D-protein printers as a given, how big a crew of medical/biology/ecology scientists will it take, to monitor and steer the biosphere inside safe mission parameters ?

How many isolation barriers and controls are necessary, to ensure that /any/ contagion can be contained to a survivable small fraction of the ecology ?

My guess is at least two dozen vehicles, traveling in a loose formation (d(nom)=1AU ?) and the only permissible transfers of /anything/ from one vehicle to another, is one-way transfers for recolonization, to commence no sooner than a century after last signs of life were detected ?

Anybody feels like writing the story about the first time the century timer experies ?

**302:**

a carefully bounded AI... hardcoded limits to keep it on task...

arrives at new planet, evaluates as potential colony... searches... detects civilization challenge level low if there's radio or other overt stuff otherwise passively observes for ten years to verify no higher level critters verging onto sapience and/or sentience

if some critter found, continues observations indefinitely... no further action...

if clear of local intelligence then chunk of land selected for 'gamma soak'

given attributes of soil + climate + water + etc there is selection of a pre-defined package of seeds which are 3D printed and carefully air-dispersed from LEO... 

important note: these are 'designed species' spliced with robust reinforcement to prevent mutation...

observations of fail or success... alternative seed packages selected until one of 'em take root and spreads outwards

at intervals other seed packages printed up and dropped down 

eventually... insects and minor rodents as means of seed dispersals and pollination...

additions of yet more species of fauna and flora to build upon into a complex ecology approximating Earth circa 1000 BCE... post-human slaughter of mega-fauna and pre-human overpopulation

**307:**

FYI the '3nm Process' that Intel (and the other manufacturers) talk about is just a marketing term and doesn't actually refer to the feature size on the silicon. This has been the case for decades now IIRC as they lost parity between marketing term and actual feature size a long time ago.

For 3nm, the Wikipedia article quotes a gate pitch of 48nm and a metal pitch of 24nm.

<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/3_nm_process>

**312:**

_I first saw Star Wars on a 40-foot screen, bright colors, beautiful people, literally overwhelming sound. I was small and impressionable._

I saw _Star Wars_ with my father the summer it came out.

It felt _real_ in a way that other SF movies and TV shows I'd watched didn't, even if I couldn't articulate why. My dad pointed out that in _Star Wars_ the technology is old and worn, not new and shiny. It's just taken for granted by the characters rather than being showcased as a character itself. 

I think he hit the nail on the head, at least for my reaction. A lot of _Star Trek_ plots turn around some technology, or solving a technological problem. _Star Wars_ had cool things like Luke's floating landspeeder as part of the background, something so common and everyday that the characters didn't even notice them. 

_Star Trek_ may have been better science fiction, but _Star Wars_ was better **theatre**.

It didn't hurt that _Star Wars_ had a young untrained teenaged boy only a few years older than myself as a main character, while _Star Trek_ only had old people as characters!

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

**314:**

Silicon fab iiinn spaaace... Yes, some techniques need hard vacuum, e.g sputtering, doping and thin film evaporation, but the lines currently use gravity to keep the wafers in place where they don't use vacuum clamps, and others, e.g. photoresist spinning, etching, currently need gravity and atmosphere to work well because fluids. An automated orbital fab would be using mechanical rather than vacuum clamping to manipulate delicate, fragile wafers, and would need some contained fluid-bath system to do the wet etches and keep the HF from getting out and into the rest of the installation. I'm not saying it couldn't come to pass, but I think the value of the microgravity / hard vacuum stages would have to be overwhelming to be worth re-engineering the rest of it to be even possible on orbit.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229)

**318:**

_microgravity_

This came up a while back on this blog when people were discussing asteroid mining and similar. My point was that much of our ore processing into useful things was hugely dependent on gravity. (And our current atmospher.) And re-engineering it to work in microgravity would likely be cost prohibitive.

**319:**

Re: 'Bad assumptions that could produce terrible outcomes:'

Agree - esp. about the bio/med and sex/social/leadership roles.

My impression/experience is that when most folks talk about space exploration, they mean 'advanced' gadgets/tech manufactured to physically move humans into and around space. Such folk rarely mention any human-being related 'advanced' bio-psych aspects. 

Microbes were mentioned ... 

Yeah - And how about all those remnants of ancient viruses, bacteria and fungi in our genes? We're stuck with them and are unlikely to delete/erase them from the astronauts/space colonists' DNA. I've no idea whether NASA has ever looked at what genetic rewriting/error checking malfunctions typically occur to their astronauts, and if they do, whether they can be passed on to future generations (epigenetic inheritance). Not sure whether any NASA mission astronauts procreated after a space stint. 

Reproduction in space ...

Because the spaceship environment would be very highly controlled, and assuming that progress would have been made in the fields of bio/medicine, I've been wondering whether a strap on placenta could work. Strap-on because then males could also (literally) carry children and because research has shown that there is some valuable interaction between the fetus and parent (language - rhythm, tone, frequency). A strap-on placenta could also have in-built antibody detectors/purifiers to ensure that neither body undergoes any extreme immune reaction.

Once the colony lands, the strap on placentas would continue to be used until the environment is deemed safe enough and the population large enough - or larger than the available inventory.

Re: ST:TNG

From my recall, the first season was sort of an attempt to copy the same feel and crew-interaction as the original series - cowboys in space! Not sure - it's been years since I last watched but sometime in the third season I think, Patrick Stewart finally stopped doing the Kirk-stereotypical hyper reactive/thinks he knows-it-all captain and became the Captain Jean-Luc Picard - calm, rational, human and collaborative. Even though Kirk makes me wince, one thing that both series excelled at was looking at the possible consequences of what might be found in space - not just the gadgets/tech but different beings/societies. 

I think that the 'god episode, Q' in ST-TNG was set up like a Shakespearean comedy/fantasy for a reason - it looks absurd but hey! a whole bunch of people like the idea of a god. Problem is when that god thinks you're a toy at best. (Wonder what people of the Buddhist mindset think of this SF series, esp. these first two episodes.)

ST:TNG also came up with the scariest (quasi) alien race ever - The Borg: when humans and computers/AI combine, the result is computer/AI. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229)

**324:**

The problem with Star Trek (any TV show really) is that you need to fill a slot, and if the best script you have available for the slot is drek, that's what you've got to go with. So you end up with brilliant scripts, like _City on the Edge of Forever_ or the one where Picard recovers from being Borged. And awful scripts like _Code of Honor_ or _Spock's Brain._ You go into a new Star Trek show understanding that it's going to be a mixed bag and the first season will probably be weak. (TNG is the poster-child for 'weak first season.')

For Charlie I'd probably recommend _Deep Space Nine,_ because an 'American-style military system' is the jumping off point and the lead character, Commander Benjamin Sisko, ends up on the frontier, encountering one weird culture/weird biology after another. The first season is a little weak, but there's a lot of good stuff in the show about what happens when a human encounters really weird aliens. (Naturally there's the occasional weak episode as well.) Also what happens when one weird alien encounters another weird alien, after the viewers have adapted to things like Klingon or Trill biology/culture.

But the show is _definitely not_ any form of Rah-Rah USA, and Avery Brooks as Benjamin Sisko is amazing to watch, IMHO one of the really great actors given an amazing, conflicted character to play, and Nana Visitor is superb as Kira Narise.

I should also note that I don't think any Star Trek show is really "middle America in space." Even the original series questioned a lot of common American wisdom and involved encounters with other cultures/ideas, some of which were provably superior. An _idealized_ form of U.S. culture is _the baseline_ for the show, a jumping-off point the average American can understand, but it heads off in interesting directions quickly-enough (and sometimes gets thing wrong, but that's what happens when you watch something almost sixty years old.) It's also something of an SF 'variety show.' One episode might involve a space battle, while another deals with an alien's weird biology and a third involves dealing with computer issues, parasitology, or an evolved being, so eventually the viewer finds something which 'hits exactly the right spot' and hopefully at least enjoys the rest of it.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199358) | [October 17, 2024 17:15](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199362)

**325:**

The problem is that free fall is like extended bed rest. Without the strain of gravity, bones and muscles atrophy, immune function decreases, and apparently other systems are affected. 

While I’m not so worried about pathogens, because, despite what you may think, it is possible to make a spacecraft usefully clean, I think it’s worth worrying a lot about decreased immune function and other skeleton-related things. This very definitely includes pregnancy, as the mother does undergo skeletal changes on multiple levels, the fetus develops in gravity, and gravity is often used during childbirth to get the baby out. And doing the medical, psychological, and social experiments to figure out how to enable humans to reproduce in microgravity is ethically icky, to put it mildly. Experimental sex in space? With samples afterward? Published and peer reviewed? And exploring the causes of multiple miscarriages? 

Thinking of what could go wrong, I suspect ethics panels might even cringe at solving the reproduction problem for mice. 

Another thing to be concerned about is cancer, because immune systems play a big role in squelching cancers at the incipient stage, and space is a high radiation environment.

Some of the issues can be solved with the current monastic approach of expecting astronauts to effective take vows of chastity and obedience while in orbit, much as they do in the military.

In the longer term, humans need forces pushing us down onto surfaces, probably at close to 1g, to keep us healthy.

Then there’s the problem of transitioning back to the ground after an extended stay in microgravity. I think Chris Hadfield’s _An Astronaut’s Guide to Life on Earth_ covers it most eloquently. The tl;dr is basically, if you thought long Covid was bad…he could barely walk when he got down, and I have no clue if he ever completely recovered after a year in space. 

The idea of space marines spending a long time in microgravity and then landing on a planet and immediately performing at high level is ludicrous. Ironically, the _War of the Worlds_ probably got it more right, with the Martians crawling out of their ship and going on a rampage with powered armor. That might be us one day, exploring a new planet after a year of free fall inside a warp bubble.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8141)

**333:**

Re: 'Or, equally likely, snaps & quite justifyably ... wastes the "teaser" ...'

My assumption is: assuming at least street-level/amateur psych knowledge, candidates would be screened out if they displayed or had a history of overt aggression. If so, then passive-aggressives would make the cut even though their behavior can result in equally tragic outcomes, i.e., their victims suicide. They then hide behind their facade of non-aggression: 'since X committed suicide, it can't be my fault!' 

My other assumption is that if there are only 200 crew then chances are that everyone had interactions with both these people. Once the details came out, it'd be likely that every member of the crew would start reviewing/questioning their interactions with all of their fellow crew members. Apart from psych/emotional trauma, this would be a recipe for deep divisions possibly ending in splinter groups which would likely lead to disaster. 

The human element is the weakest part of any space colonization dream/program and few authors ever even mention it in passing. 

David L @ 321: Re: 'NASA Twin study'

Thanks - I'd forgotten that! Just looked it up - it's going to be followed for years. Good - we need this type of info.

<https://www.nasa.gov/humans-in-space/twins-study/about/>

R. Degenhand @ 323: Re: '... cosmonaut Valentine Tereshkova had a daughter roughly a year after her mission on Vostok 6 in 1963'

Thanks for the info! 

Given the timing and the USSR's almost complete lack of (political) interest in things medical back then*, plus her quite limited time in space (in orbit for 3 days), not sure there's enough info to infer anything from what I read in Wikipedia. If you have any specific sci-level articles/confirmed info, hope you pass it along. 

<https://www.jstor.org/stable/45310937>

*Extended family lived in one of the Republics and their comments jibed with the above article. For a feel of what life was like over there - when most of the population didn't live in major urban areas - recommend Bujold's 'The Mountains of Mourning'.

**339:**

My brother in law has four kids, while my wife was one of five. Your point?

I’ll paraphrase my wife’s comment, when I showed her the Foreign Affairs article that showed birth rates dropping below replacement level across the board. The right wing author basically blamed it on women’s lib. My wife scoffed and pointed out that having kids these days is fucking expensive, so by the time a career woman can afford to have kids, she’s lucky if she can have one. What do you expect to happen when education is expensive but necessary, housing and food are expensive but necessary, pregnancy is expensive, child care is expensive and necessary and society actively does not want children taking care of their parents, at least given how people who do do (raises hand) are shamed and marginalized? That last, incidentally, means that children never get to repay their parents. A kid is a luxury good in our system.

Now tell me which of these factors would apply in a colony on an empty world? Everyone will be pitching in to care for the young, the sick, and the old. What other option will they have? If they have life support to spare, why not have more kids and put the older ones to work minding and teaching the younger ones?

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3097)

The show began with the blacksmith losing his wife. At the end, they find that the colony leader and her son had deliberatly cut them off (without telling anyone) so they could "go back". **347:**

_At a smaller scale I keep wondering just where Starship pads will allowed to be built. I mean if something goes wrong you have a 35 story building being hit by a 20 story building full of explosives. (Well not full but full enough)._

Nope: at landing, the booster weighs about 200 metric tonnes and is intentionally almost out of fuel. Fuel it carries back to the pad is by definition parasitic/surplus mass that could better have been spent on the payload. I suspect it has a margin of less than 5 seconds remaining at capture, even taking into account the weight reduction from 4000-odd tonnes at launch (for the booster) to 200 tonnes at landing.

The real problem is most likely what if there's a catastrophic failure on the pad at the moment of lift-off, at which point there's almost 5500 tonnes of propellant on board.

But bear in mind the propellant isn't a high explosive, it's two unmixed liquids that won't go boom instantaneously. And the pad and tower are massive steel and concrete structures with a deluge cooling system.

Yes, a launch explosion will probably total the tower and require a rebuild (your closest reference point is probably the 3 July 1969 failure of the Soviet [N1 Serial 5](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/N1_\(rocket\)#Second_failure,_serial_5L) test flight, which fell back on the pad and exploded (with about 2300 tonnes of fuel on board: **The majority of the N-1's propellant load had not been consumed in the accident, and most of what had burned had been in the first stage of the rocket. However, the worst-case scenario, mixing of the fuel and LOX to form an explosive gel, had not occurred. The subsequent investigation revealed that up to 85% of the propellant on board the rocket had not detonated, reducing the force of the blast.**

Still bad, but as long as the pad's a long way from anywhere it's not _terminally_ bad.

I note that the big pads at KSC are several miles from the VAB, and so apparently are the towers at Starbase: spaceports are _big_ because rockets sometimes go boom.

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199216) | [October 17, 2024 19:42](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199385)

**348:**

_As an example, I don’t think anyone would now seriously propose pushing a small asteroid into cislunar orbit for mining purposes, if the upshot of doing so is a Kessler Cascade that makes accessing geosynchronous orbit impossible._

The risk of a Kessler cascade is a _low_ Earth orbit hazard -- once you're putting satellites signficantly higher than the upper atmosphere, the volume available goes up as the cube of the orbital radius (so the risk of a collision drops as the cube root of the orbital radius).

GEO is literally 100 times higher than LEO. And Lunar orbit is ten times as far away again. So we're looking at a million-fold reduction in Kessler cascade risk (in GEO) and a billion-fold reduction in the risk for stuff happening in Lunar-adjacent orbits.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from Olivier Galibert](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199217) | [October 17, 2024 19:46](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199386)

**349:**

*My personal hypothesis on how space "colonies" could happen:

Hypothesis 1: the cost of going to orbit goes down enough to be equivalent to, say, a transatlantic flight. Given SpaceX, I wouldn't call it magic wand, not entirely.

Hypothesis 2: Some economical activities are found that work better in orbit (some examples seen before, like finer semiconductors).*

H1 _definitely_ can't happen as long as our launchers rely on burning carbon-based fuels: to put a tonne of mass into orbit takes at a minimum 10 tonnes of fuel (more typically 30-50 tonnes for a reusable 'ship), while crossing the Atlantic in a modern wide-body with high bypass turbofans takes about 0.25 tonnes of carbon per passenger. As fuel accounts for about a third the running costs of a civil airliner, even if all other costs for an orbital launch system are held down to the level of an airliner (which SpaceX seems to be aiming for) the cost per unit payload will still be about 100x higher to reach orbit than to cross the Atlantic.

H2 cannot be ruled out but there's nothing currently obvious except for: constellations of low-latency comsats, positioning clusters (which can be rolled into the comsat clusters), earth resources missions (civilian: weather and prospecting; military: spysats), and scientific research/astronomy (for which the budget is comparatively tiny).

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229)

**352:**

Seems to me that you risk creating the situation where something happens, a non-maintenance task must be done... but the only person available can only do maintenance. So either everyone dies or you rush launch a manned spaceflight to bring in the extra person. Saving a few dollars by hiring cheaper labour could get really expensive really fast. FFS, right now they're mopping up from "the new manned spacecraft seems to mostly work, we think". But at least it was cheap, right? Cheap matters more!

In my day job a big part of what I do is maintain a queue of things to do and that order of that stack get shuffled as well as new tasks being added at random positions. It's only really a queue in the sense that there's at most one task in the "currently doing" slot. Right now, for example, one coworker is whining because the task I'm working with them on keeps being interrupted by the two other "most urgent" tasks I'm doing for other people. Although I don't think they know about the second task yet, it happened overnight and might be complete before they start work for the day (I started at 0600 and they normally start about 0900). I don't _like_ interruptions and I struggle with context switches, but that's how my job works and the rest makes up for it.

**354:**

Robert Prior @ 312:

> _"I first saw Star Wars on a 40-foot screen, bright colors, beautiful people, literally overwhelming sound. I was small and impressionable."_

_I saw Star Wars with my father the summer it came out._

_It felt real in a way that other SF movies and TV shows I'd watched didn't, even if I couldn't articulate why. My dad pointed out that in Star Wars the technology is old and worn, not new and shiny. It's just taken for granted by the characters rather than being showcased as a character itself._

_I think he hit the nail on the head, at least for my reaction. A lot of Star Trek plots turn around some technology, or solving a technological problem. Star Wars had cool things like Luke's floating landspeeder as part of the background, something so common and everyday that the characters didn't even notice them._

_Star Trek may have been better science fiction, but Star Wars was better theatre._

_It didn't hurt that Star Wars had a young untrained teenaged boy only a few years older than myself as a main character, while Star Trek only had old people as characters!_

Before Star Wars there was 2001: A Space Odyssey. 

And long before that, while I was a child, the old Flash Gordon & Buck Rogers movie serials from the 1930s were on **TV** on Saturday Mornings (like they had been in the movie theaters back before I was born) ...

Star Wars was an update of those old serials with good, non-cheesy special effects à la 2001.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from whitroth](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199225) | [October 17, 2024 22:29](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199392)

**355:**

_repairing/maintaining spacesuits I would expect to be like mobile phone repair... or, perhpas, like maintaining SCUBA gear_

Mobile phones, no. SCUBA gear: a qualified "yes": if your SCUBA gear fails in use you very probably die. However, a space suit is _way_ more complicated than an oxygen tank and regulator -- way more complicated than even a deep-diving dry suit with heliox or hydrogen-oxygen gas mix. Just read up on the number of layers in a typical current-generation spacesuit if you don't believe me, and what they're specialized for.

In part, we're in this position because there haven't been enough suit designs flown to get a handle on the problem space. We're probably at the same level of sophistication as Jacques Cousteau's SCUBA kit was in the 1950s/1960s, before the huge boom in offshore oil rigs started driving development (which previously had been a thing for amateurs and the odd navy diver). It's clear that we'll get better suits by and by, and they'll probably be more efficient and simpler in design.

But I'm still absolutely certain that a space suit is a piece of kit that _can't_ be allowed to fail, any more than any other life support system can be, and it has to pack a lot of functionality into a very compact and relatively lightweight object.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5955)

**358:**

_How do you have an armed revolution without wrecking the habitat?_

You take over the SCADA controllers running the various gas/fluid circulation systems.

Per-module induced hypoxia -- or even just backed up toilets in a zero-gee habitat -- make a hell of a weapon.

(Hint: the backed-up toilet is a lot scarier in free fall than you might expect: Shuttle orbiters flew with a handheld vacuum on board _specifically_ to chase down and capture small floaters, lest somebody suffocate in their sleep on a feral turd fragment.)

**364:**

Sea monkeys, aka fairy shrimp, don’t work that way. The adults are ephemeral. The eggs, in the egg case, can tolerate dehydration for a couple of decades.

So if the goal is storing stem cells for medical use, then this might work. So far I’ve needled a bunch of biotechs to try it, because room temperature storage of stem cells would be effing useful. No response so far. 

The key question is whether animals that do anhydrous (like tardigrades, maybe some frogs and insects) live longer by doing the thing, or whether it just lets them survive conditions that would otherwise kill them. That’s not really clear, at least for vertebrates.

Being able to go into aestivation (room temperature hibernation) as mouse lemurs do might be really useful if humans can be induced to aestivate on the way to Mars or something. But it’s only situationally useful to a ticket to the stars.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8772)

**365:**

"do you know of a woman who wants five or ten children?"

See there's a whole can of worms there. The answer, usually, is "it depends" -- not that anyone should be going around asking women how many children they want in the first place. There are indeed women who want lots of children, the mother of my grand-daughter for one. But It Depends. If you take away: financial concerns, limitations of available space for growing said children, limitations on the amount of time available for caring for same, limitations on the available help and medical advancements that make it safer to bear and deliver children, there are indeed women who would love to have lots of children. Some women really enjoy being pregnant, and it's safe for them. Some it isn't safe for. Some women only find out during their first pregnancy that it's dangerous or fatal for them. Some women can never have children, or can't have them with their chosen partner.

And whether or not those women want to go to space and are more common than by those who don't want children is a whole nother question. I suspect they're the ends of a spectrum.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Vulch](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199382) | [October 17, 2024 23:40](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199403)

**366:**

I was just thinking of Mirabile! Yes, I very much agree that Raisers or similar really make a lot of sense for a colony!

There are other tricks from the ethnographic literature. For instance, on one archipelago (Kiribati?) they had a fair number of children. One practice was to adopt a younger child out to the kid’s aging grandparents to be raised by them. Then the kid would care for their grandparents when they got old and died, and then the kid would inherit their stuff. Yes, I can see where this would go wrong, but it could work better than what we do now.

I think in general, adoption and fostering within a community is not a bad thing, especially if it gets the kid into a more nurturing environment or lets them develop innate skills that the parents can’t help with.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199392) | [October 17, 2024 23:48](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199404)

**367:**

A space suit, is basically a heavily armored pressure suit. The problem is the pressure differential, because a human can’t bend joints that are stiffened by a 1 atm pressure differential between inside and outside. So they have to use 0.33 atm or less and pure oxygen.

So that’s the inside. There’s also the temperature control do they won’t freeze or boil, and the bomb tech armor that goes on top of it to keep them unholy. All those layers make it a pain to fix. It seems that the ISS was trying to go in for modular suits, so that the astronauts could mix and match tops and bottoms to get something to fit. Personally, I think that telepresence robots could be more useful, especially on the outside of a station where telemetry only goes a few meters.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3431)

[Mr. Tim](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6407) | [October 18, 2024 00:01](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199407)

**370:** of kids: 

I did meet a young (-er than me) man who told me he had 9 kids with his wife (i.e. his wife had the 9 kids).

I do admit I don't remember anybody else with so many kids (my brother has 6, and one of his kids has 5).

Survival in the wilderness: My wife and I have been watching "The Last Alaskans", a cross between a documentary and a reality show. It does illustrate how tough it is to live in the wilderness even with access to modern technology and being able to call for help.

Timeline for the _Conquest of SPACE!_ : Considering it was a hundred+ years between Columbus and Mayflower (first more or less successful settlement), and even more between when the Vikings explored North America, I think you need to accept that it will be probably at least as long between Apollo and a "permanent" moon base/space station (much less the rest of the solar system). 

There will be temporary outposts, getting bigger and more personnel who start bringing things to leave there because it is convenient until a critical mass is reached and somebody suddenly realizes they can (barely) get by without the next supply run.

**372:**

That's part of what I was saying -- I don't go around asking women how many children they want. It's none of my business, nor anyone else's except her partner's. You also seem to be completely missing another point: here and now there are so many factors that influence that question that it's essentially meaningless for the purposes of this exercise. We're talking about colonising space, not living on Earth in the 21st century.

You know what they say about assumptions, right? Some of those women I mentioned who like being pregnant don't necessarily like children; my mother loved babies but did not like children at all. On the other hand I have a very good friend who only stopped at three because she has a career and children are expensive to raise, here and now. I do in fact know people who would happily do nothing but raise children; on the other hand I personally had very negative reactions from people when they asked the very personal and loaded question "WHEN are you going to have children?" and I replied "Never".

People ask that question without knowing anything much about the individual; it's really painful for those who want children but can't have them and is at least as bad as asking someone if they are pregnant based purely on they way they look. /rant

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8772)

**374:**

The far end of the Conquest curve is Oceania, where it took circa 40,000 years to go from the big islands off New Guinea to the nearer islands of Oceania. 

There’s even evidence that they tried and failed to colonize early on, not just in terms of archaeology, but also in wild marsupials that were pretty obviously moved by humans, but a long time ago. The island fox and Falklands wolf are likely similar cases of tamed animals taken to islands by humans. 

Anyway, one _could_ posit that it takes humans up to 40,000 years to figure out how to live in space. This actually works climatologically. Apparently, aside from our idiot climate change, we’ve got about another 40,000 years of relative climate stability before the Milankovich cycles start making our climate pendulum back and forth and likely doom global civilization. That provides a ready-made plot driver for why humans would start looking for other places to live after lumping it in Earth for so long.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796)

[Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796) | [October 18, 2024 00:47](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199412)

**375:**

I have known a few people, women and men, who aspired to having a large brood of kidlets, then after the arrival of one or two actual children completely abandoned the idea of having multiples.

I could be in that category - I was quite interested in having one, pleased about a second, and within weeks of the arrival of child #2 I was taking steps to ensure there would not be a third. 

With a different socioeconomic milieu I could see that going very differently. I like the kids and really enjoy my time with them, I'd have been quite happy to have more - but not on top of working >50 hours/week, paying for childcare and insane housing costs as well as food etc. Change those inputs to the formula and my outcome might have been different as well. Of course, spouse is another person who might have made different choices as well.

Most of the people I know with >2 children tend to be in a second or third marriage. A couple of my children's friends are younger than their nephews and nieces, because dad went and had kids again after the first ones moved out. Have a couple kids early, get divorced, meet someone new, have more kids. The remaining very few people I know with multiple children are in some combination of crunchy granola/Xian fundamentalists. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229)

**377:**

Chris Hadfield’s real life memoir is a case in point. He mentioned that one of his sons is a professional poker player. His book is my favorite astronaut memoir so far, the second being _Riding Rockets._

My sense is that modern astronauts are a lot like professors and other workaholics. Their children see what the lifestyle is like and don’t pursue it even if qualified. This, incidentally, is one of the things that I think making a government out of astronauts so interesting. If colonists rarely if ever become second generation astronauts, then it’s a lot more democratic than it would be if astronauts status could be inherited.
