[ilya187](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1357) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199392) | [October 18, 2024 02:52](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199419)

**382:**

_In part, we're in this position because there haven't been enough suit designs flown to get a handle on the problem space... But I'm still absolutely certain that a space suit is a piece of kit that can't be allowed to fail, any more than any other life support system can be_

IIRC, Freeman Dyson once said "The reason nuclear power is so expensive is that it is not allowed to fail". What he meant, people who design nuclear power plants are not allowed to experiment. As long as "failure is not an option" is the design philosophy, you will always have over-engineered finicky solutions. You have to account for every potential failure mode because you are not allowed to experiment and see which failure modes actually happen and which do not.

Compare it with the airplanes: Over the last 120 years something like 100,000 different airplane designs have been tried. About 500 are currently in use, and all of them are very safe — in part because thousands of people died in the designs which were not. 

Same thing with cars, ships, trains, you name it. All started out as finicky devices with countless failure points, and eventually got very safe. For years I swore I will never dive with a rebreather — too many things to go wrong, — but over last few years rebreathers also seem to have undergone the transition into "mature technology".

If/when spacesuits start being treated as airplanes or cars — try many different designs, with understanding that some wearers will not come back, — it should take at most 100 years before solutions are as elegant and easy to use as any mature technology. As will life-support systems, and pretty much anything to do with space. But this relies on the paradigm of "failure and death will happen, we learn the lessons and keep going" as opposed to "failure is not an option".

BTW, SpaceX’s policy of "build it, fly it, crash it, figure out what went wrong, repeat" is very much in line with what I described. And antithetical to how everyone else did spaceflight before.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

**384:**

_SpaceX’s policy of "build it, fly it, crash it, figure out what went wrong, repeat" is very much in line with what I described. And antithetical to how everyone else did spaceflight before.,/i >_

That's how spaceflight _used_ to be.

Part of the problem, at least in America, was the Space Race. The only Soviet flights people heard about were the successful ones, but every American failure was in the news. It's hard to do proper iterative design pushing the envelope when the public relations people want only successes for their propaganda…

(There's a scene in the first episode of _For All Mankind_ , when the American astronauts are getting drunk in a bar after the Soviets have beaten them to the moon, and a reporter tricks one of them into saying that the reason the Americans lost was that they got hung up on not making any mistakes and not losing any rockets. Which, according to at least one history I've read, is what was happening to the Apollo Program even before Apollo XI.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from ilya187](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199419) | [October 18, 2024 04:30](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199422)

**385:**

Also worth remembering that nuclear power was introduced to the world with a bang or two….

Actually, my objection to my local nuclear power plant was when the engineers started ratting out managerial incompetence to the media, and the media then ratted out some of the execs for taking their regulators to Poland so they could bribe them. Oh, and they hired an untried contractor to up their generation capacity, who, erm, nuked the entire facility in the 2010s by installing a flawed design whose problems had been known since the 1950s, and so now the fuel rods IIRC are cooling off in stainless steel tanks buried in the beach by the ocean. Rusting? Yeah, I think I heard something about that.

Presumably other plants don’t do this, and they’re the ones Dyson was complaining about? I’d rather take my chances with wind and solar after this litany of unforced errors that made me wonder which shift Homer Simpson was on at that plant.

As for space suits, go look at the early pressure suits if you don’t think they’ve come a ways. I’d say they’re pretty decent, considering what they have to do. I’d also point out that, given the nature of spacecraft airlocks and the size of the suits, I’m a little unclear about how they’d recover the body or the suit after a fatal accident. It’s a little harder to do than pulling them up over the gunwale of a dive boat.

[Poul-Henning Kamp](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1774) replied to [this comment from ilya187](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199419) | [October 18, 2024 08:16](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199423)

**386:**

»IIRC, Freeman Dyson once said "The reason nuclear power is so expensive is that it is not allowed to fail". What he meant, people who design nuclear power plants are not allowed to experiment. «

I think you misread Dyson there: It's not that they are not "allowed" to experiment, it is that they cannot afford to experiment (any more).

From the moment EBR-1 powered the first light bulbs, the constant refrain has been "We just need to build it slightly larger to make it economical", so far culminating with the 4.5GW thermal output of the EPR, which at $10B-15B is anything but.

As costs have exploded, the acceptance of risk has evaporated - and no wonder.

One perverse outcome of this risk avoidance is that in both TMI, and Fukushima the operators fought for hours and days to "save" the reactors so they could be restarted, long time after that bridge had melted, and thus making everything much worse.

The entire raison-d'etre for the "Small Modular Reactor"-bros, is the assumption that people will probably not be quite as upset if something goes wrong at the 20MW/20M$ level, as at the 4.5GW/10B$ level.

Not an unreasonable assumption, but unfortunately their models keep telling them that they need to build slightly larger to make it economical, and they have long since stopped using the word "cheap".

As for experiments:

Even reading the sanitized "Proving the Principle" from INL is pretty hair-raising, the various personal accounts from the same events, for instance Haroldsen about BORAX, is even better entertainment. Once you learn about "nuclear plant ice condensers", it is hard to shake the conviction that all nuclear reactors are experiments.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6643)

**390:**

another non-trivial aspect of comparing GEO vs LEO

velocity

the lower the orbit around a gravitational singularity ("planet", "star", "collapsar") the higher the necessary velocity to maintain a circular orbit

so yeah volume is larger the further out you orbit but also you are moving slower, thus sweeping thru less of that volume in a given interval further reducing opportunities for impacts (though just not as severely sloped as that 'cube function' for volume)

but then again I'm just footnoting your post because it's 5:49 in the fracking morning local time and already awake

**391:**

you ought to take a brutal, math-prone approach to cell clusters... after a specific number of divides to demonstrate viability... either you have the technique to split the cell cluster into multiple chunks without fuss or you've been mixing up multiples in parallel... till you have 64... either way half of the cell clusters are flash frozen in anticipation of failure... the other half gets implanted in the 'zoom womb' with expectation of 31 of 32 will fail to thrive

in which case thaw out the reserve and repeat process to create a reserve and enough to implant... till successful

if too many successfully implant? analysis and/or randomness in reducing number by flushing out

yeah... brutal math

...if you regard a cell cluster as already being a distinct person

**392:**

one of the reasons I rarely had common ground in conversations with my hyper-religious relatives

there was times in the 1990s when I vividly recall being in certain parts of Brooklyn (and then later upstate New York) when I would mistake seeing a group of five or more children with one adult woman as a school teacher taking a cluster of students on a field trip... then a snap-into-reality of this being one mother herding her children along the sidewalk

you really do not want to hear about the economics -- bad in 1990s worse now -- of housing + feeding + dressing of five or more children 

though there's a quite active formalized chain of hand me downs in the community there's limitations of repair before cloth looks too shabby or simply shreds 

you can buy in bulk but again there's limits... one of the things I helped out with was sourcing long term safe food storage containers... so yeah a dozen families order in 50# (20 kilo) sacks of dried beans, cracked wheat, brown rice, lentils, et al... then divvy it up and carry home their share... lather + rinse + repeat every two months

ditto... a weekly drop off of 25# (12 kilo) boxes of veggies 'n fruits... apples, pears, potatoes, banananananas, etc

one neighborhood going so far as renting one small apartment as a shared warehousing space for hand me downs as old as twenty years... complete with three various typed sewing machines, boxes of patches, hundreds of shallow storage trays and ever present reek of mothballs lest insects gnaw on everything

but eventually you reach a point of expending precious limited time to substitute for insufficient money and there'd be a form of 'bankruptcy' kicking in

Homer Simpson is not the problem

it is the utter incompetent process within HR which leads to Homer Simpson being hired rather than being placed onto the "no hell no" list

while it is now funny to tell of damage inflicted onto IT by HR... a safe bet 5% of any project's personnel funding went into hiring the wrong people due to high profile university named on resume without ever asking "can you code? will you embed error trapping into every function to catch damaged data before relying upon it?"

one of those reasons we will fail in space colonization will be an inability to filter out Homer Simpson from management and force thru hiring of technically savvy non-linear engineers whose square-ish pegginess can never be precisely fitted in perfectly round niches

**397:**

I’d say the reason we’re not going to colonize space is the same reason that octopi aren’t going to start gardening on Long Island: there are too many fundamental biological issues that have to be dealt with for too small a reward.

That said, and getting back to the original question, I’d further suggest that a capitalist civilization in general, and within it, a society that is strongly biased towards isolated nuclear families, has even more self-inflicted obstacles to colonizing off Earth. If you think about it, Polynesia would never have been colonized if the colonists had had to sail back home every year to make lease payments on their boats. But that “I have to get enough out of someone else’s colony for me to let them go,” is fundamental to how we’re thinking about it. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1826)

[Robby](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1826) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199385) | [October 18, 2024 14:17](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199435)

**398:**

One comment on Kessler cascade at GEO. Whilst you are correct that the surface area of GEO is massive, the useful area is tiny - a narrow band over the equator.

Tracking small things is also more difficult up at GEO. It's difficult enough at LEO, and as you said GEO is a lot further away.

So a hypothetical small bag of marbles along the equatorial plane at GEO is a big problem. They're very difficult to see, everything you don't want hit is lined up on the same orbit, and the satellites are much bigger - around the size of a double decker bus - so the targets are bigger, and can themselves create more mess.

The saving grace is that it takes quite a long time for debris to move around the orbit, so ground controllers for the various satellites should have time to move theirs out of the way. In the case of geostationary (rather than geosynchronous) satellites, this could be as simple as doubling the daily station-keeping movement in one direction, and not cancelling it out.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229)

[Troutwaxer](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229) replied to [this comment from ilya187](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199433) | [October 18, 2024 14:17](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199436)

**400:**

_My wife scoffed and pointed out that having kids these days is fucking expensive, so by the time a career woman can afford to have kids, she’s lucky if she can have one. What do you expect to happen when education is expensive but necessary, housing and food are expensive but necessary, pregnancy is expensive, child care is expensive and necessary and society actively does not want children taking care of their parents, at least given how people who do do (raises hand) are shamed and marginalized?_

None of which is a law of nature, or even (as many people claim) an inevitable result of high-tech economy -- "on a farm children are a labor boon, in suburbs they are a financial drain". All these "expensives" are the result of the moronic invention called "nuclear family", which is a feature of Industrial Age, and completely unnatural -- but goes well with "I've got mine, screw you" philosophy of capitalism. Every pre-industrial society raised children collectively, and to some extent this happened much more recently than that:

<https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2020/03/the-nuclear-family-was-a-mistake/605536/>

_For another thing, nuclear families in this era were much more connected to other nuclear families than they are today—constituting a “modified extended family,” as the sociologist Eugene Litwak calls it, “a coalition of nuclear families in a state of mutual dependence.” Even as late as the 1950s, before television and air-conditioning had fully caught on, people continued to live on one another’s front porches and were part of one another’s lives. Friends felt free to discipline one another’s children..._

_In short, the period from 1950 to 1965 demonstrated that a stable society can be built around nuclear families—so long as women are relegated to the household, nuclear families are so intertwined that they are basically extended families by another name, and every economic and sociological condition in society is working together to support the institution._

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3979)

**402:**

I think you're over-emphasizing the profit motive a little. The problem isn't 'someone making a profit off the colony.' The problem is 'the colony running at a profit.' That is, will the colony ever create more energy than it uses? Will it ever be reasonably self-sufficient in food, air, water, metals, oils, etc? Obviously no country or area is ever perfect in this regard, but if you don't expect the 'space colony' to ever manage to feed itself or build a house or maybe 90 percent of a machine out of it's own resources (which might include paying for stuff it can't make by selling things it can make) that's a huge problem regardless of your economic theory.

The issue isn't 'make a profit' but 'become reasonably self-sufficient.'

Also note something else. Once you've acquired the land building a colony on another continent is easy because air, water, warmth, fertile soil and protection from radiation are free. _In space air, water, warmth, fertile soil and protection from radiation are all expensive._ No space colony can afford billionaires (or even multi-millionaires.) The tax rates in a space colony will be much larger than anyplace on Earth, and the standard of what constitutes bad behavior or inappropriate selfishness will be much more restrictive. If Leon Skum (see what I did there?) shows up at the Mars colony he's gonna to out the airlock as soon as he opens his mouth.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

**412:**

I’ll have to dig out my copy of _The Right Stuff_ , but I think this may be a bit backwards.

The initial problem was that the Soviets got Sputnik up first. The US initially publicized its rocket launches, most of which ended up in failure of course. This initially didn’t bother the rocketeers, but it did bother the public and then the politicians, because the Soviets were publicly succeeding where the US was publicly flailing. How many rockets did the Soviets lose? I dunno, because they didn’t publicize their failed experiments. If they experimented with, oh, FOOF and red mercury, we will never know.

It got political with the Moon Race, because many politicians thought it was a stupid publicity stunt. NASA had to make sure that Congress didn’t scrub the whole program and throw the money into, I don’t know, social welfare programs. How much would putting whitey on the moon help anybody? ( <https://youtu.be/goh2x_G0ct4> , if you don’t get the reference ).

Ditto the shuttle, and ISS. It has to pass Congress. 

Or now, Elon Musk.

This, incidentally, is nothing new. Columbus who was a Sephardic Jew at the time of the Spanish Inquisition, convinced the Spanish Crown to let him go halfway around the world to get them lucrative connections to the Spice Islands. And when that didn’t work, he started enslaving the people he did find to try to make his gamble pay off….and maybe keep him out of Spain? If you believe Graeber, Cortez invaded Mexico to pay off his gambling debts, and ended up stiffing his fellow Conquistadors to get still more money. I’d say he was worse than Trump…? 

You get the picture. If you’re borrowing money to go exploring, it really should pay off for the people paying for your ships, both in reputation and financially. NASA knows this.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

**415:**

_... Nobody slams doors in space ..._

Space stations flex: dynamic damping of something the size of ISS would be impossible, when you anchor your installation to a planet you avoid having to deal with that. The photolithography process involves a several-kilos stage with the wafer mounted to it moving rapidly under the projector, and even mounted on an isolating foundation the rest of the system vibrates and flexes in reaction. When I worked at ASML 25 years ago the inertial compensation system (big counterweights moving with micron precision controlled with interferometers) was a new development, it was the reason the thing was the size of a small shipping container. Slamming doors were not an issue at the time.

You could fly the projector free of the station while it's processing but you'd still need the (very heavy) motion-compensation rig; the spacecraft structural members would have their own vibration modes and every motion of the stage would make the chassis ring, even with damping. You'd also need heavy radiation shielding to protect the work pieces from damage and the system from getting dirty with bremsstrahlung, maybe that would be the wake shield. As I said, maybe the hard vacuum would make it worth while.

And the rigs went wrong on the regular, I was third-level tech support in Eindhoven and there were two levels of support in Taiwan. If anything would demand a crew presence in space it would be a set of these.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199451) | [October 18, 2024 16:23](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199453)

**416:**

_Just like they do back here on Earth, right?_

Yup.

Now, to unpack that, I’m particularly thinking about the Filipino family (all nurses except for the one doctor) who are taking care of my elderly mom. They run a chain of small long term care homes as a family, hire other Filipinos to do the less skilled labor, and provide elder and end of life care for white people like my mom who don’t have the family, or family culture, to care for them. I’m happy to pay them too. What they’re doing is taking a cultural norm in the Philippines (caring for the elderly in family at home), adding American medical training as necessary, and running it as a family business.

A space colony would have to work this way too, with or without the paid labor.. Who else would do the work?

This is one of the really great things you can learn by studying the Pacific cultures for models about how to colonize space: it’s not just how people live on small islands for centuries literally in each others laps, it’s how they move and replicate and adapt their culture. I’ll freely admit it’s imperfect and sexist, but they have a long history of making it work, unlike, say, the homesteading nuclear family of immigrants model. I think this gets missed a lot.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

[JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093) | [October 18, 2024 16:32](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199454)

**420:**

»You could fly the projector free of the station«

The idea seems to be to use the Gravity Probe-B trick:

* bring vehicle to desired orbital parameters

* "float" payload inside vehicle

* "fly" vehicle so that payload remains stationary relative to vehicle.

But that obviously wont buy much, if anything, if the payload consists of a lot of moving parts which recoil against each others limited mass, so I suspect the idea is open loop electron beam lithography.

Just saw this, sorry.

  * On any interstellar ship, microbial passengers will outnumber the rest by a factor well north of millions, they will not be individually ticketed or subject to security checks before boarding - and they will mutate.*



_Even taking state-of-the-art medical technologies such as "call a friend back home" and 3D-protein printers as a given, how big a crew of medical/biology/ecology scientists will it take, to monitor and steer the biosphere inside safe mission parameters ?_

_How many isolation barriers and controls are necessary, to ensure that /any/ contagion can be contained to a survivable small fraction of the ecology ?_

My apologies, this is going to be mocking, in order to make a point.

By way of analogy, I’m going to say that a starship is run by a system called the “computer science” (parallel to “the ecology”). And all the programs on the computer science are “viruses” (parallel to your use of microbes). And the viruses outnumber the humans on the ship. And the question is, how the humans are going to keep the computer science running before the viruses kill them all? Is that correct?

I don’t know if it’s ignorance or arrogance that causes anyone to use “the ecology” as a craponym for words like ecosystem or biosphere, and to do so on the blog of one of the most literate SFF authors writing. I’m mocking this usage using computers to help you understand what it sounds like. And I’m doing so in the hope that you understand that continuing to use “the ecology” once you know better will read as condescending. If it came out of ignorance, I’m quite happy to help you find better words and concepts. Just ask.

And though I’m abysmally and obviously ignorant about computers, even I know better than to call the computer systems on a starship “the computer science,” or even “the computer.” And even I know that the code includes programs, routines, modules, data files, and a bunch of other stuff. Not viruses, at least not intentionally.

On the science level, within a rounding error _all microbes ARE NOT human pathogens and cannot easily become human pathogens._. Thiis, incidentally, includes viruses, fungi, and protists. Thorough screening and simple quarantine will take care of most of the problems, as will things like vaccines. Not taking animals that tend to be reservoirs for human pathogens (bats, mice, waterfowl, or mosquitoes for example) will also help. 

It really is akin to the problem of keeping viruses out of the ship’s program stacks, programming and data libraries, and firmware.

It’s doable.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

**423:**

David L @ 413:

_Not sure if this was meant to reply to my comment or not. I was referring to the kids who came of age after being born in the 40s and 50s._

_I was one. Their parents (at least those near me) wanted to pretend that "life was good" and you could hide all the bad parts of life from their kids while growing up. And give them all kinds of trinkets (new cars?) and such without them having to learn how to earn it. Then expect them to be responsible adults the day they turned 18?_

_And those kids tried to do the same only more as they raised their kids._

_I was ostracized when I complained about giving everyone a trophy and such._

Compared to the realities of our parents childhoods during the Great Depression, our childhoods in the 1950s **WERE** pretty good. Some parents were more interested in shielding their children from the harsh realities than others were. Attitudes changed over time even with families.

Do you have younger siblings? Were they more or less shielded growing up than you were?

I certainly don't remember "everyone gets a trophy" from my own childhood. I only heard of that AFTER I became an adult (you had to be **at least 21** ; 18 came later). 

Seems like that only started after the boomers started having children of their own.

**433:**

_A military model requires the ability to ascend in rank and with that more room to exercise certain behaviors._

No it doesn't.

The _present-day US military model_ requires a promotion ladder -- but it also runs on "up or out": if you can't qualify for promotion (there are exams! Coursework, to degree level!) you must retire rather than clog up the upper echelons with dead wood.

This is a _relatively recent_ turn of affairs and I can't criticize it as a way to run a proficient military organization (compare it to the British Army in the 18th/19th century and the practice of purchasing commissions ...) _but_ there is no room for an "up or out" escallator aboard a 200 person starship on a very long duration cruise.

Also, while senior ranks are poorly paid compared to the private sector, senior _ex-officers_ are in enormous demand as managers/executives: the number of people a Brigadier, for example, is used to directing is comparable to a medium-sized multinational, and the Army has zero tolerance for incompetence (in contrast to the private sector).

But you can't retire from the staff of a starship mid-voyage to take up a post as COO of a shipping company.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1826)

**434:**

Putting the marbles in a retrograde orbit would help with hitting stuff. Getting anything into a GEO retrograde orbit would be tricky, more so than LEO. It's a long way away and takes a lot of energy to get there.

The general point of my post was that the scattered effect at GEO is minimal, any scattering means that your debris is highly unlikely to hit anything else. The one useful GEO orbit is essentially a single narrow, one-way, road.

LEO is more like a load of roads, stacked on top of each other, with angles of up to 120 degrees between them.

**440:**

_I'm asking permission to expound on this if it is OK with Charlie._

We're long past 300 comments so feel free to pop a cap in Dilbert Stark's ass.

(My personal theory is that DS is now 53: even if Starship HLS hits _all_ its target it ain't going to land on the moon before 2028, and even though he's bloviating about uncrewed Starships heading for Mars on a one-way payload delivery mission in 2026 (which is actually possible) there's no way anyone will put boots on the Red Planet before 2031, very ridiculously optimistically. By which time he'll be 60.

So if he wants to be King of Mars, he's committing to visiting the place well in his sixties, spending years on the trip even if he's just there to do Very Rich Guy Tourism, and he'll be out of direct control of his corporations for the duration. (I mean, nominally in control, yes: but with a 20 minute speed of light lag in both directions he won't be chairing any real time Zoom meetings.)

He also probably knows a lot more about the disappointing medical obstacles than he did a decade ago. Whoops.

So I think he's piping down about stuff he is gradually apprehending may not be achievable within his lifetime and focussing on stuff he _can_ have some influence on, like re-electing Donald Fucking Trump. Gack.

**444:**

_The problem with Star Trek (any TV show really) is that you need to fill a slot, and if the best script you have available for the slot is drek, that's what you've got to go with._

Worse: you end up with two continuity models -- the "hit reset button at end of every episode" model (early Trek, Space: 1999, etc), or the seasonal story arc (in extreme cases like Bab5 -- which I haven't seen -- there's allegedly a multi-season story arc to the entire series). Doctor Who falls somewhere in the middle; historically it was short episodes (25 minutes, IIRC) but grouped into 4- or 6-episode story arcs, with multiple stories per year/season/unit of production time.

The first model means you take pot luck on scripts but occasionally one story hits it out of the ball park. The second model is much richer to people who watch it regularly but is impenetrable to someone just tuning in halfway through season 3.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1312)

[Vulch](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1312) replied to [this comment from Robby](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199471) | [October 18, 2024 21:05](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199482)

**445:**

As I mentioned in 407, send your marbles round the moon. It's not hugely more expensive in delta-V terms to go from GTO to TLI. The Russians did it with a comsat that was stranded in GTO without the fuel to do both GEO circularisation and plane change from 56 degrees to equatorial. The moon did the plane change for them leaving enough fuel to get into GEO and provide a useful lifetime. Going retrograde is just a slightly extreme plane change.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3431)

**447:**

Babylon 5 did, indeed, have a story arc pre-planned for a five year run. There were also escape hatches included in case any of the principal actors became unavailable. Two of those ended up being used (main cast member had a nervous breakdown, unrelated to the show, and another left for more typical reasons).

Unfortunately the network messed around with renewal for the last season. So the plot was rapidly wrapped up, and then the show was renewed and something had to be done for the last season. Which is why some feel it is the weakest, plot wise. Still an impressive achievement.

It's worth a look if only because it had a big influence on shows that came afterwards. In much the same way that Hill Street Blues pioneered a cop show with an ensemble cast, rather than one or two leads and a handful of regular supporting characters. Also The Wire for showing an investigation happening over realistic timeframe, and a good portrayal of the internal politics of the police, the criminals and the politicians.

**449:**

_I have known a few people, women and men, who aspired to having a large brood of kidlets,_

I know one man who is a sperm donor for that reason. He's a 'known donor' and in contact with the parents/kids, some of them regularly. His whole thing is really liking kids, but not having a lifestyle that was compatible with settling down with them (military then ecowarrior). SO he has ~10 kids, then when he was about 40 one of his own. Which was weird in its own way, his early-40's partner said yeah sure let's try and was pregnant within a couple of months and had a very boring pregnancy and birth. That's _not_ normal.

It is kind of weird being at his bigger birthday parties where the (now mostly teenaged and older) kids and families are there. You just get an endless round of "this is {name}, my kid with {other names}" and you just nod and smile... The description seems to have been agreed with all the parents. Oh, and he has some semi-adopted nieces and nephews because of course he does.

**451:**

*Well, should that prove out, there's always Plan B: <https://www.jpaerospace.com/ATO/ATO.html*>

That’s my line! (/grin)

Seriously, if you want to get something to or from orbit without 3 gees of shaking, this is the only method on the drawing boards. Yeah yeah bean stalks. Figure out how they get to orbital heights without lateral vibrations in the cable, and we’ll talk. Besides, I think ATO may be faster than a beanstalk to LEO. And trying to use a giant hypersonic balloon to get lift in the mesosphere only sounds daft until you realize that inflatables have already gone Mach 10 up there and survived, and it’s a near vacuum, which is why you need an ultra huge, ultralight hypersonic wing to get any lift at all. 

Yes, I can get boring on this, but flying even in the high stratosphere is really weird.

That said, we do need to understand what satellite smoke does to things like the ionosphere, ozone layer, formation of noctilucent clouds, and so forth. AFAIK there’s not a lot of research on chemistry in the upper stratosphere and mesosphere, and that’s where the remnants of satellites end up. Maybe, if we could fly a research airship up there to collect samples and data….

**454:**

Steve Perry and Timothy Zahn used alternative rankings, to my recollection. I’ve got a few ideas stashed away for later use.

BTW, the USAF/Roman thing wasn’t particularly aimed at recreating th Roman Legions. Rather it was about using the Roman notion of a Cursus Honorum (path of honors) as the basis for colonial governance. 

There are some fundamental differences here. The big one is that in Republican Rome, there was no difference between military and civil rule. If you were a citizen, you could be called up to go fight, and being a veteran was about the only route to democratically elected office.

With a colony, replace “military service” with “keeping the colony running” and it works. In a small colony, you need everyone working on keeping the place working, regardless of gender or age. Those who can do more get more status.

Yes, there probably should be elections for offices too. However, the Roman model of having the Senate (aka Mission Control], which is composed of experienced, competent veterans, electing from within their ranks executive officers for one year stints or to deal with specified emergencies, isn’t a bad system for a colony or a starship. It also looks a lot like how NASA runs the ISS, with astronauts rotating in and out of flight and command jobs and the executive hierarchy being temporary.

Finally, Rome ran on expansion and on slaves. Expansion isn’t necessarily a problem for an exoplanet colony, at least, and instead of slaves use mechanization. 

That’s all.

Only 3% of active social media accounts are toxic, but they produce 33% of all content. Furthermore, 74% of all online conflicts are started in just 1% of communities, and 0.1% of users shared 80% of fake news.  
Not only does this extreme minority stir discontent, spread misinformation, and spark outrage online, they also bias the meta-perceptions of most users who passively “lurk” online. This can lead to false polarization and pluralistic ignorance, which are linked to a number of problems including drug and alcohol use, intergroup hostility, and support for authoritarian regimes. Furthermore, exposure to extreme content can normalize unhealthy and dangerous behavior. For example, teens exposed to extreme content related to alcohol consumption thought dangerous alcohol consumption was normative.  
The current paper explains who social media creates a funhouse mirror version of reality. <https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2352250X24001313>

**465:**

Because optical/focus aberrations in the lens, you don't scan a beam over the 35cm or whatever wafer, you focus the beam to a point and move the target, with the mask moving in counterpoint, this is necessary at nanometer level. You can't construct a lens or waveguide without some level of aberration so you compensate by limiting the beam and moving the stage. Doesn't matter what technology you use, you're dealing with heavy masses shifting rapidly and the concomitant dynamic problems in your spacecraft. And on orbit, you don't have the damping capability of multiple tonnes of concrete floating in a fluid bath as your isolating foundation. Doesn't matter that your spacecraft bus could shift freely against the motion of the stage, the second and higher order vibrations will kill your pointing accuracy - remember this is at nanometer level - and you can't damp those without the foundation mass and the planet that it's set into. I'm not optimistic that going into space would allow even as good a detail level as on Earth, you're definitely going to be going for whatever material quality the vacuum can afford.

**468:**

»Because optical/focus aberrations in the lens, you don't scan a beam over the 35cm or whatever wafer, you focus the beam to a point and move the target, with the mask moving in counterpoint«

As I understand electron beam lithography, you move the wafer to center the next chip under the beam apparatus, wait for things to settle and then draw the "mask" directly on the waver with the electron beam using electrostatic deflection.

(Isn't this how masks are produced today ?)

The problem is that this is a serial process, where each feature must be drawn sequentially, so deflection bandwidth is critically important.

As I understand it, vibrations makes it impossible to to avoid closed loop feedback at very high rate, which slows things down a LOT, and they hope to avoid that in nanogravity.

An other advantage of electron beam lithography, in space applications, is that the equipment is much more compact.

Personally I expect all the chemistry to be the major problem, I cannot possibly imagine sending wafers into space just for exposure, given the number of layers we use.

**474:**

_FFS, right now they're mopping up from "the new manned spacecraft seems to mostly work, we think". But at least it was cheap, right? Cheap matters more!_

But it _wasn't_ cheap~ Boeing's Starliner costs roughly four times as much as SpaceX's Dragon per seat - Dragon got funded as a cheap backup plan in case Starliner didn't work!

The USA also has another crew-rated capsule in the wings -- NASA's Orion -- but that'd be just ridiculous. (Flies on SLS, costs $10Bn a shot, designed for Lunar missions not the ISS, and the heat shield has problems.)

Weirdly, the current problem is that the cheap stuff works -- the products from traditional aerospace are the ones that are defective and massively over budget.

**477:**

_It seems that the ISS was trying to go in for modular suits, so that the astronauts could mix and match tops and bottoms to get something to fit._

Up to a point: the ISS inherited the old Shuttle and Mir suits. Shuttle suits were modular for the mix'n'match because Shuttle was originally sold as a "space truck" that would have many different missions and operators so needed flexibility: it was ideologically impossible to swim against that current. And the Shuttle suit was in turn derived from Apollo-era designs.

The Soviet Orlan pattern suits turn out to be a fundamentally better design: they're one-piece rear entry (there's a door where you'd expect the backpack to go) with straps for adjusting the sleeve/leg length around the cosmonaut. They're _less flexible_ but it turns out flexibility isn't that useful when you're manipulating fiddly stuff while being carried around on a cherry picker Canadarm.

And yes, the ISS has remote manipulators, the [Mobile Servicing System](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mobile_Servicing_System), aka Canadarm 2. Those Canadarms, derived from the Shuttle's arm, have multiple interchangeable heads and can be "walked" around the ISS modules, swapping head-end for tail-end as they self-relocate. It's an impressive piece of kit but it masses about 1.5 tonnes, far more than an astroaut in a suit (EVA suits mass in the range 60-100Kg, plus the canned monkey).

But again, that's descended from a 70s technology base and is horrendously expensive to experiment with

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5955)

**480:**

I already voted by mail. It’s fun here because they have “ballot trax,” which emails that the post office got it, then emails when the registrar of voters got it and counted it.

The nice bit is that the political parties also get informed that we voted, so they stop spamming us with flyers and emails, because they don’t want to waste money on us now. That’s why I suggest voting early if you already know how you’re going to vote.

**481:**

I don’t know if it’s true for spacecraft, but for decades the traditional aerospace companies deliberately maximized the number of Congressional districts that built parts of planes. The idea was that Congresscritters would hesitate to cut programs if their supporters lost jobs. I can see why they’d make less killable projects, given that before WW2 the US normally massively downsized its military after each war and wanted to keep doing that (most recently with Clinton’s Peace Dividend in the 90s). Still, it’s a hideously expensive way to build anything. 

I’m willing to bet that what we’re seeing with SpaceX is what happens when a firm doesn’t have to do this quid pro quo. Turns out it costs a lot to spread the grief around. Of course, we’ve now got a single point of political failure underpinning the entire USSF space dominance strategy*, so there’s that little problem.

If this analysis is correct, it’s not so much that SpaceX is cheap, it’s that traditional aerospace is designed to maximize political pork, with all the problems that brings. I’ll end by noting that putting military manufacturing under oligarchic control isn’t a recipe for success. Look at Russia, for example.

*Admittedly, that strategy is to shovel disposable satellites into LEO faster than they can get taken out, which isn’t much different than the mechanical soldiers of earlier eras. Or legionnaires, for that matter. And so it goes.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6931)

[Paul](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6931) | [October 19, 2024 16:42](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199519)

**482:**

I'm surprised not to have seen much mention of robotics so far.

Given the difficulties of shipping canned monkeys, it looks like the obvious solution. Send robots to the Moon. Robots build factories. Factories build more robots. Robots and factories build whatever you want.

Let me unpack that.

Start with the Moon because everything is at least two orders of magnitude easier than Mars. Right now, getting stuff to the moon by the ton is expensive. Vastly, hugely, mindbogglingly expensive. But Tony Stark's new toy is about to change that, to the point where any ordinary billionaire can do it.

Plain tele-operated waldoes won't work for robots on the Moon because of the time lag. But we are now getting to the point where a robot can act on an instruction like "Pick up the Widget and screw it into the Thing". With that capability a 2-3 second lag isn't a problem.

You don't need to build 100% of every new robot on the Moon from the start. Begin by constructing the structural bits and carry on shipping the complicated precision engineering. Over time you can build more complex factories to make wire, cables, motors etc. Then start on basic electronics and circuit boards, but still keep shipping CPUs. Over time the amount you have to ship gets less and less, but probably never quite hits zero.

Mining raw materials and building things on the moon is going to require a lot of re-invention. We know how to do it on Earth given the possibilities and constraints of a damp oxygen-rich environment. How we do it in an airless environment where water is a rare and valuable commodity is a new question. I suspect its going to involve stuff like: sodium is light, easy to work and highly reflective. The only reason we don't build mirrors from it on Earth is the damp oxygen-rich environment. Sodium levels in surface regolith are low, but not too low to make mining it infeasible. And there is likely to be more lower down. So you can build a solar concentrator from sodium. (Hat tip to Clarke for putting a big sodium mirror in "Islands in the Sky"). Or we might just stick with aluminium. My point is that conditions are very different, and we'll have to learn to _exploit_ those differences rather than just trying to work around them.

The Moon has been hit by lots of meteors, some of which were big lumps of platinum-iridium. So there are likely to be bits of lunar surface very rich in that, to the point where making things out of it in bulk becomes feasible. Solar refinaries with sodium mirrors cooking up platinum-iridium in crudely-dug pits? Who knows? We need to get up there and try it. How do we insulate wires without hydrocarbons to make plastic? Probably silicone plastics (hydrogen plus silicon). Although there is enough [water on the moon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lunar_resources) to bootstrap this, in the long term we're probably going to have to ship hydrogen in bulk.

There is almost no carbon in ordinary regolith, but just like platinum-iridium, carbonaceous asteroids are a thing. So there _might_ be areas much richer in carbon.

There doesn't seem to be a lot of uranium on the Moon as far as we know, but there is probably enough to mine, and shipping enough up from Earth to make a nuclear pile wouldn't be a big problem. Nuclear power would avoid having to shut everything down during the lunar night. The nice thing about nuclear power on the Moon is that there is no environment to worry about; if a reactor melts down, that's just a hectare of Moon that humans had better avoid for a few centuries. If it bothers you, pile some regolith on top and don't mention Space 1999.

In the long run we could probably shift quite a lot of manufacturing to the moon. The lack of carbon and hydrogen there will make plastic rare and expensive, but mining, metal-bashing and electronics are rather environmentally unfriendly back here, so we could see a magnetic catapult launching bulk manufactured goods back to Earth for assembly here.

Once you have robots and factories that can build anything, they can construct human-ready habitation should anyone feel the need to go there in person. There you can experience the athletic possibilities of sex in 1/6 gravity and look out of the windows at all the busy robots. That will be the only point in going. But if people _want_ to live on the Moon there won't be anything to stop them. It won't be any kind of "colony" as we understand it: there won't be anything to do that can't be done just as well back on Earth, so no homesteading or farming, or even work as such. Everything you need will be provided by machines that are built and maintained by robots. Plants will be grown in vertical farms. "Meat" will be grown in vats or manufactured from vegetable origins. Real meat shipped up from Earth will be an expensive luxury, although once there is enough vegetable matter farms for pigs, chicken and rabbits might become feasible. Organic matter will be valuable, so every time you go to the toilet there it will be vitally important to get a receipt.

Once we get to the point of doing this routinely on the Moon we can think about repeating it on Mars. It will need smarter robotics that can go without human supervision for several minutes, and yet another set of technologies for mining, refining and manufacturing to account for the different environmental conditions, like having enough atmosphere to be a nuisance.

Having a Mars colony means using an [Aldrin Cycler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars_cycler) to ship the canned monkeys in both directions. That way you only have to launch the giant spinning tin can with life support once. The Cycler will be based on experience with a lunar colony, so we can build something capable of supporting humans for a couple of years without running out of food, water or sanity, and get it right first time. The Moon is also the right place to build such a thing because launch costs will be much lower thanks to that catapult I mentioned. Phobos and Diemos are also handy staging posts in Mars orbit.

From there we can go out and do whatever we want in the rest of the solar system. Humans are optional: once we can do it on Mars we have effectively created a Von Neumann Machine that can do it anywhere.

The problem with all this is the business case for the bootstrap. Absent stringent rules on mining on Earth (which might be a good idea) its hard to see it being cheaper to produce an electric motor on the Moon than on the Earth, and the investment required to get to that point is slightly colossal even with that big reusable rocket.

I did wonder about all that platinum-iridium. The spot price is $37/g, so in theory a tonne of refined platinum would be worth $37M, and the world produces about 190 tonnes per year. Iridium is $170M per tonne, and the world produces about 7 tonnes per year. Right now about 50% of that platinum goes into catalytic converters for cars, and that market is going away. So if you could ship 100 tonnes of platinum back to Earth every year you are unlikely to sell it for more than $10M per tonne (wild ass guess here). Looks like the numbers are an order of magnitude too small to make it work. Of course if platinum-iridium alloys got cheap then we might do some useful things with them, like bearings that never wear out in jet engines and the like.

However this kind of thinking reminds me of the business case for the first American colony, which was to produce glassware using all that free wood. It didn't work (and all the colonists died), but America still got colonised eventually. I dunno. Maybe living in 1/6g while robots produce everything you might want would be attractive to some vegetarians.

**484:**

> _But we are now getting to the point where a robot can act on an instruction like "Pick up the Widget and screw it into the Thing"._

And we've been getting to that point for 40 years -- I remember working on that problem in the early 80's. I check in occasionally to see if it's been solved yet, and automated bin picking is still a research challenge. Most implementations depend on a great deal of custom-designed fixed automation to reduce variability.

I agree that Robots in Space makes more sense than Man in Space, but completely automated manufacturing isn't here yet. It might be like fusion power plants.

**488:**

_Which, according to at least one history I've read, is what was happening to the Apollo Program even before Apollo XI._

Remember the Apollo 1 pad disaster? (Three dead astronauts!) There's a _reason_ that after 1966 Apollo slowed right down and became more cautious.

Even so, IIRC the Apollo program accepted a roughly 20% chance of losing astronauts during the Moon program (and came very close to doing so on Apollo's 11 and 13, cf. the story of Buzz Aldrin's biro saving Aldrin and Armstrong's ass on the way back up to Lunar orbit).

The real step down in risk acceptance came with the Shuttle program, which was pitched as a "space truck" that could accept non-test pilot mission specialists and then civilian supercargo. Which meant it _had to_ be run with safety in mind. Except that as it turned out it wasn't ...

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229)

[Troutwaxer](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229) replied to [this comment from JReynolds](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199520) | [October 19, 2024 19:12](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199526)

**489:**

According to the Honorverse wiki: _"The commanding officer of the ship that had taken the prize received 6 percent of the total awarded prize money, with the other officers receiving 24 percent and the noncommissioned officers and enlisted personnel splitting the remaining 70 percent among them."_

The bulk of the money is allocated to the enlisted personal.

In the real world the distribution was much worse: _"The rules and regulations for prize money were enacted by Parliament at the beginning of each conflict, and rarely updated through the course of the 18th century. In 1793, the commanding admiral, captain, wardroom and gunroom officers received 3/4 the value of the prize money, and the common sailors had to settle for the remaining 1/4. An update in 1808 modified the distribution between captains and admirals, reduced the classes for distribution, and gave 1/2 the value of the prize to the junior officers and seamen."_

<https://www.hmspsyche.ca/library/seamanship>

I wouldn't say that Weber was being particularly liberal here, but he's definitely paying his enlisted people at least 140 percent of what the Brits did. (I suspect he didn't actually do the math with regards to how this would work for the crew of _Fearless_ in the capture of _Mondragon_ in which not a shot was fired. Notice, for example his poor conversions between English and Metric systems of measurement in two different series. Honor Harrington is approximately 6'8", while one of the admirals in his _Stars at War_ series is less than four feet tall. Anyway, properly-done math might have resulted in his redoing the number!)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6931)

Retiring @ 484: _I check in occasionally to see if it's been solved yet, and automated bin picking is still a research challenge. Most implementations depend on a great deal of custom-designed fixed automation to reduce variability._

This company seems to have a practical solution for real world production, and it sounds like it's not the only one. Plus, in this scenario a human can be readily on hand to micromanage any problems, albeit with that pesky 2.5s delay. And we are dealing with a predictable environment.

**496:**

Apropos of the origin topic, I feel like the following has been done, but I can’t think where.

It’s a dark comedy version of the space colonization story. The aliens invade Earth. We can’t stop them (maybe because of dystopian crash? Whatever). But it turns out that We Make Great Pets. They adore us! We love them! They export us through their empire. Then they find that pet humans are just as invasive off Earth as on. And, as with feral cats, every place a feral human colony springs up, the local chapter of the SPCH goes into action to keep them from being exterminated, even though it is so hard to get long term chemical contraceptives to work on us.

Has anyone played with this idea?

**497:**

Good for them! Of course, that's a marketing page.

Now that I think about it, we claimed, forty years ago, to have solved the problem, as well. What we'd done was to constrain the original problem enough to allow us to make it work well enough to do a demo. Bit like Tesla's Full Self Driving.

But for some reason there are still research papers on bin picking showing up in journals like IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering. I saw a couple just this summer, I think.

**498:**

_noting that putting military manufacturing under oligarchic control isn’t a recipe for success_

I vaguely recall that Stark has already played that one on the Ukrainian military with his 'gift' of Starlink that works most of the time in most places. But then some countries have done similar things with their weapons, the USA for example being adamant that under no circumstances can Ukraine be allowed to use their weapons on Russian territory (interestingly in that regard Trump may have exactly the same policy and 'just' adjust official US understanding of Russian territory, much as successive US presidents have done with Palestinian territory (and also with Lebanese and Syrian territory)). Not that Australia is much better, having the official position that if Palestine does become a country it will have no territory at all (perhaps we could donate some desert to them, since we have completely legitimately acquired so much of that).

It would be funny from outside if one of Stark's very good friends got into some kind of conflict with the US while it was ruled by a Stark subordinate. One suspects various shenanigans would ensue, not least of which being a discussion with said subordinate about exactly which side ol' Dilbert would be supporting and the consequences if the US government interfered (we don't use the term "strike" when it's capital rather than labour, but OTOH with Stark a strike in the other sense is also possible - he could likely use starlink satellites as jammers of something other than astronomy if he wanted to).

But I fear it would go the same was as post-9/11 negotiations with Saudi Arabia. The US accepting whatever terms were offered and much propaganda being issued explaining what glorious leader did and how its for the best really. Likely also the problematic destruction of some random bystander in reaction, too. Horribly, I hope that would be the restoration/imposition of a dictator on some formerly democratic country rather than the imposition of a competing bunch of warlords with a 'free market' of arms merchants competing for business (see also Iraq, Afghanistan, Sahel).

On that note, the African Union 'peacekeepers' have been misbehaving (again). Maybe the Fijians aren't so bad after all.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1357)

**500:**

A few comments on colonies:

Homesteading was a Social Engineering choice, with deliberate laws around land claims to enforce it. A more libertarian choice was made in Argentina, where a rich chap in the capital would send out flunkies to claim land instead of going himself. The result was Argentina: big areas owned by big land-owners, a much bigger divide between rich and poor, much fewer "family farms". 

Penal colonies are a very common model. Usually not pure penal colonies, usually as a mixture. I believe about a quarter of British settlers who went to the USA in the 1700s were convicts sent there. On the other side of the coin, less than 50% of Australian settlers from Britain were convicts. But access to penal labour let the govts of the time to accomplish projects they couldn't otherwise afford in colony setup, as govt tax base was low and having colonies suck money was not popular.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1322)

**501:**

And yet when that couple you knew pulled out their kids to homeschool, I think we can all guess which partner stayed home all day to run it. After all, the husband is likely to be making more money, and it would be more cost efficient to keep the lesser earning partner home. 

_Nobody_ is skilled in all disciplines. That's why we have teachers who specialize. 

It's a really good way to force women to stay home and stay dependent on the (usually male) breadwinner, though. 

And forcing it can lead to extreme outcomes, like Andrea Yates.

**502:**

I recall reading an Eric Frank Russell story along those lines when I was a teenager. An alien sophont notices infestations of humans appearing here and there around the Galactic Empire in ever-increasing numbers and tries to raise the alarm to little success. What I didn't notice particularly in those innocent days was that the "humans" in the story were all Chinese or Asian (the humans were running laundries and carrying out other scutwork businesses for the aliens) and this was an allegory of the Yellow Peril. In more modern times this sort of racism is the threat to our way of life from illegal immigrants polluting our society's precious bodily fluids.

There was another story I read back then where humans are surviving as "rats in the walls" of the buildings constructed by giant alien invaders who have conquered Earth. Can't recall the title though.

Humans as pets -- The Tripod trilogy by Wyndham had that as a concept, ditto for Thomas M. Disch's "White Fang Goes Dingo" first published as "The Puppies of Terra".

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8141)

**508:**

I find the Honor Harrington stories amusing for the most part. A few things stand out though.

The, almost fetishistic, regard for the Manticorian monarchy. Despite queen Elisabeth eventually proving why monarchs with political influence are a Bad Idea (the killing of a possible peace treaty because of her personal hatred of the opponents). Also a lot of the Manticorian nobility come over as a waste of life support.

The world is carefully constructed to "prove" that social assistance programs are a bad idea. That broke down a little with Eric Flint starting to write in the setting. But, initially, it was way to smug.

If the Manticorians try a new technology it works first time without a problem. If the Havenites try something it has the inevitable teething problems. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229)

[Troutwaxer](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229) replied to [this comment from PilotMoonDog](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199545) | [October 20, 2024 14:52](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199546)

**509:**

Yeah. Weber's an interesting case. I think he has a deep, romanticized love for the age of sail (and all that goes with it.) He's a conservative, but also a fundamentally decent person, even as he gets most of the social science wrong, and of course his description/characterization of Emily Hamish is... speaking as someone who's lived with a badly handicapped person for 34 years... moderately offensive. On the other hand, he does quite well in taking a feminist/non-racist view, though he's incapable of writing romance in any form.

It's a combination which produces very interesting books when you dive into the deeper implications of what he's writing.

My big dislike of the man (as an author) is that he has no idea of how to conclude an epic and I think there are also some major considerations of what it will mean for his income to stop writing Honor Harrington books... 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6529)

**512:**

Thanks for the references!

The notion I’m think of here is based on the old saw among biologists, that humans are the most invasive species on Earth. 

So the upshot suggestion is to take this literally, and posit humans as both a pet and pest, that colonizes the galaxy because we’re soooo cute and fun to have around. I mean, when we start LARPing? How adorable is that? Who cares about the furniture? 

Given politics these days, I want to be very clear that this isn’t an anti-immigrant idea. Rather, it’s more a comment on how humans treat pests and pets.

In some ways, Williams’ Maijstral series might come closer, come to think of it.

**517:**

AND IN OTHER NEWS DOOMSCROLLING

given the shitstorm escalating in the Middle East has been drawing off attention from Putin -- not once has he been cited in headlines this week! -- my unhappy gut is he'll be saber rattling any minute now

though not overtly losing, Russia certainly is not winning in Ukraine...

Everyone knew there were those in the ruling elite in Moscow who were happy, nay eager, to go to extremes. 

They will backstop Putin's usage of smaller nukes in a tactical context somewhere in Ukraine. They will claim it was a test that got outta hand, or an accident. 

So unless we (US, NATO, EU, UK, or some subset) punish the Russians for daring to use a strategic weapon, "_the threshold for the unthinkable_ " will be lower and lower, until it is no longer unthinkable. 

Just another tool in warfare.

The generations-old policy of mutual assured destruction will be replaced by a calculation that horrific damage downwind of a battlefield from radioactive ash just a cost of doing business. 

A shrug, then another bucket of instant sunshine scouring clean pesky uppity Ukrainian peasants from a city they refused to surrender to the inevitable forces of a gloriously restored empire.

There in the Middle East is a war nobody wants and (almost) everyone wants to end. Ditto, Ukraine.

At some point, will the ruling elite of Israel decide to follow in the footsteps of Putin?

Easiest way of rebuilding Gaza at this point would be instant sunshine for fast, fast urban renewal.

**519:**

Re:Honorverse interstellar technology, 

As with my comments about STL technology, the energies involved, and siting that technology on a planetary surface instead of in space…

Well, what to say? Honorverse technology, like so many others, has a “Ships of Steel, Planets of Kleenex,” problem. With apologies to Larry Niven, of course. 

To be fair, most such technologies have this problem. Thing is, if, for some completely bizarre reason you care about realism while write SFF set on exoplanets, then it might be worth contemplating drive technologies that won’t sterilize a continent or homogenize an entire planet if they’re turned on too close to a planet.

**526:**

_I don’t know if it’s true for spacecraft, but for decades the traditional aerospace companies deliberately maximized the number of Congressional districts that built parts of planes. The idea was that Congresscritters would hesitate to cut programs if their supporters lost jobs._

They've gone one better with SLS: to prevent a future congress from cancelling it, first they legislated how it would be built using existing recycled Shuttle bits'n'bobs, and then they distributed the work across enough states/districts that a majority of the senate will lose contracts in their seats if it's cancelled. Otherwise, it would _for sure_ have been cancelled already.

(Even in its current prototype state, you could fly an Apollo-equivalent moon program using SpaceX kit, and it'd be cheaper in real terms -- Apollo was roughly $4Bn per flight in 2024 money. Whereas SLS is set to cost $10Bn or more per flight. That's a _lot_ of headroom for the lander and crew side of things, given that a Starship prototype flown in one-shot mode costs about $100M. Starship is a shoestring budget effort compared to SLS: each of the new build RS-25 motors they're building for it, which will be flown away after flight, costs about $120M, and it needs four of them for a total of about $500M per flight. Whereas a Starship stack's complement of 39 motors on both stages cost about $1M each in 2019, but with volume production they're due to be $0.25M each.)

The fact that SpaceX can build reusable cryogenic rocket motors for 0.2% the cost of Rocketdyne's equivalent is striking.

[DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5685) | [October 20, 2024 20:27](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199568)

**531:**

In the interest of adding some idea for potential fictional settings, I offer the following off the cuff speculation: 

What if the "colony" (reason for quotes will be apparent) is not intended or designed to be in any way independent of Earth, but is instead an extension of our biome, like a pseudopod extending tentacles across interplanetary space? 

Let's say we were trying to supply some place really out there, like a base in orbit around Jupiter. I don't know how long it would take to get there, depends on the drive technology you want to propose, but let's say it's five years one way. A single supply ship isn't going to do it, you need a fleet. That builds redundancy into the supply chain, and more flexibility in terms of what they are sending. So say they build enough ships that one can arrive each month. That's 120 ships making the round trip all at the same time. So these ships are probably about 85% automated, but ten years is a long time, so they send some human crew along. How many is speculative, but let's say 20 apiece. So that's 2400 people in space at any one time, plus whoever is living at the destination end, so lets say 2500 even. 

As for why they are going, I can imagine only one reason that really stands up to skepticism: discovery of an alien ecosystem. Bugs are living on Callisto, or your favorite Jovian moon. That would be an absolute game changer. Unless you think that an alien evolutionary system would exactly duplicate Earth life, the potential for biological discoveries is literally incalculable. No nation with any pretense at Great Power status could afford not to send out their best and brightest. So it looks like the college town scenario, except divided into national sectors. 

That means not just one line of supply ships, but as many as there are nations who could afford it: the US, the EU, China, Russia (?), India (?)... So now we are pushing or exceeding 10,000 people living long term in space. 

The interesting bit is who ends up crewing these vessels, and how they develop over time. The ships are largely automated, so they don't need to be astronaut level, but they can't be uneducated dummies either. Two year collegiate level at least, and reasonably physically fit. 

If this is primarily a commercial venture (as it probably will be, even if the Jovian colony isn't) then I imagine they recruit volunteers. These people end up spending decades in space, sigh up enough times, and you spend your life there. Eventually they start modifying their ships to make them correspond more closely to their own needs, Resources are difficult and expensive to come by, but not impossible. The ships of any one national line are spaced at regular intervals, so I imagine it would be possible to travel between them, perhaps using modified emergency shuttles. Then there are the ships of the other nation's lines, and you have a nice little community of people, communicating by radio and even exchanging personnel and supplies and supplies at regular intervals. 

What are they supplying? Probably a combo of high tech items and biological products. They're flying farms. 

There will be a high attrition rate, at least at first, until they figure out what works and what doesn't. But 500 or so field experiments make trial and error viable. The survivors will share knowledge and experiences, so they will get better at this as time goes by. Eventually they feel secure enough to try to begin their own families. 

What happens to their national identity? After enough time, will they not begin to identify with each other more than the government that originally sent them? (legally speaking they are probably corporate contractors). 

Ten years is enough time to work out survival techniques, new technologies, social institutions that make sense in their context, a communal identity and a political agenda of their own. How will the governments/corporate sponsors back home react to this? Plenty of room for conflict and drama. 

The formal colony isn't where the action is. It's all about the supply chain. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199568) | [October 20, 2024 20:46](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199569)

**532:**

The short answer is that this is what anyone would expect for something like a moon base.

The challenge for a place like Jupiter is that Jupiter's electromagnetic radiation belt (equivalent to our Van Allen belts) is lethal to humans, and it covers all the big four Galilean satellites, including Europa.

So, absent magic radiation shielding, humans are unlikely to visit a place like Europa, even if we know there's life under the ice.

...

Now, to turn this around a bit, we have the Exomoon problem, illustrated by Pandora, the setting for Avatar. Possibly, if you had an Earth-sized exomoon around a gas giant, it would be bathed in a similar EM hellscape, but the exomoon would have it's own magnetic field that _might_ protect the exomoon enough for surface life to evolve and live there. Although admittedly, the auroras would be spectacular, and there are likely other electromagnetic phenomena I'm not thinking about. And possibly you could run the colony off electricity generated from the moon moving through the planet's electrical field, since living there might be like living with an eternal Carrington Event. So just lay out the wires if you want power.

Anyway, getting back to the Polynesian settlement model, the Polynesians and Micronesians basically figured out similar sets of formulas for how to find and live on volcanic islands, and that enabled them to go from island to island.

If interstellar travel and colonization is possible, it's equally possible that some groups will develop formulas for the kinds of planets they hunt: alt-Earths are certainly one. Another might be red dwarf stars, while a third could be exomoons. Whether settling Europa is a prelude to settling Pandora I don't know, and that isn't what you're asking about, but it is something to think on, maybe.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1312)

[Vulch](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1312) replied to [this comment from DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199568) | [October 20, 2024 20:55](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199570)

**533:**

You're going to get clumps of ships rather than a nice regular supply line. Direct launch windows to Jupiter happen roughly every 13 months, the further from the launch window you get the more energy it takes. You can increase your launch opportunities by going via Venus or Mars, but the trip will take longer so arrivals will still be lumpy.

[Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796) | [October 20, 2024 21:45](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199572)

**535:**

I think there would have to be some very significant preconditions to any long-term space 'expansion' by humans. Exploration - laudable, ongoing and essential now. Occupation is an entire different circumstance.

Precondition 1: We will have to get past our 'rabbits in Australia/ Rats in a grain silo' mode of existence, in some form that reflects human control of the process. It will happen, but if it happens in a controlled fashion or in a crash remains to be seen. I choose to be optimistic, but that is less than easy. In my opinion it will require a wholesale rejection of most of our current economic assumptions.

Precondition 2: We succeed in (1) without losing our interest in expansion or exploration. There is no guarantee that removing the ability to externalize costs or maximize profit won't also remove the incentive or interest in galactic expansion.

Precondition 3: Assuming the above, we are able to expand without undermining either of the above. Though, humans being humans, with some advances in technology we may end up with groups of humans 'going rogue' and trying new or old economic practices outside the purview of whatever precondition 1 involves.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

**538:**

Let me know what I got wrong about the radiation environment around Jupiter.

Anyway, IIRC, ice is a fairly decent radiation shield, and Europa has 50-150 km of ice atop whatever the ocean is. On this basis, I wouldn't expect Europan sealife to be particularly radiation tolerant. It might explode on contact with a terrestrial atmosphere, due to the pressure differential, but that's a different matter.

Basically, if you assume the ice is a decent radiation shield, settling Europa requires being able to burrow far enough into the ice to be safe from the outside, then setting up a colony in side tunnels. Easy is not the word I'd use for the operation, but that's obvious answer. Now, if you want SFF bait, an obvious story involves the researchers trapped in ice station Europa when they get to the Europan Ocean 100 km below their feet and whatever comes up the drill shaft to investigate them. Plus, once they're infected, going home is no longer an option. Whether this works out as _Heart of the Comet_ or _The Thing_ is up to whoever's writing it. A working title of "chillaxing" does come to mind, as does bringing along enough flame throwers for everyone.
