**540:**

Troutwaxer @ 479:

> _"When the "Treecats" reach the centre of P. R. Haven, then the shit will really hit the fan, as their mind-skills unmask the fascists. And it's going to be very complicated."_

_I think he's already distributed treecats to some of the Havenites (I stopped paying much attention to the series some time ago, to the point where the most recent book is propping up one corner of a piece of furniture) but when the treecats hit Mesa, where they build the bio-slaves, they'll certainly unearth all the bad people._

_But this is a solvable problem in plot terms if the Mesans also have telepaths available, and it's not a hard conclusion for any author to draw. I think the real problem for Weber is that he's gotten sick and tired of writing in the Honorverse, somewhat akin to the problems Charlie has described with a series that's lasted twenty years!_

Weber is already dealing with Harrington becoming so over-powered by shifting the burden of the stories onto the shoulders of "lesser" characters - Michelle Henke, Victor Cachet/Captain Zilwiki & Abigail Hearns from Grayson ... the same way OGH shifted it onto the shoulders of Alex Schwartz, Cassie & Mhari in the later Laundry Files Novels.

And Weber has introduces several possible branch series that seem to serve the same purpose as the New Management stories.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

[JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093) replied to [this comment from JReynolds](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199520) | [October 21, 2024 00:32](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199578)

**541:**

JReynolds @ 483:

_I liked Honor Harrington back in the day. Tried to do a recent re-read, and bounced off the 18th century prize money equivalent. Officers get a huge amount, other ranks get meagre amounts._

_I can't imagine that this is good for morale, yet it's presented as a perfectly normal thing. After that, I set it down and let it go._

The way I read it the prize money distributed to the crew was a good bit more than meager; substantially so that some enlisted personnel could accumulate sizeable nest eggs (by investing prize money some kinds of markets) AND prize money was non-taxable. 

Plus, I read it that better captains would donate a substantial portion of their own shares to the crews - such that a good captain could get better crew if they had a reputation for generosity.

**544:**

To get most satellites to somewhere you can process them (let alone process them without risking a Kessler cascade), you need to spend delta-V (then they or the refinery need to spend more delta-V to intercept each other). Adding the fuel to do that will raise the cost of putting them in orbit.

Professor Sam Lawler is talking about the need for taxes or recycling to deal with light pollution, Kessler pollution, atmospheric pollution, and ground collisions from the cheap disposable satellites but getting those laws and treaties passed will be tough <https://www.uregina.ca/science/physics/people/faculty-research/samantha-lawler/index.html>

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199517) | [October 21, 2024 02:01](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199585)

**548:**

_I’m willing to bet that what we’re seeing with SpaceX is what happens when a firm doesn’t have to do this quid pro quo. Turns out it costs a lot to spread the grief around. Of course, we’ve now got a single point of political failure underpinning the entire USSF space dominance strategy*, so there’s that little problem._

A part of it. The other is the concept in medium to big construction of "multi-prime". The stated benefit it that each bit (foundations, structure, electrical, plumbing, HVAC, etc...) is big out to the lowest bidder. And the political reason is it allows local firms to bid on bits of projects instead of "big corp construction" getting the entire bid and not "spreading the wealth" locally. The single biggest issue with multi-prime construction is that NO ONE is responsible for the entire project. You tend to have a manager but if the electrical guys run their conduit through the only spot an HVAC duct you then get to pay them to do it again. So everyone gets paid for "mistakes" and no one (usually) is to blame.

Boeing didn't build the thrusters on their crew spaceship. They farmed it out. Along with a lot of other things. And Boeing and the subcontractor were both on fixed price deals. So when some issues comes up everyone wants to throw it over the wall so it comes out of the other guys profits. Which leads as much to spending money on paperwork and blame meetings as it does to fixing issues as they come up.

And with something like a crew capsule design, there will always be holes in the spec unless you spend years writing them. Keep it all under one roof and the cost to deal with surprises or "oops" goes way down.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Retiring](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199521) | [October 21, 2024 02:08](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199586)

**549:**

_And we've been getting to that point for 40 years -- I remember working on that problem in the early 80's. I check in occasionally to see if it's been solved yet, and automated bin picking is still a research challenge. Most implementations depend on a great deal of custom-designed fixed automation to reduce variability._

Boston Dynamics is working hard on this. And to be honest agree with you that it is HARD. But like much of things done today that seemed impossible 20+ years ago the vast increase in computer power per size and power unit seems to be helping out a lot.

Land a booster rocket on its tail anyone?

They key point with robotics and automation is that once you do get it working, training the second item is way simpler than running another shaved ape through a school.

To the OP's point a putting screw in a hole, THIS CAN BE HARD. Those of us who work such things more than face plates on wall switches understand there is a learning curve to not striping things out and getting it right. As the auto plants and similar found out when they first started automating things.

[Moz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298) replied to [this comment from DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199584) | [October 21, 2024 03:44](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199590)

**553:**

_There must surely be some way to compensate for this. More power?_

Two ways. More ships, you just send some more slowly so they arrive at a less efficient time. Loosely, in space you can always hang round doing nothing.

The way that's easier on the crew ("what do you mean it's _just_ an extra three years in transit??!?!?") is to boost harder for longer at each end. The problem is that you're taking a longer path in the same time, so you need to go faster along the less efficient path. And then the rocket equation bits you on the bum so now you're down to less than a ppm of payload from GEO. What it takes to get that to GEO is left as an exercise for the student.

The energy costs like make ablative laser-powered rockets look attractive (you shine a laser at the back of the rocket, vaporising some of it to create thrust). That way the power generation end of the system isn't on the rocket and you save weight.

In ye olde navy terms it's a bit like going round the bottom of Africa to avoid the Suez Canal. You _can_ , but it's a longer route so you need faster ships as well as more fuel, so you might want to refuel on the way and now your logistics start to get ugly even on top of the problem of making great big navy ships that cruise at 30 knots instead of 20 knots (they have fuckoff big turbine "auxiliary engines" to get to 30 knot battle speed, but you're looking at 5x-10x as much power delivered)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

[Moz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199586) | [October 21, 2024 03:48](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199591)

**554:**

The bin sorting problem is really hard for people to do. We've discussed it here before in the context of it being cheaper to buy new, sorted boxes of bolts etc than to sort a bin full of jumped 'assorted' ones. As anyone who fell for the discounted box of really cheap nuts'n'bolts from the fastener shop will tell you. Or successfully walked away from a murder charge when someone maliciously jumbled the contents of your shed into a skip ("the jury will inevitably find it more than justified and offer to buy the defendant a beer").

People make Lego sorting machines out of Lego, though, so many of the basic problems are relatively easy to solve. As with the "pick a 45mm long bolt out of a bin of random-length but otherwise sorted bolts" task that is also relatively easy (at the 5mm quantisation level normally found).

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Moz](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199590) | [October 21, 2024 04:18](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199592)

**555:**

Warp drives will solve a lot of the transit time problems, because they exchange the rocket equation for some other form of mathematical tyranny yet to be discovered.

Absent a warp, one of them hypothetical dusty plasma sails ( <http://www.rbsp.info/rbs/PAPER/STAIF02/index.html> See also <https://toughsf.blogspot.com/2023/05/riding-sunbeams-with-solar-sails.html> ) might be amusing enough for a SFF story. The small challenges of getting such a contraption aimed properly AND how it flies within the magnetosphere of Jupiter are left as exercises for the student. But it will propitiate the ghost of Fred Hoyle if Black Clouds fly within our solar system again. 

Or you could posit a Zubrin-esque Magsail ( <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetic_sail> ) Or maybe mix the two? Dusty black cloud photomagnetic sails? Maybe with a deployable silver lining for tacking against sunlight?

Anyway, fun times. Or something.

**556:**

_The other is the concept in medium to big construction of "multi-prime". The stated benefit it that each bit (foundations, structure, electrical, plumbing, HVAC, etc...) is big out to the lowest bidder. And the political reason is it allows local firms to bid on bits of projects instead of "big corp construction" getting the entire bid and not "spreading the wealth" locally. The single biggest issue with multi-prime construction is that NO ONE is responsible for the entire project._

Thanks for that take on Boeing!

It crossed wires in my brain with part of the Wikipedia description of the NRO ( <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Reconnaissance_Office> ): "The Director of the NRO reports to both the Director of National Intelligence and the Secretary of Defense. The NRO's federal workforce is a hybrid organization consisting of some 3,000 personnel including NRO cadre, Air Force, Army, CIA, NGA, NSA, Navy and US Space Force[9] personnel.[10] A 1996 bipartisan commission report described the NRO as having by far the largest budget of any intelligence agency, and 'virtually no federal workforce', accomplishing most of its work through 'tens of thousands' of defense contractor personnel."

This is the "Black NASA" that purportedly is bigger than the daylit NASA we know. I don't know if NRO runs more launches than NASA does, but it sure looks like "multi-prime" heaven in your terminology. 

Makes me wonder how much of that purported big budget it has is caused by being multi-prime and paying the costs for that decentralization?

**559:**

The Nazis had labour camps in Emsland, Saxony from 1933 for Communist anf Social Democrat political prisoners. See "die Moorsoldaten" a song written by the prisoners who were forbidden from singing existing protest songs. A subset of them formed the core of the German battalion of the International Brigades during the Spanish civil war (taking the song and the title with them), and a subset of those fought for France after the Falagist victory either in the FFL or in resistance units, thereby fighting the whole of the long WWII on the anti-fascist side.

**563:**

I saw that Guardian thing today too. I guess I can laugh at that because it truly does seem ridiculous, and also it is the silly end of the spectrum. There's likely to be much worse to come in terms of actually forcing people to have kids and likely it's just another way to persecute enemies.

The people trying to restart the forced birth stuff here are scary. Not least because it is USA political influence and money driving it, and there's no practical way to stop those things.

Hmm, maybe I need to focus moe on the running away from problems like that. Into SPAAAAAACE!

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199568) | [October 21, 2024 11:23](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199601)

**564:**

_If this is primarily a commercial venture (as it probably will be, even if the Jovian colony isn't) then I imagine_

Stop right there!

I for one _can't_ imagine such a venture being run on a commercial basis: the resupply time is too long, unless there's some sort of breakthrough in human longevity first.

Institutional missions drift over time as people who were there at the inception age and retire/move on, and new people join. Eventually the Iron Law of Bureaucracy cuts in -- one of the vanishingiy few things I agree with the late Jerry Pournelle about: within a career generation, the vast majority of people employed by any organization are primarily oriented on ensuring the continuity of their pay cheque, not the purpose for which the organization was founded. (There are a very few exceptions: religious orders, for example, and institutions that attract idealists -- for example the NHS relies on cheap labour provided by committed carers who drive down the costs of nurses/doctors/other professionals compared to private sector medicine.)

Anyway, I'm not talking about the base on Callisto or wherever, but about the organization pumping stuff into their resupply chain back on Earth. Folks working _there_ will, if it's commercially run, be looking to turn a profit. Or they'll be looking to cut overheads. So they will either cut corners or jack prices. And over sufficient time you will inevitably see someone like Martin Shkrelli rise to board level.

At which point the cost of the fifty year obsolete SCADA controllers the colony relies on to keep life support running is going to rise by 8000% overnight. _And because nobody is going to die within the next 90 day accounting cycle, everything looks good._

Until 19 accounting cycles later a supply ship arrives with 5% of the number of replacement parts the colony ordered and by the time the deficiency can be fixed everyone is dead.

_You can't fix this failure mode under actually-existing capitalism_. And nobody sane, planning on heading to Callisto to follow up their PhD on alien life, would trust their continued existence to a supply chain run under such constraints.

NASA, for all the inefficiency and pork barrel politics, is at least backed by the continuity of the USA as a system of government. But a current day corporation is a terrible fit for maintaining any enterprise across a time scale longer than three months.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199572) | [October 21, 2024 11:28](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199602)

**565:**

_I think there would have to be some very significant preconditions to any long-term space 'expansion' by humans. Exploration - laudable, ongoing and essential now. Occupation is an entire different circumstance._

There's another option, which is: stop thinking about human space colonization and start thinking about _terrestrial_ space colonization.

In other words, life on Earth eventually comes up with a suite of adaptations that are transplantable to other bodies, but _humans are not the dominant/controlling species_. Who knows, maybe eusocial insects (really smart bees) working under direction from uplifted octopi (who stay in the water tank, managing the recycling and algae farms and sheltering from radiation while the sterile and short lived worker bees do the open air work)?

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from Moz](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199600) | [October 21, 2024 11:46](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199603)

**566:**

_There's likely to be much worse to come in terms of actually forcing people to have kids and likely it's just another way to persecute enemies._

As I keep telling people: read up on the history of Romania's [Decree 770](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decree_770). Now try to work out how, as a functionary in the Republic of Gilead, you could integrate modern smartphones/positioning technology and digital pregnancy test kits (which are A Thing, and adding a bluetooth-equipped microcontroller to the kit is barely any extra cost) to create a pregnancy panopticon for any persons with a menstrual cycle.

(In Romania all women of childbearing age had to be checked for pregnancy monthly by a nurse or doctor: immunoassay pregnancy test kits weren't available then and there. The Project 2025 people could do the same thing with nightmarish efficiency ... then you'd have the Romanian orphanage problem, at a minimum. Which is just one more reason why it is **essential** that Trump and his backers lose the election in two weeks' time ...)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6643)

**569:**

I'm _not_ optimistic about NASA! I'm just noting that it has the sort of continuity of mission you need for anything beyond lunar orbit -- not that it's efficient, cheap, or even effective.

As for democracy, the _only_ definite advantage it offers over any other political system is that it provides for a peaceful transfer of power when the current government has outlived its legitimacy. (Which is what made the events of January 6th, 2020 so shockingly dangerous.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199606) | [October 21, 2024 14:26](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199607)

**570:**

_I'm not optimistic about NASA!_

NASA is every Congress Criter's fav place to cut funds. "Explore the moons of Jupiter when we need ROADS!" is an easy way to cut funds.

Even during Apollo a majority of Congress was neutral or against it. But public opinion was there so there they voted. Many were happy when "I don't really care about it" Nixon showed up. And they got to pretend the tech for a space truck was ready for prime time and budgets could be slashed.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199606) | [October 21, 2024 14:50](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199608)

**571:**

_Which is what made the events of January 6th, 2020 so shockingly dangerous._

I wonder how many people outside the US know the story of Huey Long. Aside from the movies loosely telling his story. With some major details left out.

He was Trump in early 1930s. Getting ready for a run for president. Promising a chicken in every pot and similar while specializing is corruption and graft for friends and family. Was the governor of Louisiana and for a while had the state police riding around the city of New Orleans as armed thugs to keep his political enemies in check and basically make it a police run city.

He got shot and killed before he could run for President in 1936.

Many, including FDR, thought he had a good chance of winning via FUD.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199601) | [October 21, 2024 15:27](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199609)

**572:**

_Eventually the Iron Law of Bureaucracy cuts in -- one of the vanishingiy few things I agree with the late Jerry Pournelle about: within a career generation, the vast majority of people employed by any organization are primarily oriented on ensuring the continuity of their pay cheque, not the purpose for which the organization was founded._

I keep wondering about this and SpaceX. So far they are still in the sugar high stage. But they have become a vast sprawling organization. Full of HR, payroll, building maintenance, contract negotiators, writers, vetting folks, (buying and selling) and so on. At some point inertia starts to take hold as the 20 and 30 somethings become the 50 and 60 somethings who are more concerned with their teen aged kids than getting the next launch right.

[DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5685) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199601) | [October 21, 2024 16:05](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199611)

**574:**

"NASA, for all the inefficiency and pork barrel politics, is at least backed by the continuity of the USA as a system of government. But a current day corporation is a terrible fit for maintaining any enterprise across a time scale longer than three months.'

Well, I think there are more options than just the binary choice. The East India Company lasted longer than that. 

There's government subcontracting, where the cargoes are government supplied but the ships and their crews are private. Competition helps ensure quality (as we are currently observing in the US) so arrange for more than one trucking company. Or the ships are government owned but the cargo is sourced from a private supplier. 

The best way to plan for long time scales is redundancy. There is more than one way to do this, and most of them will be tried. If there is an incentive in place, anyone who can get there will get there. If the US is sufficiently stupid (entirely possible), it might lose a baseload of personnel or two, but if the incentive is strong enough, they'll send more. Over time, everyone will learn how to do it well. 

After all, that's how North America was settled. 

The only thing that could stop this would be accidentally exterminating the alien ecosystem, which is also entirely possible (indeed, the first ones there might want to do exactly that, in which case put quotes around "accident"). 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199611) | [October 21, 2024 16:15](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199612)

**575:**

_Well, I think there are more options than just the binary choice. The East India Company lasted longer than that._

Just noting that the British East India Company wasn't a "company" in the modern sense. It held limited liability in the UK but it functioned as an empire-building colonial government overseas, raping and looting the various nations of the sub-continent until it turned them into an empire. It had an army, it had a fleet, it levied taxes, and it committed genocide: in the end its rule proved so tyrannical that it provoked the Indian Mutiny -- a rebellion along the lines of the US War of Independence that failed to go the distance -- and ended up being taken over directly by the British government.

Frankly it's best considered as an exemplar of criminality, when a gang of thieves grows to their logical maximum extent and becomes a government.

_Not_ who I'd want running my space colony ...!

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8783)

**580:**

News from this weekend:

<https://spacenews.com/intelsat-33e-loses-power-in-geostationary-orbit/>

The Intelsat 33e satellite has broken up in geostationary orbit (GEO) and lost power, ceasing communications services for customers across Europe, Africa and parts of Asia Pacific.

Intelsat said in an Oct. 19 news release it is working with satellite maker Boeing to address an anomaly that emerged earlier that day, but “believe it is unlikely that the satellite will be recoverable.” An Intelsat spokesperson said the satellite was not insured at the time of the issue.

The U.S. Space Force reported Oct. 19 it is tracking around 20 pieces of debris associated with the spacecraft.

“U.S. Space Forces-Space (S4S) has confirmed the breakup of Intelsat 33E (#41748, 2016-053B) in GEO on October 19, 2024, at approximately 0430 UTC,” states an alert posted on SpaceTrack, the U.S. Department of Defense’s space-tracking platform.

**582:**

_The canonical movie is "All the King's Men", based on the 1947 novel by Robert Penn Warren of the same name, based on Huey Long's rise and fall._

Read the book twice, maybe three times. Saw the original "great" movie and mediocre later one. Great but dated. Many scenes will elicit laughter in a college crowd.

And the book needs to drop 90% of the middle 3rd. When he prattles on seemingly forever about that time in Lexington Ky. Still a great book. And my friends who read it all felt the same way. But it was interesting to walk by the apartment where the fictional character was supposed to live while at school at the Univ of Ky when I lived in the area in the 1970s.

A PBS 2 part show on him (HL) is interesting. But he was a complicated person (like DT) and it is hard to summarize him in a single portrayal.

And to my question if anyone outside of the US knows about him, most people these days in the US don't either.

[Grant](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4403) replied to [this comment from Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199620) | [October 21, 2024 19:09](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199624)

**587:**

The combined gravitational influence of the Sun, Earth and Moon on an unpowered object in GEO can pump it up to a 15 degree inclined orbit and a 4km/s velocity, so it doesn't have to be anything very big to total a satellite and produce debris.

GEO is sufficiently far removed that mapping the debris objects in it is pretty hard and the catalogues very incomplete.

Just as long as its not an ASAT attack on the US from India, Russia or China... All have the technology and all have previously shown themselves willing to employ it - even when it does produce clouds of debris.

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199630) | [October 21, 2024 21:14](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199633)

**596:**

Bear in mind Starlink satellites routinely (i.e. all of them, at least once a day) maneuver to avoid coming too close to other objects. They're actively powered, with ion thrusters for maneuvering and station-keeping, and when they run low on fuel they de-orbit.

The five clusters with ~50,000 satellites projected for a couple of decades hence sounds hairy, but that's distributed throughout about that many cubic kilometres of vacuum. The real issue is that they need meticulous tracking and a lot of compute power to calculate those trajectories -- but these days, we've got that.

As a point of reference, there are roughly 50,000 civil airliner flights every 24 hours around the world, often coming very close to each other as they leave/arrive at airports ... and the number of collisions between airliners is vanishingly small (on the order of once a decade, or less) because we have procedures in place to make that unlikely.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2)

[](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=2) replied to [this comment from Greg Tingey](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199631) | [October 21, 2024 21:20](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199634)

**597:**

Boeing is responsible for about 30-50% of the civil airliners in service worldwide. Airliners with a design life of at least 30 years, at that.

Boeing can't just "stop", if it did there'd be catastrophic knock-on effects.

This ignores the defense/military side of the corporation, and the satellite business.

I can see a government-mandated breakup of Boeing happening, with different daughter companies havign different degrees of government intervention to keep them standing. But that'll take a long time to fix the management problems that have overrun the company since the (government promoted) McDonnell-Douglas/Boeing merger in the 90s that set us up for this.

In fact, a lot of the problems appear to be side-effects of outsourcing. So a "fix" for Boeing might be to force a return to vertical integration, starting with a Boeing takeover of Spirit Aerosystems, the huge component supplier who Boeing spun out about 10-15 years ago (and whose QA on parts destined for the Boeing final assembly line led to some of the 737 and other airliner problems of late).

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

**598:**

_No point in emergency rations that you actually want to eat because they might be eaten before the emergency._

That leads to the problem of emergency rations that are 2x or 3x past their use by date but that's what you've got so you take your chances.

My policy is that every week I have at least one meal from my emergency stash or my camping supplies just so they turn over regularly. I'd rather open a jar of pasta sauce that's a year old and discover that it's off now when I can go shopping than a week into a supply crisis.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6931)

[Paul](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6931) | [October 21, 2024 21:41](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199636)

**599:**

[Here is a new take](https://arstechnica.com/space/2024/10/solar-power-from-space-actually-it-might-happen-in-a-couple-of-years/) on a concept that's been discussed here in the past: solar power sats.

This one uses an infra-red laser to beam the power from LEO back to earth, getting over the issues with steering giant microwave antennae. The initial model is to provide power to off-grid places like disaster relief efforts. Maybe later beaming power to ships so they don't need bunker fuel.

Sounds intriguing. But the energy density at the receiver is going to be much higher than the original uWave concept, and unlike those uWaves it will presumably heat anything it hits. Also, how do you convert IR radiation into electricity? Do clouds block it?

The other problem will be night time. Satellites in LEO spend a fair amount of time in darkness, and at midnight I'm not sure that any illuminated satellite is going to be above the horizon. Batteries?

**603:**

Recent discussions above on congressional districts divvying up NASA pork barrel spending got me thinking about federal jobs policy in general. So some thoughts on public employment replacing private hiring, now that A.I. could start threatening everybody's jobs:

Stephanie Kelton's "the Deficit Myth" outlines a full employment economy strategy, based on Modern Monetary Theory (the idea being that fiat currencies can sustainably support any level of spending, constrained only by inflation.) It suggests federal hiring of underemployed workers at wages sufficient to live decently on, she says $15/hr but the book is a couple years old. 

An estimated twenty or so billion hours of work would be made available this way to states and localities for addressing needs identified by area interests, paid for by three trillion of new money creation (8% of GDP), with expansions and cutbacks presumably based on well monitored inflation trends. Or just leaving the work programs alone they could use progressive tax increases to soak up excess liquidity, before it ignites wage and price spirals.

Fairly controversial stuff, but the part I found most interesting was trying to imagine lists of "make-work" projects drawn up in advance by legislatures and city councils to absorb all the new help from federal programs. Maybe include cheap public daycare centers, free public health clinics offering checkups and longterm outpatient physical therapy, free public psychological counseling and social work interventions and so on, construction schemes to remedy infrastructure weaknesses like levees, dams, roads and bridges, all the familiar Civilian Conservation Corps work from the 1930s still needed today like parks and public land maintenance....

What else? Billions of tree plantings to help fix climate change flood erosion problems? Roving squads of bin sorting personnel, to identify, catalog and arrange online auctions for disposal of vast unclaimed drifts of accumulated possessions, once hoarded by invalid or deceased Boomers?

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

**606:**

Weird thing about lifeboat bread. I had some in my earthquake kit. One day I saw it had expired, so I unwrapped it and tossed it in the worm bin. Several months after that, the worms hadn’t touched it, so I pulled it out and tossed it in the trash. 

I agree on the flavor though. I tried one before it expired.

My preferred survival ration for many years was Logan Bread. Not sure what my body would think of it now, but I used to keep a batch in the freezer just in case. According to gable, if you dry it out properly, it can be used as any-bear ammo akin to Dwarf Bread. The stuff I made was much softer than that.

**615:**

Here's my favorite science blogger on colonizing the Galilean moons. According to him (reliable in most ways) the two most inner ones have dangerous radiation levels on the surface, but the third one (Ganymede) seems survivable for short periods, and the outermost one (Callisto) is downright mild: "The distance from Jupiter (1.86 million km) provides another effect: less radiation. It measures only 0.01 Rem per day, which is survivable without any protection at all."

However, that's dated 2017. Here's the wikipedia article, that says basically the same thing: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Galilean_moons#Callisto>

Here is a peer reviewed article (scroll down to table 3): <https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Bipasa-Raha/publication/260245912_EXPLORATION_OF_JUPITER_THROUGH_OBSERVATIONS_BY_AUTOMATED_SPACECRAFT/links/02e7e5304c06b9b50c000000/EXPLORATION-OF-JUPITER-THROUGH-OBSERVATIONS-BY-AUTOMATED-SPACECRAFT.pdf>

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Keithmasterson](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199640) | [October 21, 2024 23:43](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199653)

**616:**

_What else? Billions of tree plantings to help fix climate change flood erosion problems? Roving squads of bin sorting personnel, to identify, catalog and arrange online auctions for disposal of vast unclaimed drifts of accumulated possessions, once hoarded by invalid or deceased Boomers?_

Personally, I'd rather give them all a basic living wage, and hire people who know what they're doing. Speaking from experience.

The tl;dr is that, even though I dislike the bell curve for describing people, there are those who are at the bottom of the curve or list in terms of performance. Trump as US president, for example. There's a point with many jobs when it's cheaper to pay certain people not to do them, rather than pay the costs of correcting their mistakes. 

Tree planting is hard work, and actually getting the trees to survive is harder still. Since it's an easy bit of PR, I can already tell you that, worldwide, billions of tree seedlings have died due to inept planting and lack of follow on care. That's one huge reason why we're not getting forests growing to take up CO2. Locally, a big tree planting program had the end result that many of the seedlings were stashed where the supervisor couldn't see them, and never made it into the ground. I audited their success data, such as it was, and found that while they were reporting planting in different areas, the seedlings were actually going to replant the first area over and over, with not many established seedlings and lots of weeds as the result.

Bin sorting? Who's auditing the sort? A lot of supposedly sorted stuff ends up in the landfill anyway, because even sorted, it's not worth anything to anyone. This is a huge problem for plastics. Getting lead and aluminum out is easy. Mylar? Contaminated plastics? Also, it's freaking hard work. I had a relative involved for decades in county-level waste management, and they toured a lot of facilities and had LOTS of stories about how many intractable issues they had to deal with. Throwing low-skilled labor at this won't solve it.*

Dealing with an elderly relative's stuff? I'M DOING THAT RIGHT NOW! IT'S EFFING HARD if you want the stuff to not get thrown out. Our recycling system is rudimentary and at flood stage, and every effort is made to get you to pay someone to throw it out for you, at which point it gets landfilled. I know what all the stuff is, and one of the things I've found is that no one else does and most don't care. And antique dealers around here are going out of business thanks to online marketplaces and social media. Do I want the job of marketing every separate doohickey? Frack no! Do I have a choice not to these days? Also no. Do I think that Joe Random Underperformer could do that marketing job better than I do? Um....no. Not really. 

If you think that childcare is untrained labor, that any dope could do it, I might sic my sister in law the elementary school teacher on you. I actually won't, because she's overworked and doesn't need the provocation. She does need to be listened to about the benefits of good childhood development, though. 

So no, I wouldn't employ people unless both they and I are comfortable that they know what to do. If you want to be nasty about it and make sure the freeloaders give value to society, then make it expensive to rear children, so that people have to make above basic living wage to reproduce. That's more or less what we're doing now. That should at least shut up the "freeloaders will drown us" crowd, even though it doesn't leads to its own intractable problems. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199653) | [October 22, 2024 00:00](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199654)

**617:**

  * Oh yeah, the asterisk from the previous post, about garbage and recycling.



Here's a suggestion for a colonization story. It's a play on the old 60s Niven trope of the left-wing libertarian Belter culture, itself somewhat manufactured out of stories of old mining camps and prospector settlements. The twist is: set the stories on Earth. Instead of asteroid miners, make them garbage miners. Why? Asteroids and old landfills are about equally useful and problematic to mine, so...Tales of the Garbage Mountains.

Yes, this is a hopepunk scenario, set in miraculously still-living future, where independent miners stake their claims to the decaying landfills of vanished civilizations. It's filthy work, but occasionally they strike it rich. And they always find relics, some of which are valuable McGuffins. Who needs fusion-powered singleships when you've got wheelbarrows and wagons? 

Have fun with it.

Any appearance that this is a send-up of libertarianism is entirely coincidental. Really.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

[Moz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199653) | [October 22, 2024 00:02](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199655)

**618:**

Local landcare have a couple of new sites we've been "given" by council after official planting days, but with a month or two of followup by paid staff to make sure things are not completely crazy. I took the 'plant a tree day' opportunity to bang in some random seedlings badly just to find out (I have seedling at home. It's a deliberate propagation programme, I swear). Anyway, because it's spring, it's wet and it's warm the death rate across a couple of thousand plants is close to zero. I think the experiment is a bit of a fail in that regard.

But it is very important that this is the first step. The whole point of handing it over to landcare volunteers is that we will weed around those seedlings and replace the woodchips when they rot/wash away, maintain the desire paths through the sites and generally keep the sites looking active so that people don't mistake them for trash and mow them, remove anything that looks useful, park cars on them etc).

On that note one of our volunteers has planted some stuff in a place the council do mow regularly so I'm discussing the options. Specifically, do I relocate some council star pickets to there so it's harder to mow. It's not as easy as "yes", because the council native planting department would be on side but not necessarily the council mowing department)

I also found some more mulberry treelets so I think it's time to plant those in suitable locations (black mulberries get _big_ Not quite fig tree big, but 10m isn't out of the question. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ficus_macrophylla> vs <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morus_nigra>

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796)

[Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199653) | [October 22, 2024 00:14](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199656)

**619:**

I can't speak for the Antipodes, but here in Canada there is a massive treeplanting industry that puts in hundreds of millions of trees every year (to replace cut and burnt trees). I worked in the industry for over a decade, some of those years as a supervisor.

It is not difficult for the supervisor to spot when trees have been hidden ('Stashed'). If you have a plantable area of 7ha and want 1200 trees/ha, you will notice if somehow 150000 trees go in there. Criminal charges could then ensue, in some cases. 

Quality control is an ongoing job, but not particularly complex. Every day I had a comprehensive random sampling process across various hectares which was almost infallible in catching problems. It was possible to put my thumb on the scale, but the way things worked at my company meant that any QC issues affected my pay directly - so I tended to oversample the potential problems to make sure I caught everything. It's been a few years (math person help me here) but IIRC sampling a circle with a 1.99m radius gave me a 1% sample of a hectare. I would typically sample 3-9 times per hectare, depending on whose work I was checking (seasoned veteran vs newbie with attitude). Problems always meant going back to redo the work.

The thing about doing something 1-3000 times/day (such as planting seedlings) is that if you are doing it wrong it shows up quickly. It is also equally easy to do it properly, once you learn the technique. Those who do not learn quickly find themselves back in the city quite quickly, but most people seem to manage. 

Another part of the industry was follow-up. Forestry companies are on the hook for meaningful fines if the regrowth doesn't meet a minimum density 7 years after cutting, so they tended to go back and make sure - repeatedly if necessary.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199656) | [October 22, 2024 00:33](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199657)

**620:**

I do respect your professionalism, and I agree that, when it's done properly, the results are precisely as you say.

On the other hand, you can do a search on "reforestation failures" and get the other side. There are A Lot of failures in reforestation projects globally. 

The particular project I'm thinking of (and the documents we got through a freedom of information act-type request are about two meters away) were supposedly for a postfire restoration of a local wild park that everybody was fond of. The money came from Disney and Pepsi as a carbon-sequestration/PR thingie, and the deal was brokered by then-governor Schwarzenegger. Last time I checked it was the furthest south carbon sequestration program by several hundred miles. It was poorly controlled, inmate labor was used on the first tranche, resulting in most of the trees being buried under rocks. The data are, politely, substandard, but when I checked in the field, it was quite obvious that they were replanting existing areas rather than expanding as they said. 

It actually gets worse from there, but Charlie doesn't need a libel suit, so I need to end it. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5955)

[Thomas Jørgensen](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5955) replied to [this comment from Keithmasterson](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199640) | [October 22, 2024 00:39](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199658)

**621:**

That is not what MMT says. What MMT says is that the constraint on economic output is never money. The constraint is labor, and if you are maintaining a reserve army of the unemployed you are throwing economic value into the void. 

And also throwing away human capital. "Bob the useless dole consumer" is not a normal outcome for any human being who isn't a medical invalid. - you Create that type of wastrel by keeping them idle/unemployed for years on end. A productive worker is forged on the job. 

If you print money and use this to put people to work productively this increases the total amount of value in the economy at the same time it increases the amount of money so the inflationary impact is very modest/non-existent. 

This does not work for make-work projects and it also stops working when you can't mobilize any more people into the workforce. Though one of the points the advocates of MMT are making is that most places are just stupidly far away from the limits on that. If your labor force participation is below 75% are you even trying? 

So you can't fix unemployment by having people dig holes and fill them in.... But you can fix it and housing by having them quarry stone and build palatial apartment buildings until everyone has a nice place and "we can't afford that" is a nonsensical objection as long as there are bobs you can reforge into stone masons. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

**623:**

Apologies - I had you and Moz mixed up. You are in California. 

Anything worth doing can be done badly, but it can also be done well. It's not easy to get the incentives dialed in correctly. It is still worth trying, even when some marquee effort gets derailed by people not giving two damns about the end result.

In the years I was in the industry I saw it evolve from predetermined monoculture planting into a much more nuanced process of planting a range of species depending on specific site location (moisture, relative height, light, soil content of planting site), often as many as 3 or 4 species at a time in my bags (~300 seedlings, depending). People I keep in touch with tell me it has become much more subtle and technical since.

There have been many failures, but the forestry companies being highly incentivized at least some of them tried to learn from what worked and apply things to their own operations. Of course they aren't noble heroes, there were plenty who saw the requirement to reforest an area as clueless government overreach etc. But in my experience the actual frontline foresters were, for the most part, very interested in good outcomes.

I actually like the idea of a universal income, and an ever changing list of 'community work' that people can take on for a bonus to that income. I'm sure there will be a few, but very few people don't want to do anything at all.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

**624:**

I loved the study recently that found that a small UBI experiment didn't encourage people to work or invest, they just kept doing what they'd been doing. I'm gunna call that cultural effects :) Or as posited above, the statistical distribution of personality traits.

A lot of that stuff is very hard for people like us to reason about because we're not in the groups likely to really benefit from a UBI except in extreme cases ('AI makes 90% of jobs go away" scenarios). I suspect many here are like me: given a year with no need to do paid work we would quickly find ourselves wondering how we ever found the time to do paid work before. I understand that some of you "retired" people are experiencing that :)

I would really like to see a UBI experiment that had the reach of the Alaskan one but resourced more like Norway. And now I'm wondering whether Norway's approach is better despite being all paternalistic and shit. "everyone gets a comprehensive welfare state with free university education etc because we don't think making you pay for privatised versions of those things is a good idea". Suddenly a $US20,000/year UBI doesn't seem all that wonderful.

**632:**

_There must surely be some way to compensate for this. More power?_

More fuel (or delta-V) will extend the launch window, but you’re still constrained by orbital mechanics. If you have constant-boost ships the transit time drops off immensely, but you still get variations because the distances are always changing. 

I dealt with this a bit when writing stuff for _Traveller_. Even with a 1G (or more) constant boost ship the travel time to the outer system wasn’t insignificant (not months though) and changed depending on orbital positions. 

With unlimited launch energy (but ballistic trajectories) you could design a system that had either constant departures and clustered arrivals, or constant arrivals but clustered launches. Assuming by back of the envelope math is correct. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

[Robert Prior](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199588) | [October 22, 2024 02:39](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199670)

**633:**

_Under one roof can make things amazingly more productive._

Like how moving management to a different roof often ends up impacting a company’s long-term productivity?

I remember when I worked at BNR and they moved admin to a different building. Suddenly what used to be solved by walking to someone’s desk or meeting them in the hallway required a scheduled meeting and a trip across the city. 

And of course, we have Boeing moving their headquarters away for manufacturing so engineers wouldn’t interfere with management...

**634:**

One problem is that SpaceX junk often survives de-orbiting and there is no existing legal structure for What to do when they hit someone or someone's building or just have to be cleaned up. The existing laws and treaties assume that space junk is rare and launched by states. The light pollution is the same, there is no legal framework for regulating a private company which is polluting the night sky for everyone.

Sam Lawler the astronomer in Canada recently spoke to a UN conference in Milano about this issue.

**639:**

I can't help thinking that the term "rescue" is being misused here, and a more accurate interpretation of the cocky's view would be "abduct" or "evict". Right now it has food and water readily available, no competition, it has staff and entertainment and anything else it wants. I don't like their chances of keeping it out in the event they do manage to capture it, and there's some chance that being a social animal it will return with a whole gang of friends (the official term for a group of these is gang not flock. It's accurate).

<https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2024/oct/22/efforts-continue-to-rescue-cockatoo-living-on-brioche-for-four-weeks-inside-sydney-supermarket>

flaw in your list?

you are fixated upon high end skills

what you need to identify is a combo of low end skills widely found in those masses of 'workfare hires' to be matched up to projects able to soak up those hours

sadly, a lot of the projects will needs be de-focused, deliberately reduced in scaling and/or pacing to avoid over loading various individuals who simply cannot be counted upon to operate heavy equipment safely along with a vast number of slackers who would be deliberate in fouling up simple tasks to demonstrate they are "nobody's bitch"

so... sorting of inbound recycling not once but twice due to arsewipes so unreliable as to include plastics in with metals... and still some gizmo providing QA by way of spectra-scanning to spot wring bits on the sorted conveyer 

likely certain work assignments will be utter nonsense... 

EXAMPLE: laying out chairs for a concert by group "A" on Tuesday and then on Wednesday group "B" restacks 'em but there was never a concert scheduled... just lots 'n lots of chairs being rearranged without possibility of harm upon society... soaking up a couple thousand manhours of sweat from least capable folk

**645:**

That satellite breakup is not ideal, and the reporting hides one of the most important bits between the lines. Around 20 fragments are being tracked, but this can also be interpreted as "there are around 20 fragments large enough to be tracked".

Silver lining is that the debris field is quite small. To me this would suggest a low-energy breakup caused by something on board coming apart. I avoid the term "exploding", because the reality is likely less dramatic. This means there is less risk of high speed fragments on an elliptical orbit that repeatedly intersects the equator at GEO. So less risk of hitting other things at GEO.

Enemy action here is unlikely, it looks like a failure. One of the other advantages of GEO is that enemy action is less likely. Pretty much anyone with the ability to reach GEO is interested in it remaining useful. Outliers like North Korea could cause damage at LEO, but lack the capabilities to reach GEO with any precision.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4809)

**652:**

_One problem is that SpaceX junk often survives de-orbiting_

No it doesn't.

What got headlines was a specific problem: IIRC SpaceX had begun de-orbiting Dragon capsules from the ISS so that the capsules would splash down off the coast of Florida. They're now moving them back to the Pacific Ocean so that the trunk fragments land in deep water. [Details here](https://arstechnica.com/space/2024/07/spacex-moving-dragon-splashdowns-to-pacific-to-solve-falling-debris-problem/).

Note that Dragon (and its trunk) are very different from Starlink satellites, in both mass and density/structure. (A crew Dragon is about 12.5 tonnes at launch, when fully loaded with crew and fuel; a Starlink generation 1 satellite is 250kg or so, while the new big Starlink 2 is 1000kg.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8141)

**660:**

Back when we had a local crisis of lawsuits stopping the opening of the planned new landfill[1] there was an analysis done of what was filling up the existing one. Turns out by volume (not weight) it was newspapers, magazines, and diapers. So the city got a serious recycling program started. Which extended the life of the existing landfill from 1 or 2 years to 5 or more. Construction is big but much of the concrete type of waste goes into holes that need to be filled. Typically miles away but cheaper than paying the city to make a hill with it. And of course newsprint isn't all that much of the recycle stream anymore. But plastic is. You know all of those chasing arrows. And most of it now gets buried after being sorted.

[1] City/county buys land in very rural area in the 70s for when existing land fill gets full. County was and still is fast growing. So due to landfill plans the dirt around it was cheaper. So builders build subdivisions "over the hill". And don't mention coming landfill. When city/country starts work on opening new landfill (and of all people you know how much work that can be these days) neighbors riot. And sue. But they loose. But it took 10 years to clear it all up through the courts. So to prevent existing landfill from filling up city/county gets serious and start a curbside recycling program. Which allows existing landfill to keep going for 5+ years. Now the people who bought homes in subdivisions next to existing landfill sue as they were "promised" (maybe by realtors?) that it would close and become a park within a year of them buying their new home. They also lost. But a lot of money got spent on lawyers and experts.

Who could see it coming?

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

**665:**

_I swear, you folks let your weird-ass non-convertible inches/feet/furlongs gaslight you into thinking unit conversions have to be hard ._

Not really. I do conversions in my head multiple times per day. At times every 5 minutes. You get used to it after having EVERY SINGLE TOOL SET having to have two sets of everything. And it not being obvious at times which applies to the current task. And at times BOTH. Sockets, wrenches, allen wrenches, drivers, screws, nuts, bolts, ..... big sigh.

Staying with SAE was promoted as a cost saving measure as the US would not have to change. (Plus it was a secret commie plot by Carter.) Yep. Sure. At least you rarely run into any single auto with both anymore. THAT was seriously fun times for a while.

Brexit and Trump promises anyone?

When we in the US see tonnes we have to figure out which flavor of spellings and usage it means. Is this metric or color vs colour or a typo or what?

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) | [October 22, 2024 19:39](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199722)

**671:**

Boeing, Spirit AeroSystems, and Airbus

Guys. It is a done deal.

Boeing is buying most of Spirit with $5.7bil in stock and absorbing $3.6bil in Spirit debt.

At the same time Airbus will buy all of the Spirit bits that apply to them for $1 to Boeing and a a weird payment from Spirit to Airbus of $559mil. I smell some tax issues at play. Or maybe this is a buyout of the contract from Spirit to Airbus which was loosing barrels of money.

[JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199722) | [October 22, 2024 20:19](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199725)

**672:**

David L @ 671:

_Boeing, Spirit AeroSystems, and Airbus_

_Guys. It is a done deal._

_Boeing is buying most of Spirit with $5.7bil in stock and absorbing $3.6bil in Spirit debt._

_At the same time Airbus will buy all of the Spirit bits that apply to them for $1 to Boeing and a a weird payment from Spirit to Airbus of $559mil. I smell some tax issues at play. Or maybe this is a buyout of the contract from Spirit to Airbus which was loosing barrels of money._

Reading a bit deeper into the Wikipedia article, the same "investment" company, [_Onex Corporation_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Onex_Corporation), that bought the Boeing factories in the midwestern U.S. (renaming them as Spirit AeroSystems) also bought BAE factories in Scotland & England, Bombardier Aviation's plant in Belfast & Casablanca which were sub-contractors for Airbus.

I was wrong about Spirit NOT building Airbus parts in North America, they apparently opened a plant down in Kinston, NC where they build Airbus 350 sections that are sent to France for assembly into fuselage sections before being sent on to Airbus for final assembly.

[

> _"As part of the deal, Boeing will take ownership of the Wichita and Tulsa plants it previously operated, along with the MRO facility in Dallas. Airbus will take over the facilities in Casablanca, Kinston and St. Nazaire, along with A220 production assets in Belfast. Spirit also plans to divest its operations Subang and Prestwick, along with Fiber Materials, and non-Airbus operations in Belfast."_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spirit_AeroSystems#History)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6643)

**673:**

To David L @ 671   
From the boss...........   
Obviously Airbus are taking on a lot of underlying debt in the Euroepean operations that it is buying, given the $559mil contribution from Spirit. It would be interesting to know just how much, as that number is missing from the information provided.   
Looks like Airbus is protecting the bits it needs for its own business and keeping its part of the supply chain paid up and operating by paying off the debts, so potentially expensive but commercial sense.   
No obvious tax issues that I can see, apart from the normal commercial sort in a deal like this.   
"_It passes the smell test, and after 38 years in this game there is nothing too much wrong with my sense of smell._ "

**674:**

From the source of all true knowledge (The World Wide Web):

"As we all know 1 ton = 2000 pounds = 907.18 Kg. But 1 metric ton = 2204 pounds = 1000 Kg. The metric ton is officially called as tonne (i.e. S.I standards called it as tonne), but the U.S government recommends to call it as metric ton."

So, if Tennessee Ernie Ford is singing about it, a ton is 2000 pounds, if OGH is writing, a tonne is 2204 pounds. :)

You might be surprised. I write Mm and Gg occasionally and people often blink or just assume it's some weird thing they can't possibly cope with. But driving 5Mm in Australia or flying 15Mm anywhere is something a lot of people do without thinking (they should but they don't).

But imperial units are much more fun. Who wouldn't love to know that Greenland is melting at about 2000 great gross acre-feet per year instead of the silly "300Tg" metric equivalent or the quaintly antipodean 2/3rds SydHarb.

**679:**

I think Tg is an awkward unit, but so is acre feet. Acre-feet at least works with the math of “how high the dam, how deep the reservoir?” Kind of thinking, without converting cubic feet of water into gallons. Instead of Tg, I’d use tonnes of meltwater, because a tonne of water is 1 m3, so going from volume to weight is more straightforward than with Tg to 1000000 m3. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

[Moz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298) | [October 22, 2024 23:18](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199737)

**680:**

Is it really easier to visualise 27524 trillion gallons (that's US trillion) than 300,000,000,000 cubic metres? Or tonnes, or kilolitres? To me a Tg of water is a kilometre cube and that's about all you need to know. Or Australian irrigation water is measured in Gl which to an American is a square mile 422 yards deep. Or some random number of acres an equally silly number of feet deep. Or 2.4 White Houses a Washington Monument deep.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

[Moz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298) | [October 22, 2024 23:27](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199739)

**681:**

And then I realise I slipped a prefix, because obviously I'll do that when I'm saying it's a natural unit.

One tonne = one kilolitre = 1m³. And 1 tonne = 1000kg = 1Mg. Then (1000m)³ = 1Gm³. 1Gm³ x 1Mg = 1Pg.

This does somewhat rely on people knowing kMGTPEZYRQ as the set of prefixes. I think we can (and should) ignore the cddah prefixes as much as humanly possible. If you want area talk about ares like any good metric person. Or just fucken don't and use square metres like a reasonable human being.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Moz](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199739) | [October 23, 2024 00:50](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199741)

**682:**

You're ignoring both the practical and confusticatory usages of acre-foot.

For a farmer with X acres of crop that he's flood-irrigating (as used to be done in much of California), X acres of crop times Y inches of water needed equals the number of acre-feet he can withdraw through his local irrigation district. Since he's measuring water demand in inches (based on how dry the soil is), this isn't entirely stupid. I mean, yes, flood irrigating is inherently stupid in many places, but given that the land was parceled out by the square mile (aka section) back in homesteading days, and there are 640 acres per section, doing the water math in metric units means you have to pay attention to more decimal places than most farmers want to deal with.

So that's the practical use. Blame it on the old Homesteading acts that gridded up the country, and possibly blame that on how many of the founding fathers made their initial stake as surveyors marking sections for homesteading.

As for the confustication, that's equally important.

One acre-foot equals 325851.42857143 gallons US. Yippee. I had to look that up. How much water do farmers use, versus citycreeps like myself? Well, farmers measure in acre-feet, we measure in gallons, and most people cringe away from trying to convert between the two units. That nicely draws a veil over the fact that agriculture, in places like California, uses 80% of the water supply for a fairly small chunk of the economy, and many of those products (almonds, grapes, lettuce) are exported rather than locally eaten, because it isn't water efficient to grow staple foods like wheat here anymore.

This couldn't be hidden if everybody used the same units, now, could they? And if people were forced to confront the agriculture industry head on, a lot of wealthy companies, including (gulp!) a bunch of retirement funds, make a lot of their money off export agriculture. So rocking that boat gets fuggly, does it not?

Instead, politicians can it doesn't matter by using acre-feet, while industrial ag and farmers struggle getting enough water to stay in business, while citypunks like myself try to conserve water occasionally.

See? It like totally makes sense. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3431)

**699:**

"I think there was a lot of illiterate but skilled labor."

No doubt! Cathedrals took generations to complete. By the time some of those masonry teams spent decades on the same job they would have looked like death defying acrobats climbing the spires and flying buttresses.

But astonishing skill and proficiency are commonplace all through history and pre-history; flint knapping to make stone tools and Clovis points was a difficult, genuine art form. Use of boomerangs for hunting by indigenous Australians looks like a magical technique and had to have taken years to perfect. Movietone newsreels of the 1930s show workmen on the Empire State Building hurling rivets glowing from an on-site furnace for dozens of yards, the catchers intercepting them with steel baskets one after the other to pass along hot and malleable to the riveters, all day long.

Expertise and shrewd craft even develop among the homeless is large cities, who know right where the sidewalk steam vents are that can save your life on a cold night, which restaurants are most likely to dispose of edible product at what time on which days, what areas to avoid and where to find shelter. 

The point being, since we know impressive abilities appear routinely, why wouldn't we expect similar levels of competence to develop among federal workfare employees, given adequate time and supervision. The only example from a capitalist work environment mentioned above was the riveters, but it proves nothing, riveters in Stalinist Moscow back then had to use the same set of skills. To say nothing of Rosie the Riveter and the millions of women who replaced critically needed servicemen on the job for years. Expertise happens, even when you don't really need it to just to get by. The learning curve, productivity gains over time, efficiencies of scale, it's an emergent property.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

[Moz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298) replied to [this comment from whitroth](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199753) | [October 23, 2024 03:35](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199762)

**700:**

_The people who you really don't want to work with, some would just not work._

Reports of unemployed people voluntold to do agricultural labour are informative. You get the obvious people who aren't physically cabale of it regardless of what the computer says, but you also get a lot of people who are mentally incapable as well. Doing repetitive manual labour is a skill, even if it's the non-injurious style (there's a difference between, say, picking pumpkins wearing gloves and cutting asparagus using a sharp-ish knife in terms of what happens if your mind wanders a bit).

Farmers and I assume other 'unskilled' labour hiring people are well used to the first day being a mutual evaluation period and it's not uncommon to see people fired in the first minute/hour/break rather than waiting until the end of the day. Depending on the viciousness of the farm economics and punishment of unemployed "firing" can mean sitting in the break room until the end of the day then "not hired" because of low productivity. It's about whether the farmer can afford the days wages going out for nothing coming in.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

Of course it is. It appears the "long ton" - which was the ton everywhere pre-metrication except in the USA - is closer to the metric tonne that the USA'ian "short ton".

Not sure why - but the USA seems to have its own versions of many of the pre-metric measurements for weight and volume - such as US Pint, US gallon etc.

For all these weird conversions (and others), the site <https://www.unitconverters.net/> is quite helpful.

**712:**

"Not sure why - but the USA seems to have its own versions of many of the pre-metric measurements for weight and volume - such as US Pint, US gallon etc."

...and helpfully refers to them as "English units".

For other weird mixed-unit systems, see aviation. For example the UK definition of an aerodrome traffic zone (ATZ): 

"a zone extending from the ground up to 2000 feet with a radius of 2.5 nautical miles around the midpoint of the longest runway if the longest runway is longer than 1850 metres, or with a radius of 2.0 nautical miles around the midpoint of the longest runway if the longest runway is shorter than 1850 metres."

**715:**

_"Not sure why - but the USA seems to have its own versions of many of the pre-metric measurements for weight and volume - such as US Pint, US gallon etc."_

According to the usually-reliable Wikipedia:

_The unit of volume, the gallon, has different values in the United States and in the United Kingdom – the US fluid gallon being about 0.83 imperial gallons and the US dry gallon being about 0.97 imperial gallons. The US fluid gallon was based on the wine gallon used in England prior to 1826._

_After the United States Declaration of Independence the units of measurement in the United States developed into what is now known as customary units. The United Kingdom overhauled its system of measurement in 1826, when it introduced the imperial system of units. This resulted in the two countries having different gallons. Later in the century, efforts were made to align the definition of the pound and the yard in the two countries by using copies of the standards adopted by the British Parliament in 1855. However, these standards were of poor quality compared with those produced for the Convention of the Metre. In 1960, the two countries agreed to common definitions of the yard and the pound based on definitions of the metre and the kilogram. This change, which amounted to a few parts per million, had little effect in the United Kingdom, but resulted in the United States having two slightly different systems of linear measure – the international system, and the surveyors system._

<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperial_and_US_customary_measurement_systems>

_...and helpfully refers to them as "English units"._

Or in the case of some publishers, "Imperial units" even when they are talking about the American versions.

**734:**

What stopped the Clathrate Gun hypothesis at that time (methane clathrates in polar ocean sediments emitting vast quantities of methane into the atmosphere) was that, when they observed methane clathrate emissions never made it to the surface, because methanogenic microbes got to them first. Could those microbes be overwhelmed? Dunno. But they are ubiquitous, IIRC. 

As for the unexpectedly rapid rise of global temperatures, one problem that I didn't know about is with the models (got this from <https://e360.yale.edu/features/gavin-schmidt-interview> ). Some of the critical data sets they're using are 10-15 years old and past due for an update. 

Is China involved in providing the needed data and might it get political? Possibly. Why do you ask? 

Indeed I would not be entirely surprised if everybody's fracking their data to cover their asses for their climate change failures, lying being the fashionable hanks to the dark enlightenment and other intellectual metastases in the same clade. But I would be sad if everybody got that stupid. Mutually assured survival works better than ass-covering and picayune power plays any day in my book, but apparently I'm a silly little white dude for thinking that. Oh well.

**735:**

Superfluous precision. I defy anyone to load coal to an accuracy of even 12 significant figures, let alone 16.

Just for reference, that's roughly 1.3e+7 grams, so the final couple of digits or the original represent about 300 nanograms, or 200 picoitres of coal. For US readers that's about 40 billionths of a US teaspoon (US billionths)
