**739:**

A) I'll stand corrected, because I have no wish to insult people with Long Covid

B) Astronauts and cosmonauts are purportedly told that the choice of whether to walk off the spacecraft or be carried comes down to whether they want to exercise two hours per day or more in freefall. Even with the exercise, you get Hadfield's problem, which, along with bone loss, included all the fluids in his body adjusting to freefall and suddenly getting pulled down, sensitive feet that hadn't borne his full weight for a year (which apparently hurt like hell while he reacclimated), and reflex changes that range from embarrassing to dangerous.

In regards to the latter, experience has taught NASA to tell Astronaut spouses NOT to hand their small children to their astronaut spouses, because the latter have forgotten that gravity means that things drop, and so they might thoughtlessly try to leave the child floating in midair...IIRC this advice also covers anything fragile, like drink glasses.

Those are the problems that go away relatively quickly. Other problems (bone loss, radiation exposure, and so forth) take much longer to go away.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

**740:**

_This is an alt-government idea: what if engineers ran things? Could a system where everyone in a position of authority has to be an engineer work?_

For a time China was run by engineers, although their influence seems to be waning.

<https://issues.org/perspective-the-benefits-of-technocracy-in-china/>

As an engineer, I'd argue that engineering training is about getting things done, with little-to-no emphasis on whether those things _should_ be done, and a lot of emphasis on applying tried-and-trusted methods to get those things done.

**743:**

My impression was that they're going off the results they can find, and urging that precautions be taken now and urgently. They're not ruling out that things might be worse than they currently suggest.

There's an ongoing fight in the science communication field about exactly how to convey the seriousness of problems, and any mitigations or solutions. Especially in light of the current political situation where the 'conservative' side want definite answers, absolute certainty, and take any change in facts or advice as proof that those changing their minds have no idea and should be ignored. The great global warming 'hoax' being a slightly terrifying case in point.

Dammit, Mastodon doesn't really do 'history' so I can't find the other article I read today that was specifically about the topic. Here's some TechDirt instead:

<https://www.techdirt.com/2024/10/23/everything-is-a-conspiracy-theory-when-you-dont-bother-to-educate-yourself/>

"I'd still be curious about if things are headed wrong, what are the options. Fire off all the engines and head down range? Blow it up? Just aim it at the ground?"

CRS-16 is an example.

**746:**

_As an engineer, I'd argue that engineering training is about getting things done, with little-to-no emphasis on whether those things should be done, and a lot of emphasis on applying tried-and-trusted methods to get those things done._

Yep. I went through that schooling also. But like with most things there is a bell curve. There are those who totally don't give a damn about the "should". There are those who don't care about the quality of their work, just give me a paycheck. And in school we had that one guy who had cheated his way to within 2 classes of a Masters in Elec Eng before it blew up. I don't know what happened to him. And not all clumps of engineers organize by merit. Just like in the rest of the "real" world.

But to H's point. Run by engineers can be fine. Or go off the rails totally. Just in different ways from other types of "in charge".

**747:**

Because the 8mm nut is the only part anyone cares about?

Seems unlikely, I admit. I don't have an MGB and have never particularly cared about them. If I had one and cared about it, I probably would replace most of its internal bits, and the new bits would have new metricated fastenators holding it together.

If you have an MGB and care about it, then I'm sure you have its maintenance sorted as you see fit. As to why the mixing of fasteners, my 1995 Chevy s10 pickup not only had mix-and-mismatch fasteners but also a cursed combination of OBD-I and OBD-II components, which didn't comply with either spec. 

At least it didn't have carburetors...

**749:**

_Sadly the Texicans are not doing their stuff for humor, more likely because the Book Burning Committee isn't allowed to ban nonfiction yet._

Um, point of order. Call them Texans, please. Given the history of Mexico, Texas, and the US, it's more than a little insulting to link Mexicans with Texans as Texicans.

The tl;dr is that Mexico outlawed slavery in IIRC 1838. This led to American slaveholders in the Mexican state of Tejas (it had a slightly different name) to secede from Mexico, flail around for awhile, then beg the US to take them in as a slaveholding state. In 1845, the US offered to buy a big chunk of northern Mexico, including what's now California, Arizona, New Mexico, and the rest of Texas, because Mexico really wasn't developing it, they'd just owned it for most of 300 years. When Mexico refused, the US invaded, took Mexico City, and forced Mexico to give those lands to the US. This left Mexico bankrupt and with no functioning government, leading in turn to about 30 years of civil war and conquest by France at one point for nonpayment of debt, because whoever the Mexican government always bankrupted the country while trying to stabilize it.

So if you want to be insulting, just call them Texans.

The election has started.

As of last night, Wednesday October 23, About 23 million votes have been cast nation wide. (million = 1^6) This is the rough total of mail in ballots received plus early voting.

Given the total votes in 2020 and some rough extrapolation, there will likely be 160 million or so votes cast.

So by Monday there's a good chance that about 20% of the eventual votes will have been cast. Note that in 2020 100 million or so were in before "election day". But then again 2020 was a weird year.

My wife and I did early voting yesterday. Most of my friends will vote early.

Besides, one could argue that effective politicians are in fact "social engineers". Which illustrates the central problem: nobody understands the extremely dynamic systems of equations that might explain how societies work. What, start with the Lotka-Volterra model and build on that? A whole lot of society seems to be about predator-prey relationships.

But a "real" engineer still wouldn't know what to do. Though it's a tempting idea. Engineering is largely about finding optimal solutions to multiply constrained problems, so if we only knew what the constraints actually were, and what a solution actually looked like, maybe...

**806:**

levity leaven @ 747:

_Because the 8mm nut is the only part anyone cares about?_

No, it's just an artifact. Twin carburetors have to be synchronized. The SU carburetors and linkage were sourced as a unit from SU ... the bolts that held the SU carburetors to the manifold are SAE standard. Later single carburetor cars didn't require synchronization.

_Seems unlikely, I admit. I don't have an MGB and have never particularly cared about them. If I had one and cared about it, I probably would replace most of its internal bits, and the new bits would have new metricated fastenators holding it together._

_If you have an MGB and care about it, then I'm sure you have its maintenance sorted as you see fit. As to why the mixing of fasteners, my 1995 Chevy s10 pickup not only had mix-and-mismatch fasteners but also a cursed combination of OBD-I and OBD-II components, which didn't comply with either spec._

_At least it didn't have carburetors..._

The MGB was designed for export to the U.S. from the very start. The designers made the (I think wise) decision to spec it in SAE just so the purchasors could have the tools to work on them **and therefore they'd sell a lot more of them in the target market** ... unlike say the Morris Minor, which I think was English Whitworth (it was NOT SAE and it was NOT Metric, so no one over here could work on the damn things). 

Another interesting "mix & match" metric/SAE was the Ford Pinto - the German made engine was SAE while the U.S. assembled body was Metric. 😏

Be that as it may, I was simply `_**refudiating**_` your contention that _"SAE measurements are for machinery you don't care about"_

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8141)

[gasdive (he, him, ia) ](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4107) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199852) | [October 24, 2024 21:07](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199900)

**811:**

"I’m, not sure who you’re trying to target with that screed,but Elon Musk is currently Trump’s biggest financial supporter and is funding his get out the vote effort."

People are saying that I should avoid buying a product because buying it supports a company that has a bad person in it. 

Sounds like a fine concept, but the level of support that's too much is seeing some adverts. That's a very low level. I haven't actually given them any money. 

So why this one company, and this one person? There's not much evidence that this person is any worse than about 50% of the population of the USA (that's the percentage that also support Trump), and given their contributions to electrification, you could make the case they're better for the planet than 99.999% of people. 

So if you're going to attack me for insufficient purity, which other companies are you making sure you don't support, even indirectly. 

Do you avoid all companies that use or sell Nestle products? Nestle is _indisputably_ worse. So nearly all restaurants and all supermarkets. I'm assuming you grow all your own food, or walk barefoot to a farm for your food.

Do you avoid all companies that use fossil fuels, and so support Russia, (and Putin) Shell and British Petroleum? So you get your food from a very special farm that doesn't have a tractor, use steel fencing wire and so on. 

Do you enquire about the morals of the parents of the proprietors of a lemonade stand before supporting them?

My point is that this sort of moral purity is impossible, and applying it to only one individual, that I'm not convinced is even worse than a median human, is a moral panic. It's just fashionable to hate him in particular.

If it floats your boat to not use Twitter, go right ahead, but if you want to imply I'm a bad person because I haven't joined you, maybe get the log out of your eye first.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4107)

**824:**

<https://kevinbonham.blogspot.com/2024/10/the-commonest-arguments-for-opv-are.html>

Australian election geekery featuring the very relevant line _There is a lot of resistance to understanding the concept of helping a lesser evil beat a greater one._

I recall in the past on this blog talking about this in the context of requiring absolute perfection as a condition of accepting a view people disagreed with, or from someone of a disliked class, vs accepting/supporting anyone doing good things for whatever value of good seemed relevant. Sure, that was about whether Greta Thunberg is a shrill annoying woman to be shouted down or someone doing good things who should be supported, but there's a general principle hiding inside the discussion.

IMO it's reasonable to do the same with opposition. You might not care about astronomy, worker's rights, whether wealth trickles down or gets sucked up, but maybe you have a preference for democracy over authoritarian government. In which case you might decide that actively supporting the authoritarian is something you don't want to do. Or you might decide that until there is an absolutely perfect democracy to support you don't have a preference. Netanyahu is just as good as Mette Frederiksen...

<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mette_Frederiksen>

**826:**

Yes you should stop buying products from companies run by unpleasant people. I don’t go out of my way to uncover these people but if I become aware of them I do my best to avoid buying any of their products unless there’s a very good reason to buy them. So for over 40 years I’ve avoided buying anything from a Murdoch company. No satellite TV, no Collins books, no Times or Sun. There are probably some I don’t know about but if I learn about more Murdoch companies I’ll avoid their products too. I know this won’t collapse the Murdoch empire but I feel better. There are other things I try to avoid: Tate and Lyle products, palm oil and others I won’t bore you with. I don’t boycott all companies using fossil fuels. But I have an electric car. I abandoned my account with X but I never really saw the point of twitter so it wasn’t much of a hardship.

Well, there's some questions here and they're not related to blurring the lines between businesses and parties.

It was pointed out that I'm morally reprehensible for supporting space Karen by seeing adverts on their website. 

So the questions are why is Space Karen so bad that you'd bother to tell someone they're bad for supporting them to the tune of single digit cents? The answer to that seem to be they support the other political party and that party is bad. No other particularly heinous things seem to be mentioned.

So that leads to the questions, Why is this other party so much worse than the current one? The answer to that seems to be that it might become a dictatorship. It didn't last time, but this time it will. Oh, and it will be even less supportive for Ukraine than the current admin which has been slow walking support, just enough to keep the conflict going and give both sides hope of victory. This seems to be fine tuned to maximise the number of conscripts killed and weapons sold. 

And given that this party is so bad, why are we dog piling upon this particular group of companies, and boycotting their products, but not all the other companies that have people in them that are also supporting this other party. The answer to that question seems to be........ Crickets.

So if you look at the top 5 donors to the stolen election campaign that resulted in Jan 6 insurrection we get: 

National Beer Wholesalers Association Political Action Committee

American Bankers Association PAC (BANKPAC)

National Automobile Dealers Association Political Action Committee

AT&T

The National Rural Electric Cooperative Association Action Committee For Rural Electrification (ACRE)

So I'm assuming that along with avoiding Twitter our American cousins are avoiding beer, money, telephones, cars sold through dealerships (ie every car that isn't a Tesla) and electricity outside cities.

If you want to go further down the list, you'll find people avoiding houses built by builders, people who shop at Home Depot, fly on airlines that use Boeing planes, (or Lockheed Martin, but there's not many LM airliners left), get parcels delivered by UPS, eat beef, wear cotton and so on. 

<https://accountable.us/projects/corporate-donations-tracker/>

**836:**

Explain like you are five. Ok.

Not everything is simple. Many things are complicated, and have multiple elements to them. Sometimes grown ups need to make choices between two options, neither of which are perfect, because there isn't a perfect option.

In my opinion, one of the two leading candidates for the US presidency will seek to preserve what is good about the world and hopefully improve on steps taken to do that. The other leading candidate has expressly stated that he will put a stop to all efforts to mitigate climate change and other grownup, complex issues. 

That other leader has also stated that he will continue the repression of women, subjugation of GLBTQ persons, and begin mass deportations of immigrants on the scale of millions. None of which has been expressed as a goal of the first candidate. This is not projection, this is taken directly from his statements of intent.

Now, about the Israeli slaughter in Gaza and now Lebanon. I'm opposed to it. I'd like it to stop, 40 years ago if possible. Given that isn't possible, today would be fine. I see little chance of it happening if Trump is elected, and slightly more chance of it happening if Harris is elected. On the other hand, both of them are running for leadership of a country that (this is important) is NOT ISRAEL. Yes, there is support going to Israel that should stop, but given the actions of both parties on that front I see little difference in likely outcomes.

I'd like to see that change. Given that the former president moved the US Embassy to Jerusalem in a blatant pander to the extremists who are currently propagating the ongoing slaughter in Gaza, and given that his son-in-law has been musing about property development post-depopulation of the region, I have little hope that he will do the right thing. Also, he has never done the right thing in his life.

As for Ukraine, I have only ever heard people claim that the US is 'slow-walking' support to Ukraine, I've never seen a shred of evidence that is actually happening. I do know that Russia invaded a sovereign nation, and has been quite happy to bomb and murder civilians in their thousands in pursuit of its goals. I disapprove. However, the former president appears to approve, or at least appears wholly unable to express any disapproval whatsoever of that invasion. 

Final thing. Dilbert Stark's support of one party in the presidential election is merely the 85th checkmark on the list of why I don't and won't support him if I can avoid it. Like many people, I began being impressed with his accomplishments and have been slowly backing away in astonishment for several years now. He's a jerk, he is anti-union, hostile to non-white persons, hostile to GLBTQ persons and I just see no reason to support him. His association with the genuinely vile person running for office recently is more icing on that shitcake, in my opinion.

**837:**

I think the problem is that you've been receiving some careless information. Yes, the Biden administration has given Israel money and arms. _So has every American administration going back to Truman._ This isn't because the Biden Administration is bad or good, (pick one and check the box carefully) but because not supporting Israel is a super-bad idea if any American politician wants to remain in office. So Biden is not giving Israel a different level of support than other presidents have.

The 'careless' nature of the information you're being given is that _only Biden_ has given Israel money while they made war. In face, every U.S. President including Trump has supported Israel no matter how horrible that country has been.

The difference between Harris and Trump is that Trump is remarkably prejudiced against women, Blacks, LGBTQ people, hispanics, Jews, etc., and is incompetent at being anything but a mob boss, while Harris has actual government experience and will tend progressive whenever she has a chance to do so. This doesn't mean she's gonna be great - she'll have the same business ties as any American Pol - but that she'll be substantially better than Trump.

As for 'explaining it to a five-year-old:' The difference between major evil (Trump) and the lesser of two evils (Harris) is a lot of evil!

Veering somewhat further off topic, but relevant to the theme that occasionally pops up here about trusting trust, per Ken Thompson: [Removal of Russian coders spurs debate about Linux kernel’s politics](https://arstechnica.com/information-technology/2024/10/russian-coders-removed-from-linux-maintainers-list-due-to-sanction-concerns). 

I realise there are ways to reduce the risk of direct code injection attacks in open source communities, but there's a space between lower risk and no risk. the international realpolitik of an American suburbia cliche, an eight year old girl known to be in her horse-madden phase demanding a pony for her birthday, but willing to settle for a new bicycle

the threat of massively attacking Iran ought to be a very serious, very detailed bluff, to force everyone into the mode of talking + threatening + bribing the combatants into backing away along with cooling the active 'hot war' in Gaza, etc

but what exactly is Benjamin Netanyahu's intent? his 'end game? what is he aiming for?

other than grasping tightly to power for just one more day and then tomorrow doing it again...?

Gaza has already been wrecked

any with the opportunity will refugee out to just about =anywhere= so over coming months there will be yet another million-plus penniless refugees scattered across Europe and Asia and every other elsewhere

this, at the same time there are Ukrainians and Russians doing the same... alongside dozens of other groups... 

not just climate change shitstorm... war-torn failed states each producing yet more refugee waves

right there is a labor surplus if only there was a way to offset job openings with refugees... but... no education... no expertise... no shared language... 

{ 30 minutes of cat videos, now, right now }

[ilya187](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1357) | [October 25, 2024 13:37](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199939)

**847:**

One of the strange attractors of this blog is the housing shortage -- globally, or at least in the Anglosphere. Here is one attempt at solving it:

<https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/personalfinance/real-estate/2024/10/22/empty-offices-become-tiny-apartments-housing-crisis/75784459007/>

TL;DR: Back to century-old idea of an apartment building with tiny individual living spaces, and shared kitchens, bathrooms and shower facilities. The proposal is not to build them form scratch, but convert existing office buildings. Their layout of plumbing hookups (usually in the center of the floor) makes this kind of conversion much easier than trying to split an office floor into "traditional" apartments, each with its own bathroom and kitchen.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

**848:**

_The biggest problem is successful "technical people" tend to feel they can apply their smarts to everything. Just plain everything._

I met many of those when I worked as an engineer. 

I was close to being one myself, but I'd had my nose rubbed too many times in things I _couldn't_ do to fall all the way into that trap. If social media had been a thing back then and I could have hung out online exclusively with people who convinced me that the things I couldn't do weren't important, on the other hand, I'd likely be a believer in the superiority of _Homo technica_.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

[Robert Prior](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199885) | [October 25, 2024 14:27](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2199941)

**849:**

_she realizes that moving it a bit at a time is better than "glorious defeat"_

I met with my MP last week for a chat, and he kept repeating "politics is the art of compromise" when we talked about policies. 

Consider pharmacare, which is new up here. It currently covers diabetes medication and birth control, which produces the greatest good for the greatest number with the least cost. Plans are in train to expand it to other medications, but that hasn't stopped people who _should_ be supporting it (because it will eventually cover their meds) being vocally opposed to it because it didn't cover their uncommon condition right out the gate. (And of course after the next election who knows what will happen to it. There's opposition to supporting birth control from the right-wing, who will almost certainly form our next government absent something like our two leftish parties merging.*)

*There's precedent for that in Canadian politics, from the other side. At both provincial and federal level the right-wing party has split and later recombined after losing the election (because of FPTP vote splitting), each time with the righter-wing secessionists ending up in control of the recombined party. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6643)

**851:**

_Unfortunately, the Democrats really are the lesser of two evils._

Kinda like how I feel about the Liberals up here. I work with them and voted for them, because in my riding with FPTP the choices are Liberal, Conservative, or waste your vote and I _don't_ want the Conservatives to get in.

On the bright side my (Liberal) MP is a decent chap who is good enough at politics to get Emancipation Day declared unanimously by the House of Commons, and who supports social programs like dental care and pharmacare and housing. 

_It's a bit like being on a hockey team with Wayne Gretsky, except that the dude is all-potential and never comes to practice_

That would be the evil Gretsky who wears a goatee, right? Because what made the actual Gretsky such an amazing player was not just his technical skills (which were good) but the way he supported the entire team. Where other NHL stars would take a shot on goal themselves, he would pass to a teammate in a better position rather than hogging the spotlight. At practices he worked with other players to build skills and plays. When Gretsky was on a team _everyone_ played better.

(Not that I'm a hockey fan, but he was unusual enough in that regard that even I heard about it.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

**857:**

I'm kinda with gasdive here. I very well understand that Democrats are the lesser evil. But to me the idea that immediately after the election the genocide will stop is wishful thinking.

For example, do you think the refusal to let the uncommitted movement to speak at the DNC is evidence of the Democratic party agreeing with them and wanting to implement their proposals but pretending not to so as not to spook donors/voters? Or is it evidence that the faction of the Democrats that holds power, and that Harris is very much a part of, is not interested in stopping the genocide? Do you notice that every story about Israel using 2000 pound bombs, such as those used in Lebanon to kill Nasrallah, mentions they are supplied by the US? The US could, and did for a few months, stop sending them. They could do much more. But I am starting to think that the theory that they are using this opportunity to remake the region (cf Adam Tooze) is very persuasive. It's the comeback of early 00's Neoconservatives. Have you noticed how they are coming out with endorsements of Harris?

So, yes, lesser evil. But don't credit them with good intentions they do not hold.

I think the analogy to Labor winning this year is very instructive. Let us know when they fix the NHS, or whatever other good thing it was hoped they would do once in power.

**868:**

In a lot of places the housing crisis is more accurately an income crisis. A lot of people just don't have enough income, so they have bad housing, bad food, bad whatever else costs money. Those places do tend to have a surfeit of billionaires though, so on average everything is fine. I suspect that even cities with a shortage of homes actually have enough area-of-homes for each person to have a small apartment, but again that's because they have an excessive number of very large mansions and very little social housing or even 'affordable housing' (because modern language is _awesome_ at euphemism)

Cory mentioned a book on the topic the other day, but all the usual 'income inequality' experts have broadly similar opinions: <https://pluralistic.net/2024/10/24/i-dream-of-gini/>

And then you have the fun combo of reasonably successful business people getting involved in politics for that reason, then showing that they are not as omnicompetent as they think they are. **870:**

Remember, office building converted into tiny apartments can be spelled “tenement,” while an area of tiny homes with minimal services can be spelled “slum.”

We’ve got four interlocking problems that I’m aware of. One is a general loathing for tenements and slums among planners. I get their concerns, but the amenities they insist must be included in any development have on occasion driven the price per unit too high to be affordable. 

A second issue is that homeowners are forced to consider their home their chief asset, so they have to defend the estimated value of the home, which includes what’s going on in the neighborhood around it. Like densification. Yes, it’s middle class greed, until you realize that they don’t want to be homeless either.

A third problem is that many units have been bought up for short term rentals, which takes them out of the housing stock. Investment companies get blamed a lot for this, but ordinary people do it too.

A fourth problem is that real estate investors have often demanded a certain, high level of return on investment in order to finance projects, and that usually means high end homes built on undeveloped land. That market is glutted and useless for affordable homes, but it’s still a problem.

Soaking the rich will help ameliorate these problems, but do will a magic wand. Neither of these is easy to get. Letting slums and tenements be built is an easy short term solution that causes intractable infrastructure and epidemiological problems later on. What a mess.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3431)

A land grab excused by need for a widened buffer zone? Encouraging mass waves of starving refugees to bug out in all directions? Openly attacking a long time adversary who'd been exploiting proxies?

Yeah. All of that.

I'm looking at the current politically defined map of the Middle East and have not a clue for how this roiling mess will be settled short of all out-n-out war. 

After there's a declared winner in the upcoming American election, there will be changes announced by various players in the Middle East.

**878:**

_Non technical people are factory workers, food sales and prep people, electricians, and all kinds of others._

You remind of the poor bugger who had to teach a bunch of university graduates that "electricians have no occupational health and safety training" as part of a basic safe to enter a construction site course. The curriculum and resulting test were designed by folk with your level of knowledge of their chosen subject.

_"Technical" isn't a good word but I don't know a better one_

I think you mean "rich". You definitely don't mean educated since there are lots of successful businesspeople without university degrees, and quite a few who appear not to be very intelligent (even if we define success as _making_ money rather than losing it)

My point is that they're claiming that their qualification is being good at business. I'm trying to make a distinction between them and people who are merely rich, or well born, or whatever. On the one hand you have "I ran a corporation, I can run the country" claims, which are distinct from the Trump-style "I am rich, I should be in charge" or the Boris/Bush type "I am your natural ruler" (Jacob Rees-Moggs is the cliche aspirational example of this)

**890:**

_After the collapse of 2008, investors moved heavily into rental properties. I see that currently, they own about 33% of all rental homes._

And there is a strong correlation between investors moving into an area and unaffordability.

Correlation is not causation and all that, but there are respected analysts who have studied the connection. Here (Canada), REITs are incentivized to maximize tenant turnover (no limit to rent increases between tenants) and minimize maintenance. 

For example:

<https://www.taxfairness.ca/en/resources/reports/how-tax-breaks-are-worsening-canadas-housing-affordability-crisis>

<https://share.ca/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/SHARE-State-of-the-Sector-EN.pdf>

<https://rabble.ca/politics/canadian-politics/long-term-care-reits-fueling-the-housing-crisis-in-canada/>

<https://www.efmaefm.org/0EFMAMEETINGS/EFMA%20ANNUAL%20MEETINGS/2022-Rome/papers/EFMA%202022_stage-3032_question-Full%20Paper_id-190.pdf>

From the last paper: "Building on the dramatic rise in REITs valuation in the last decade, we ask whether housing markets exposure to financial market dynamics is exacerbated by REITs. We find that REITs push up house prices significantly around the world. The effect is stronger in advanced economies, where REITs have grown the most, and in market-based pension systems. REITs channel foreign investors in domestic markets and represent a transmission channel for both domestic and global financial shocks into housing markets."

This may not be what we want, but it's what the system we have designed does.

Oh, and in response to whoever talked about economists having the answers, Dan Davies (an economist) asserts that they most definitely _don't_ , because they lack the data and the training to interpret it. (Reference: _The Unaccountability Machine_ , which is an interesting read even if I'm struggling with the cybernetics bits. That's Stafford Beer's cybernetics, not any computer stuff.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229)

_What should I do and why?_

BY a 20 year-old used car from a repair shop or online. The major companies often have stopped making parts, but there are still after-market suppliers making them. If the problem is the car companies supporting fascists, reusing their products rather than buying new is a way to rebel.

And before you sneer, I've got family members who specialize in older cars. This is how they do it. 

Also, Tesla has dealerships, so that won't work. I wouldn't buy a Tesla anyway, given the number of lawsuits, recalls, manufacturing defeats, and various lawsuits they're tangled in. Leon Stunk really messed them up at the end. The entire fleet of Cybertrucks has been recalled twice this years, IIRC. 

If you have the money, go to one of the smaller companies, buy a Volvo or other foreign make with appropriate politics, etc.

Anyway, I thought you were in Australia. Buy whatever's being marketed there that's not 'Murrican owned and suitable for your needs, and stop bothering me about it.

**896:**

"So I don't want to buy a car that's passed through the hands of a dealer, or requires I buy parts from a dealer."

"BY a 20 year-old used car from a repair shop or online."

So your solution to a difficult question is to just reframe the question. I got a hard time for supporting someone who supports Trump to the tune of about 5 cents a year. The old Volvo has been through a dealership, and by supporting the second hand sales of cars that have been through a dealer I'm inflating the second hand value which incentivises people to buy new through a dealer. So cars like the Volvo are out of the question. 

This is not a hard question. 

Ask me a question about a subject I'm passionate about and I'm delighted to fill you in. There's clearly a lot of people on here who are extremely passionate about hating Space Karen. Yet none of them can articulate why. (I'm ignoring the lunatic reasons like he wants to become king of the world) 

The responses are basically "he supports the team I don't, and that's enough" (but we're not going to boycott anyone else who does the same for reasons that the internet is too small to contain but which are quite elegant) or "I would tell you but as you asked, you're not worthy of a response" which is pretty much the distillation of your response.

If memory serves, the only one who has ever articulated a reason they don't like him is Greg who likes trains and hates him because he built a subway but didn't put trains in it. A reason I can understand, even if I don't agree with it (because if it had trains it would have been too expensive and would never have been built)

**897:**

<https://nebula.tv/videos/occ-why-ethical-consumerism-is-a-trap>

"Ethical Consumerism is a Trap" from Our Changing Climate, which I assume is also on youtube. Covers a lot of the greenwashing/ethicswashing side as well as the more brutal problem of consuming your way out of overconsumption.

Which does not mean that the negative activism of not buying things is pointless, but that 'encouraging' Amazon by shopping at Whole Foods instead of your local farm coop is misguided.

**900:**

Slipped the subject again. Why I don’t like Musk:

—He buys tech (cars and rockets especially) that others invented, then claims to have invented it.

—His inventions are often junk: the cybertruck (to be fair, he didn’t design all of it, but the others are even less his designs), the boring company and its flamethrowers. 

—-he cuts corners in production, leading to car steering wheels that fall off, and cybertruck body panels and brake pedals that fall off. IIRC his labor problems stem from similar issues.

—he gets bored and stops innovation. The cybertruck is their last new design, while the model 3 was from what, 2017? Nothing new on the horizon. See also how he went from genuine innovation in electrification (Tesla, powerwalls, battery power plants, the Tesla car charging network) to now making common cause with the fossil fuel industry and leaving Tesla to wither.

— he jumps into things he doesn’t understand and causes problems. See Twitter and his current PAC for Trump, which is trying to figure out how to do a get out the vote campaign two weeks before the election. 

—He has problems with relationships. See all his exes, children, relationship with California, the reported need at Tesla to have a dedicated staff (since departed) to keep him on task and from causing proble with genius ideas that weren’t.

—he appears unusually susceptible to propaganda and cannabinoids. All of us are, but a wise man knows his limitations. It’s entirely possible to be ethically problematic without getting red-pilled. 

So basically he’s turned from someone who could have been one of civilization’s great heroes in the fight on climate change to getting suckered into helping Trump. As the book title says, everything Trump touches dies (written by a Republican, not a democrat). so if Trump wins, Musk gets to follow The Way of Giuliani all the way to the loss of reputation, career, and fortune at the end. IMHO, the best thing that could happen to him starts with Trump losing and ends with him flushing most of his fortune before he gets over his midlife crisis and manages the redemptive final act Giuliani couldn’t pull off. 

And yes, that’s the short version. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4107)

**911:**

_There's clearly a lot of people on here who are extremely passionate about hating Space Karen. Yet none of them can articulate why._

I'm not certain I care enough about the Muskrat to hate him. Extreme dislike, certainly, but not enough passion for hate.

I _am_ passionate about loving and supporting my nieces, however, and the Muskrat has repeatedly made statements attacking who they are and denying that they have the same rights the rest of us do. (And yes, I count a retweet of a homophobic/etc post with the tag "interesting" as support.) That is a good enough reason to dislike him.

I'm also a mandated reporter for child abuse, and I know that most abuse is by parents or close family members. The Muskrat got his panties in such a twist about a law that protects children from parental abuse that he moved SpaceX in protest. That's another good reason to dislike him.

<https://www.advocate.com/news/elon-musk-companies-california-texas>

He's a grandstanding blowhard who's using his bully pulpit to pander to the people who deny that my nieces have human rights. Is that articulate enough?

**930:**

I agree that there’s an existential struggle going on, but it’s between the Enlightenment and the Dark Enlightenment. Or, to put it less mysteriously, it’s the latest iteration of rule by authoritarian strongmen who want to organize themselves in dominance hierarchies, and democracies, who want to rule themselves in groups organized by laws and norms. This shit has been going on since before civilizations first kicked off, and this is the latest iteration.

Note that the struggle is a Red Queen race, not static. Fascism grew out of colonial slavery, which grew out of feudalism, for example.

In this struggle, nation-states and corporations are the forms, norms, and tools of the people struggling, not the entities that are struggling themselves. Putin and Musk are the critical entities, not Russia and X. Each of those men control more than just the obvious entities.

One huge reason to be anti-authoritarian at the moment is that we’re stuck in a polycrisis of our own making. There isn’t a simple solution to it other than nuclear war, and it’s vastly too complicated for a single god-king to solve it or even to top-down organize a solution. Again, except for nuclear war. Tanya ability the Great Men have to help solve it is degraded further when they get caught up in maintaining their power within their dominance hierarchy, because any solution that weakens them, even in the short term, can’t be used.

I’m not saying democracy will necessarily solve all our problems (hah!). However, getting a lot of people working on differing approaches at different levels, in different regions, is how we’re going to have to deal with this mess. This can’t happen if all our energy is sucked into billionaire/autocrat dominance duels.

My 0.02 cents.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6229)

**934:**

Re: '... he appears unusually susceptible to propaganda and cannabinoids.'

My impression was that he didn't use cannabinoids (THC/weed). He mentioned using ketamine for depression about once a week in an interview earlier this year but I don't think he mentioned the dosage. In the same interview he also mentioned working 16-hour days. Sleep deprivation is not healthy for body or mind: 33% increase in dementia, increased risk taking, depression, fuzzy thinking, etc. Ketamine and sleep deprivation: not a healthy combo at any age. 

Ketamine toxicity includes:

'Neurological – altered mental status (disorientation), paranoia, dysphoria, anxiety, confusion, slurred speech, dizziness, ataxia, dysarthria, trismus, muscular rigidity, psychomotor, psychomimetic, or acute dystonic reactions'

<https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK541087/>

How it works: per the article below, ketamine remodels the endocannabinoid receptors in the brain - mouse study. 

<https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-19780-z>

Ketamine is used in hospitals so check with your local hospital pharmacist (spouse) for details.

**941:**

Twitter is poison.

It was always garbage, but back in the days before ubiquitous availability of SMS gateways, it served a temporarily valuable purpose. It was deliberately made as addicting as possible, to "maximize engagement" and sell ads.

People can become addicted to any old thing. It's a wierd quirk of human psyche. There's no shame in it. However, if you are an adult, it's something you are responsible to look out for, and correct in yourself if you can. Otherwise it will diminish your ability to take care of your other responsibilities.

I think some folks here might be addicted to Twitter. (I will not call it "x", that is a deliberate namespace collision engineered by a scummy troll.) I think defending addiction sometimes leads to denial, motivated reasoning, and generally showing one's ass in conversation.

I would like for some folk who have been thread-sitting here to consider these ideas and maybe take a little time-out. Touch some grass, or maybe go for a dive if that's your thing. 

Personally, I'm a go do some druuuuuugs

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8829)

[levity leaven](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8829) | [October 27, 2024 01:57](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200035)

**942:**

Another thing: "technical" people work with tools, using techniques. Tools and techniques are "techne", that's what "technical" fields are about. Electricians, plumbers, mechanics, janitors, cooks all are "technical" in this way. Most jobs are, since relatively few jobs involve no use of tools.

The "better word" the commenter above is looking for is "professional", I think. Professionals have degrees or certifications, dress in a "professional" manner at work, and are usually governed by professional trade associations. Many (most?) professional people are also technical people, but not economists, political advisers, astrologers or haruspices. Instead of using tools, these folks are themselves tools.

Also, lots of people call themselves or others "engineers", even when the folk in question neither manage an engine nor are bonded. A "real engineer" in my (unpublished, non-authoritative) book is on the hook if something he signed-off on fails. 

This has been your pedantic semantic prescription

NOW TALK RIGHT gee

**943:**

I've yet to see any evidence that a society without a healthy middle class is capable of accomplishing much of anything other than autocratic games of thrones, at least beyond the lifetime of the occasional charismatic and brutal autocrat who manages to get everyone settled for a little while. 

We are going into a massive population drop, one way or another. Rapidly declining birth rates are a really good way to do it (currently happening in many countries with functioning middle classes). Massive population crashes due to famine, war and disease are the other option - so far more of a thing in top-down autocracies.

We can all see our population shrink or crash. I would like to see a graceful shrinking.

**946:**

Japan has been doing it for a couple of generations. A low birth rate, declining population and a correspondingly 'stagnant' economy. Which has caused economists to wring their hands and talk about Japan's 'struggles', meanwhile their productivity continues to rise, their population shrinks and their standard of living is pretty decent.

There are obviously some unique cultural elements that are connected to that process, they have ~3500 years of cultural continuity to work with and use as social glue. But there are and will be parallels in any other country that is going through the same thing.

The US, Canada, UK and Europe have been using immigration to offset falling birth rates and continue 'economic growth', but of course that does get all the morons upset for all the usual reasons.

**968:**

_Educate women and allow free access to contraceptives._

Lots of people _want_ children, but only if they can practically do so. There's some evidence that structuring society so that everyone has to work all the time (called "increase GDP at all costs") means that having children is very expensive. If you can maintain a deeply sexist culture while doing that even the most idiotic of women will realise that the worthless scutwork of raising children will fall on them and that sucks. South Korea has done this very effectively.

But even without extreme sexism, just making homes unaffordable and reducing job security reduces the number of people who feel able to have children. When "can have children" means either voluntary poverty or being rich enough to buy a home _and_ hire a nanny for the first year or three. That's not universal, obviously some people will be rich enough and others are already so poor that having kids doesn't make them significantly worse off (many right wing governments attempt to the fix the latter, with varying degrees of success)

But with the right economic incentives you can drop the birth rate from 2-ish to one-ish while getting that sugar high of GDP growth as a generation of women enter the workforce. What happens later is a problem for some other government.

**977:**

_Lots of people want children, but only if they can practically do so._

If you look at a graph the American birth rate over time you can see that the baby boom 'after' WWII actually started during the war, and was preceded by a baby bust during the Depression. And the 'excess' children born during the boom is about the same as the number of 'missing' children during the bust. It seems that people deferred having children until they could afford them.

**978:**

I still love how the Marie Stopes organisation in Australia didn't feel any need to change their name until well into the current century. I mean, sure she's not as notorious as certain other famous eugenicists, but at the same time "best known Australian {category}" is something that people in Australia do tend to be aware of. Especially in context. **981:**

"People can become addicted to any old thing. It's a wierd quirk of human psyche. There's no shame in it. However, if you are an adult, it's something you are responsible to look out for, and correct in yourself if you can. "

In that case, maybe thought patterns themselves can be self- reinforcing. Which would help explain why even creepy or disgusting mental images can be hard to shake sometimes, good or bad content makes no difference as long as they elicit a neurotransmitter surge. Like the reason why attendance at horror films depends on the scare phenomenon kicking off fight or flight reaction with its attendant adrenalin squirt, systemically that feels like a gain for some evolutionary reason.

At microscopic scale the neuron bundles passing along the troublesome message probably register nothing but a certain chemical intensity, and must have been naturally preset to accept any old surge as reinforcement. At a larger scale an unwanted thought may indeed trip off a sensation of unpleasantness, but that's already too far downstream of the initial stimulus, and too late to stop it. Worst of all would be a self defeating spiral of ever increasing disturbance, with attempts by higher cognitive function to suppress the offending pattern, which if left to its own devices keeps repeating until fatigue sets in, or the available reserve of neurotransmitters gets depleted below threshold levels. 

My experience suggests a feedback loop which is best stopped not through blockage or signal interruption, but by curtailment of the fuel source the pattern feeds on, whether it's glucose, atp, dopamine or something else that gets kicked off at a cellular level, I can sort of identify a mental hunger for the chemical mix involved, but it needs a repeating step of conscious recognition to keep the loop going. 

That's where mental leverage may be helpfully deployed, by shortstopping the reward mechanism. This is a handwavy description of a nebulous conjecture, but I find it easier to imagine the process as similar to circuitry, with certain wires heating up from excess current, which can be reduced by dampening the upstream energy supply. Failure to do so can breed what amounts to an internal parasite, but it's just an unfortunate pattern that wastes brain resources, like the way a protein molecule can fold into unusable or malignant shape.

Eventually it may be possible for fmri scanners to image such a thesis-antithesis-synthesis procedure right down in the brain tissue, but until then we're on our own to inhibit the responses targeted by dogwhistle manipulations, whether from social interaction, political propaganda, commercials, psychological gaslighting or just our own bad habits of mental hygiene. We're all stimulus junkies at heart, and that's the big button advertisers like to push. Doesn't have to be destructive, but the potential is always there for exploitation unless prevented somehow.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4645)

**986:**

Greg,

In my time I have read over 100,000 UCAS forms, and admitted 7,500 students to read CS at Manchester. There were even a few US applications.

What you need to understand is that school education in the US is pretty much a States’ responsibility. And admission to a US university is primarily based on SATS (effectively a standardised IQ test), though some selective universities will insist on AP results in particular subjects. We used APs as a way to ensure a students’ subject knowledge, even though in the US they’re supposed to permit automatic progression for first year exams.

As to UK A-levels: there are better approaches. Specialising in just Maths, Advanced Maths and Physics and nothing else after the age of 16 means they have to re-learn essay writing when they get to uni.

As to better ways, I quite like the International Baccalaureate. Romania and Finland are also very very good.

**991:**

It obviously takes me about a thousand posts before I get my thoughts square on one of Charlie’s blog posts, but here goes…

A recent paper describes the gravitational waves generated by the Alcubbierre drive. <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.02466> Interestingly the stretch-factor is 10^-22 at 2Mpa. Our current gravity wave detectors (LIGO etc) have a sensitivity down to 10^ -23/sqrt(freq).

But the frequency range of our current detectors (10-2,000Hz) will not show a collapsing warp drive from a 1km starship in the Andromeda Galaxy, at the moment. That’d require operation at 300,000Hz.

So TL;DR, primitive monkeys capable of low earth orbit may be able to detect signs of an intergalactic kegger without outside assistance. What are the effects on human civilisation? Note we will know of aliens even though we cannot communicate with them. And even though they are in a totally different galaxy.

**1019:**

_What's your excuse for them not doing that when they had both houses and the presidency?_

The US system is NOT a parliamentary system where the majority coalition had mostly free rein. The president can't dictate to Congress what they should do. And when they try Congress tends to get its back up and takes the ball home until the President plays nice.

And without a 20+ vote majority in the House and 60 (66 at times) vote majority in the Senate the majority gets stuck with a lot of limits. And even in with those majorities, they have to wrangle all of those votes for EVERYTHING.

It is a different system. Intentionally so. Maybe it was a mistake. Maybe not. But controlling both houses and the Presidency but without large majorities just makes things easier then flat our hard.

PS: Most of us here on this blog may be dead before the Senate sees a 60 or 66 vote majority by either major party.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4107)

**1030:**

I'm beginning to see where you misunderstand things, so I'm going to try to tell you where you're wrong and where you're right.

You're right that Democrats have had 'majorities' several times recently and have had opportunities they've failed to take advantage of.

This is because the Democratic Party is basically under the control of Clinton holdovers who _wish to occupy the center_ and serve their less-horrible corporate masters - people who can see the need for a middle class and believe in equality for women, people of color, and LGBTQ+ folks, but still want lots of handouts from the federal government, including tax-cuts and generally anti-union behavior. (As opposed to the Republican billionaires who want to be treated as nobility while denying all rights to women, people of color, LGBTQ+ people.) These Clinton holdovers are desperately afraid that if they come out as, for example, pro-union that the press will call them commies and stuff. (It's not entirely rational.) _These Clintonistas have literally tried to kick democrats who are too-liberal out of their seats in Congress._

So the first big thing you're failing to understand is that the Democrats really aren't a single party and they don't represent a single group of voters. Further, each Democratic Senator or Congress-person has a very specific set of demographic challenges to overcome in order to be elected. So you end up with Democrats representing everything from very liberal and progressive cities, like AOC (Alexandra Ocasio Cortez) in New York, to people representing very conservative areas, such as Joe Manchin in the rural coal-mining areas of Virginia. Essentially, Joe Manchin, who owns a coal mine, would have been a Reagan Republican back in the day, (Reagan's policies are too-far left at this point get him elected in a Republican primary) while AOC is essentially a socialist.

Republicans have a much-more unified party. They're all either Christian Dominionists or racists, or they've been propagandized to think the Republican Party will defend them from the horrible 'wrong people.' (Christian Dominionism is already pretty racist, so it's easy for both factions to get along.) 'Moderate Republicans' are a dying breed who generally can't win their primary elections.

Obama was essentially a 'Reagan Democrat' and said so. Liberal Democrats who've successfully represented a Congressional District and are popular risk having the Democratic Party itself finance someone to run against them to the tune of millions of dollars. (After all, control of the party is at stake!) The average Democrat is socially-liberal and economically-conservative.

How this works on the ground is that AOC puts out a bill for a 'Green New Deal' which is basically intended to wind up the last problems with racism the Federal Government is capable of addressing, fix the U.S. Medical system, strengthen unions, etc., then once the inequalities have been addressed take on Climate Change as the major national issue.

And fellow Democrat Joe Manchin says, "Hey! I own a coal mine! And you're a commie! What do you mean you want nationalized health care? What the fuck is wrong with you on every issue you're discussing?" The Republicans don't even have to look up, because Joe will make sure AOC's bill never gets a vote!

The thing that unites Joe Manchin and AOC is their dislike of modern Republicans, who range from batshit-insane Christian nationalists (they literally believe that God has called upon them to dominate America) to straight-up Nazis. It's essentially twenty-percent of the nation, but they've got roughly fifty-percent of the voters due to their ability to frighten/propagandize poorly-educated people.

To add to the problems of Democrats, (and this is something else you misunderstand) the news media in the U.S. treats Democrats very differently than they treat Republicans. In Democratic circles the phrase "It's OK If Your A Republican" gets bandied around alot. For an example, notice how the press treats 'Clinton's emails' versus how they treat 'Trump's theft of national secrets from the White House.' So it's far riskier for a Democrat to take a particular position than a Republican. A Democrat who advocates for Roe V. Wade has the very real worry that their local paper will call them a "Baby-Killing Communist Satan Worshipper," while the same paper might not mention that their Republican opponent wants to forcibly convert every American the Christianity and shoot all the Gay people.

_It's NOT a parliamentary system, and it's currently rigged pretty-badly against liberal Democrats by some really scummy people, SOME OF WHOM CLAIM TO BE DEMOCRATS._

Ok, got it. 

You, personally, and people just like you, are what killed the Old Left in the US, the New Left, and what's happening right now. There is a saying, "the perfect is the enemy of the good", and you want perfection, or you'll take your bat and ball and go home. 

As opposed to, say, France, where what, two? three? times in the last 15 years, almost everyone on the left held their noses, and voted as a block to stop the fascists. **1046:**

If you look for informed opinions the USA often comes up as democratic, but not very. They'll categories it as "at risk democracy", "mostly democratic" or "limited democracy", because there are systematic problems at all levels. The USA still has archaic electoral systems (FPTP/2party etc), gerrymandering, a very limited franchise, partisan control of election processes, unlimited spending and influence efforts, very poor media, oh and also the explicitly antidemocratic electoral college.

It's come up here before and different people will point to different problems and say either "this is why the whole system is fucked" or "but this actually makes it _more_ democratic, not less" etc.

And per the ongoing "no perfect alternative so we have to support fascism" discussion, every democracy is imperfect in some way so fascism is inevitable. The imperfection has been proven by psephologists and disagreeing/being upset about it is generally a marker of ignorance. But but exactly the same token Godel showed that we can't have a coherent set of physical laws that cover everything... thus science is also pointless, as is logic, reason etc. Or maybe that original "without perfection, hell" statement is the part that's wrong. Who knows?

**1048:**

Great example of burying the lede in that last line. 

I’ll rephrase it: life is unsatisfactory, which is Buddha’s first noble truth and the one we can agree on, even if we don’t believe in reincarnation.

Rather than blathering about democracy though, let’s go by size of polity. There are five US-scale polities in the world: the US, EU, China, India, and Russia. Of these, the US is the smallest by population, the richest, most productive currently, and has had the longest history of continuity (since 1865, for us).

I’d suggest that all five systems have serious problems, exacerbated by two (Russia and China) trying to destroy two others (US and EU), with India playing fifth wheel. Thing is, we don’t have a lot of samples, or a long time series, to say what works best at this scale, the more so because we’re stupid enough to be pointlessly accelerating technology as we’re all doing.

So what works best? I’m guessing that authoritarian systems have a shorter mean time to failure than American democracy, but we’ll find out soon enough.

Anyway, politics is like poker, in that you play the hand you’re dealt as best you can, rather than refusing to play unless you have a winning hand. It’s also like poker in that there are a lot of variants, and whoever’s got the buck may try to change the game played for all sorts of reasons. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1312)

**1051:**

Fair enough. I was also going by nukes, but that does include Pakistan. 

I think the key question is how well governance scales, especially when there are big tech things, like Azi, space travel, and abyssal/internet backbone warfare, that a country has to be really big to engage in at all. 

For example, Aotearoa might have a really good democracy, but how much of that is because they’re a bit out of the way and not worth messing with? If size and location are irrelevant, then we should all adopt a kiwi style system, including China. If size and location do matter, then we have to be careful In arguing what the best democracy is, because it’s conditioned by factors that have nothing to do with democracy.

**1053:**

If you're going by nukes North Korea and Israel would like a word. They're both very proudly democratic. And have very large, very impressive whatever it is you're asking for. On the topic of nukes it's interesting that there are two well-publicised instances of individual Russians refusing to launch nukes but the USA claims that's never happened to them. Maybe authoritarianism has an unexpected upside?

_If size and location do matter, then we have to be careful_

There's also a lot of noise because the US is famously resistant to ideas suggested from outside. Partly that's empire in action, those outside the ruling centre are very aware of the centre, but it's easy for those in the centre to forget that those outside can do anything better. You could perhaps look at outside systems as small scale trial runs rather than proving the ideas can only work at that scale.

Talking about the USA as "working for a long time" is misleading: the 13 states might say that, but if you claim continuity past those changes then China is still authoritarian after a couple of thousand years. Using millennia instead "increasingly democratic for nearly a third of a millennia" doesn't sound very impressive. "increasingly" suggests that things might need to keep changing, when the whole objection is that "the same" system has been running for nearly 1/3 of a millennia... I guess US democracy means white property-owning men since that's what the founders intended?

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Greg Tingey](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200198) | [October 29, 2024 23:36](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200200)

**1054:**

_Assuming Trump goes down - by the polls, that's a big assumption, but, IIRC, the bigger/higher the turn-out, the worse it is for him, yes?_

As with most punditry, we're in a situation where there's a bit of an incentive for forecasters to be different from each other. After all, who needs to get paid for "I agree with Nate Silver" or whoever? So I've heard every single opinion, including the idea that the polls to date are full of shit. 

I find the last one somewhat believable, because I've hung up on any number of pollsters, or refused to respond to their texts. Why? Many are push-polls, not interested in data but in getting me to do something. So I don't respond to any polls, or indeed, any phone call I don't recognize.

The pollsters are perfectly aware of this problem, so they've been using models to try to deduce the opinions of noncommunicative punks like me, who appear to be in the majority of people polled these days. The pollsters claim their models are accurate, as one must. Are they? Or are they full of shit? 

We'll find out. As the truism goes, this is why we use elections to choose our leaders, not voluntary opinion polls. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298)

[Moz](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7298) | [October 29, 2024 23:43](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200202)

**1055:**

I'd also point out that Aotearoa has been democratic in the same sense that the USA has been, through a similar colonial history with wars and competing foreign powers etc, including recent challenges. But it has somehow managed to a: reform its electoral system; and b: fight off demands from much stronger powers that it change laws that had wide democratic support. The USA has not, AFAIK, resisted a far larger empire demanding obeisance in recent times?

I don't know _how_ the USA can reform its political system, I'm just pointing out that people who are so proud of how flexible and admirable it is might want to look at what it's doing right now and think about how to carry out desperately needed reforms. Quickly. Because one side of US politics has a plan to improve democracy in the originalist sense of "a small group of rich white men are in charge and disagreeing with them even from inside the group is unsafe".

Oh, and I suspect the English might like to have a word about how long your democracy has been around, partly "your continent hadn't even been discovered when we became a democracy" and a bit of "we gave you that system stop pretending to have invented it". The gap between "divine right of kings" and "do sign this bill, there's a chap, finding a new king is _such_ a palaver" is far greater than from "only owners get to vote in the colonies" to "it's not just owners that get to vote".

China's stability date is probably 1948-ish (Maoist takeover), ditto for North Korea (Communist takeover), Israel (founding), India, Bangladesh and Pakistan (partition), Indonesia (declared independence). They are all consequences of WW2, our last major bout of fascism.

Russia has had its current system for, what, 25 years (Putin's era)? Or date it from 1987. for the overthrow of communism. The EU dates from 1993. Nigeria's fourth Republic was founded in 1990, while Brazil became democratic after a military takeover in the 1980s.

**1061:**

_If you look for informed opinions the USA often comes up as democratic, but not very. They'll categories it as "at risk democracy", "mostly democratic" or "limited democracy", because there are systematic problems at all levels. The USA still has archaic electoral systems (FPTP/2party etc), gerrymandering, a very limited franchise, partisan control of election processes, unlimited spending and influence efforts, very poor media, oh and also the explicitly antidemocratic electoral college._

**Of course** the U.S. is not very democratic. The Founding Fathers intended us to be that way! 😏

Seriously, the founders were torn by various rivalries - big states vs small states (thus an equal number of senators and the Electoral College), slavery vs non-slavery (thus blacks count three-fifths of whites), rich vs poor (thus only the rich could vote), and many others.

The founders, mostly wealthy men, feared the average people of that time, which is one reason why they made it so hard to amend our Constitution - a big reason why we have a less-than-ideal democracy.

It's really a small miracle that we even have a U.S.A., but it's no surprise that many of the faults of the founders live on with us today.

It's not perfect, but it's better than when I see happening south of the border…

This is a decent collection of resources describing the process (as part of civics lessons for schools, so easily understandable).

Question for Americans: when you register as a voter, are you obligated to state a party preference? I keep hearing of people being a "registered Democrat" or "registered Republican" which seems very strange. Up here you are a "registered voter" but Elections Canada has no interest (and no way of tracking) which party you support.

**1070:**

NC districting.

To you and Robert.

We are roughly split between registered D, R, and none. With a near trivial number of smaller parties.

One thing the look at in re-districting is prior voting patterns.

And by lumpiness I mean in terms of population distribution and voting patterns. 7 counties surrounding the two major cities vote heavily D. The most of the non urban areas vote R. With the mid sized cities a bit of a balanced mix.

Which means to get close to a 7/7 split you have to start breaking up the big cities and mixing them with rural voters 50 or 100 miles away. Which suddenly creates a map that looks like gerrymandering. Especially if you don't know the local data.

All of those general guidelines sort of fall apart when you apply them to our state. If you decide to keep counties and population groups together you almost always wind up with a 8/6 or 9/5 split. For a state where people tend to vote 50/50.

As to that 50/50, we elect governors in presidential election years. Looking back this gives you an idea of how close we are and have been for a while.

Governor  
2000-2020 7 D's and 1 R win  
1976-2020 11 D's and 3 R's win  


President 2000-2020 7 R's and 1 D win  
1976-2020 12 R's and 2 D's win

**1071:**

Are you required to register your party affiliation in the US? No.

The classical rationale for doing so is when there are two elections per year: the primary election in the spring to choose who the party's candidate for a particular seat is, the general election in the fall to choose who gets the seat. In the primary election, you only get to vote for the candidate whose party you are registered for, and if you aren't affiliated, you don't get to vote for anyone.

California (and I don't know how many other states) has gone to a non-partisan primary election, with the top two vote getters regardless of party advancing to the general election. If there are only two candidates, there is no primary election for that seat. This was done because it was felt that partisan primaries disenfranchised too many voters in any particular district. In our state, you still can register your party preference, but I don't think it matters much for which ballots you get.

In a partisan primary system, often only the primary election matters, so there is a representation problem that only some people vote for whoever's going to get all their party votes in the fall. That's something I like about nonpartisan elections, in that it doesn't favor extremists. The flip side is that the middle of the US political road is pretty conservative by WEIRD standards, so if you're concerned about things like climate change, you may want more progressives fighting for stuff you care about.

**1074:**

Robert Prior @ 1068: _Question for Americans: when you register as a voter, are you obligated to state a party preference?_

Since US elections (including voter registration for same) are mostly administered at the individual state level, the details vary **a lot** from one US state to another. 

In some states, which administer primary elections to select nominees for each partisan office, from each of the political parties currently registered with the state's elections authority, a voter who wants to vote an his/her chosen party's primary must declare a party preference, and is allowed to vote _only_ in that party's primary. In these states, a voter cannot vote in more than one party's primary at a time. (In these states, primary voting for specifically designated "non-partisan" offices (often judicial positions) is typically done on a separate ballot document, which does not require the voter to declare a party affiliation.)

In other states, voters may be allowed to vote in the primary for a candidate from any of the parties that have one or more candidates running for a specific office, but generally cannot vote for more than one candidate for any individual position. This is commonly referred to as a "blanket primary". In some of these states (specifically including Louisiana, Washington, California, and Alaska), all candidates for a specific office -- regardless of party affiliation, if any -- run against each other in the primary. Both types are sometimes also referred to as a "jungle primary".

In Louisiana, if one candidate gets an absolute majority of the total votes cast for that office in the primary, s/he is elected, and no further voting is required. If no candidate does, the two top finishers (regardless of party affiliation, if any) go on the general election.

In Washington and California, the top two finishers in the primary (regardless of party affiliation, if any) go on to the general election.

In Alaska, the top four finishers in the primary (regardless of party affiliation, if any) go on to the general election.

In any of these states, it is not unusual to see a primary send multiple candidates from the same party to the general election for a specific office -- especially for state legislator positions from areas where that party is locally dominant.

In each of these variations, the state-level role of the individual political party organizations (as distinct from individual candidates and interest group caucuses within that party) tends to be less active than in states where state primary elections actually determine a party's nominees.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4934)

[Leroy F. Berven](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4934) replied to [this comment from Moz](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200205) | [October 30, 2024 06:14](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200223)

**1075:**

Moz @ 1058: _I wonder whether running a big state on the same system for a long time is even possible._

The few known examples with at least kinda-sorta continuity of government over a multi-century period would IMNSHO include the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire, at least one (and possibly more) multi-Dynasty periods of pre-Roman Egypt, probably England / Britain (ca. 1300-1900), and possibly the Roman Republic and/or Ottoman Empire. 

The Venetian Republic and pre-Shogunate Japan Would probably qualify on the "long time" axis, but are questionable as qualifiers for "big state" status during any significant fraction of those periods.

The Holy Roman Empire was at least formally "big" during most of its legal existence, but for most of that time status as an actual functioning "state" seems to have been somewhere between "legal fiction" and "wishful thinking". During portions of its colonial period (ca. 1500-1900), Spain managed to make at least a somewhat plausible showing on both duration and strength, although usually not at the same time.

From what we know of the Chinese, Indian, and Korean polities of the last 2500-3000 years of so, their respective cultural continuities are generally much stronger than their political continuities. (Swapping out an old dynasty for a new one is generally a lot more politically disruptive when the replacement gang is an invading barbarian army from outside the borders of your existing civilization, than when it's just your previous honcho's second cousin's nephew, or uncle's bastard son.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8640)

[AlanD2](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8640) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200215) | [October 30, 2024 06:20](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200224)

**1076:**

_So even if the goal was no gerrymandering. Tell me how to define the term and give that 7/7 result._

In my opinion, gerrymandering is when the R/D split in representation (i.e. in the U.S. House of Representatives and state legislators) is far from the R/D split in votes. If a state has eight representatives and 25% of voters are Democrats, I'd expect to see two Democratic representatives.

So if Republications (who are usually - but not always - responsible for gerrymandering ) have eight house seats, and the Democrats have two, but Democrats had 50% of the votes in the state, I'd definitely suspect gerrymandering.

The other clue is which party had control of the organization which sets the boundaries of the congressional districts - especially if that party gained an unfair advantage.

I understand that drawing fair boundaries is hard - even with good intentions. Democrats tend to inhabit cities, while Republications are usually spread out over rural areas. This usually results in the weird district shapes that you mentioned.

> [BREAKING: Hundreds of ballots possibly destroyed in Vancouver ballot box arson](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UaBmxv2VNSM)
