> 
> [YouTube: > KATU-TV2 News, Portland, OR]

Yah. Three ballot boxes hit, as well as in Phoenix. <https://www.newsweek.com/map-shows-locations-where-ballot-boxes-have-been-set-fire-1976975>

One box in Portland had three ballots damaged by the externally applied incendiary. A fire suppression system had been installed within the box _genius move! and police will be eyeballing that box henceforth._

Two boxes across the river in Vancouver WA had more ballots destroyed & damaged as their fire suppression system malfed. Washington state has no income tax, as Oregon does, so that Washington border county attracts large-mouthed Libertarians, tech bros, and quite a few Russian emigres who may not have a firm grasp on this Democracy thing. 

Vancouver's hiring $23/hr temp workers to sit in their cars, run their engines, and yelp for help if any would-be Fascisti repeat the event. Portland's using law enforcement to do the same. OBTW, the same tagless Volvo sedan was spotted by CCTV at all three conflagrations and is being sought (probably stolen, given the frequent car theft in these parts).

As a Democratic Party precinct committeeperson, I'd previously volunteered to watch the ballot box in my suburban town, so the whole thing's a matter of interest.

**1079:**

Robert Prior asked on October 30, 2024 # 03:38 in #1068:{snip}

> Question for Americans: when you register as a voter, are you obligated to state a party preference? I keep hearing of people being a "registered Democrat" or "registered Republican" which seems very strange. Up here you are a "registered voter" but Elections Canada has no interest (and no way of tracking) which party you support.

You may register with no affiliation or with any party recognized by your state of residence. Nonaffiliated voters will be able to vote for non-partisan races and for measures in the Primary Election, but will not be able to vote for major party candidates in the primaries. All registered voters can participate in the General Election. So, choosing a party lets you participate in its internal discussions and vote for the candidates also affiliated in the primary election, but has no effect on who you may choose in the general election. Many state and local offices here are non-partisan, with no primary race. and we're slowly moving towards ranked-choice voting (as are a few other states).

Minor Political Parties recgnized in Oregon currently are:​  
​  
Constitution Party​  


Independent Party​  


Libertarian Party

No Labels Party​  


Pacific Green ​  


Progressive Party​  


We the People​  


Working Familie​s Party​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

**1081:**

Leroy @1075

The idea that Britain has some sort of continuity from 1300 onwards is a rather “generous” interpretation.

The big one would be 1689, when we made it clear that the Monarchy was a mere constitutional nicety. Before that we experimented with military dictatorship (Cromwell), and Judicial Supremacy.

Since then, we’ve had the Great Reform Act of 1832 which tried to ensure MPs were elected by approximately the same number of voters. Then various expansions in suffrage. But perhaps of more significance was 1911 when Lloyd George showed the Aristocracy who was boss. I think we finally get rid of “the hereditaries” in the House of Lords next year.

But you are also right: the appearance of continuity is valued, even if it is a mere fig leaf.

**1088:**

_for most of that time status as an actual functioning "state" seems to have been somewhere between "legal fiction" and "wishful thinking"_

I think the issue is that it was really more of a modern style federation, and multi-ethnic/cosmopolitan in an era and context where this was not favoured. There are historians who seem to think the Habsburgs did a reasonably good job holding it together against the odds, though our view of it is really hugely distorted through the lens of 19th century ethnonationalism and resentments. Arguably the HRE made things better for most people in some polities within it, not others and the scope of the imperial jurisdiction within the empire was constrained by a twisty maze of customary laws and privileges that predate all the post-Westphalian nation-building stuff.

**1089:**

_California (and I don't know how many other states) has gone to a non-partisan primary election, with the top two vote getters regardless of party advancing to the general election._

So it would be possible for two Democrats (or Republicans) to face off against each other?

Silly question, but if everyone can vote for anyone in the primary, why not just have a single election? I can see that the double election functions a bit like a not-so-instant runoff election with just two stages, but mathematically is it more likely to give the choice most voters support (or don't oppose)? (My math isn't up to this on my own, but I'd be very interested in an explanation from an election wonk, as long as it's suitable for a Bear of Very Little Brain.)

Up here party candidates are chosen by the party memberships, and _those_ contests aren't under Elections Canada supervision which is a weak point in our system. One of our parties in particular has a reputation for dirty tricks in choosing candidates and leaders.

**1092:**

_Silly question, but if everyone can vote for anyone in the primary, why not just have a single election?_

Ranked choice voting mystifies a lot of people. And the party likely to loose out in such tends to say it is a bad idea. Both D & R.

"Jungle Primaries" (the name for what California does) is a simplified ranked choice voting system and is easy to explain to the average person who only pays attention a few days a year. If that.

**1093:**

Mars One (a very defunct company) tried to sell the “reality TV goes to Mars”. And it collapsed long before it built a rocket. It before they collapsed, MIT put together a paper shooting holes in the Mars One Plan. And it came down to: Spare Parts and Oxygen Regulation. Spare parts becomes an ongoing issue because spare parts come to dominate the mass being shipped from the home world.

The oxygen regulation part was straight forward: the energy concerns to regulate the amount of oxygen in the system will dominate the energy demands of the colony. Due to plants producing oxygen, the extra oxygen has to be mechanically removed, or it becomes a high oxygen environment with a very high risk of fire (and that fire is in an enclosed space). 

And if the nation state supporting this endeavor stops supplying funds, parts and supplies; then there will be a catastrophic failure within months. And that is assuming the high oxygen issue doesn’t kill everyone first.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Damian](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200238) | [October 30, 2024 14:39](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200246)

**1094:**

_There seems to be an absolutely bewildering range of NGOs providing assistance._

Asheville and and surrounding areas are a total mess. But getting better. And all kinds of volunteer groups are helping out. From churches to collections of folks driving in for a few days.

But as my brother in law calls them, the "wanna be heroes" are pissed that they show up and want to do their thing and are told to go away. They are just getting in the way and creating confusion. Flat space is limited and people who don't want to play nice are just not welcome. Show up, ask what you can do, and then do it and you're welcomed. The wanna be heroes then go on FB and Tiktok spewing anti government nonsense.

The Asheville area, in addition to the music scene, has a serious concentration of craft breweries. Due to the vib of the area and the fantastic water around the area. Some of those breweries got totally flooded. Others that were on higher ground are sort of back in operation with trucked in water. Which required some plumbing changes before they could start. (They were not set up to get water from a tanker truck sitting in the parking lot.)

The lakes that feed the various water systems are full of suspended silt. So much so that the water systems can't operate as the filtering systems would immediately clog up. Asheville started flowing unfiltered water a week ago just to look for leaks. Actually to find the 100 or more major leaks they know they have then start looking for the smaller ones. But it is untreated with lots of warnings of DO NOT DRINK. They are working on getting the silt out of the lakes. Aluminum Chloride (?) and similar being put into the lakes.

But drinking and bathing water for the entire area is being trucked in. For 100K or so people. With distribution centers all over so people can pick up the daily ration of bottled water. And big tanks clearly labeled for flush water. Of course the later doesn't help if half of your back yard septic system is now in the road in front of your house.

Lots of ATVs distributing supplies up roads that even Greg's thing can't navigate. Lot of cute babbling brooks running under roads in 1 meter pipes cut 10 meter ravines into the roads. So for now they have made trails the ATVs can navigate. There are clumps of people totally cut off due to such ravines. And various groups are working to build temp bridges. They were using mules to do search missions for a while. They may still be using them for some deliveries.

There are people who totally lost their house. Either it got crushed, slide down the mountain, or went down the river. They are living in shelters or tents trying to figure out next steps. As most don't have insurance that covers "swept away by river" or "slid down mountain slope".

It is a mess. But being worked on. And in a side comment to the much of the rest of the current comments here, all but 3 polling places (out of over 100?) are in operation. Plus a few new ones. I suspect some are in quickly converted containers or some RVs parked in a row.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200243) | [October 30, 2024 14:43](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200247)

**1096:**

  * From what we know of the Chinese, Indian, and Korean polities of the last 2500-3000 years of so, their respective cultural continuities are generally much stronger than their political continuities.*



Not particularly. Japan for instance went from the Heian Buddhist aristocratic regime to roughly a century of civil war to the Tokugawa military shogunate to the rapidly modernizing Meiji imperial system to straight up fascism to democracy.

Korea went from being an open trading country to the Hermit Kingdom, with an ultra-Confucianist system where verb suffixes expressed the relative ranks of speaker and audience (something that’s disappearing now), not counting occupation by Mongols and Japanese.

And China is best thought of as equivalent to all of Europe masquerading as a single country. With diversity and history to match that of Europe within its borders.

I’m not mocking you. Their history is as crazy as anywhere else, and they’re not at all culturally static. They’re worth reading up on. 

The critical point is that they use the Rhetoric of political stability, but it has little to do with the actual Reality. For instance, AFAIK, China’s famed Mandate of Heaven was first deployed by the remnants of the Tang dynasty bureaucracy to persuade the Song Dynasty founder (basically a warlord) not to kill them all, that he could rule with their loyal help. And he still made massive changes anyway (every dynastic founder did), but he kept the story for its rhetorical utility.

Don’t mistake the rhetoric of cultural continuity for reality. And if you like reading history, go read up. It looks a lot like our own.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

[David L](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028) replied to [this comment from Robert Prior](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200241) | [October 30, 2024 14:53](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200249)

**1097:**

_Up here party candidates are chosen by the party memberships, and those contests aren't under Elections Canada supervision which is a weak point in our system. One of our parties in particular has a reputation for dirty tricks in choosing candidates and leaders._

The US had somewhat evolved into such a system as that and what the UK has. But while the outdoor fighting at the 1968 D convention that whitroth talks about resulted in a lot of changes in terms of the party policies, the indoor arguments (and fighting) over party bosses picking delegates to the convention and candidates in general blew up. (It sort of got ugly in 64 but in 68 totally blew up.) And the country mostly went to the primary system. Which party bosses still had a lot of influence over. With a few things like Terminator Arnold in California being outliers. Then social media happened. And now you win primaries many times based on Facebook, Youtube, TikTok, and similar engagement. Which is how we got the crazy due as the North Carolina Lt. Gov who is running for governor just now. And like to get wiped out.

_So if Republications (who are usually - but not always - responsible for gerrymandering ) have eight house seats, and the Democrats have two, but Democrats had 50% of the votes in the state, I'd definitely suspect gerrymandering._

To my point. To get 50/50 in NC you have to draw boundaries where a number of small farm communities get tied to a tech hub like around Raleigh. Spread over 100 or more miles. **1101:**

And in space news… remember that?

NASA’s 80 m2 solar sail test (launched April 2. I didn’t notice, did you?) deployed its 4 booms successfully, but one of the booms is bent and it’s slowly tumbling. 

<https://www.space.com/nasa-acs3-solar-sail-bent-boom>

Neither is a mission-ending problem, because the mission is to figure out how sail. For what it’s worth, Japan has had more luck with their deployed sails, apparently.

Just a reminder that everything about space travel is weird, and propelling a probe with fishing poles and space blankets (but bigger) is harder than it looks.

**1102:**

I, personally, have grave doubts about the validity of _any_ of the polls. For one, most poeple (not including me) don't have landlines anymore, which means they cannot chose a geographical area by area code (first three of 10 digits). Ditto areas _withing_ the area code (second three digits in 10 digit phone nu.). 

Than there are the vast majority who won't answer unknown calls from phone numbers (because they're expecting those to be about the extended car warranty). Or who don't take phone calls, because they _only_ text. 

And then there's the biased polls - if you can't reply as a, b, or c, you're an outlier, and tossed.

**1115:**

_Apparently 950 wheels of high quality cheese were stolen from a London distributor and retailer._

Three possible explanations:

* Robin Hood's ghost has repossessed a number of merry men, and they're cheesing off the high end mongers to give to the poor this holiday season.

* It's a marketing ploy. The cheese will shortly be found by anonymous parties, then aged in police custody vaults for a year until it turns out that no thieves can be found, at which point it will be sold for three times the prices because of its backstory and unique provenance.

* a) The crooks were idiots, and/or b) they can actually fence that much cheese somewhere (an anonymous fondue shop in Switzerland?).

I'm betting on 3a myself.

**1117:**

AlanD2 @ 1061:

> _"If you look for informed opinions the USA often comes up as democratic, but not very. They'll categories it as "at risk democracy", "mostly democratic" or "limited democracy", because there are systematic problems at all levels. The USA still has archaic electoral systems (FPTP/2party etc), gerrymandering, a very limited franchise, partisan control of election processes, unlimited spending and influence efforts, very poor media, oh and also the explicitly antidemocratic electoral college. "_

_**Of course** the U.S. is not very democratic. The Founding Fathers intended us to be that way! 😏_

_Seriously, the founders were torn by various rivalries - big states vs small states (thus an equal number of senators and the Electoral College), slavery vs non-slavery (thus blacks count three-fifths of whites), rich vs poor (thus only the rich could vote), and many others._

_The founders, mostly wealthy men, feared the average people of that time, which is one reason why they made it so hard to amend our Constitution - a big reason why we have a less-than-ideal democracy._

_It's really a small miracle that we even have a U.S.A., but it's no surprise that many of the faults of the founders live on with us today._

The one thing _**the founders**_ feared most was the United States Government under the Articles of Confederation was headed towards defaulting on the DEBT from the American Revolution ... because the central government did not have any independent source of revenue and no way to force the independent states to pay their bills.

The United States (and the states themselves) **under the Constitution** is prohibited from defaulting on the debt (Article VI).

The primary purpose of the U.S. government being only slightly democratic is:  
• To prevent the government from becoming a despotism like the colonial administration  
• To prevent **radicals** from taking over the government, changing the rules and DEFAULTING ON THE DEBT!

[_

> _“I propose that we draw the maps to give a partisan advantage to 10 Republicans and three Democrats, because I do not believe it’s possible to draw a map with 11 Republicans and two Democrats,” one of us said in 2016, as the North Carolina legislature drew new congressional maps._

_](https://www.theatlantic.com/ideas/archive/2019/03/ralph-hise-and-david-lewis-nc-gerrymandering/585619/)

[_Start here for a brief look at recent gerrymandering & court decisions regarding gerrymandering in North Carolina ... _](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Carolina's_congressional_districts#Constitutionality_of_the_2010_redistricting)

The 2022 maps that produced a 7/7 split were the result of a **North Carolina Supreme Court** ruling that partisan gerrymandering violated the North Carolina Constitution's "Declaration of Rights:

[

> _"The civil rights guaranteed by the Declaration of Rights in Article I of our Constitution" including the free elections clause, N.C. Const. art.I, § 10, and the freedom of assembly clause, N.C. Const. art. I, § 19, the free speech clause, N.C. Const. art. I, § 14, and the freedom of assembly clause, N.C. Const. art. I, § 12, "are individual and personal rights entitled to protection against state action."_](https://www.politico.com/f/?id=0000017e-c71d-de19-a97e-e7bd64c60000)

Republicans won a majority on the NC Supreme Court in the 2022 judicial election and overturned the ruling in a 5–2 decision, clearing the way for Republican gerrymandering in the next redistricting cycle. The maps approved by the General Assembly in October 2023 are expected to favor the GQP at least 10/4 and possibly 13/1 (while the electorate remains split approximately 50/50).

**1133:**

_a) The crooks were idiots, and/or b) they can actually fence that much cheese somewhere_

I'd bet on (b) myself. Theft of high-value food is becoming a problem over here: as prices have risen lots of shops and restaurants have decided not to examine the mouth of the very reasonably-priced horse that they have a chance to acquire.

Meat theft tends to be retail, but dairy, maple syrup, seafood — all have been stolen with mostly limited success tracking thieves or goods. (The maple syrup heist was an exception; being as it was the largest theft in Canadian history, it got more police attention. And they got very sloppy towards the end.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4402)

**1138:**

Re: the great cheddar heist

<https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/10/31/the-great-british-cheese-heist-who-stole-390000-of-cheddar>

Turns out it wasn’t 950 wheels of one cheese, it was a bunch of different artisanal cheddars. This kind of sucks for the cheese makers and cheese mongers, but it also sucks for the crooks, because they’ll need to keep It refrigerated to be worth anything. Also, it’s identifiable stuff with all sorts of labels, so unless the burglars already had a plan in place to rapidly move the haul, the stuff looks traceable.

Valencia, with nearly a hundred dead last time I checked, and many missing

There was to be an Ingress event (an Anomaly) there this weekend. Two of our local friends (ones who we sometimes get to feed our cats) have gone out there for it. Unsurprisingly it has been cancelled, even if the city itself is relatively unaffected

My wife and I had considered going too, but we have just done 5 conventions in 3 months as well as an anomaly, and there's a limit to the number of weekends away we can afford

_"On the other hand this banging the table (with your shoe, as it were, like Khrushchev) demanding someone shut up is merely rude."_

gasdive was trying to tell me how my country works from from a standpoint of actual negative knowledge, and with no interest in either the complexities or the details. **1172:**

_My wife and I met 12 years ago. I don't remember when was the last time either of us was surprised by the other's outlook._

Sounds ~~boring~~ err, less exciting.

I grew up my first 20 years outside of a smaller city where my great great (great?) grandfather settled in 1824. My high school building was the one both of my parents attended (1-12 for them) and was built before they were born.

My wife's father was career army officer. And wound up with good mentors and was thus promoted frequently. Her first 3 years of schooling were in Italy. After that a different state in the US every year for 7 years. The last two years before college in Heidelberg then Stuttgart.

We have ingrained in us a few 1000 different ways of looking at things. We will likely be surprising each other till the day one of us dies.

I'm here. 2nd worst part was the usual - for the nurses trying to find my veins. Fir worst... waking up at 04:40, and getting up at 05:00, to show up at the facility (and then call for them to call for security to open the doors for us) at 06:15. I was the first patient for the doc, and we all enjoyed each other's compan (and I got to give a flyer for my book to a recover nurse). 

Got home by 11:00 or so, and in bed most of the day.

**1196:**

_Stumbled on the below article about a 20th century US auto pioneer's stab at writing international policy and his fixation with conspiracy theories_

Old Henry was very proud of his _Verdienstorden vom Deutschen Adler_.

Also of note: his famous doubling of his worker's wages wasn't so they could afford to buy the cars they were making, but so that they wouldn't keep quitting for less monotonous work elsewhere. And the extra money wasn't technically wages, but a 'bonus' that they had to earn by living according to his wishes: wives stayed at home, homes were clean, no drinking or gambling, no taking in boarders, and mandatory contributions to a savings account — all enforced by the Sociological Department (which could make unannounced home inspections, check bank records, etc). 

Ford also wanted to establish a settlement in the remote jungle, and figured it would be easy with his engineering knowledge. 

<https://www.theguardian.com/cities/2016/aug/19/lost-cities-10-fordlandia-failure-henry-ford-amazon>

Techbros are gonna techbro, no matter what generation they're from.

_His NY sentencing is scheduled for November 24, 2024. I hope he gets maxed out!_

Nice thought. When he first got convicted, a law professor got interviewed and gave the following (from memory):

-the people who did things for him got convicted and did (or are doing) time. As a general principle, they like to punish the boss more than the underlings. Iirc that maxes out at four years for his crimes. Some other experts expect probation, as if he’s not a flight risk. I think the first guy sounded more sane. So hope for four years.

-Of course his team will file endless inane appeals and delays. This exercise will take up another year.

My own thoughts since then: 

-Likely his team will start arguing that he’s no longer healthy or mentally competent enough to be imprisoned, and Eris knows there’s lots of footage they could draw on, even though I’m quite sure they’ll turn around and scream that he won the election on social media.

-Hopefully Biden will exercise his SCOTUS-granted presidential authority to strip Trump of his passport and confine him under unfriendly Secret Service watch to keep him from heading to Russia or communicating with Putin or other unfriendlies, whatever his fate in this case is. He’s still on the gaffhook for Jan 6th and those document cases after all. I can fantasize, can’t I?

This all depends on him losing bigly, of course. And, as Doonesbury pointed out last Sunday, if Trump loses and tries to rebel, Biden now has the authority to detain him in protective custody until the election is certified and Harris is sworn in. He’ll probably just call out the National Guard instead.

**1201:**

_... assuming Vance does not simply take over directly prior to 2026_

I think 2026 is the outside limit for when Vance will make a move for the presidency. Some possibilities, if the crazies win:

1) Trump wins but New York state doesn't care. He's already been tried and convicted, so there's nothing in the law preventing the Justice system from putting him in prison. Often white-collar criminals are allowed a 'get your affairs in order' grace period but it's not required; given the high flight risk involved and extreme difficulty of extracting him if he didn't cooperate, New York would be fully justified in taking him into custody on the spot. Since the nominal winner is in prison on election day, the nation is delivered a Constitutional crisis before the administration even takes office, totally in character for the Trump gang. Vance will be present and available.

2) Trump catches a prison sentence starting after election day, either by somehow delaying the New York sentencing or being found guilty in one of the other cases he's facing. At that point Vance brings up the 25th Amendment, loudly announces that Trump "is unable to discharge the powers and duties of his office," and steps up as Acting President until further notice.

3) Trump wiggles out of all his many criminal problems but continues to get more unhinged. (He was never very hinged to begin with.) Vance can bide his time and sound out cabinet members privately. When the time is right and the news cycle is full of "the president is senile" stories, that's the time for them to pull a coup and declare him unable. This would require a two-thirds majority in both Houses if Trump could assemble a written note claiming he's bigly sane, the sanest person ever, experts are amazed at how sane he is. Since Congress couldn't manage a two-thirds majority against Trump a few weeks after he'd sent an angry mob to break into their work place and murder some of them, it might be convenient for Vance if Trump has an 'accident' around this point.

4) Trump wiggles out of prison time and stays semi-coherent for two years of dictator rallies and tyranny by whim. Since everyone in the MAGA world is required to believe that Trump won the 2020 election, that means that in January 2026 he hits the Constitutional ten year limit, automatically timing out of the presidency. Trump may be unaware of this until he's told that Vance is planning an inauguration ceremony. Much gnashing and wailing and clutching of pearls ensues in MAGA world, many circular firing squads, very fanatic, much wow. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8894)

**1204:**

Scott,

I normally think your analyses are very good, but I think that — because you are steeped in US Constitutionalism — you aren’t seeing all of the possibilities open to an autocratic tyrant.

In case (1), Trump declares the New York Justice system as personal enemies and dispatches “Seal Team Six” (or whoever in government service will do the job for him).

In case (2), apart from the solution proffered in case (1), it has already been determined he can serve as President from jail. In that case I expect the unhingary to escalate exponentially.

In case (3), should Vance start making such approaches, it is extremely likely that Trump, or a henchman like Stephen Miller gets wind of it. This is extremely valuable information in what is effectively a mediaeval court political scenario. You can get a hated rival out of the way by dobbing them in. It is also worth pointing out that your mainstream media will be subverted as a first priority. Dilbert gets the WaPo from Bezos along with any other morsels he fancies. Don jr runs the NYT after Sulzberger suffers a mysterious domestic accident. NBC and CNN are sued to oblivion.

In case (4), term-limits are for ninnies who obey the law. First thing we do is pass an Enabling Act.

You had just better hope he doesn’t win, either by getting enough votes or further subverting the judicial system. If he does, pray that Biden has the cohones to use a stealth version of “Seal Team Six”[TM], and hope that MAGA believe that their hero died of a stroke suffered from the excitement of a prospective victory. Nah, I can’t see that happening, can you?

_On today's polling ... I predict a narrow Harris_

Polls are all statistically dead even. This election is coming down to small slices of voters in 7 states. And the slices are so small that polling picks them up more as noise than data.

Puerto Ricans voters may swing Pennsylvania to D and this would be a huge win for KH. Or maybe not.

Here in NC we have the reverse coat tails of our governors race which might win it for her.

And there are similar things on different topics in the 7 states.

But the national and local ads about KH seeming to advocate government surgical trans re-assignment for people in prisons are playing non stop. Especially on sporting events.

I'm for a KH win. I think she will do it. But I'm far from certain.

“Keep Calm And Carry On”

rather than clarify and educate, the goal of media (old and new) has become clickbait and aggravate... immediate gratification and spiking emotions made into their priority... this election is not going to be easy nor obvious... which will fail to fit into the current mode of journalism... 

but what will 'sell soap' are the visuals of armed thugs howling for blood of their Orange Lordship Trump's enemies and silent, unblinking National Guardsmen (and soldiers and police) standing the watch... and holding back the barbarian hordes... 

necessary...because...

it will not be ‘**Election Day** ’ but rather ‘**Election Month** ’ …

between the mail-in ballots, advance in person ballots, arsewipes insistent upon hand counts, demands for re-counts, nasty rumors leading to one or more minor riots, lawsuits of little credibility, very loud declarations of fraud, the brutally plausible potential of _major_ riots, etc

never mind the count, or the re-count, there will be demands for a re-re-count

we should expect a not-quite-accepted outcome no sooner than the first of December

with another roiling shitstorm right up to the moment US Army soldiers deploy barbed wire around the Capitol and there’s a mandatory curfew from 05 JAN 2025 thru 07 JAN 2025 keeping Washington’s streets empty

**1228:**

Damian,

AOC was invented in 1934 to protect wine growers in Chateauneuf-du-Pape during a downturn in demand. The original statute prescribes the permitted grape varieties (13 from memory), and eventually the French law says things about viticulture (yields, non irrigation), and most importantly: labelling. In Burgundy the AOC designation must be 33% larger than any designation of the particular sub-plot.

The labelling restrictions are so bonkers that I have advised growers in both Burgundy and Rousillion to invert their labels: put the legal label in the back of the bottle with all the distracting guff, and use an unrestricted front label to sell the wine. Not my own idea, I stole it from Zilliken in Saarburg, who was having similar problems with German legislation.

Now, as to how things have developed, you have a point. What was originally a communally owned marketing gimmick is now a juggernaut of market fixing. That said, the US alternative is branding. For example, one US company now holds the “brand” of pilau rice. Do we think that’s OK? Or is that trading off of others work?

By the way, if you think “Brie” is silly, just imagine if Margaret Thatcher had decided to make “Cheddar” an AOC on joining the single market. My guess is that she decided not to because most UK cheddar comes from regions miles from North Somerset. There is a cheese maker in Cheddar, but it is far from the best.

But Wensleydale, ahh, now you’re talking Grommet!

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

**1229:**

_Some time before 10.00hrs_

Silly boy. You forget that in the US, 10pm on the east coast is 7pm in Arizona and Nevada. And Arizona voting goes until 9pm local, midnight eastern time. So it gets complicated.

NC and GA will be mostly done by 11pm eastern. We (NC and GA) are very automated (and organized). 

Polls in NC close at 7:30pm.and mail ins have to be received by poll close on Tuesday. And I think they can be counted but not released as they come in. Early votes are already tabulated but the results sealed in the machines. And when people vote Tuesday in NC the votes are tabulated as each voter sticks their ballot into the box (machine). Machines are not Internet connected. But when voting is over there they print out their results which are taped to the wall of the polling place. And most poll captains and other workers take a picture of this. Then the systems are taken to county HQ. All totaled up. Again, printed out and publicly posted. Then counts forwarded to state BoE HQ. So if the mail ins from the last day or few and provisional don't amount to enough to change things, we (and you if you want the link) can see the results here.

MI MIGHT be done overnight.

But the rest of the "battleground" states will take a day or few.

Now toss in that over this past weekend it seems Iowa might be in play. Apparently those pesky women have some strong and growing opinions.

If you can access them:  
<https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2024/us/elections/results-timing-presidential-race-calls.html>  
<https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/when-polls-close-on-election-day-in-these-7-battleground-states>

Also, in those "battleground" states, the security has been ramped up by crazy amounts in many places.

Still might get ugly.

And on a side note, early summer NC was in the DT column. Harris changed that. She was here (in Raleigh) Friday, Trump will be here today. And they or their VP pics have been in the state most days of the last few weeks.

**1232:**

So I was a bit out it’s the dates, but Wikipedia is your friend: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Appellation_d%2527origine_contr%C3%B4l%C3%A9e>

Appellation d’Origine Controlee is the French term and means approximately: (legally) controlled named production area/methods.

DOC is the equivalent Italian designation. German wine law was so hamstrung by an attempt to “even things up” in 1971 legislation that they had no equivalent. For example, the village of Piesport has made high quality wine since Roman times. The 1971 law permitted practically anyone to label a wine as Piesporter. The consequence was that consumers became confused, and gave up buying it. Of course that means that if you know what you’re doing there have been bargains.

What made Piesport so good was a steep south-facing amphitheatre carved by the Mosel River. On the other side is a flat alluvial deposit. One side is great, the other side is, er, not. Both are in the village boundaries.

What else could a POTUS campaign be but _exhausting_? The candidates having to keep on the move, needing to make speeches every couple hours, keeping up appearances, et al, in their campaign’s final stretch.

Donald "I'm-not-a-crook" Trump is 78 YO. Not in best possible muscle tone nor is he anything less than obese. Then there's long standing clues of his stimulant usage, verging upon abusive addiction. 

With any luck, he'll will tip over at one of his rallies, artery in his brain rupturing due to stress and thus Trump will qualify for a federally funded state funeral. 

I for one will volunteer to help dig his grave for no charge. Afterwards I'll be sure to water the grass planted over his coffin. 

(USSS be advised, I am not advocating for assassination but instead here I am, seated with folded hands, passively awaiting the impact of a lifestyle filled with bad decisions and too much grease and too few heart healthy salads.)

**1236:**

David L @ 1229:

> _"Some time before 10.00hrs"_

_Silly boy. You forget that in the US, 10pm on the east coast is 7pm in Arizona and Nevada. And Arizona voting goes until 9pm local, midnight eastern time. So it gets complicated._

FWIW, "10.00hrs" is 10 O'clock **AM**. If he means "10.00hrs" where he lives (i.e.10:00 UTC), that's only 5:00 AM here in NC; 2:00 AM in California and midnight (00:00) in both Alaska & Hawaii. 

Polls won't even have opened anywhere in the U.S. at that time.**1**

IF he means 10:00 **PM** , that's 22:00 hrs, but still 22:00 hrs in London, or the east coast of the U.S., west coast of the U.S. or Alaska/Hawaii?

**1** Unless there's some town in New England where they open the polls immediately after midnight so they can claim to be the **first** voters in U.S. elections.

**PS:** And yes, **THEY** ARE prepared to counter him if he tries pulling that bullshit.

**1251:**

Thinking of buying a gun, especially one optimized for killing people?

Some things to think about:

-Get licensed and trained to handle it safely and hit whatever it is you want to aim at and not hit things you’re not aiming at. It’s not a magic wand. Buy it to use it.

-It takes regular practice to acquire and keep your gun skills, so it’s a new hobby. Factor in the cost of practice ammo, range fees, maintenance equipment, and replacement parts. Things wear out.

-You’re just going to bluff with it? You’ll be a better bluffer if you know WTF you’re doing and can hold it straight. Practice.

-If you’re really slow due to age or whatever, you're only going to win a hypothetical gunfight if you shoot from ambush or otherwise get the first shot in. That’s hard for anyone to parse as self-defense, so you’re basically planning on assaulting people and hoping subsequent events somehow justify your actions. Think this through carefully before committing to it as a plan. 

-Statistically the people most likely to get shot by your gun are yourself (suicide) and your family members (homicide or accident). If you’re prone to depression, intoxicant abuse, or worse, maybe get a self-defense weapon that’s harder to commit suicide with, like a long spear? Or don’t arm yourself at all?

-Lead is toxic. If you’re biologically unlucky and prone to Parkinson’s or some other diseases, it’s really toxic, and other metals are toxic too. So think really, really hard about firing lead bullets in an indoor range and breathing in the fumes. There’s lead in there. Also be really careful when cleaning so that you don’t breathe in or ingest things that shorten your life or disable you,

-If any of these present a major barrier, maybe rethink owning a firearm?

Hopefully JohnS, Martin, or other real experts will chime in with better ideas.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6643)

**1258:**

David L @ 1257:

> _"I'm talking self-defense"_

_You still need to practice. Not just shooting. Carrying it around. Cleaning. Getting it out of the locked case and ready to use in a hurry. Carrying spare ammo around. Especially reloading not at your kitchen table._

And you need to learn & ingrain deep within your soul **ALL** of the circumstances where you should not pull the trigger. 

**THAT** is the hardest thing to learn ... and the most important!

When we were undergoing "active shooter" training after 9/11, much more than half of the training was how to recognize when NOT TO SHOOT.

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from whitroth](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200487) | [November 4, 2024 21:51](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200492)

**1259:**

_I have no intention of bluffing anyone. If they come for us, why on earth do you think I would pretend to be a gunfighter from an old movie? You shoot from cover. And, as my late wife noted, you never point a gun at someone that you aren't intending to kill. And... suicide with a full-sized rifle is a bit hard._

Sorry, this is going to be harsh. 

In reverse order, since my version of PD has included serious depression, I did in fact check every gun to see if it could fit under my chin. The fact that I thought this was important, and they all fit, was a big reason I got rid of them. You’ve done this too, I take it?

Second, lots of people point guns at people they’d really rather not kill. It’s a standard police tactic. Hell, it’s a standard military tactic. Shoot first and ask questions later is macho posturing that even Meal Team Six probably would snort at. They at least try to intimidate people first.

Third, have you bothered to chat with your neighbors about whether they want you to take up arms to defend them? It’s not a bad idea, especially if they’re down range from wherever you’re planning to shoot from. Are you going to do it anyway if they say no?

You’ve got a strategy and tactics problem—what are you trying to do, what are the best means to do it?—and without doing the prep work and thinking it through, you’re already committing to sniping at strangers without any greater goal in mind. Even stochastic assassins think out their killing sprees better than that. If you’re planning on protecting yourself and your neighborhood, please do a thorough job and try to optimize your chances of success.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

**1267:**

_And were there?_

Yes. We have a LOT of hunters across the state. And many of the smarter ones take their kids through an official training and/or target practice before taking them out with a loaded rifle. And go practice themselves a bit before the first hunt of the season. Mostly for deer.

And we have a lot of scared older folks who think a concealed handgun in their purse or car storage box will allow them to defend themselves against, well, somebody. And don't get why I think they are nuts. At least as it relates to this topic. As the ex-military here have said many times, an active firefight is not quite the same as shooting at targets 10 and 30 meters downrange inside a building.

**1268:**

_A quick google reveals that the rounds will go through drywall, studs, aluminum siding, and cindercrete block walls, so paying attention to what's downrange is definitely important._

Yup. This is why the advice for home defense used to be shotguns, or hollow-point handgun ammo shot by a pistol or carbine. They don’t penetrate through walls very dar. Nowadays, body armor capable of blocking these munitions is getting more common, so you need something more powerful to be effective. IF you’re dealing with a paramilitary incursion and not just some desperate person looking for food or safety, that is.

Anyway, yes, learning to shoot straight is just step one. A big part of home defense is figuring out where you’re shooting from and to. And a basic element of all gun safety training is knowing what’s behind your target, so that when you miss, you won’t hit something or someone you care about. As an anecdote, I lived in one place where all the drywall had kids on the other side. Since the engagement distance was about two meters, I got a spear and practiced with that. Never needed it, but I didn’t want to shoot my neighbors’ kids by accident, and.a spear has a decent chance of going a bullet proof vest.

If you’re going to defend your neighborhood in the event of a civil breakdown, things like getting to know your neighbors and planning out what to do about looters or other incursions really s a good idea. Practicing them is even better. Leave the lone wolf stuff to John Wick and Batman.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6643)

**1270:**

You should believe me. My friends and I have had guns pointed at us (me in an armed robbery), them scaring the cops). Brandishing a gun (waving it around) is illegal. Threatening someone to get them to stop what they’re doing and go away) is less illegal, because it’s a form of self defense.

Anyway, Josh Marshall of TPM has been pointing at some weird polling data for weeks. There are a number of swing states, like North Carolina, where a Democratic candidate for governor or senator is way ahead of the Republican candidate, but Trump is polling tied with Harris. This is a really weird vote split if true. In recent races, it’s split the other way, with a democrat on top and republicans lower down. Also, the presidential polls are known for mostly herding together, and using each others’ results as a check on their own. There’s one recent Iowa poll, high quality and independent, that shows Harris trouncing Trump, in contrast to the mainstream polls which show a tie.

This is obviously whistling past the graveyard, but we might be surprised, and the result might be that our political news and polling systems are badly broken and need to be overhauled.

**1273:**

blindfold reloads are a standard part of training for most military 

advanced training modes includes doing so after being shaken awake on just 20 minutes of sleep with screaming sirens and flashing lights

and you have been issued the wrong sized ammo or there's a deliberately bent ammo round to jam up the works because shit happens and you gotta be able to clear the jam now-now-now

so get dummy rounds... and send the kiddies and the spouse off to the movies... and remember in the heat of crisis you will fumble

if you really are serious?

there's an ongoing debate whether or not to squirrel away baggies with six rounds in places where you'd likely be hiding and/or observing... yeah you ought be carrying enough ammo but nice to have a backup

then there's hardening this or that observation point... cinder blocks are cheap but ugly... slabs of metal not cheap nor easily installed... 

important safety tip: sofa + bookcase + teevee are not bullet proof so plan your hiding places

**1275:**

_Unless there's some town in New England where they open the polls immediately after midnight so they can claim to be the first voters in U.S. elections_

Yes! That's [Dixville Notch, New Hampshire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dixville_Notch,_New_Hampshire), a tiny place where the residents realized they could stay up late on the eve of election day and then close the polls, count, and announce a result within minutes after midnight.

In 2020 Biden won the Dixville Notch vote, 5 to 0. As I write the results are already in, Harris and Trump tied with 3 votes each.

([2024 coverage from CNN](https://www.cnn.com/2024/11/05/politics/dixville-notch-new-hampshire-2024-results/index.html))

**1279:**

For just numbers, don't expect much before 8pm or later Eastern Time. Most states have rules about not releasing any results before all the polls in the state close. Dixville Notch many have a special exemption in NH.

All the major national cable news and newspapers tend to have election count dashboards. CNN, MSNBC, FOX (yes them for just numbers), NY Times, Washington Post, etc... These are basically aggregation systems for the various state agencies. Most paywalls let you through for just the dashboards and some analysis. (It's a good way to sell a new subscription.)

In North Carolina our state board of elections, ncsbe.gov, posts results as they come in after the polls close. County by county and we have 100 of these. You can see the counted totals in relation to the vote totals by county or the state. Mail In tends to be in there also as they process them as they come in so the totals for those except for the last few days are in these totals. Last few day received mail in and provisional ballots take a few days to sort out. But most times you can tell by the totals they will not change anything. We know most everything before midnight except for very very very close contests.

And for those NOT in the US as to why it takes as long as it does, we vote on too many things. I'll agree. But changing this gores too many oxen and thus will likely not change anytime soon. Which leads us to 1000 to 2000 different paper ballots (maybe more?) in the state. Here's mine if anyone cares:  
<https://s3.amazonaws.com/ncsbesb/20241105/1170_92_B0015.pdf>

And if you want to track our odd mix of races at the state and federal level, go here:  
<https://er.ncsbe.gov/>

And I'm also a fan of 270towin.com. Personally I'm curious about what will be happening in Iowa. No one at the national level has been paying attention as everyone figured it was a done deal. R all the way. Now it seems the D's MIGHT flip 2 of the 4 hours seats and go for Biden. A well respected Iowa pollster found big surprises in her last poll last week.

By way of followup, it is looking very much like that Daily Show live stream is unlikely to be available outside the USA anyhow. This is not an unusual pattern by any means (American tech companies like to see themselves as international citizens because it's profitable, but American media companies as a rule are extremely parochial, because there's money in separating markets), and in general per what Dramlin said earlier we're looking to traditional websites rather than TV or streaming services for reliable information.

simple, brutal situation wherein someone has much to gain by encouraging delusions in others... religion having always been a useful tool by which to twist-fold-bend-staple the minds of those in reach of their sermons

problem?

_US circa 1990s_... AM band talk radio

_US circa 2000s_... radio... lots of cheap computers and modestly fast data cabling... first gen social media and half-arsed web sites with shabby tinfoil-esque content

US circa 2010s... radio... cheaper laptops... mobile phones... second gen social media and more polished content from ever more extreme batshit crazies

US circa 2020s... still lots of folk reliant upon basic radio... almost everyone reliant upon mobile phones for almost everything... yet more willingly exposing themselves to podcasts, ammosexual websites, idle chatter via social media, with X/twitter having been turned into a fetid swamp of delusions

====

but the difference being from 2010s onwards, over time, those with dreams of gaining political power having organized themselves to better leverage existing batshit crazies as well deliberately creating new and better BSGCs (worse from perspective of the sane and tolerant) who are goaded into doing more than mouthing off... arming up... marching together... burning things... brandishing weapons... shooting civilians... fools deluded into believing they'll be rewarded by their new overlords for being on the frontlines of Civil War 3.0

with ever more fools still blaming minorities for what went wrong in their lives ("my cow stopped giving milk, blame the Jews") rather than flawed governmental policies and short term focus upon easy modes of greed by gigacorps...

Yes. Let me cover the other comments to me here. 

If I have to, and pull it out, there are a _*holes in a Trumped-up home-made tactical driving through this neighborhood (_ heavily* integrated). If they're shooting, I'll shoot. I have no intention, as many in 'Nam were according to DoD reports, firing randomly, hoping to scare off the VC. I will aim with intent. 

Cops - I've read there was some insane study published in '92, that said a cop's chance of staying alive was better if they were, effectively, the aggressors, and a high percentage have been doing that since. Sorry, but one bad apple can spoil a barrel, and most barrels of cops are now spoiled. Finally, note that I specified an M-14. That was preferred by my late friend, the writer David Sherman, who'd been in combat, early, during 'Nam. Range, I see, of well over 600m. I do not want to let anyone come close to me. If they're busting in my door, that's when I pull out the SCA mace (by the door) or my real steel swords. 

My swords are real, not wallhangers. If I buy a firearm, it will be a rifle - a longarm - so forget the idiot handgun in the glove box (that doesn't want to open).

Scott Sanford @ 1275:

> _"Unless there's some town in New England where they open the polls immediately after midnight so they can claim to be the first voters in U.S. elections"_

_Yes! That's[Dixville Notch, New Hampshire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dixville_Notch,_New_Hampshire), a tiny place where the residents realized they could stay up late on the eve of election day and then close the polls, count, and announce a result within minutes after midnight._

**1313:**

Re: 'Q: who chooses the methodology for massaging the raw data?'

My guess is that most political polls don't use a lot of stats massaging this close to the final date. 

From what I've heard during a journalist panel talk about this election, one key problem has been sample selection. Make that sample self-selection bias because some pollsters have become overly identified with a particular candidate/party. Or, just outright rejection of 'data'. 

Anyways, it looks like a lot of people (skewing Rep and older voters) have been refusing to participate in polls. Meanwhile, the stereotypical 'random respondent' who would normally not participate in a survey/poll all of a sudden participates because they want to express support. 

My guess is that the above explanations/hypotheses might be based on how quickly particular demographic survey completion quotas are filled. (Wow - we got all of our under 25/first time voter quota filled in record time! Yikes - we still haven't hit our (historically rt-leaning) demo profile quota!] 

BTW, none of the pollsters in the panel said that they had sufficient info to figure out what the respective numbers/proportions might be for participation/non-participation even though most of the panel agreed that non-participants probably skew MAGA.

**1314:**

David L @ 1279:

_For just numbers, don't expect much before 8pm or later Eastern Time. Most states have rules about not releasing any results before all the polls in the state close. Dixville Notch many have a special exemption in NH._

Dixville Notch is a human interest feature story with miniscule effect. Announcing those totals won't matter in the overall scheme of things.

In North Carolina the board of elections are not allowed to begin counting early voting ballots or mail in ballots before the polls close at 7:30pm EST (assuming there are no local emergencies that cause some polling places to remain open later).

Broadcast of east coast results will be somewhat delayed because there was a mini-scandal a few years back where there were complaints about the news networks calling the election based on east coast results while people were still voting on the west coast, which depressed voter turnout (in California IIRC).

As I write this ALL races in North Carolina are currently (@ 14:00 EST [UTC -5]) **tied** at 0.00%

**1323:**

_In North Carolina the board of elections are not allowed to begin counting early voting ballots or mail in ballots before the polls close at 7:30pm EST (assuming there are no local emergencies that cause some polling places to remain open later)._

Sort of. Early votes are counted in the machines as the ballots are fed in. All this means is they can't extract the totals and feed them to the state board until after 7:30pm. So these totals show up most times within an hour.

And the workers can process mail in ballots starting first thing Tuesday so all but that day's mail might be released at poll closing.

**1343:**

Breaking the US into a Confederacy was tried twice. The first time was why they wrote the Constitution, the second was by the South during the Civil War. It was a messy failure both times. Considering the power vacuum it would cause globally, I suspect WW3 would be the result as everybody who could tried to grab whatever they could get. 

It’d be worse this time, because the split is urban-rural more than among states.

Basically I see three options. One is we continue under the current system. Best outcome from my perspective is that Trump invalids out, Vance takes over, proves to be utterly incompetent and uncharismatic to boot, and competent people win in 2022 and 2024 because Trump’s out of the picture.

The second is that this breaks the Constitution, and a cabal takes ver in full on authoritarian mode. Billionaires with military connections are an obvious choice for the new rulers.

The third is Biden decides to go out in some sort of blaze of glory to try to improve the situation. what he could do is unclear, and it does set a most-likely bad precedent. 

If you want something to jitter about, picture RFK Jr, antivaxxer, in charge of US health care, and bird flu breaking out as a global pandemic.

_Question for Americans:_

**1347:**

> _However, what if governors just stop listening to the White House?_

Seems to me states tried that, in 1986. Elizabeth Dole said, enforce your speed limits, or lose Federal highway funding. The two states involved, Vermont and Arizona, caved immediately. In 1987, South Dakota didn't want to raise their drinking age, and Federal funds were once again withheld. The state caved.

One problem with the states rights theory is that states have increasingly punted taxation to the feds, who then redistribute funds to the various states that need it. The rural states are typically net beneficiaries of this process, while keeping their own taxes low.

**1353:**

_Based on what I remember of the last election (and vaguely previous ones) I was expecting it to take longer to get the results, so this seems unexpectedly fast. What happened that made the difference?_

The 2000 election exposed some serious issues with how we (USA) counts. Suddenly the loose edges mattered. So laws were passed saying you must live in these guidelines. And no the feds can't force you to change but we will pay for the changes if you do. Pretty much all the states changed. No more crazy punch a hold ballots and such. Here in NC and in many other states it resulted in a state wide system of marked paper ballots and ballot scanning machines not modem or Internet connected. We had similar before local to me but the various security improvements including no Internet or modems caused us to get new ones.

Now to 2024. Again, the mail in issues that the pandemic caused exposed some more edge cases of slow counting. And the volume of mail ins was such that they impacted the final result. So it took a while. Most states implemented better ways to deal with mail in and the volume was lower this time.

To the other point by JohnS these results are not yet official as there are provisional and more mail in to deal with. And random sport audits. Plus Arizona and Nevada are slow counting states for various reasons. But even without those two states DT has the votes. As the various things left to count in the rest of the states will not change things.

When last I watched the news, he appeared to even have won the popular vote. (I'm swearing off political news for a few days.)

As to previous years, 2020 and 2000 were aberrations. I think you have to go back to 1960 to get to where it wasn't clear by midnight or so who the winner was. Illinois, with a side dish of Texas, was the big one then. The Dem Daley machine in Chicago was basically not releasing results until they knew how many votes were needed. And the southern end of the state was doing the same for the Nixon. (I lived next door and this is local history to me.) Finally Nixon told the southern Illinois folks to release their numbers and then the Daley machine did the same and the count in Illinois put Kennedy over the top.

The folklore was that Texas was going to be reported as a Kennedy win no mater how close it was. (See LBJ and landslide Lyndon.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

_When last I watched the news, he appeared to even have won the popular vote._

I just got back from an errand, where I was chatting with a small businessman. Pretty sure he was a small-l liberal from his opinions. He (like me) was worried about the way the world seems to be going, with increased incivility etc.

Then he brings up that he's glad Trump won, because we need a strong man to hold firm against the forces of disorder. Had no idea just how disruptive and disorderly Trump is (not to mention how "civil" is not a word that describes him). Knew that he cozied up to dictators but somehow thought that he would stand against them to protect democracy and order. (Or maybe just order, I didn't dig too deeply on that.)

Turned out he also had no idea of actual crime rates etc. One of the things in the headlines here is the "epidemic" of car theft in Canada, how it is several times what it was a few years ago. Lots of quotes (with stats) and headlines. No mention that car theft is _half_ what it was twenty years ago. In fact, virtually _all_ crime rates in Canada are down compared to 20 years ago (and massively down compared to the 60s and 70s); the recent small uptick is being presented as a massive crime wave.

It's almost as if someone (or several someone's) with a lot of media influence want a frightened population, isn't it? 

ANyway, I wonder how many times that kind of decision was being made in America yesterday.

Um, that's not what I said. 

In his first term, he never got around to appointing some number of people he needed to appoint, so his departments ran on a lean staff, ignoring the epic turnover rate at the top. And Musk is promising to cut staff still further.

And Congress was epically gridlocked under the Republicans, to the point where it took them weeks to agree on who had the gavel and they couldn't even vote to kick the can down the road. 

So, let's assume, for the sake of argument, that he's even more unfocused, incoherent, and forgetful than he was in 2016. In 2016, he came onboard as a small businessman running the one of the biggest companies in the world, and he had little idea how to make it work, to the point where he never fully staffed the senior management he had budget for. 

Now he's coming on board with mild cognitive impairment (I'm not a doctor, but I have seen mild cognitive impairment at close range for an extended time, so that's my layman's guess). The scenario I'm positing is that, even if his minions want to do Project 2025, they're going to be hampered because there aren't enough of them in place to make it all work, and the boss can't focus long enough to understand what they're trying to do, so his help will be hit or miss. Things to legitimately worry about are Ukraine and an invasion of Taiwan. In fact, I'll bet President Biden's quietly doing what he can to shore up NATO and Ukraine from being fracked by Trump.

As for Taiwan, when better to invade than when an incompetent is in charge of the opposition? If Trump strokes out too soon, Vance is at least a veteran, so presumably he's clueful enough to get out of the soldiers' way if China ignites a war. Trump? 

Two other thinga I'd suggest: One is to read Heather Cox Richardson's diagnosis of the election: the tl;dr is that waaay too many people have been red-pilled by conservative media into believing the country's been ruined by Biden. They have little interest or way of finding out otherwise. On one level I can't blame them, because I have no interest in going into Faux world. OTOH, getting to a consensus analysis reality is a serious problem we're not dealing with at all well. To me her idea is a bit saner than the idea that they're all irredeemable idiots or fascists. 

The other is to realize that a lot of people, including basically all our ancestors (especially female ones), have overcome worse tyrants and conditions. It's pretty freaking pathetic to drag anyone else down with a _it's game over man. We're doomed_ style message. You can do better, right?

**1378:**

Seems to me our once and future overlord understood two things:

  * The old marketing adage: "There's no such thing as bad publicity." Stories and jokes about him were endless; about Biden/Harris, they were pale and pro forma afterthoughts. All the great orange one needed to do was keep making his rallies wilder and more outrageous. And more coverage would follow.

  * Morality is a constructed artifact. It has no basis in mathematics or physics. A simple-minded take on this is that discarding any notion of morality and its hanger-ons -- norms and manners -- was an effective way of competing successfully against those hampered by proper behavior. Of course, what this misses is the next level of analysis: with a shared morality, cooperation, with an acceptably low number of murders, robberies, etc., is possible, which makes what we lightly term civilization possible, which allows higher achievements.

[Keithmasterson](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7488) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200705) | [November 7, 2024 20:36](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200729)

**1382:**

Take Trump out of the mix, which time will accomplish, and what's left looks more like the Reagan 80's or possibly the robber baron 1890s, just a big cluster of corporate interests. They need, and will fight against removal of certain things that keep their structure intact, like courts, public infrastructure, and at least enough regulation to keep entry of any new competitors into their markets prohibitively expensive.

Trump in the equation affects appearances more than reality, so the question becomes, how much wiggle room is there for anyone to fundamentally change things before unspoken corporate consensus blocks real action? Like a game of jenga, functional parts of society can be tampered with only so much before political tremors force immediate reassessment of strategy.

The 1980s I remember weren't all that terrific, Jim and Tammy Faye Bakker, Jimmy Swaggart, David Stockman, Oliver North, David Milliken the junk bond king of Drecksell Burnem, Donald Trump himself and assorted other frontmen for the rise of financial glorification. Which suggests that the real dangers ahead, aside from obvious climate threats, are more economic than socio-political, with mergers and monopolization feeding a brief stock market and real estate frenzy, ending in a severe recession. Depression and deflation might favor some of the old rentier money, but productive sectors will bolt from the sloppy ruling alliance when their gravy train slows down. And the political pendulum will swing leftward once again like it did after the 2008 credit crunch collapse.

So my point is, skip the existential angst, we've seen this bad movie before and muddled through, maybe this next time the public will smarten up a bit. The Boomers shuffling offstage leaves room for a new clique of millennial yuppies to get burned by their own adventurism, and to get disillusioned with the self defeating right wing nonsense, much the same as last time it all blew up. As RFK Senior said, "the tawch has been payassed to a noo generation."

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7913)

**1387:**

Yeah, on that project 2025 thing, don't worry about it, it's an artifact of ancient corruption. Right wing wish lists have been around since Nixon, and they haven't changed much in fifty years. Their perennial reissue and presentation as new and improved probably speaks more to the structure of "think tanks" like the Heritage Fund, which really serve as little more than booby hatches to pay off an elite corps of pinhead hack self proclaimed experts, political operatives past their prime, and close relatives of policy makers. 

This requires those time honored procedures by which to bribe and make payoffs without actually transferring legally trackable briefcases of cash. Such entities have to make at least a perfunctory show to justify their nonprofit status, so they need plausible products to display from time to time, and therefore they'll release "white papers" addressing favorite hobbyhorse topics, but it's really just recycling old crap that's been floating around since the so called Club of Rome way back in the 1970s. 

Nobody takes them seriously, they're like pass around party favors, projects to assign the least well connected member on the foundation payroll, most likely cobbled together by volunteer interns. I can imagine the phone calls, "Oh and don't forget that report, the senator's staff urgently needs it to prepare for our campaign" Yessir, right away sir, here ya go, project 2025! "Not a moment too soon, let me see that, perfect, just what we need to prop up this wobbly table leg, bonuses for everybody all around."

Kind of like the revolving door "consultancy" arrangements for Pentagon buyers and Wall Street regulators, only with more of an academic focus. What are you gonna do, this is human beings we're talking about, rents are high in Washington D.C. 

Beats having a Confucian essay contest, like they used to do with the old imperial civil service exam system in China.

<https://www.npr.org/2023/08/08/1192663920/southern-baptist-convention-donald-trump-christianity>

> **Who is he?** Russell Moore was one of the top officials in the Southern Baptist Convention. 
> 
> On why he thinks Christianity is in crisis: It was the result of having multiple pastors tell me, essentially, the same story about quoting the Sermon on the Mount, parenthetically, in their preaching — "turn the other cheek" — [and] to have someone come up after to say, "Where did you get those liberal talking points?" And what was alarming to me is that in most of these scenarios, when the pastor would say, "I'm literally quoting Jesus Christ," the response would not be, "I apologize." The response would be, "Yes, but that doesn't work anymore. That's weak." And when we get to the point where the teachings of Jesus himself are seen as subversive to us, then we're in a crisis.

Well, I think they're accurately labeled as [**Christian Nationalists**](https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2023/04/03/how-christian-is-christian-nationalism), more accurately described as an offshoot of the [**White Christian Nationalist**](https://www.cnn.com/2024/04/28/us/evangelical-christianity-false-white-gospel-wallis-cec/index.html) movement, a toxic blend of White Supremacists and militant "conservatives".

**1407:**

For more information, see the glossary in the article linked below:

[**Cracking the Christian nationalist code: A glossary for the confused**](https://www.salon.com/2024/05/26/cracking-the-christian-nationalist-code-a-glossary-for-the-confused/)

> In addition, the media generally fails to depict social and religious movements clearly or understand how they function. A handful of key terms can help clarify matters, from overarching concepts like “dominionism” to deliberately vague phrases like "covenant marriage" (as associated with House Speaker Mike Johnson) or "government schools" (rather than public schools). 
> 
> To help meet that challenge, I’ve put together the following glossary of terms, drawing heavily on the work of others: First there’s the [“Reporter’s Guide to the New Apostolic Reformation”](https://religiondispatches.org/christian-right-denialism-is-more-dangerous-than-ever-a-reporters-guide-to-the-new-apostolic-reformation/) co-authored by Gagné and Clarkson, recently published in an [updated and condensed version](https://religiondispatches.org/a-reporters-guide-to-the-new-apostolic-reformation/) with a glossary. Clarkson’s group has a broader [glossary](https://politicalresearch.org/glossary) of terms, including some that provide relevant context. And Julie Ingersoll, author of "Building God's Kingdom" (Salon interview [here](https://www.salon.com/2015/07/31/secrets_of_the_extreme_religious_right_inside_the_frightening_world_of_christian_reconstructionism/)), helpfully provided additional terms. 
> 
> The glossary is divided into three sections. First is an alphabetical set of terms with broader applicability, setting the stage for the terms that directly describe dominionism and its dominant forms. Second is a set of terms that describe America’s dominionist landscape, ordered to reflect its internal logic. Third is an alphabetical list of terms used by dominionists themselves, often with meanings that differ from their more conventional definitions. Millions of people who use these terms do not identify as dominionists, but are arguably helping to spread profoundly anti-democratic ideas. 
> 
> **Preliminary terms**
> 
> Christian nationalism: The belief that America was founded as, and intended by God to be, a Christian nation. This is based on an Old Testament-based worldview fusing Christian and American identities, rooted in parallels between America and Israel, which was commanded to maintain cultural and blood purity, often through war, conquest and separatism. It’s an important premise for most of the Christian right, which claims it is seeking to restore or reclaim this mandate. The Christian nationalist vision has been used, for example, by theologian Francis Schaeffer to justify the anti-abortion movement, and by advocates of dominionism to advance a theocratic society. Sometimes identified as “white Christian nationalism” to distinguish it from the Black Christian nationalist tradition identified with the civil rights movement. 
> 
> Christian Zionism: Primarily an evangelical doctrine, originating in 19th-century British premillennialism, that regards supporting the State of Israel as crucial to fulfilling prophecies in the Book of Revelation. This culminates in the battle of Armageddon, with the mass slaughter of Jews as Israel is covered in “a sea of human blood,” as described by Hagee. While Jewish Zionists hold a wide range of views and some support a Palestinian state, Christian Zionists oppose any such accommodation. In the extreme, they support Israel stretching from the Nile to the Euphrates, and are especially eager for war with Iran.

**1415:**

_Worse, skill at rationalization is often accompanied by skill at compartmentalization and forgetting, so they learn slowly if at all._

Once again I recommend Altemeyer's book _Authoritarian Nightmare_. Skip past the rants about Trump (which I suspect are mostly his coauthor's) and look at the studies about Trump's followers.

He looks at things like compartmentalization, and how that's a skill that children raised in authoritarian households have to learn young. Also looks at how some of Trump's biggest critics have become abject supporters (and the psychology behind their conversion). As well as many other points.

Well worth reading.

**1416:**

I’ll have to read it, thanks!

One thing to add is that compartmentalizing isn’t just a skill that’s learned in authoritarian households. It’s also fostered among caregivers in the medical profession, and I’ll bet in many others. I’d also say, from experience, that deliberately de compartmentalizing isn’t an unalloyed boon, either.

Oh well.

Incidentally, one of the lefty writers I like, Josh Marshall, is suggesting that some data suggest that Harris lost because of the late switch from Biden. The evidence is that the public turned towards Trump in most states compared with 2020, but less so in the battleground states that she saturated with ads. I doubt we’ll ever know, but if this happens to be the case, 2022 is going to be a brutal election for the Republicans, along the lines of what happens in a toxic relationship when you go back to someone and remember why you dumped them in the first place.
