**1419:**

There is a recent relevant a US Congressional Report Service PDF article R44648 on the current post-election presidential succession: 

  * [R44648 - Presidential Elections: Vacancies in Major-Party Candidacies and the Position of President-Elect, October 6, 2016 – October 9, 2020](https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R44648)



> **Between the Election and the Electoral College Meeting**. On election day, voters choose members of the electoral college, which formal y selects the President- and Vice President-elect several weeks later, December 14 in 2020. Although the transition has begun, party rules still apply: a replacement candidate would be chosen by the national committees of either party, or by a reassembled Republican National Convention.
> 
> Notwithstanding these party rules, the presidential nominee would likely have a decisive influence in selecting a replacement for a departed vice-presidential running mate. If a presidential nominee were to leave the ticket, the vice-presidential nominee would arguably not be considered the automatic successor for their running mate at the top of the ticket, although they would likely be seriously considered.
> 
> **Between the Electoral College Meeting and Inauguration**. The balance of scholarly opinion holds that the President- and Vice President-elect are chosen once the electoral votes are cast. The electoral votes are counted and declared by a joint session of Congress, held January 6 of the year following the election, although Congress occasional y sets a different date for the joint session.
> 
> During this period, succession is covered by the Twentieth Amendment to the Constitution: if the President-elect dies, the Vice President-elect becomes President-elect. Although the amendment does not specifically address the issues of disability, disqualification, or resignation during this period, its language, “failure to qualify,” could arguably be interpreted to cover such contingencies. Vacancies in the position of Vice President-elect are not mentioned in the Twentieth Amendment; they would be covered after the inauguration by the Twenty-Fifth Amendment.
> 
> If no person qualifies as President or Vice President by inauguration day, then the Succession Act (3 U.S.C. 19) applies: the Speaker of the House of Representatives, the President pro tempore of the Senate, and duly confirmed Cabinet officers, in that order, would act as President. 
> 
> Succession Between Election Day and the Meeting of the Electoral College: Party Rules Still Apply
> 
> Presidential transitions are generally considered to begin immediately after election day. Throughout the first phase of transition, which lasts through the meetings of the electoral college, 7 the winning candidates are considered to be the next President and Vice President, but their status is informal and presumptive. There actually is no President- or Vice President-elect until after the electoral votes have been cast. The apparent winners, during this first period, are still the nominees of their parties. The use of the titles “President-elect” and “Vice President-elect” is a time-honored courtesy that anticipates the ultimate status of the candidates who won an apparent majority of electoral votes.

Note: There is a **potentially grim loophole** in the article ["Constitutional Topic: Death of a Presidential Candidate"](https://www.usconstitution.net/consttop_pvp-html/) at USConstitution.net, but I'm not certain the italicized segment on elected candidate death matches what congressional service report R44648 calls "failure to qualify":

> **After the election**
> 
> While it may seem odd for voters to cast their ballots for someone who has recently died, if the death should occur after the election, but before the electors cast their ballots on Elector Day, the onus will fall on the electors. Constitutionally, the electors are always able to exercise their judgement when they cast their ballots, though except on rare occasions, they always vote for whomever the presidential candidate is. In the event of a death, the party will choose an alternate candidate for the electors to vote for, and direct the electors to do so. The electors will have to make their own decision if they wish to stick with the party’s choice or not.
> 
> At this point a question may arise: What if one of the electors dies? This eventuality should be anticipated by state law. For example, in Vermont, on Elector Day, the assembled electors choose an alternate at the time of the meeting. The elector so-chosen then submits his or her vote with the other electors (17 VSC 2732).
> 
> **After the electors vote**
> 
> Once the electors’ votes are cast, the votes are bundled and sent to Congress. It is here, if the candidate dies after the electors vote, that the election can really turn on a dime.
> 
> The votes for president are sent to the House to be counted, while the votes for the vice president are sent to the Senate for counting. **_Any ballot which is invalid would not be counted – and a ballot cast for a person who is dead would not be considered valid_**. In this case, if the dead person was the winner of the electoral votes cast, and assuming a normal two-party election, the person with the most valid electoral votes would be the person who actually received the lower portion of the electoral votes.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8718)

You are quite welcome!

By some coincidence, there were quite a few article published in 2020, after trump was hospitalized for COVID-19, and both candidates were elderly men.

So, here are some more article links from my presidential-elect succession web foraging:

  * [Presidential Succession: Perspectives and Contemporary Issues for Congress](https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R46450), Jul. 14, 2020
  * [Presidential Succession: An Overview with Analysis of Legislation Proposed in the 108th Congress](https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/RL/RL31761/3), June 29, 2005 
  * [What happens if the winning presidential candidate becomes incapacitated before taking office? (2020)](https://ballotpedia.org/What_happens_if_the_winning_presidential_candidate_becomes_incapacitated_before_taking_office%3F_\(2020)), see also the [related articles and footnotes](https://ballotpedia.org/What_happens_if_the_winning_presidential_candidate_becomes_incapacitated_before_taking_office%3F_\(2020)#Related_articles) links in this article
  * [What happens if a president-elect dies before inauguration?](https://qz.com/1929017/what-happens-if-biden-dies-before-inauguration-day)
  * [AP EXPLAINS: What happens if a candidate for president dies?](https://apnews.com/article/election-2020-donald-trump-constitutions-elections-us-supreme-court-91ce484b8046e6a555e172f42c4441f9)
  * [What happens if a president or nominee dies or is incapacitated? Around elections, it gets thorny](https://www.politifact.com/article/2020/oct/02/what-happens-if-president-or-nominee-dies-or-incap/), 2020
  * [What if the U.S. president and vice president-elect die before being sworn in?](https://web.archive.org/web/20210901091309/https://www.straightdope.com/21341305/what-if-the-u-s-president-and-vice-president-elect-die-before-being-sworn-in)

The outcome of the election is: America does not believe in America.

Against-America _won the popular vote._

America was already over as soon as the Democratic Party conceded that that guy was a real, possible candidate for President in 2024, instead of a traitorous multiple felon. The Republican party pushed this line and the Democrats went along, and there is _no other representation available_ in the United States.

Or maybe America was already over when they decided to wait until after the election to prosecute him.

Or maybe it died after _Citizens United_ or earlier than that. It doesn't matter. 

What matters is that America has decided that America is over. Anyone can look at the United States Government and its constitution and see the disconnects there. Americans seem not to believe in Constitutional crises, but they are as real as constitutions are, as real as nations. The US has been stuck in multiple overlapping such crises since at least 2000. Its institutions will not fix this. The end is not "coming". It has come, it was here, it goes on.

**1428:**

I know Charlie is not a great fan of the Guardian, but the following article — suitably rewritten — might make a good chew toy for a bunch of aging SciFi fans:

<https://www.theguardian.com/tv-and-radio/2024/nov/08/gabriel-gatehouse-coming-storm-conspiracy-theories-trump-america>

The thesis is that William Rees-Mogg’s 1997 book is being used by billionaires as a play book to make themselves “Sovereign Individuals”, using “accelerationism” to move fast and break things in late stage capitalism. So far, nothing new.

But it has me wondering how “they” expect this to work; in short how can one become independent of any society and yet retain wealth. In medieval terms, what good is a pile of gold if the rest of us choose to ignore it? In more modern terms what good does it do you if the ones and zeros are favourably arranged in your bank account or bit coin wallet? 

The only billionaire who I can think of as fully Sovereign is MSB, and of course when he inherits that’s precisely what he’ll be. We could discuss whether Putin qualifies, but I don’t know enough about the power structures supporting him. And then there’s the next President who’s been handed Imperial Power. But how does that help other billionaires realise their Sovereignty?

**1431:**

_This is going to get ugly or Trump will just claim it is going marvelously and ignore that hardly anything is being done._

I see three levels to this. One is that Trumps going to lie about whatever’s going on, once extent that he can.

Another is that the threat of deportation will be used to force more undocumented workers into more inhumane working conditions. Use of migrants is very much a successor to slavery, and unfortunately we’ve wedded our food supply to it, via the mechanism of keeping food cheap to keep wages down across the board.

A third is that if companies can figure out how to make money of contracts for migrant concentration camps, some will certainly try. They’re already getting pretty miserable along the border.

Greg,

I agree about the camps, though it’ll take a long time to get it all going.

But I disagree about the lynch mobs, at least on a large scale. The Rural USA is pretty uniformly Republican, so there won’t be many targets for any putative MAGA mobs. They’d need to be organised to travel into the ‘burbs, which they regard with horror. The American public needs to be desensitised to violence on their doorsteps. Obviously, if that starts to happen, it’s time to worry (more).

Now, I’m not saying it can’t be done, but again it’ll take quite some time. My advice to the older US members here is to feign dementia and hearing loss if they run into trouble. That won’t save you if you’re in the target demographic, but might work against opportunistic threats. More long term one might try getting MAGA factions fighting one another, though that can be dangerous. Of course having developed a mildly eccentric persona might protect a bit.

The near-term goals will be trying to capture those parts of the media that he currently views as non-compliant. Plus intimidating Republicans in Congress and the Senate.

Luckily, like Hitler, he usually tries to play off supplicants against one another. That should thin the field quite a bit. And there’s no good reason to think that competence will have much to do with who wins and who loses. Since it’s grift all the way down, those that talk the best game live to fight another day, and those who quietly get on with things can expect summary dismissal.

**1444:**

Sigh. Elon Musk’s citizenship is his 413 billion dollars, worth of estimated net worth. If he was a country, he’d be Argentina and Qatar in total worth. ( <https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_total_wealth> ).

Considering how miserably the US DOJ fared against Trump, whose net worth is about 1% of Musk’s, don’t think that they have a chance in hell of touching Musk unless he goes bankrupt. I mean, he’s the quintessential security risk, but he has clearance because of SpaceX regardless. He untouchable right now.

That said, Musk is the real wild card in this. If he gets rolled by Trump and Putin, the question is whether he loses his reputation and goes bankrupt, or whether he uses things like Twitter, SpaceX, Starlink, and the Tesla charging network to ratfuck them back. I mean, he can literally keep the US military out of space right now. Will he?

Conversely, if he tries to gut the federal government handouts, which, again, go more to Red than Blue states, we’ll find that a lot of his value is reputational, and he’s screwed, unless he abandons the effort. Which he probably will, actually.

So we can take it as likely that Trump will screw him over, and he’ll likely damage himself. Then what? Unlike basically everyone else, that’s not the end of his story.

…..

What I don’t know is how seriously he takes climate change right now. If he’s not totally delusional, conceivably, if he’s in power he might force the US into renewable electrification. My environmental friends will hate this, because a number of species will go extinct due to mining and badly sited wind and solar farms, but he’s on the same financial scale as the US oil industry. If he has officiall government power, he absolutely can mess with them. He can’t put them out of business, but he can make them into a supplier of plastics and industrial and military fuels, while he phases out a lot of their business faster than they want. How many people will this frack? A lot. A bunch of states too. The payoff for him is if he gets to own the national car charging grid. And given that his Tesla stations are more reliable than any other network and the other car companies are switching to his standard, he might have an incentive to try.

Will he do this? Unlikely, especially while Trump’s in control, because no one takes Trump’s spotlight. If Vance becomes President, I think it’s more likely, because of the massive power imbalance. Who’s Vance without billionaire support?

I'm not an immigration attorney, so I have no expert opinion 😉 - but a near worst-case scenario could create a MASSIVE expatriation tax bill 😈

I read that exposure as all $200+ billion of Musk's assets are IMMEDIATELY taxed as income on expatriation, as if he sold them... 

That exposure requires no new laws, simply corrupt application of existing immigration and tax laws.

Truly worst case scenarios would include Enabling Act forced assets forfeiture and divestment

trump, with his newly-minted immunity, once his 'Schedule F' loyalists take over the civil service (e.g. the state department) - NONE of the assumed norms should be assumed to remain - He threatened to deport jack smith in october

IIRC, it's a policy, not a law, that the US doesn't make people 'stateless' by stripping their citizenship...

the point of the "schedule F" reference was that that rule change - that he previously created so late in 2020 that Biden could revoke it - effectively would allow trump to fire most US federal employees at his whim. And his Project2025/Agenda47 helpers have already been vetting their replacements, with personal loyalty to trump as MAGA believers as the top qualification for hiring their replacements.

so, the kinds of federal staff who refused/debated/ignored trumps illegal commands in his first term - such as when he asked the Secretary of Defense and Joint Chiefs to shoot the black lives matter protesters - probably will be purged very early in a second trump term

<https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual/volume-12-part-l-chapter-2>

> "A citizen will be treated as relinquishing his or her U.S. citizenship on the earliest of four possible dates: 3\. the date the U.S. Department of State issues to the individual a certificate of loss of nationality; or 4\. the date a U.S. court cancels a naturalized citizen’s certificate of naturalization."
> 
> "IRC 877A imposes a mark-to-market regime, which generally means that all property of a covered expatriate is deemed sold for its fair market value on the day before the expatriation date."
> 
> "Under IRC 877A, individuals who are “covered expatriates” are treated as having disposed of all worldwide assets on the day before their expatriation date, are required to pay a mark-to-market exit tax on the gain (subject to an exclusion amount) resulting from the deemed disposition of their worldwide assets, and are subject to additional tax consequences with respect to certain deferred compensation items and trust distributions."

**1449:**

The scale and scope of potential damage seems limited only by the degree to which employees of the executive branch and federal judiciary comply with the whims of an autocrat.

We don't yet know how much compliance will happen, nor do we know how much effective resistance will oppose the autocrat.

Note: the existing US Federal and State laws on [assets forfeiture](https://www.justice.gov/afp/types-federal-forfeiture) are already a nightmare 

e.g. seizing assets and then creating a civil court case against the **ASSETS** themselves

or using that power in a claimed RICO prosecution of an alleged conspiracy ... so as to seize all assets claimed to be involved with the conspiracy or considered revenue/profit from the conspiracy, or worse (see below).

two wikipedia references:

  * [US Law section of Assets Forfeiture article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asset_forfeiture#United_States)
  * [Civil forfeiture in the United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Civil_forfeiture_in_the_United_States)



Here's a nasty note from 1988 on the potential scope of assets forfeiture allowing seizure of entire businesses:

[RICO Forfeitures of Business: The Scope of Forfeiture Should Fit the Crime](https://www.ojp.gov/ncjrs/virtual-library/abstracts/rico-forfeitures-business-scope-forfeiture-should-fit-crime)

> **Annotation**
> 
> Criminal forfeiture provisions under the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act (RICO) have been litigated for 10 years, but it is not yet clear how those provisions should apply to businesses.
> 
> **Abstract**
> 
> RICO forfeiture provisions forfeit capital interests in corporations and partnerships. Currently, **RICO provisions can be interpreted to authorize forfeiture of a defendant's entire enterprise regardless of the amount of illicit profits or the seriousness of the crime. Under existing case law, a Federal district court has no statutory base or discretion to calibrate the enterprise interest forfeiture and thus, a defendant's interest could be forfeited even though racketeering played only a small part in acquiring or maintaining that interest**. Courts have observed in dicta that disproportionately large RICO forfeitures constituted cruel and unusual punishment under the eighth amendment. The challenge for Congress and the courts is to devise a statutory or constitutional method by which the most serious RICO sanctions are applied only to the most serious crimes.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

Musk is a security risk in part because he’s holding private talks with Putin. First, Putin also holds private talks with Trump, so do you think he’s interested in his two best assets having a falling out? And if he can’t stop it, where do you think Musk will end up, with lots of SpaceX secrets in tow?

The thing to realize is that, for billionaires, citizenship is literally negotiable. The fact that Musk violated his green card terms is about as relevant as the fact that Melanie did too.

In the bigger picture, what we’re seeing has been going on for decades: the super-rich taking over the world and imposing a feudal system that has similarities with Medieval feudalism, among other things. Democracy never entirely died under feudalism, and it won’t entirely die now. The problem is that the world’s way too complex for autocrats, no matter how smart they are, so we’re in for a mess.

Incidentally, I’m reading _Authoritarian Nightmare_. Thanks! I’m matching it with Rick Wilson’s _Everything Trump Touches Dies_ , which is also very good and has a good takedown of Democratic failures too. The author is a long time Republican campaign operative.

Another take on the election ( paywalled at <https://voiceofsandiego.org/2024/11/09/politics-report-it-was-baked-months-ago/> ):

“The presidential race? It was baked a year ago. All those billions of dollars, months of angst, the Biden withdrawal and Harris nomination. All pointless. The election would have looked largely the same a year ago as it did this week.

“David Wasserman, the analyst who came for Politifest, crunched the numbers. “… across the seven battleground states, the ’20-’24 swing towards Trump was ~3.1 pts. Across the other 43 states (+DC), it was ~6.7 pts,” he wrote.[looks to be on Twitter, but I’m having trouble finding the tweet]

“In other words, the inertia toward former President Donald Trump was nearly 7 points. As evidenced by the margin in swing states, the $1 billion Harris campaign was able to slow and reverse some of it, but not close to enough.

“Stuff costs too much: People frustrated with inflation and immigration, and blaming all of it on President Biden and de facto Vice President Kamala Harris was all that seemed to matter in the end. It was baked early. Biden’s decision to run for re-election and eliminate any serious primary challenge set it in motion.”

If this analysis is correct, let’s see what Trump does with the economy and social programs, shall we?

We don't know yet, and we really cannot know yet, as it depends on the collective individual responses of tens or hundreds of thousand of armed people.

We shall see how it goes over the next couple of years.

There are examples of other very wealthy people who appeared untouchable, but later were touched when they fell out of favor with authoritarian leaders.

Some, like Jack Ma, vanished into custody before emerging as a more compliant Chinese citizen.

Others, like Russian Oligarchs, have taken up defenestration as an extreme sport.

**1453:**

In less crapsack world news, two of the authors behind [Selectorate Theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selectorate_theory) believe that converting the United States into a full autocracy is an impractical transition.

Let's hope their opinions are correct...

[Will Trump try to end democracy? Yes — but these scholars claim he can't pull it off](https://www.salon.com/2024/09/29/will-try-to-end-democracy-yes--but-these-scholars-claim-he-cant-pull-it-off/)

**"Dictator's Handbook" authors say a second Trump term will be dreadful — but he can't push US into autocracy**

Published September 29, 2024

> In an autocracy, all that’s required for the leader to stay in power is for him or her to keep a small group of cronies happy — think of the oligarchs and military leaders in Vladimir Putin’s Russia, or the inner circle of Kim Jong-un’s North Korea. The leader secures the loyalty of this tiny coalition through corrupt practices, often heavily taxing the outsiders (in other words, most of the society) and then redistributing this wealth to cronies in the form of “private goods” for them. Hence, the essential supporters of these autocrats are given special access to expensive cars, luxury apartments, the best schools for their children, family vacations on the beach and so on. For the cronies, life is pretty darn good, while everyone else in society struggles to get by.
> 
> Trump 2.0 will likely be so disastrous, the authors claim, that Trump will lose some of his base. They might still support his policies, but they will be so negatively impacted that they’ll turn against the man they voted for.
> 
> If this coalition expands, the number of people the leader would need to keep happy will also increase. As a result, the welfare of the leader’s cronies begins to decline. Why? Because there’s only so much money for the leader to throw around, and the more people there are on the gravy train, the less there is for each individual insider. People in the coalition, therefore, oppose such an expansion: They don’t want to share the private goods they’ve become accustomed to receiving. It’s a kind of local minimum in which autocratic societies sometimes get stuck, although it’s not impossible to escape that trap, and in fact the number of autocratic countries has declined over the past century and a half while the number of democratic countries has risen. (Nearly everyone lived in autocratic states in 1850, while just over 50% of countries today are more or less democratic.) 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6643)

**1455:**

> In less crapsack news, two of the authors behind [Selectorate theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selectorate_theory) believe that converting the United States into a full autocracy is an impractical transition.
> 
> Let's hope their opinions are correct...

In case anyone is unfamiliar with the political science Selectorate theory introduced in [**The Logic of Political Survival**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Logic_of_Political_Survival), there are a couple of [CGP Grey](https://www.youtube.com/@CGPGrey) videos produced in 2016 that were adapted from ["**The Dictator's Handbook: Why Bad Behavior is Almost Always Good Politics** "](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Dictator%27s_Handbook), the mass market explanation of their theory.

  * [The Rules for Rulers](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rStL7niR7gs)
  * [Death & Dynasties](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ig_qpNfXHIU)



The selectorate the president-elect used to gain power seems fueled by groups motivated by some combination of greed, racial resentment, economic class grievances, hunger for power, frustration with the status quo, and the desier to punish some presumed external source of their problems (e.g. othering migrants).

So, we have a large group of [Authoritarian Followers](https://theauthoritarians.org/donald-trump-and-authoritarian-followers/) in a cult of personality around a would-be autocrat, with some wildly differeing end goals . Fun Times ahead.

[**Selectorate theory summary**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selectorate_theory#Overview):

> The fundamental premise in selectorate theory is that the primary goal of a leader - regardless of secondary policy concerns[2] - is to remain in power. To remain in power, leaders must retain support from every member of their winning coalition. When the winning coalition is small, as in autocracies, the leader will tend to use private goods to satisfy the coalition. When the winning coalition is large, as in democracies, the leader will tend to use public goods to satisfy the coalition.
> 
> In The Dictator's Handbook, Bueno de Mesquita and Smith state five rules that leaders should use to stay in power:
> 
>   * The smaller the winning coalition, the fewer people to satisfy to remain in control.
>   * The larger the selectorate, the easier it is to replace dissenters in the winning coalition.
>   * Extract as much wealth as you can from the population without provoking a rebellion or an economic recession.
>   * Give your essential supporters just enough rewards to keep them loyal.
>   * The remaining funds are yours to spend at your discretion, but do not give your essential supporters extra rewards or they will grow too independent and become a threat.
> 


[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Damian](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6134) replied to [this comment from amalgamy](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200821) | [November 9, 2024 23:56](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200827)

**1457:**

I think this is another way of making the same observation I've been passing off to my friends and others who listen as the main saving grace and pathway for hope with the Trump administration. It goes more or less like this: the first time around, Trump's organisation tried to do a range of things that were impractical, damn stupid or wildly authorisation and adversarial to the continuation of democracy (as we know it anyway), and for the most part the reasons this organisation did not succeed in implementing these changes were much less to do with resistance from institutions and individuals, and much more to do with incompetence and an inability to take in information and advice about how it could be done. 

While I have no doubt there are thousands of people who are entirely capable of steering a Trumpian vision through the various obstacles it would need to navigate, and what's more people who are highly motivated to sell their abilities to Trump's organisation, the thing I see as the saving grace is that the incompetence in the latter goes so deep that it makes the sort of elementary HR processes required to work with the former impossible. To be blunt, the Heritage Fund people could all be Machiavellian geniuses, but they'll never convince Trump they know better than he does about how to implement policy X, and therefore policy X will go down in flames, with Hollywood explosions and everything. 

And if you were to counter that the organisation has had time to learn, I'd say that it's an organisation that does not learn. In fact part of its very nature is represented by its built-in mechanisms that prevent learning. If you dig deep enough that's actually where its appeal lies too, and it plays into a dynamic that conservatives have been grappling with - that you can't have the Trumpian electoral miracle without the Trumpian personality, and the incompetence is a baked in feature, not a divisible aspect at all. 

So while the outcomes in terms of impacts to human life, suffering, infrastructure, trashing the US economy (perhaps doing it permanent damage) and so on look like they are very real, it seems quite likely that US democracy, institutions and ways of life are, if not exactly safe, at least quite possibly beyond the skill level of any Trumpian administration to destroy. Not because it's impossible to do, just because the skills required to do it are things the Trumpian mindset actively disdains, pushes away and distances itself from. This sort of populism is strongly anti-intellectual, and this isn't a pattern unique to the USA. And that's a hell of a weakness. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8718)

**1460:**

A question for former military members: to what extent might people obey lawful orders (which they are obligated to follow) that they know are corrupt and immoral - and which previously would have been unlawful orders (which they are obligated NOT to follow)? 

Also note that SCOTUS explicitly granted a US President the power to order the Department of Justice to open an investigation on anyone he desires, and granted official-acts immunity from prosecution for a vaguely defined scope of _core powers and closely related conduct_ as president - despite the **_explicit questions before the Appeals Court and Supreme Court about a President ordering assassinations of his political opponents_**

I was disappointed that none of the lawyers explicitly pointed out that a sitting president could then kill any member of Congress attempting to impeach him, as well as any Supreme Court justice opposing him - such as taking revenge for the SCOTUS decisions against Trump's 2020 stolen election claims

> We conclude that under our constitutional structure of separated powers, the nature of Presidential power requires that a former President have some immunity from criminal prosecution for official acts during his tenure in office. At least **with respect to the President’s exercise of his core constitutional powers, this immunity must be absolute. As for his remaining official actions, he is also entitled to immunity**.
> 
> we conclude that the separation of powers principles explicated in our precedent necessitate a**t least a presumptive immunity from criminal prosecution for a President’s acts within the outer perimeter of his official responsibility**.
> 
> Justice Amy Coney Barrett agreed with the majority “that the Constitution prohibits Congress from criminalizing a President’s **exercise” of his core constitutional powers and “closely related conduct**.”

So, he likely will have the power and authority to cause quite a lot of damage without facing any legal hazard.

**1464:**

_I'm actually kind of surprised that those who think all Americans are heartless fascists haven't pointed to the shit we pull on our southern border as an example_

TBH I do occasionally look at San Diego-Tijuana in google maps and street view and stuff. More places too, but that's a pretty interesting one. In just a slightly better world it would be one big city with limited if any restrictions on movement across the border in either direction, people would live and work wherever it suited them. That's something that people in Europe have been able to realise (I think of Copenhagen-Malmo, but really the Schengen Area has made it a commonplace), and I suspect it's not too far off that with places like Detroit-Windsor. 

In my head, whether it's entirely right or perhaps slightly off the mark, but I'm pretty sure it's not too far off, the USA already has the most subsidised and the most tariff-protected agriculture in the world (and the most aggressive stance in the WTO for that matter). It also depends on cheap-to-free labour from undocumented workers via the porous southern border. To me the fact it's already fully leveraged in every other way possible means that a crackdown and mass deportation/incarceration of undocumented workers will mean a dramatic crash in US food security and the price of domestic produce, something that further poking tariff stuff won't help. 

The Trumpian solution presumably entails moving the incarceration industry fully into the realm of modern slave labour, and I suppose that is an amazing opportunity for those who see what they could do with it. It will mint more fresh billionaires who will owe it all to Trump.

No Greg, you’re getting ahead of yourself.

An Enabling Act would require the House and the Senate to pass such legislation. Under normal circumstances they will not relinquish any of their power, but perhaps after a few assassinations they’ll all fold like cheap suits.

Currently, there are rumours of discontent with Mike Johnson, and Mitch McConnell is organising his successor tomorrow — ahead of the new Senate, with much wailing and gnashing of teeth amongst MAGA — with the express idea to freeze out Trump supporter Rick Scott.

As to your fears about Theocracy, these are unfounded because such ideas are utterly impractical. Remember _You and I_ both live in a vestigial Theocracy; does this impact you? Do you even notice it in day-to-day life? Of course not. Well, if a US Theocracy ever gets started, English Theocracy is where it’ll end.

As to why I’m extremely sceptical about a US Theocracy ever getting started: religious disagreements amongst Christian Sects. If there is ever any concrete proposal about legislating religious belief, the howls of protest from Christians will completely drown those from anyone else. The worst I expect is a suggestion that there be School Prayers, subsequently retracted when Pagans demand equal time. Or Catholics. Or Latter Day Saints, or, … well actually a near endless number of possibilities.

Where you are right of course are your worries about subsequent elections. They should be able to sort out election stealing for a typical election swing, but may struggle if things went bad financially very quickly. See the demise of PIS in Poland for an example of a fairly right wing party eventually getting the boot.

I guess we’ll see. I’m more concerned about the charisma-free Labour leadership going up against Farage after five years of austerity.

San Ysidro. And its two neighbors are the crossings between Mexico and the US, and they’re the busiest border crossings in the world. Local traffic reports give the wait times at the border, and any people live on one side and work on the other. Heck, school kids live in TJ and go to school in the US. Tijuana was largely built up by American businessmen.

The border fence is fairly new, actually, and most of the more objectionable shit dates from the War on Terror. Prior to that you could go to Friendship Park on the beach ad party internationally

The detainee camps are east of there, out in the desert, including in Arizona and Texas. They’re currently designed to be miserable and cruel to discourage asylum seekers and others from coming across. 

I’m not going to get into what I expect around the Border, but I need to point out, based on what happened last time, that our border system is underfunded and overwhelmed, and that it’s possible to cause enormous suffering just by underfunding and overwhelming them some more. The performative cruelty is the point.

**1477:**

Greg, if you want to worry about theocracy, you’ve got to take your blinders off.

The US, as with the UK and almost the entire globe, has been a polytheistic theocracy for most of a century.

Christianity, Islam, and Judaism? Hah. Those are the minor cults. The Great God of civilization is, of course, money. It’s worth what we believe it’s worth. The dollar is backed by the full faith and credit of the United States. Magnates who we believe have a lot of money are worshipped for their power, even though what they are worth is a matter of belief and speculation, not fact.

If you don’t believe in other gods like Jesus or Allah, but you do believe in money, you’re a monotheist, not an atheist. That’s fine. True atheism, that doesn’t even venerate money, is rare and considered dangerously nihilistic, probably with cause.

The currently dominant sect of Mammonite money worship is the revolutionary capitalism unleashed by the collapse of hard-line communism in the 1990s. It’s been on the March ever since, and probably it will go do in history as the most successful religion ever.

So yes, we live in a theocracy, all of us. And yes, the US re-elected a billionaire, even though we knew he couldn’t govern for shit. The default government that is consonant with revolutionary capitalism is, of course, kleptocracy, not anything more functional. But with Trump, belief gets in the way of analysis or even self-interest sometimes. 

That’s the big fat secret with worshipping money. It works better than what Christ or Marx preached for governing large groups, but it is, by itself, not good enough. We know this, just like we know that money requires belief to work, just like any religion. But we don’t talk about it, it’s too gauche. 

Reality is messy, and governance has to be focused, above all, on keeping the system going, not on ideological purity. Worshipping money gets in the way of that, just as any other fervent worship does. Government has to be pragmatically polytheistic if you want it to work, and, like any group of soldiers going into combat, it will never pass ideological inspection when it is in working order.

Hope that helps or something.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

**1490:**

Speaking generally, a nation state might designate any action lawful within its borders. International human rights law might offer an avenue of redress, but it might not help much if the country involved does not recognise universal jurisdiction or the jurisdiction of the ICC to prosecute crimes against humanity. I think at this stage the USA and Russia are the only two countries to have previously ratified the Statute of Rome, but subsequently withdrawn from it, although several players never signed in the first place. The USA is the only country that has signed but not ratified the Convention on the Elimination of all forms of Discrimination Against Women (several have done neither). 

Also speaking generally, there is a presumption that individuals can and should operate their own ethical judgement about their actions. Even Nazi Germany did not, for the most part, force its soldiers to murder civilians and for the most part didn't punish those who refused to do so (surprisingly in some cases, per the Tim Schneider book). This might not apply to a frontline soldier in the heat of combat, but it's still a presumption at least from the junior officer level up. 

And while some (nation) states might not recognise universal jurisdiction it still means that the subject of an ICJ arrest warrant can have difficulty travelling outside those states. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8718)

[amalgamy](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8718) replied to [this comment from JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200854) | [November 11, 2024 03:17](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200868)

**1491:**

Thanks for the article link, it led to a very relevant legal opinion.

For those readers like me, with neither military experience nor deep outsider knowledge of the US military, the author of this article formerly held the second-highest legal rank in the US Air Force Judge Advocate General's Corps (military branch lawyers and prosecutors).

His article is partly in response to other op-eds articles promoting the idea of military resistance to lawful-but-awful orders.

Given his service in the organization that would prosecute personnel for refusing orders,this is a pretty authoritative statement on the **_legality_** of disobedience, independent of the perceived morality of an order.

[**Yes, the law of military orders matters, and here’s how**](https://sites.duke.edu/lawfire/2024/11/02/yes-the-law-of-military-orders-matters-and-heres-how/)

> by Charlie Dunlap, J.D. 
> 
>   * Charles J. Dunlap Jr., the former deputy judge advocate general of the United States Air Force, joined the Duke Law faculty in July 2010 where he is a professor of the practice of law and Executive Director of the Center on Law, Ethics and National Security
> 

> 
> 2 November 2024 

Early segment:

> **Lawful orders must still be obeyed even if they offend an individual’s personal ethics, political judgements or moral beliefs**.
> 
> To reiterate, lawfulness is the decisive issue. Along that line, military law provides a rather wide definitional aperture. 
> 
> The all-civilian Court of Appeals for the Armed Forces (CAAF) cited with approval a section of the Manual for Courts-Martial that says that a lawful order must relate to a military duty, but such duty “includes all activities reasonably necessary to accomplish a military mission, or safeguard or promote the morale, discipline, and usefulness of members of a command and directly connected with the maintenance of good order in the service.”
> 
> We need to keep in mind that in a democracy, elected leaders have a right to enact policies they promised to put in place if elected. Not everyone will agree with those policies but, so long as any directives implementing those policies are lawful, the military must carry them out.
> 
> Unfortunately, none of the writers made it clear that military law does not brook disobedience of lawful orders, even if the troop concerned sincerely thinks the orders are very unwise, and even damaging to national security

Later segment:

> **Patently illegal orders**
> 
> The issue of “patently” illegal orders was discussed by the all-civilian Court of Military Appeals in the case of Lt. William Calley. (The CMA was the predecessor to today’s similarly all-civilian CAAF).
> 
> Calley was the infamous commander in the Vietnam era My Lai massacre incident. The court in his case described a patently illegal order as “one which a man of ordinary sense and understanding would, under the circumstances, know to be unlawful, or if the order in question is actually known to the accused to be unlawful.”
> 
> Moreover, the court cited with approval this quote from Colonel William Winthrop (1831-1899), who the Supreme Court calls the “The Blackstone of Military Law”:
> 
> “But for the inferior to assume to determine the question of the lawfulness of an order given him by a superior would of itself, as a general rule, amount to insubordination, and such an assumption carried into practice would subvert military discipline. Where the order is apparently regular and lawful on its face, he is not to go behind it to satisfy himself that his superior has proceeded with authority, but is to obey it according to its terms, the only exceptions recognized to the rule of obedience being cases of orders so manifestly beyond the legal power or discretion of the commander as to admit of no rational doubt of their unlawfulness…”(Emphasis added.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6643)

**1493:**

_Musk likely will benefit from any protectionist policies re: EVs. (Wonder who supplies his EV parts.)_

He's made a real push to bring everything in-house as much as possible. He'll have to persuade Trump that Taiwan isn't the same as China, because he gets a lot of chips from there.

I was watching a program on EVs last night. According to it one of the biggest reasons we don't have cheap cars in North America is that activist investors on the boards of car companies are pushing for higher profits every quarter, so the companies are emphasizing profit-per-unit rather than higher volumes, which means selling fewer but more expensive models, while the Chinese are playing the long game and willing to make smaller per-unit profits to grab market share. The presenter joked that in China they design in months and expect profits in years, while in America they expect profits in months and design in years.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

**1494:**

And apropos of Tesla (or any other car with electronic door locks), make sure you know how to get to the manual override. They are apparently not very obvious (and can be quite difficult to get to). After a crash has disabled the high-voltage power system you only have about 10-15 minutes of power left in the low-voltage system (which includes the doors). 

_For front passengers, the manual door release is an unmarked lever directly in front of the window controls… For rear passengers, the manual release first requires you to pull out a mat at the bottom of the rear door pocket, then remove an access door to finally pull a mechanical cable._

<https://www.thestar.com/news/gta/she-couldn-t-get-out-deadly-toronto-tesla-fire-draws-attention-to-risk-of-electronic/article_c9313fbe-9ad0-11ef-998a-93ba9a9927d5.html>

[DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5685) | [November 11, 2024 16:33](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200875)

**1497:**

My opinion, with respect to the academics cited above, is that they make some good points, but ultimately I think they are being too optimistic. Not that I think that the US is likely to become an authoritarian dictatorship, but I do think the odds of the Trump administration doing serious and permanent damage to our constitutional rights is significant--force me to give a number and I might say 20%. Odds against, but not enough for comfort (I learned as a tabletop player long ago not to ignore <50% chances--the other side can get lucky). 

There are several paths for Trump to leverage power. A terrorist attack, for instance, or large scale protests, could lead him to declare emergency powers. A war. A large scale purge of government officials. Encouraging the militia movement. Serious legal harassment of his political opponents. None of which would directly harm his core base. They don't care about anything except treating their mass case of social anxiety disorder. 

The two things the Selectorate Theory people underestimate, IMHO, is the effect of increasing wealth disparity--reading that graph of welfare by Coalition Size, it seems to me that a perception on the part of the "Outsiders" (common people) that they are making less money effectively moves us to the left, toward authoritarianism, as does a perception that the welfare of the Coalition is increasing. Both of those things are happening. 

The other factor to take into account is a climate of fear and intimidation. You don't have to spread as much largesse around if the public is afraid to complain. 

Still, there are about 50 million people who will be very much against all this, so not the time to despair. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5685)

[DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5685) | [November 11, 2024 16:35](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200876)

**1501:**

_Not long after the 'partnership' ended, in the sense that the Chinese end of it stopped communicating, kept all the IP and the money that the Canadian end had invested._

Sounds a bit like the experience an acquaintance had with an American 'partner', minus the replacement of investor by military guy. Lawyer instead, and they would have needed to bring a lawsuit in a particular county where the 'partner' was hugely influential.

It's not really a partnership when there is too large a power imbalance between the sides, no matter what's written on paper. As a friend who's a lawyer frequently points out, the Law is fair and impartial as long as both sides can afford the same legal resources; if not, one side can just outlast the other and win by attrition.

**1506:**

_A pair of "Illegals" who have been in the US for, say 15-20 years are seized - what happens to their teenage US-CITIZEN kids? Or are they also conveniently "disappeared" I wonder?_

Back in the 30s, there was a crackdown on illegal immigrants in America, which quickly became a crackdown on Mexicans in America, because actually being a citizen didn't stop them deporting you.

And then there was Operation Wetback in the 50s.

_During Operation Wetback, tens of thousands of immigrants were shoved into buses, boats and planes and sent to often-unfamiliar parts of Mexico, where they struggled to rebuild their lives. In Chicago, three planes a week were filled with immigrants and flown to Mexico. In Texas, 25 percent of all of the immigrants deported were crammed onto boats later compared to slave ships, while others died of sunstroke, disease and other causes while in custody._

_It’s not clear how many American citizens were swept up in Operation Wetback, but the United States later claimed that 1.3 million people total were deported…_

_Mass deportations of Mexican immigrants from the U.S. date to the Great Depression, when the federal government began a wave of deportations rather than include Mexican-born workers in New Deal welfare programs. According to historian Francisco Balderrama, the U.S. deported over 1 million Mexican nationals, 60 percent of whom were U.S. citizens of Mexican descent, during the 1930s. Balderrama toldFresh Air’s Terry Gross that the program was referred to as “repatriation” to give it the sense of being voluntary. In reality, though, it was anything but._

_Despite a widespread belief among native-born Americans that Mexicans came to the United States to steal jobs from American workers, many were invited to the country to work in its fields. In 1942, the U.S. Mexican Farm Labor Program, also known as Operation Bracero after the Spanish term for “manual laborer,” began. The program funneled Mexicans into the United States on a legal, temporary basis in exchange for guaranteed wages and humane treatment—an attempt by the Mexican government to stave off the discrimination faced by earlier immigrants._

_However, not all employers wanted to follow the guidelines or pay the thirty-cent-an-hour guaranteed wage (about $4.51 in modern dollars). Nor did the Mexican government want Mexicans to work in Texas, which continued its discrimination against Mexican people, and the state was excluded from the program between 1942 and 1947. That’s where “wetbacks” came in. The racial epithet was used to describe Mexicans who illegally entered Texas by crossing the Rio Grande River. The government turned a blind eye to Texans’ employment of these undocumented immigrants, even after hiring undocumented workers was declared illegal._

<https://www.history.com/news/operation-wetback-eisenhower-1954-deportation>

I think the odds of a repeat performance have gone up. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

[JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093) | [November 12, 2024 02:07](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200910)

**1507:**

In Flanders Fields

In Flanders fields, the poppies blow  
Between the crosses, row on row,  
That mark our place; and in the sky  
The larks, still bravely singing, fly  
Scarce heard amid the guns below.

We are the Dead. Short days ago  
We lived, felt dawn, saw sunset glow,  
Loved and were loved, and now we lie,  
In Flanders fields.

Take up our quarrel with the foe:  
To you from failing hands we throw  
The torch; be yours to hold it high.  
If ye break faith with us who die  
We shall not sleep, though poppies grow  
In Flanders fields.

**1511:**

Greg #1510

Obviously they will try to export their political philosophy. But our next election comes after their next Presidential election.

The results of the next UK election will depend on:

(1) The economy. I can’t say I’m optimistic.

(2) The extent and nature of the fractures on both left and right. I think Badenoch and Farage are both far too egotistical to campaign together (“There can be only one!”), but Badenoch is likely to be replaced before our next election. Will her successor surrender to Farage? And of course on the left Labour and the LibDems had an implicit agreement about which seats each would campaign in; will that continue? I think a contingent “yes” would be my answer.

By the way you’ve had some quite splendid answers to your questions down in the weeds of this blog item. I’m thinking in particular of Heteromeles @1477. Given that the theory of money is that it _is_ a shared delusion or belief, how well are you doing in taking an atheistic line on it? Because, though I recognise the truth of H’s assertion, I can’t get my head around the disbelief required. Amalgamy and DeMarquis have also had a few pertinent observations.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6134)

[Damian](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6134) replied to [this comment from Dave Lester](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200920) | [November 12, 2024 11:32](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200922)

**1512:**

_Because, though I recognise the truth of H’s assertion, I can’t get my head around the disbelief required._

I think it helps a lot to recognise how much our modern international version of mammonism is inflected by its inheritance from the Protestant Reformation. The chosen are elevated not by merit or any other knowable criteria but simply because they are chosen, but we now attribute the agency for that choice to abstract entities that exist because of our faith in them (the market and its invisible hand in particular). We then almost automatically give those entities moral agency too. 

More than that, these ideologies pervade the space where we translate data into theories and models, so we reflect it by default and while I think most domains of knowledge incorporate some sort of correction for that, the "common sense" thinkers all rely on this background of things that are "obviously true" for a platform from which to snipe at heretical forms of independent inquiry. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6529)

**1515:**

_Or is most of the time spend on bases that are miniature bits of the US?_

Most of the US grunts stationed abroad tell me they tended to live in isolation of the local country. Language issues and the locals seemed to prefer it most of the time.

My wife's father was in Germany as a Lt. Col and full Col when she was 16-19. They got out and about and lived on the economy for a year but I get the impression it was more about his rank that allowed it than most anything else. Plus her mom grew up in the area years before so there were relatives.

Personally I'd have been out wandering about. But that's me. And what I did as I moved around for jobs in the US.

**1516:**

Greg, it’s entirely possible to recognize Gods without making their worship the center of your existence. 

Money is like chi in Taoism, which is their subjective conception of energy. Religious Taoism looks a lot like Pratchett’s take on good, that they only exist because humans believe they exist. As a result, Taoism and Chinese folk spirituality contain rites focused on a number of fictional characters, such as Monkey from _Journey to the West._. Modern Jediism is exactly the same.

Most traditional “religions” actually work this way. They know their gods aren’t real, but they also know that believing in them and going through their rituals is the best way to organize their society. Without writing, a ritual calendar is a really essential way to remember when to sow and when to reap, or where to be when a wild food is ready to be eaten.

So we believe money has value so that we can buy the things we require from total strangers. That’s fine. It’s essential for living with billions of other people. Mammonism is when we worship that belief, focus our lives on amassing money, believe that the only value something has is how much money it can be traded for, and believe that those who have lots of money can do anything. 

If you want to make this easier to swallow, I’d suggest that you might reconsider worshipping atheism. Humans relief to exist, including belief in things like money. But believing it exists and worshipping it are two different things. If you’re getting stuck because your practice of atheism requires you to disbelieve in any god, however useful that god is…might it be more useful to prioritize not worshipping any god, including money? Just a suggestion.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3979)

**1521:**

PilotMoonDog @ 1513:

_So, a thought. Assume the US withdraws from NATO. Presumably that means all troops currently posted on NATO assignments are shipped home. Does living in other countries broaden the mind much with such people? Or is most of the time spend on bases that are miniature bits of the US?_

_Essentially, how loyal would all those returning military folk be to an increasingly dysfunctional government?_

We take our oath to the **Constitution** rather than to the current government in power.

Don't know how individual soldiers might interpret that, but my experience was members of the military had political opinions as diverse as the rest of the country.

I hadn't thought about what withdrawal from NATO might mean for service members stationed in NATO countries, but I can't see those countries being willing to host American bases & personnel if the U.S. does withdraw from NATO.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

[JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093) | [November 12, 2024 19:02](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200942)

**1522:**

Life has been kind of surreal around here lately. I haven't commented on my personal life lately.

My brother is out of the hospital. He's supposed to have gall bladder surgery as an outpatient, but it's going to be a problem because Duke Hospital has this `_**"facility fee"**_` they tack on to every visit to one of their outpatient facilities - $290 that insurance WON'T pay. **EVERY** visit, including routine office visits, which he needs a lot because he's insulin dependent diabetic.

He gets mad and refuses to go to doctor's appointments if they're going to charge the `_**"facility fee"**_` (instead of doing what I suggested, just ignoring the bill when they send it to him). His insurance is through the Affordable Care Act & Medicare, so they can't deny him coverage or care.

The day he was discharged from the hospital, my sister had a massive stroke AND a heart attack. She's in Duke Hospital. They can't use the drugs they need to treat the heart attack because those drugs will cause the bleeding in her brain to get worse. She seems to be slowly getting better, but she's going to be bedridden for the rest of her life and will have to be in a nursing home with round the clock care. She has medicare.

When the ambulance came to get her they notified the county & housing inspectors came out and condemned her trailer as "unfit for human habitation", so she's got nowhere to go even if she does ever manage to heal enough to get out of a nursing home. Not that I think she ever will. 

My family are hillbillies even if we don't live in Appalachia.

Yesterday was Veterans Day here in the U.S. and a local restaurant chain has a "free meal" for veterans event every year. This year it actually came on Veterans Day. I ended up sharing a table with a family of Trump supporters. When asked I simply told them I would not vote for someone who called service members - particularly service members who had paid the ultimate price - "suckers & losers". We talked about other things for the rest of the meal.

I do not understand the election. It's hard for me to believe half (50.3%) of American voters are actively evil. I think it's more like 40% actively evil and 10% actively STUPID.

I still have the T-shirt my mom gave me years ago, _"Life's a bitch and then you die!"_ ... meanwhile you just have to soldier on.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3036)

[Dave Lester](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3036) replied to [this comment from JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200942) | [November 12, 2024 19:23](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2200943)

**1523:**

Sorry to hear that John. Medically, things here are not quite as bad, but last week was a shocker.

Mrs L went to see her GP (HMO gate-keeper medic) at 09:30. We then spent the next thirteen hours getting her eyesight checked. The next day we had stroke MRI, and then Doppler checking of her veins. So far the good news is nothing found, but we’re still left wondering what it actually is.

On the matter of Trumpers, I think we’ll find that many of them have fallen down the rabbit hole of poor information. I read somewhere that many US 18+ somethings feel that their heavy social media use means that they are “high information” people compared with Grandpa who uses old-style media. I fear that research might actually mean: scroll through a personalised, AI-mediated, list of google hits until I find something that agrees with me.

If I’m right this would account for (I think) DavidL’s Jewish friend who believes that Trump has already built his wall.

In short: there’s a whole alternate reality out there that you and I know nothing of.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

Sorry to hear about your family troubles, John. Good thing they have you!

> _I do not understand the election. It's hard for me to believe half (50.3%) of American voters are actively evil. I think it's more like 40% actively evil and 10% actively STUPID._

I share your puzzlement. I've been turning it over in my head all week.

I keep coming back to this: Never attribute to malevolence things that can be explained by sheer incompetence.

We don't like to think about it, but most voters in this country (and I suspect most other countries) are simply not equipped to vote for non-local offices. This of course was the original reason for the electoral college, which was quickly warped into its current form.

**1534:**

It's been a week since the election. Apparently I'm the only one skeptical that the guy who did an insurrection, claimed over and over that the vote was "rigged", openly asked various officials to intercede for him, and claimed "you'll never have to vote again" somehow mysteriously "won" this election.

Per Seattle Times there was at least one massive leak of vote-machine passwords. Oops! Surely the rest of the system is _perfectly secure_ , yes? Let's just ignore the yearly beating of _all_ available voting machines at DefCon Voting Machine Village every damn time they have it. I'm sure no one else has the ability to compromise these machines, and all the people who do so at DefCon would never try it outside that context. Right?

It's been a week, and no one in my various feeds of "hysterical left-wing journalism" has anyone questioned the election outcome. Even in the linked article above, the author is at pains to claim that "Elections nationwide remain fair and reliable. "

Fair to whom? Reliably what? Can you, US Citizen, prove that your vote was counted correctly? I bet you can't.

As to the "odds" of the US "becoming" a fascist/authoritarian dictatorship hellscape: it's a matter of degree. If you think the US is something other than an authoritarian dictatorship, I think you have never worked a corporate job nor been to jail/prison here. I've been stuck in this shithole my entire life for the crime of lacking enough money to leave (or knowledge of any place less likely to abuse me for said crime). For my whole life I've been dictated to, stolen from, and arbitrarily punished by officials and bosses, without recourse. It's getting worse. It's _been_ getting worse. Under Trump Too it will get worse faster, but so what. The trend is established, the opposition had no realistic plan to reverse it. That's what the Unites States is & where it's been going at least the last 40 years.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

I'm sorry about your troubles John. It seems that life swings between surreal and absurd so much of the time. At least when the needle is pointing to the "absurd" quadrant, Camus has your back, albeit "we must imagine Sisyphus happy" probably isn't much help right now. 

I too am keen to know how your new dog is getting on, though. I'm pretty sure our younger dog is coming into her second season now. We'd originally planned to have her desexed after the first one, since the vet's advice was to wait till then, but currently are seeing it as a relatively invasive procedure that isn't entirely necessary as there are more straightforward ways to avoid her getting pregnant, such as not leaving her alone with intact males, something that isn't likely to present as an issue for the foreseeable future. Will probably revisit when she's 2 or 3. Our older boy, a rescue, was desexed when he was retired from the show circuit early several years ago.

**1537:**

Dramlin @ 1530:

_Very sorry to hear all that John -- tell me about your new dog is going, to change your focus._

I would lift my siblings' burdens if I could, but I can't. Life is what it is. All you can do is soldier on.

The new dog does seem to be slowly coming around. She is almost house trained. I know that's my failing and not hers. She's pretty good about alerting me when she needs to go, but not so attuned to the household schedule yet, waking me at 3:00am because she's got to go RIGHT NOW - but at least she did wake me & managed to hold it long enough for me to get my pants on & take her out.

She's also crazy. When I go to bed I have to lift both of 'em up on the bed and that's her signal to leap and dig and pounce and run around in circles. She still hasn't get the message that I'm not one of her chew toys. I think that might be anxiety. I'm beginning to think she was completely feral before animal control took her to the shelter. I'm prepared to dig in for the long haul to bring her back to civilized. 

_I'm glad your tablemates were at least polite enough to change the subject instead of ranting on as various people have been talking about happening to them up there. And on that note... I wrote a very long reply to a Trump supporter on Will Wheaton's site who, amongst other things, said that the Democrats wanted to stack SCOTUS (too late, GOP already did that) but also, and more relevantly, said that a "majority" of voters has "democratically chosen a better path." I ran some numbers and told him I didn't think that the 30.6% of eligible US voters who voted for donny counted as a majority._

I've encountered some of the ranters, but my dinner couldn't have gone any other way. If they had been "ranters" I would have certainly asked our hosts to reseat me with more congenial companions.

Looking at the numbers on the various results web sites it looks like Trump got a tiny sliver of a "majority" in the popular vote (50.3%) - those who could be persuaded to vote and **those who DO bother to vote are all that counts**. Looks like turnout was around 65% of eligible voters (down from 67% in 2020)

Of thirty some percent of eligible voters who didn't vote, I'm only concerned about any who wanted to vote, tried to vote, but were in some way prevented from voting. I don't have any firm grasp of how widespread that was, so I can't say that number was sufficient to have changed the results.

[_<https://www.brennancenter.org/our-work/analysis-opinion/damage-conspiracy-theories-about-noncitizen-voting>_](https://www.brennancenter.org/our-work/analysis-opinion/damage-conspiracy-theories-about-noncitizen-voting)

But the non-voters can kiss my ass. Their opinion **doesn't** count.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

_It's been a week since the election. Apparently I'm the only one skeptical that the guy who did an insurrection, claimed over and over that the vote was "rigged", openly asked various officials to intercede for him, and claimed "you'll never have to vote again" somehow mysteriously "won" this election._

I'm not happy with the results, but there's no mystery about it.

In 2020 with a 66% turnout, Biden got 51.3% of the popular vote to Trump's 46.8%; in 2024 with (approximately) 65% turnout Trump got 50.2% to Harris 48.2%. 

It looks like most of the decrease in turnout came among Democratic voters. 

First time this century the G**Q** P candidate actually got a majority of the popular vote instead of squeaking out a win based on Electoral College annomalies.

**1549:**

To which "election system" do you refer? I claim that democracy is not undermined by questioning. It's up to the Secretary of State of the relevant state (in the United States) to demonstrate evidence that the election system they maintain is as "fair and reliable" as they claim. It's not on me to prove it isn't.

I know exploits are found regularly, and I know _some_ of them get patched.

I know that I _cannot_ know, legally, how some electronic voting machines work. I have to take the vendors' word for it when such machines get patches.

I know this so-called system emits shitty, deadly results. I am _not prepared_ to certify it. 

Responding to Greg: Unfortunately I am old (not as old as some here, but old enough) and poor, broken and effectively unable to work. I was a "Full Stack" web developer, back when the web had a few dozen fewer layers of pointless middleware and nested hypervisors. I have other computery-related-type experience and interests which make me a plain spoon-faced fool for OGH's work, most particularly the Laundry books (twas Fuller Memorandum that caught me first) and "Unwired", which touches on things I tried and failed to make happen in places where I used to be at.

I know AIPAC also contributes to Republicans. I was responding to Scott Sanford who was implying only one party was getting assistance from foreign nations. I guess, we could quibble over what "hostile" means. Since the primaries are party business, it's entirely on Democrats if they don't want to keep foreign money out of it.

I look at Russia's behavior like the weather. Foreign nations will interfere, nothing you can do but bring an umbrella. That doesn't make your opponents' party illegitimate. That way lies foreign agent laws and unrest, the kind of thing we denounce in Moldova and Georgia. Amateurs are going to troll and call in bomb threats, professionals will form PACs and lobby. I know which one has more of an effect.

I am also worried about whether we will get an election and I completely agree that Trump is an authoritarian. I will never vote for a Republican. I just don't think blaming the people who didn't vote for the other party is helpful or fair. We know how bad the Republicans are, so what does that say about the alternative that it couldn't beat that? **1555:**

So….are you leaning towards Heather Cox Richardson’s argument that enough Americans are propagandized by the stranglehold of Fox and Sinclair Media on their news feeds, along with others’ arguments that newspaper editors and owners are abysmal failures at covering the news? We know, for instance, that Fox was cutting away from Trump when he started babbling.

How much of it was the economy? Inflation sucks and that’s a problem. But there’s an argument out there that voters correctly deduced it was due to corporate price gouging and not inflation due to the pandemic? That’s at odds with the previous idea: that voters correctly deduced, not only that they were being bullshat but what the truth was.

How much of it was that Biden is too old and Harris is a black, woman, left coast liberal lawyer who didn’t shine as VP? My comment about “but he’s a billionaire, he must know what he’s doing” falls in here. I should also note that the billion dollars the Harris campaign spent swung the electorate in seven battleground states about three percent towards her, but that otherwise the election could have been run in January with Biden against Trump and the result would have been the same, minus that small swing.

And how much of it was basically evil: fascism and worse?

I think, with 75 million voters, it was all of the above. I’m not copping out, but I don’t think that the rich folk in Beverly Hills or Newport Beach are at all in the same media environment as a farmer in rural Ohio, nor does a KKK member have the same interests as someone who’s chosen with side in the Gaza genocide. But they all voted for Trump. Oh well. 

Good time to not get pregnant, I guess.

**1558:**

The US election was yet another reminder that the US is most definitely a different country, with different culture than my own - though we are close neighbours.

I don't think I will ever know how someone could watch the last 9 years of madness and choose 'more please'. 

The Tyee wrote a very good piece on the choice made by US voters: <https://thetyee.ca/Analysis/2024/11/06/Just-Learned-American-Values/>

Key quote: "In return for the promise of two chickens in every pot, and deporting millions of migrants, the majority of voters were prepared to look the other way on what used to be core American values. They shrugged off the rule of law, freedom of the press, support for democracy at home and abroad, and basic civility in the nation’s public discourse."

Personally I've chosen to decouple from all US based news, and anything to do with that person that doesn't affect me or my own country. I just can't and won't. The US is in for a very rough ride, and may well be at the end of the experiment. Hopefully something of the original intent will survive this event.

Feels like the US has decided to move on from being a 'shining example' into being a 'cautionary example'. I just hope it doesn't kill the rest of us in the process.

**1561:**

Frankly, the economy over here isn't that bad. The 2008 crisis was much worse, and people re-elected Obama. I suspect that to a certain extent, economic anxiety is much deeper seated than just the after-effects of the Pandemic and the supply chain crisis. It's a proxy for social fear, then sense that the social order is crumbling, and no one knows where we are going. No one trusts the experts anymore, no one trusts anyone outside their in-group, anymore, and they think a loud mouthed populist represents the best chance they have at preserving something familiar. Remember, to them, 2020 was stolen and voting for Trump _is_ preserving democracy. 

Sexism had something to do with it too, but not just in the obvious way. It wasn't so much that men voted for Trump (which they did), but that women didn't vote for Harris (much like they didn't for Hilary). Stereotypes die hard.

**1565:**

Well, I’ve finally finished Dan Davies’ book _The Unaccountability Machine_. Recommended. It’s a cybernetic look at economics and business management, with a several-chapter digression to explain what cybernetics is. (Stafford Beer’s cybernetics, not the Silicon Valley anything-to-do-with-computers buzzword.)

A couple of call-outs to Charlie, although missing one to John Bruner which I think would have fit in the last chapter. And an extensive reading list with commentary. 

Charlie, I think you might enjoy it. Heteromeles, you too, and I bet there’s biological connections there he’s missed. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1357)
