**1569:**

_It's a proxy for social fear, then sense that the social order is crumbling, and no one knows where we are going. No one trusts the experts anymore, no one trusts anyone outside their in-group, anymore_

Alvin Tofler's _Future Shock_ in action.

Davies makes the point in his book: that we've allowed economists (specifically Friedmanites) to remove various cybernetic systems that regulate variety, so there's no way left for the lowest levels of the system to communicate with the upper levels but scream in protest, which is why populist movements in so many places look the same.

I'm not going to reproduce his argument here because I can't do it justice, but it's worth looking at. 

As an engineer, I'd say that we've spent decades pursuing economic efficiency without considering that efficiency is the enemy of resiliency. Instead of organizations taking the brunt of changes we've allowed them to offload the risks onto individuals while giving those individuals no chance to cope. As the Whitehall studies showed, being lower in an organization (ie. being held accountable without having control over what you are 'accountable' for) is bad for your health, and we;ve had a decades-long climb in suicides, substance abuse problems, etc that are the precise kind of health outcomes one would expect from risks being downloaded (while rewards aren't).

Another point Davies makes is that capitalism now isn't the same as it was 60 years ago. He suggests reading J. Paul Getty's autobiography to contrast how he viewed capitalism with, say, Jack Welsh. This ties in with discussions here about historical continuity —.it's not just countries that are less continuous than their official histories suggest.

(One of the things that I recently learned about Welsh is that he would close profitable factories, laying off all the workers, because other parts of GE had had a really spectacular sale and he didn't want a spike in the profit numbers because they the quarter after would look bad, so he deliberately created losses to lower the numbers — and to hell with the workers. Lovely guy.)

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

**1571:**

_I'll see if I can find it. I'm really just in the waiting stage for a loss that I know is coming. Nothing else I can do._

A couple of other songs on the album relate to that, too. "Let It Go" was written about the expected loss of a close friend of decades, IIRC.

I do like Ian Tamblyn. He's a bit older than me, so when I hit a life stage he's already written music exploring it. I can't think of _any_ big life event in decades that hasn't had at least one of his songs playing in the back of my mind, putting into words feelings I'm struggling to express. 

If you have a music streaming service I think you can find him. If not here's his website:

<https://www.iantamblyn.com>

If you need a distraction, listen to _Epic Rock CD_ (one of his free albums, along with _Frog's Night Out_ which is also good). Written for a geology conference, it's an entire album of geology-themed songs with lots of puns and wordplay (and some actual science too).

**1577:**

_(One of the things that I recently learned about Welsh is that he would close profitable factories, laying off all the workers, because other parts of GE had had a really spectacular sale and he didn't want a spike in the profit numbers because they the quarter after would look bad, so he deliberately created losses to lower the numbers — and to hell with the workers. Lovely guy.)_

As someone who watched and read the business news over the decades, I say this is not quite right. Welch was all about money. Yes. But he would also look hard at a company / division and project profits out 5, 10, or 20 years. If they didn't look like they could generate big numbers for the medium to long term, he would sell it off while the price was high. Eventually it got to where GE was mostly a banking / leasing operation. And after 2008 those bits got stripped out and it exposed most of the rest as empty shells. Aside from a few very profitable bits like jet engine design and manufacturing. But by then most of the grid scale power systems operations and similar where long gone.

He did tend to only shut down profitable setups if pieces could be sold off or taxes written off or pension funds raided or whatever to make more money short than keeping it going. As a worker bee in a subsidiary of a subsidiary of a subsidiary in the ITT empire of the 80s, I got to following the news of these monster amalgamations. GE was showing other big conglomerates how to do it better.

FYI - My opinions about D Trump were formed in the early 80s when he first started showing up in the national business news. And I have never really changed my opinion of him.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1357)

**1584:**

I read yct re "economic efficiency", and my reaction is to wonder what that even means. Is not the whole purpose of a society to provide for _all_? This definition of "economic efficiency" is right up there with the question I wrote to Paul Krugman, and never got an answer: why is ->tax policy<\- NEVER included in economic calculations? 

And that matters. For example, in the mid/late eighties, at the height of Friedmanism, there was a penny shortage in the US. This was because, having paid consultants at Wharton to calculate how many pennies the US should mint, they did _not_ pay any attention to the fact that three large-population states, PA, NY, and NJ, all raised their sales tax rate by a penny on the dollar. 

About Getty vs. Welch - when I was still in school, I got that when sales went down, you lowered prices. After Friedman, the std. response is to _raise_ prices to maintain the cash flow. 

Now, it the GOP meant it when they rant about "running the government like a business", taxes would constantly be going up, rather than cutting what they're paying for.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3431)

**1591:**

_OK, what happened in the US, approx 1820-1835?_

Not much. Very small federal government. Not much of a standing army. There was a Navy but they and the army dealt with their own payroll and such.

Most of the federal government at the time was customs agents who where in charge of collecting tariffs.

If a federal official needed a staff or to rent an office they took care of it from allocated funds.

And all of the above led to money amounts, small and large, going into various places it should not have.

Immigration? Walk off the boat and figure out where you were going to sleep and eat.

**1597:**

I don't count on it, but it was odd. I was expecting Rick Scott to bull his way in.

Also, my understanding is that the slow roll is a typical DC tactic, especially against stuff done by Presidents. Their administrations are only going to be there for a few years, but a Senator or Congresscritter has no term limit and might have a job on K Street or a university afterward if they don't screw up too badly.

That, and I don't think any of them particularly like Trump.

So take Gaetz for Justice. He's already quit Congress ahead of them publishing findings into his ethics investigation, and he deep sixed the previous speaker out of spite. What do they gain by making his life easy? Ditto Robert F Kennedy Jr (already disowned by his family and at best a Republican carpetbagger), Gifford (like RFK, a former democrat wobbling off into Trumpland), Musk (rich, has powerful connections with the military, but...wow, Congress will have to create an entirely whole new bureaucracy simply for him to slash bureaucracy), etc.

If they're being good little fascists lining up behind der GropingFuhrer, yeah, but he's likely to kick off before he starts a revolution. Then they're stuck backing Vance who's...um a shill for Left Coast Tech Bros? A massively inexperienced politician who became famous for writing a book? Maybe. 

The other thing is, if they slow roll, they might pull in a bunch of democrats. They've got majorities, but only if they can hold them together, which Congress especially has had a lot of trouble doing. 

Also, the second or third critter behind someone like Gaetz may be quite toxic, but not compared to Gaetz or similar. 

And...as others have pointed out, voters didn't like democratic politicians, but for the most part they like things like the ACA, social security, medicare, and access to abortion and family planning. Deep sixing those is not good for political career longevity, as a bunch of people found out in 2018.

So no, I don't count on it, but do remember that the last time Trump came in he had majorities and got almost nothing done for the first two years. So we'll see if that happens again or not.

**1605:**

The Treaty of Waitangi between the British Crown and Maori peoples underlies a lot of Aotearoan law, culture and everything else. The original treaty is in English and Maori, but they are not exact translations. A lot of gaps and cracks have been filled in over the course of time by legal precedent, but one of the minor parties ( a local equivalent of Reform and UKIP) in the current government coalition insisted on bringing forward legislation to define things exactly as a condition of joining the coalition. The proposed bill does this badly and people, especially the Maori are not happy. 

The protest in parliament is in the form of a Haka, a traditional Maori "thing" (Greeting, challenge, war dance, etc, the Haka can be any of those and many more) combining spoken word chants, gestures and (for want of a better word) dance moves.

<https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/cg4ln6ddgy9o> for a good version of the event.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5685)

**1606:**

"Davies makes the point in his book: that we've allowed economists (specifically Friedmanites) to remove various cybernetic systems that regulate variety, so there's no way left for the lowest levels of the system to communicate with the upper levels but scream in protest, which is why populist movements in so many places look the same."

That reminds of "Seeing Like a State" by James Scott. There's also an article by Richard Holzl in "Science as Culture" that makes a similar point using the history of scientific forestry in Germany as a case study--that streamlining and modernizing organic systems harms them in the long run. 

Wow, _Future Shock_. That takes me back. I first read that in HS. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201040) | [November 15, 2024 15:29](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201041)

**1607:**

The flip side of this is that many forms of complexity don’t scale. That’s a key point of _Seeing like a State,_ that standardization is essential for running big systems, but that this destroys often necessary complexity. 

Scott’s example is of villages of maybe 100 people. Assuming they know what they’re doing and have a halfway decent area to work with and a way to trade for necessary outside goods, they can be sustainable for a long time. Problem is, what they have as surplus for trade varies by year due to climate, accidents, fire, or whatever. When they get incorporated into a larger system that forces them to produce a defined surplus every year, and they’re not so sustainable, because they’re now forced to bear the brunt of changes they formerly adapted to.

Meanwhile, the system that assimilated them can’t afford to let their output vary, because it needs their inputs to work.

It’s nice to think that AI will solve this. Unfortunately, for the AI to work, you have to structure the input data to be readable, and that’s literally what Seeing Like A State is about. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

[Robert Prior](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201008) | [November 15, 2024 16:01](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201042)

**1608:**

_As someone who watched and read the business news over the decades, I say this is not quite right. Welch was all about money. Yes. But he would also look hard at a company / division and project profits out 5, 10, or 20 years. If they didn't look like they could generate big numbers for the medium to long term, he would sell it off while the price was high._

Sorry for late reply. Had to skim part of the book to find the passages as I hadn't bookmarked them.

_We've already seen how accounting systems can act as an information-reducing filter. It follows from this that the greater emphasis on accounting-based targets set by the CEO, the greater the filtering effect. The process of financialization exacerbated this in two ways. First, it cemented the dominance of financial reporting over any other kind of information system. Rather than making a resource bargain between the operating and strategic management and tailoring it to the specific requirements of different processes, every unit had to contribute to a smooth progression of quarterly numbers, often with absurd effects._

_In 1997, for example, GE closed on a transaction with a defence contractor which gave them a one-off gain of more than $2bn. But recognizing such a big spike in profits would have made it different to show steady growth in future years. So they closed down some factories, making more than 1000 people redundant, in order to generate a corresponding one-off cost. 'Offsetting the gain has been our practice,' was the comment at the time from Welch's spokesperson._

I have no doubts that the closings were dressed up in other language for the press. Admittedly I've been through layoffs and so may be overly cynical about the dissembling. 

In any case, Davies is an economist and has been an analyst for investment banks (and worked as a regulator for the Bank of England), so I'd take his view on this over financial reporters relying on corporate press releases.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

**1609:**

_Apparently Dilbert Stark is already rubbing a lot of Trump insiders wrong by acting as if he's smarter than everyone else and thus no one should get to disagree with anything he says._

**1615:**

It’s not just a matter of control. AI couldn’t solve all the problems even with the best intentions. 

Here’s a basic example: let’s say society runs on wheat. That wheat has to come from somewhere. But crops fail even under strict management for yield, as we have now, and under sustainable agriculture, they’ll wobble even more. What can AI, or any system do? Warehouse wheat during bumper crops, export surplus and import to match needs. That’s about it. When those don’t work, like now, with Ukraine farm fields unusable, you get shortages. 

A sustainable group may eat mostly wheat one year, mostly potatoes another, a lot of grasshoppers another year, forage in the woods a fourth….that’s the diversity resilience model. It’s entirely doable with small groups and low population densities, but probably impossible to implement sustainably when you’ve got billions of people relying on skilled farmers to feed them via very big supply chains. I’m pretty sure that AI can’t solve this, although everyone who dreams of centrally planned economies probably thinks I’m too negative.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6643)

**1632:**

Heteromeles @1607 @1615

_It’s nice to think that AI will solve this. Unfortunately, for the AI to work, you have to structure the input data to be readable, and that’s literally what Seeing Like A State is about._

One of the genuinely useful advances in AI has been the ability to get insights from unstructured data. You don't need AI for central planning. You just build up a huge matrix of all the inputs and outputs and run dynamic programming algorithms to maximize production - 70's comp sci. The problem as you say is getting the inputs, which AI could help with. However, there's not much incentive in providing accurate data and often some advantage in lying. So I am not very optimistic about central planning either.

Francis Spufford's _Red Plenty_ is very good about central planning, told in an engaging novelistic form and one of the best books in English about what it really felt like to live in the Soviet Union (it wasn't _1984_).

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1066)

So one thing I've been wondering about is how much inflation was caused by Biden's pandemic stimulus. Many will point out that inflation was worldwide so those policies are not to blame. But the world economy largely runs on the dollar, so couldn't US domestic policies have played a part in worldwide inflation? Kinda how 2008 crises was mostly triggered by the US housing bubble pop but was much more damaging to other countries, even ones like Russia that you wouldn't think have much to do with it.

I would _like_ to believe that inflation is from price gouging, Russia-Ukraine war and supply chain disruptions. But what if the fiscal conservatives have a point? They really are out to discredit Modern Monetary Theory.

**1637:**

I’ll avoid HCR then. I enjoy reading what she’s writing, but I entirely get your point.

One thing I try to keep in mind is that, if everybody has a different opinion, just by chance someone’s likely right. it’s only worth believing that they have some insight if they’re right more often than chance would predict. Does anyone meet that criterion? Right now I don’t know.

One thing I want to is….well, I hate to call it stupidity, but…the fastest growing county in the US is Polk County Florida, home of Tampa. South Florida’s booming, along with places like Houston. _Marching Morons_ aside, it’s far more affordable than where I live, but it’s not a place to settle, as it will be underwater well before the end of the century. These people voted overwhelmingly for Trump. You could kind of see them voting for Attila the Hun, too. I’m sure they’re mostly nice people when you get to know them, too. And I also suspect many of them aren’t as ignorant as I just portrayed them to be, and that there’s a certain hopelessness involved in their decision making, a combination of anger, desperation, and trying to make the best of things.

Financially, I naively figured that, with money being dumped into the system, of course there would be inflation. the story I haven’t really seen is how much the money moved around before it got sunk into some wealthy person’s hoard. That’s probably a more useful question: Odesa stimuli get gobbled up.rapidly, or does it pass through a lot of hands first? My understanding is that the latter is better than the former.

Another thing that’s worth looking at are birth rate maps over time, such as <https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/pressroom/sosmap/fertility_rate/fertility_rates.htm.> Births are still outpacing deaths, but birth rates plummeted with the pandemic and hadn’t recovered as of 2022. That’s another kind of confidence vote. I’m guessing it signals that women,especially younger women, don’t feel that they can afford to get pregnant. That’s not a vote of confidence for the current order of things. .

**1651:**

I don't know if Charlie's still moderating this mess (sorry Charlie), but I've got a new/old SciFi setting to pitch to him or anyone who wants it. 

Back in the 80s and 90s, Daniel Keyes Moran published three books (_Emerald Eyes_ , _The Long Run_ , and _The Last Dancer_) set in a US that had been conquered by the a massive United Nations coalition to save the world from climate change.

Ahem.

Maybe time to update it?

Here's the deal. Russia is not putting 10,000 North Korean soldiers in the Ukraine fight as a show of force, they're doing it because they're short on recruits and running up a huge debt fighting this thing. There are predictions that they've got a year left. 

Of course, with Trump in charge, the US won't be the arsenal of democracy.

However, if you want something to hope for, the EU decides to go from broke and breaks Russia instead, using Ukraine as the point of the spear.

Meanwhile, Trump massively disables the US DoD by doing loyalty tests and similar shit. He and the tech bros massively ramp up GHG production, going big on gas and on LLM AI training.

the EU (possibly with the UK as an ally) forms an alliance of convenience with China, India, the African Union and basically every other non-authoritarian state to deal with the global threat that is the US government under Trump and successors. The US military is crippled, and the coastal metropolises (as in DKM's novel) welcome the invasion as a wave of sanity. 

The US government falls (presumably in a non-nuclear war or this is the end of the world), and the MAGAts go the way of the Nazis. A new Cold War descends between the EU and China, and the EU (and possibly China) institute the reciprocal of the Marshall Plan to wean the US cold turkey off AI, massive military spending and our fossil fuel addiction, these three being meshed.

With the US out of the way, there's now a fight for global domination, using much smaller militaries. Presumably this starts with the Matter of Russia, and giving it the US treatment too.

Does the US get divided between Chinese and EU sectors? Hmmm. If so, I'd be heading east ASAP.

Note that I'm fully aware that China's reverted to Maoism and reeducation camps. This is simply a SFF scenario for anyone who wants to play with it. Or who thinks it might be hopeful. I'm not at all advocating for a Chinese takeover. Or an EU takeover, for that matter. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8141)

**1655:**

I was talking with an old friend today, possibly for the last time. He said one of the comforts he takes in being old (and having cancer) is that he won't have to live long in the future. 

I kind of understand, but I have too many hostages to the future to be so calm about what I fear is coming.

On that cheerful note, have you read Christina Dalcher's novel _Vox_? A _Guardian_ reviewer called it an 'overblown feminist dystopia jumping on the _Handmaid's Tale_ bandwagon' — but given recent(ish) news that the Taliban have forbidden women to speak in public I think she's exploring another part of the same (nightmarish) space.

<https://globalnews.ca/news/10841150/taliban-women-morality-laws-praying-voices/>

Recommended, but not as a distraction. (Unless it's the distraction of stubbing your toe to distract yourself from your toothache.)

<https://www.nytimes.com/2024/11/15/opinion/donald-trump-orban-putin.html>

Masha Gessen was born in Soviet Union about the same time I was (we are less than a year apart), and emigrated to US also within a year of me. Our life experiences are rather similar, and I always found her writing very perceptive. What she perceives here is both true and depressing: “Liberal democracy offers moral constraints without problem-solving” — a lot of rules, not a lot of change — while “populism offers problem-solving without moral constraints.”

**1661:**

Also worthwhile: pick a word that describes the leaders who would not ally with a fully fascist but militarily crippled US in this scenario…

I agree with your observation of course. 

The more interesting question is whether, in the absence of a US hegemony, every remaining great power tries to become the next US, regardless of the damage inflicted. Or, just possibly, do the remaining powers pour more effort into trying to deal with climate change than into conquering each other? 

This is a less stupid question when you think about the fact that the US currently has at least two of the world’s top five militaries. If we’re disarmed and nobody really wants to pour a trillion dollars annually into becoming the next US, what kinds of conflicts will even be possible? The existence of the US military at that scale forces arms races to exist. If our massive military couldn’t save us from our own politics, what lessons will other countries draw from our example about the utility of arms races? Especially in an era of accelerating but still manageable climate change?

Also, if the US is defeated without it turning into a nuclear war, will anyone bother with maintaining nuclear arsenals thereafter? 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3036)

[Dave Lester](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3036) replied to [this comment from ilya187](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201096) | [November 17, 2024 17:45](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201100)

**1662:**

I think Masha is slightly off on diagnosing both democracy and populism.

If we look at Athenian Democracy, there were few moral constraints on the actions imposed by conforming to the “demos” (= voters, in modern parlance). They voted for what we’d call “war crimes” today during the Mytilenean Debate <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mytilenean_Debate> only to change their minds the next day.

And I think a better characterisation of “populism” is Demagoguery, which carries the connotation of rabble-rousing, rather than something that is actually popular. You may find the following portrait of Cleon interesting: <https://simple.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cleon> . And of course your typical demagogue will think nothing of promising things he cannot do: Huey Long being a left wing US example. A better fit for Boris Johnson would be Alcibiades.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8141)

[Howard NYC](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8141) | [November 17, 2024 17:57](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201101)

**1664:**

Well, Masha specified "liberal democracy", which is a rather modern phenomenon and is largely defined by the existence of constraints on the "rule of the majority". Athens definitely did not count as a liberal democracy.

As for "populism", I agree that it is far too mild a term for what it describes. But we are kind of stuck with it.

**1665:**

Re: 'I’m guessing it signals that women,especially younger women, don’t feel that they can afford to get pregnant.'

Afford in terms of healthcare - cost for having a baby in a hospital, increased odds of not having emergency surgery if pregnancy/labor goes critical, domestic/intimate partner violence removed from CDC list of harms that medicos should be watchful of/report.

About decapitating the DOD:

Half wondering whether this is part of a strategy to increase prison farms/factories - haven't checked recently but the last time I looked, people (mostly males) of color made up the majority of the enlisted.

Also - no idea how or whether there's any fed gov't pension for vets*, but if 'yes', this could be a scam set-up to defraud vets by repossessing their pensions funds, i.e., invest in DT crypto or whatever scam he and his inner circle of cronies come up with. 

*I know that the VA has become more active in research (PTSD etc.) within the past 15-20 years. Maybe John can provide more background/detail about the current level of medical/health VA benefits ... after he finishes watching his recommended daily dose of cat vids. :)

This is what I found and my guess is that hardly anyone qualifies ... mostly for not good reasons, i.e., died before 65, unrealistic (not adjusted for inflation) qualifying low income levels, definition of 'war' vs. police action vs whatever-the-hell-the-POTUS-called-it-but-not-a-war-in-name', etc.

**1674:**

_How common are incidents like this?_

The arrest. Not so much. But somewhat growing.

Mass media, cable news, and now social media have created a need for material. And lurid draws views better than life is OK types of stories.

I grew up playing in the woods at that age, cutting vines in trees on hills to swing out over the creek. And so on. A mile or few from home.

Today more and more parents are scared of the world. And some of the extreme get laws passed because someone across the country got abducted and killed.

Are you saying things like this aren't starting to happen in Canada.

BTW my father and mother would be on opposite sides of this issue. Mom all for the arrest. It got so bad with her and dealing with our kids growing up we got to where we would not let our kids visit her. Luckily my parents lived a 5 hour drive away. My father was driving small farm tractors by the age of 6 and 18' long bed logging trucks at age 12. He was born in 1925.

Times they are a changing.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6134)

[Damian](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6134) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201115) | [November 17, 2024 23:26](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201117)

**1675:**

I listened to a podcast of a pretty learned discussion recently about what populism is and isn't, where there seemed to be an attachment to the concept of majoritarianism, and I found myself sort of disagreeing. I think that to me populism is a special case of that innate, oxytocin-mediated in/out-group bonding/othering that seems to be part of our primate heritage, while I think majoritarianism is a special case of it, or at best is a form of special pleading associated with it. It's about pandering to a specific group, perhaps making the group more exclusive specifically through "othering", and the conceit is that if it isn't actually the majority they still think it should be. 

But really where I used the word was not as a pencil I've personally sharpened, rather as a referent to link into the phrase I was responding to... actually really just using as a hook to hang my reference to the Dodgy Brothers comedy skit from Australian television of the 1980s. You have to say "It's cheap because it doesn't work" with the huckster sales pitch inflection. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

[Robert Prior](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201116) | [November 17, 2024 23:39](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201119)

**1676:**

As the article notes, we've had cases of CAS being called on parents in Canada. (Including, notably, a woman whose children were playing in her enclosed back yard while she watched them through the kitchen window.) 

But Canada is a socialist hellhole of government overreach, so you have to expect that kind of thing here.

Rural Georgia strikes me as the kind of place that would _against_ such interference. Kid walked a mile to a town of 370 people — hardly the kind of place where liberal do-gooders are allowed to run rampant. Yet in this case the poor woman was actually _arrested_. 

For all the populist rhetoric about freedom and limited government, I've noticed that right-wingers on both sides of the border are very willing to use government power to crack down on non-conformity and difference, especially when someone disagrees with their views on raising children. Although our police generally don't get involved (unless you're indigenous).

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

**1685:**

_When I was younger than ten I learned that if you were walking along a road with no sidewalk you should walk on the shoulder facing traffic. This was part of safety programs at school._

There was a skating rink about 1/4 to 1/2 mile by the road from my elementary school. Various kids would have birthday parties after school starting around age 9 or 10. Everyone in the class would walk down after school. MAYBE 0.5 M of walk space on each side of the 1 lane each way road. And it was a sort of main road. We just walked against the traffic and didn't fall in the drainage ditch (1-2 M deep) walking to the rink. No curbs or sidewalks.

This would have been in the mid 60s.

I guess the police would have had to arrest 20-30 parents and some school admins.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

[JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093) replied to [this comment from SFReader](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201103) | [November 18, 2024 04:07](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201130)

**1686:**

SFReader @ 1665:

_Half wondering whether this is part of a strategy to increase prison farms/factories - haven't checked recently but the last time I looked, people (mostly males) of color made up the majority of the enlisted._

My experience was ethnicity of enlistees reflected the U.S. as a whole, skewed towards lower income families. Those whose family incomes were at or below the mean. I think most enlistees stay one or two enlistments (3-6 years) to get GI Bill benefits - there are college education benefits available WHILE you are in as well as those you get once you get out ... plus whatever skill training you receive.

_Also - no idea how or whether there's any fed gov't pension for vets*, but if 'yes', this could be a scam set-up to defraud vets by repossessing their pensions funds, i.e., invest in DT crypto or whatever scam he and his inner circle of cronies come up with._

_*I know that the VA has become more active in research (PTSD etc.) within the past 15-20 years. Maybe John can provide more background/detail about the current level of medical/health VA benefits ... after he finishes watching his recommended daily dose of cat vids. :)_

Howard does cat videos. [_I kill virtual zombies & try not to let the bandits get me while I loot through the garbage on a post-apocalyptic island wasteland_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arma_3) as well as randomly wandering through YouTube, Wikipedia & a couple other blogs collecting sarcasms ...

_This is what I found and my guess is that hardly anyone qualifies ... mostly for not good reasons, i.e., died before 65, unrealistic (not adjusted for inflation) qualifying low income levels, definition of 'war' vs. police action vs whatever-the-hell-the-POTUS-called-it-but-not-a-war-in-name', etc._

[_<https://www.va.gov/pension/eligibility/>_](https://www.va.gov/pension/eligibility/)

That site has a list of what is considered "wartime" for VA pension eligibility.

Those pensions are kind of a stopgap meant to alleviate abject poverty among elderly veterans. Mostly those who served in wartime, got out and got left behind in peacetime - came home and got a factory job only to have the banksters loot the pension fund when they off-shored all the jobs. 

Those VA pensions are [_Means Tested_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Means_test) (like welfare benefits), military pensions are not.

Military pensions come through [_DFAS_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Defense_Finance_and_Accounting_Service). I don't get a VA pension, I get Retired Pay from the Army. Military retired pay is one of the few remaining examples of a [_defined benefit pension plan_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Defined_benefit_pension_plan)

**PS:** I do get my health care through the VA and that ass-wipe `_**"Vivek Ramaswamy-lama-ding-dong"**_` has been sounding off about gutting veterans health care as part of Cheatolini iL Douchbag's `_**"Project 2025"**_` TURD reich.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

**1687:**

_For all the populist rhetoric about freedom and limited government, I've noticed that right-wingers on both sides of the border are very willing to use government power to crack down on non-conformity and difference, especially when someone disagrees with their views on raising children._

This applies in the US to both major party. Helicoptering kids is just too pervasive.

Yes, for all practical purposes the US is a 2 party political system. But those 2 parties are both full of fractions. Which is why the previous 3 R House Speakers gave it up. Unless the R's (and the D's to some extent) have a 30 or 40 vote majority, the fractions can and do gum up the works on all kinds of things.

The R's include libertarians who want everyone to do what ever they want. Including how their raise their kids. And on the other side of the same party you have hard core fundamentalists who want the government to proscribe all details of life including child rearing.

And the D's have similar but different folks.

**1710:**

"the belief that the world is destined to get much simpler than it currently is" -- meaning you don't need fancy-schmancy degrees to navigate it."

That is a deep rooted cultural myth that goes back at least as far as the Roman foundation myths (See: Cincinnatus). The notion that what really matters is a simple, pastoral lifestyle and that men (always men) of virtue will spend their time hunting and being woodsmen/farmers unless called upon or forced to serve in some capacity.

It is a common trope in movies and literature, so common we don't even realize it. We see it in politicians playacting as plainspeaking rural folk, and we see it in the modern myths of manhood and self-reliance. Remember GWB trying to pretend he was a Texas rancher? 

It also explains a lot of the popularity of zombie movies - no need to worry about technology or politics, just survive through violence.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8141)

**1712:**

Retiring @ 1707:

_The libertarians are all over this incident (and others)._

[_<https://reason.com/tag/free-range-kids/>_](https://reason.com/tag/free-range-kids/)

_I wandered all over the place when I was 8 or 9. Including taking our canoe out poking along the lake we lived on. To and from school alone in first grade, etc. Through our small town's downtown._

So did I, but that was back in the 1950s ... although we didn't have a canoe or a lake for me to poke along ... 

I got a ride TO school in the mornings because it was on the way to where my father worked. But I walked home from school every day up until I was in High School.

Elementary school and Junior High were both just under a mile (1.36 km); High School was 2.8 miles. 

My first year in High School I didn't walk home, I walked down to where my father worked, where they'd let me sit at a table in the back of the computer room where I could get my homework done while I waited for him to get off work so I could ride home with him. I walked under the infamous [_"can opener bridge"_](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=u_9rWH0p43A) every day after school.

My junior & senior years in High School I worked for the advertising department of the Durham Herald Sun newspapers as a gofer, walking from school to downtown Durham and then walking home at night when I got off work (5:00pm to 9:00pm M-F).

It was about a mile from school to my father's office and 2.8 miles from school to home (2.12 mi from the newspaper's office) ... 

That was my experience walking alone from September 1955 through June 1967 - age 6 to 17. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)

[Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44) replied to [this comment from Rocketpjs](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201154) | [November 18, 2024 20:48](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201157)

**1713:**

_That is a deep rooted cultural myth that goes back at least as far as the Roman foundation myths (See: Cincinnatus). The notion that what really matters is a simple, pastoral lifestyle and that men (always men) of virtue will spend their time hunting and being woodsmen/farmers unless called upon or forced to serve in some capacity._

Yeah, probably the death rate in urban Rome had something to do with his view?

It's not a myth. If you want sustainability, urban lifestyles aren't and never have been. Heck, the city with the longest known record for continuous habitation is Damascus, and it's lasted, minus a few horrible wars now and then, for something like 3,000-4,000 years. Compare that with Acheulean hand axes, first invented by Homo erectus, and used from Africa to Europe, China, and Indonesia for over a million years. Civilization isn't stable. 

The myth here is that a pastoral lifestyle is simple. It isn't, and it requires a lot of learning and practice to do well. The trope of the city slicker, dude, or gentleman farmer fits in here. Worse for the myth of simplicity, there's been evidence for years now that even Neanderthals burned forests to make savannas over 100,000 years ago. This wasn't random, they were deliberately making an area in Germany more favorable for the food plants and animals they preferred, and for hunting them. This was done all over the world until the 20th Century. You know how to do it? Me neither. I do know it would take a huge amount of work and practice to redevelop such a system too. 

But when the super-wealthy ditch the cities, buy huge ranches, and live at low population densities on wild foods when they can get them, they're not being delusional, they're being human. What's inhuman in their actions is that they're coercing the rest of us to labor in cities to support their healthy lifestyle. Given how many of us there are now, it's unavoidable that most of us will live in cities, but that doesn't make city life any more sustainable, especially on time scales of ten thousand to a million years or more.

As a side note: if someone wants to do a truly novel science fiction story, set it in a city on Earth that's been continuously inhabited by humans for a million years at a constant level of technology. That may take some doing...

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=3431)

**1717:**

_probably the death rate in urban Rome had something to do with his view?_

IIRC London was a net population sink until some time in the latter half of the 19th century -- after they installed a proper sewer system and got on top of the cholera problem. Before then, more people died there than were born in the city, and it grew due to incomers.

Cities were amazing disease magnets before the germ theory of disease began making inroads into the world-view of urban planners. (Nobody mention Robert F. Kennedy Jr, please ...)

Since the middle of the 20th century you've probably had a better life expectancy if you lived in a large city than otherwise: much better medical facilities closer to hand, for one thing. (If you're going to have a heart attack you want it within a 15 minute ambulance ride of amajor teaching hospital, not two hours' drive from the nearest WalMart.)

**1728:**

"That is a deep rooted cultural myth that goes back at least as far as the Roman foundation myths (See: Cincinnatus). The notion that what really matters is a simple, pastoral lifestyle and that men (always men) of virtue will spend their time hunting and being woodsmen/farmers unless called upon or forced to serve in some capacity."

"Yeah, probably the death rate in urban Rome had something to do with his view?"

Cincinnatus views were a matter of some debate. His purported actions were to retire from public life to his farm and a pastoral existence twice, only to be called into action on two separate occasions when Rome was in mortal peril, made dictator for a year, win the fight, then retire back to his farm again.

No idea how true the story is - it was early enough on Rome's origin story that we don't have a lot of consistent documentation, and a lot of what is recorded was written centuries later by people who wanted it to be true.

As for death rates in urban centres etc. I agree, probably a hunter gatherer existence is more sustainable for the long haul in an evolutionary sense. My understanding of 'civilization' writ large* is that it is an active effort to escape the bounds of evolution and chart some new path. Outcomes yet to be determined, but I will admit that as much as I enjoy being in the woods I do rather like being in a warm house with healthy teeth and food in the cupboard, not to mention discussing complex ideas on sites like this with people on the other side of the planet. God help me if I could only talk to my neighbour, he's an ass.

*'Civilization' in this sense being the combined notions of scientific advance, expanding peace, prosperity and equality. A work in progress that is deeply flawed and managed to skip over a lot of what was good about diverse approaches, but still something appealing.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6796)

**1733:**

(Apologies for any tardiness in my responses: my wife is in hospital with a brain bleed/stroke, so I’m infrequently posting here to take my mind off of things.)

Adrian

Boris and his popularity is easily explicable. He makes people like my auntie Carol laugh (ex Vauxhall shop floor worker). As I explained to her, having been to Oxford at the same time, “he’s OK in homeopathic quantities”.

And to Ilya, whilst I agree that elite in the US might be an anti-intellectual term, here in the UK it’s all about social class. We have a trope about inbred chinless upper class twits for a reason. (In fact mildly intellectual upper class twits have historically been sent to Winchester School — hence the Wyckhamist Fallacy, I.e. that political leaders will do what’s best for their people, or at least not do bad for them for no obvious purpose.) So our historic elite are avowedly anti-intellectual, and used the word “boffin” as a mild put down when it became clear that the rule of the nerds was beginning.

[I don’t know whether it is still the case, but at Oxford in my time you could still see the huddles from each posh school. Manchester Grammar School, Eton, Winchester, and so forth. Each had a distinctive “type”. My type would have been “working class kid (son of a bus driver), made good through bursary to local minor public school, mathematician.” And there were quite a few of us misfits.]

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1357)

Troutwaxer   
(Pantomime mode ON ) - Oh YES IT IS!   
Seriously:   
Religion offers a supposed comfort-blanket, or carrot to the believers, and waves a stick at the unbelievers.   
“Do as we say, and you’ll go to heaven, don’t do as we say, and you’ll go to hell.” What they conveniently leave out here is the unspoken threat, which is only made manifest in those societies which are theocracies: “If you don’t do as we say, we can make sure you go to hell really painfully, and quickly.”   
Thus all “priests” are liars and/or blackmailers. They may not be deliberate liars, but nonetheless, they are telling untrue fairy-stories. 

Fear of exclusion from the community, in one form or another, is a standard part of the power-structure of any religion or cult. Excommunication, anathema, banishment, exile, fatwah, etc, …   
Fear of entry being refused in "the next world", or "the community of saints", or "the party".   
Fear of real physical punishment by the "secular arm", the NKVD, the Saudi religious police, or whomsoever the current set of spiritual thought police happen to be. Hence:   
Corollary: 2a ] Marxism is a religion. 

I believe Bertrand Russell was the first to note this, but the behaviour of both individual Marxists, and marxist organisations, and the construction of their internal power organisation and heirarchies conforms to classical religious behaviour.   
For example: people read a set number of Trotsky’s saying each day, just as if he were Jesus, or Mahmud. Or appeal to “the historical inevitability of the revolution” etc …   
I may add that it passes ALL the tests, if one cares to list them:   
1] It has a “Holy” book or books. 2] The words in those books may not be questioned, even when demonstrated proven wrong.   
3] It has sub-divisions and sects and “heresy”, and heretics, in Trevor-Ropers phrase are “even wronger” than unbelievers.   
4] Those sects fight each other, either by open warfare or in internal pogroms.   
5] It is structurally based on the RC church, complete with its own “holy office”   
6] Which leads to the gulag – the communist equivalent of the churches years of penitence and autos-de-fé   
7] Thousands if not millions are killed in the name of the “holy cause” to bring about a supposed millennium   
8] It persecutes all the competing religions   
9] In some sects it even denies Evolution by Natural Selection (look up Trofim Lysenko)

well, duh... no new technology is quickly adopted nor do people adapt their behavior immediately... it took twenty years of incremental deployment of infrastructure for BEVs to become 'an overnight success story'

<https://archive.ph/AmUJH>

"_For Decades, Installing E.V. Chargers Didn’t Pay Off for Retailers. Now It Does._ "

QUOTE

Now, new studies say retailers’ charging efforts may well be paying off: One peer-reviewed study by researchers at Boston University and the University of Wisconsin-Madison published this year looked at the impact of nearly 1,600 Tesla Supercharger stations in more than 800 U.S. counties and found a **4 percent increase in monthly visits** for retailers within 200 meters of chargers after they were installed. The effects were most pronounced for retailers within 150 meters. The researchers also found a **5 percent increase in spending**.

**1743:**

Dramlin @ 1719:

_The people who didn't put in a footpath? How ridiculous._

Mineral Bluff, GA is WAY out in the country up near the state line with North Carolina.

Most **country** roads in the U.S. don't have sidewalks. I expect that's true in the U.K. as well. I can only speak from direct observation (November 2004) to a small number of roads in the vicinity of Sterling, Ft. William, and Drumnadrochit in Scotland. 

There was a very nice paved footpath along the A830 From Mallaig to Morar.

For Mineral Bluff, GA I guess you'd have to arrest the Fannin County county commissioners.

**1744:**

While we're on the subject (London sewers & such), I don't have any water right now. 

Had my water tested about a week ago & it's got E. coli & coliform bacteria.

The guy from the county who took the water samples pointed out several problems with my well-head which probably allowed contamination to get in. Had a couple of guys from a well company out this morning to repair everything & chlorinate the well.

They set it up so the chlorine solution will recirculate through the well for 24 hours & they're coming back tomorrow to set the system to recirculate through the house plumbing for another 24 hours and then they'll flush the chlorine solution out of the well & the house plumbing. 

I should have water again by Friday, and it should be clean bacteria free water. I'm still going to install a multistage water filter & UV treatment for my household water.

The joys of country living. Never had any problems with Raleigh's city water & sewer. 😏

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

**1757:**

_A lot of CITY streets in Raleigh don't have sidewalks; not just the suburbs ... it was an argument I frequently had with Raleigh's city government._

Back in the 80s I had an argument with the Edmonton city management about lack of sidewalks in the industrial area I worked in (and had to walk through because the bus route didn't go near my office). In the summer I could walk in the ditch, but in the winter the choice was walk in the (mostly unlit) road or walk on the 5' high snow banks left by the snowplough. (Being so far north, I was arriving and leaving work in the dark.)

Apparently there was no need for sidewalks because no one walked there. The fact that no one walked there because it wasn't safe…

I went back years later and there were sidewalks and streetlights, so eventually the problem was solved. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

[Robert Prior](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537) replied to [this comment from JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201237) | [November 20, 2024 01:14](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201241)

**1758:**

_"Don't be calling the cops on some kid just because he's walking along side the road minding his own business!"_

I'm wondering if there was history between the 'witness' and the kid (or mother), or the kid/mother and cops.

Very small (370) rural town, so I'm guessing everyone knows everyone. 

I know that in small-town Ontario people who have caused trouble for the cops (by insisting that they do their jobs) have sometimes found themselves targeted and have to be squeaky-clean until either they or the OPP officer move away. It shouldn't happen, but it occasionally does. My knowledge of small-town Southern American sheriffs (based entirely on TV and movies) says that this much more likely down there.

**1766:**

About how civilization comes about: by far the best record we have of civilization developing in a chiefly society is from Hawaii, where we have a relatively recent record, archeology that backs the oral record that was written down, and outside accounts of the final part of the unification. This is a much more thorough record than we have for anywhere else.

My source for this is Pat Kirch’s _A Shark Going Inland Is My Chief,_ which is worth reading and aimed at a lay audience.

Thing is, it doesn’t support the notion that intensification of production came about in response to shortages, nor that civilization arose to help allocate resources. As I read it, production intensification was basically about power games and bragging rights, much as innovation is today. Civilization happened when the results of innovation and a growing population broke the existing clan based political structure, opening the door for the most powerful members of each island to subjugate the rest. The paramount chiefs then fought with and copied each other until, after several generations, Kamehameha took over, conquering all the islands save Kauai, which surrendered. Kamehameha lived long enough to see his kingdom decimated by pandemic disease and other toxic interactions with the colonial powers. Fortunately for us, he also oversaw the creation of written Hawaiian, and he and his successors wrote down their entire oral history while Hawaii was still an independent monarchy. 

Really worth a read, unless you want to keep your preconceptions. What’s going on now in the US looks a lot more like the rise of the Hawaiian god kings than any of us would like to admit. But we need to realize that primary civilizations tend to be authoritarian takeovers. It may well be that civilizations start as authoritarian takeovers of cultures in crisis, not as rationally designed interventions to allow more people to live together. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

[JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093) replied to [this comment from Robert Prior](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201241) | [November 20, 2024 19:26](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201279)

**1767:**

Robert Prior @ 1757:

> _"A lot of CITY streets in Raleigh don't have sidewalks; not just the suburbs ... it was an argument I frequently had with Raleigh's city government."_

_Back in the 80s I had an argument with the Edmonton city management about lack of sidewalks in the industrial area I worked in (and had to walk through because the bus route didn't go near my office). In the summer I could walk in the ditch, but in the winter the choice was walk in the (mostly unlit) road or walk on the 5' high snow banks left by the snowplough. (Being so far north, I was arriving and leaving work in the dark.)_

_Apparently there was no need for sidewalks because no one walked there. The fact that no one walked there because it wasn't safe…_

_I went back years later and there were sidewalks and streetlights, so eventually the problem was solved._

I'm not sure when I first became aware of the problem in Raleigh. Probably in the mid-90s when I was no longer traveling extensively for work. While I was traveling, I mostly stopped at stores to do my business while returning from out-of-town work. It was while I was working a more or less regular schedule in the local area that I realized there were many parts of town that I couldn't get to without driving. It was two miles to the nearest grocery store and to walk I'd have to go through here.

**1768:**

Not disagreeing at all, but using this comment as a hook to hang mine on because it is the latest in the subthread. Along similar lines, I wanted to question the notion of "original surplus" that has popped up. 

I think this is an area where our usual mode of thinking "first one thing then another thing, looks like causality" lets us down. I think both the "post" and the "propter" are questionable, and even the "hoc" isn't really clear. 

I think that for much of prehistory the human position had to do with managing reserves of food resources and using them as needed. Whether you call it fire stick agriculture, game management, wild sowing, fish traps, kangaroo funnels, the point is that many of these techniques that predate what we consider to be agriculture embedded vastly more knowledge of the environment than we're used to considering and should be thought of as at least as sophisticated and probably in many ways more so than farming methods right up to and including modern times. The point is really about isolating monocultures to optimise yield, and that's what generates huge quantities of output. I'd suggest that the knowledge to do the latter has been present for much of that time, and it's really social relations that cause it to be used in this way. 

Not to say agriculture is intrinsically associated with the rise of hierarchy and power, I think the last book by Graeber makes its first task to debunk that, the point is that there isn't a clear line of causality. Just that such an association does emerge as an outcome of humans having tried out pretty much every possible way to organise themselves, and this is a pattern that has achieved something of a breakaway success (for certain values thereof). 

To me what civilisation is about is baked into the word, that is it describes the emergence of cities. Sure there's obviously some sort of relationship with food production (describing this as a surplus as such seems very 70s to me, on a continuum between _Day of the Triffids_ and _The Good Life_) and power relations there, but I suspect it's not an entirely straightforward one. And also that's supposed to be a humorous understatement, I may not be in the right frame of mind to pull off "dry" very well. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

HOT WASH

anyone able to wait it out, ought consider making purchases as late in the holiday season as possible… retailers will be burdened with inventory and thus prone towards ever deeper discounting if inventory fails to sell… just which categories will be slowest to move (and thus heaviest discounting) not obvious today but watch closely as Target + Amazon + Marshalls + Walmart + etc announce discounting with big, big loud advertising

this is something institutional investors immediately recognized and as result shares of Target fell off a cliff (-15%)… as other retailers post their projections, there will be other moments of plunging share prices…

**1773:**

_I apologize for the funky URLs, but I couldn't figure out how to shorten them._

In Street View, there is a small box/subwindow in the top left of the window showing an approximation of the street address you are close to, and in that box in the top right there is a three vertical dot (aka "kebab") menu icon. Click on _that_ , then in the menu that appears, pick "Share or embed image". In the window that appears, one of the options is a shortened GUID-based URL to the exact view you are looking at and a button that says "Copy Link". In my experience this feature works quite well.

In Google Maps but not in Street View, the same feature is available via the "hamburger" menu icon on the left of the Search box. Click that, and pick "Share or embed map" from the menu, then the same window appears.

I've no idea whether the GUIDs generated are permanent attributes of the view/map you were looking at, or whether they are generated and cached at the time (therefore at least potentially temporary), but I suspect the former. I've not noticed such a link to stop working, and I've been using it for some years. 

For unavoidably clunky URLs I've found the markup features here pretty easy to use, though there are issues with some characters in the URLs and this randomly breaks wikipedia links that contain parentheses. The workaround is to use the HTML character code for the close parenthesis, but that's a bit gonzo.

> **The tell: A historically absurd number of Trump-only bullet ballots or undervote ballots.**  
>  There are always a handful of voters who cast a vote in one race which they care about, and do not make other selections on the ballot. These are called bullet ballots. In Presidential Races since 1980, these bullet ballots rarely account for more than 1% of the total votes including in Mr. Trump’s winning 2016 election and losing 2020 election, and when they do it warrants further investigation. In 2024 in the 43 non-swing states, bullet ballots make up a nominal >1%. In the seven swing states the numbers are so high to be unbelievable, unprecedented and demanding of further investigation.

**1780:**

I suppose the real underlying point to make here is that behavioural and institutional prejudice and discriminatoin are features that can only really be quantified in terms of prevalence and distribution, while we have a built-in urge to make categories out of whole societies and regions. It's like saying all Americans are gun nuts discards consideration for the majority of Americans who are nothing of the sort, and the massive number who have struggled to change things. 

Massachusetts didn't go to war with the USA over making more slave states though. So there is that somewhat high level categorical distinction, and a lot of people won't look past it.

**1781:**

Racism is endemic in the US, because it enabled both colonial expansion and slavery. We’re not unique in that regard.

With regard to the slave-based industries in the US, the industry wasn’t confined to the Old South. Wall Street and northern investors prospered too. 

The slavers basically bought all three branches of government, including the SCOTUS who delivered the Dredd Scott decision. They also used a military filibuster to take Texas out of Mexico, then join the US as a slave state ( Mexico had abolished slavery in 1838 IIRC), and of course, seceded when an abolitionist President was elected. And that doesn’t even get to the mess of Jim Crow and the Gilded Age

So are the US and average Americans racist? Yup. Are there both qualitative and quantitative differences between the damage caused by the slavers and their ideological descendants and order Americans? Yup. 

If you’re having trouble telling the difference, I’d suggest working on it. A lot.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

**1785:**

Thanks to everyone who’s made supportive comments.

The neurologist saw her yesterday and gave her a clean bill of health, so I’m expecting her home today.

After numerous tests turning up nothing — after an earlier test in casualty (ER) suggested a brain bleed — the provisional hypothesis is: “migraine”. Which was a bit of a shocker to me. It’s lasted nearly two months, it started with tinnitus (both sided then right side only), followed by splitting headaches (RHS), and visual disturbances (RHS) and culminating in the consistent use of a wrong (nonsense) word. That’s when I phoned for an ambulance, suspecting an TIA or stroke. All credit to our GPs who’d already started hospital investigations a fortnight earlier for suspected rheumatoid vasculitis.

Now the only problem is that she’s hooked on morphine, but she’s gone cold turkey on that before, so that should be no problem. Apparently there are now good drugs for migraines; I guess we’ll find out.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4645)

[Scott Sanford](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=4645) replied to [this comment from Heteromeles](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201157) | [November 21, 2024 11:51](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201319)

**1786:**

_As a side note: if someone wants to do a truly novel science fiction story, set it in a city on Earth that's been continuously inhabited by humans for a million years at a constant level of technology. That may take some doing..._

Why yes, it did - but the results were worth it.

[The City and the Stars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_City_and_the_Stars) (and its first draft [Against the Fall of Night](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Against_the_Fall_of_Night)) by Arthur C. Clarke is still a fine science fiction story, and holds up much better than most 1950s science fiction. 

The city of Diaspar might not be infinitely stable and eternal, but it comes close enough that at the beginning of the story nobody can remember when the last true crisis happened to within a million years. Humans had a star empire, or empires, once - but that was back in the Dawn Ages when humans first evolved and set about inventing wheels, and robots, and starships; from their perspective that was basically all one spasm of inventing. They're pretty sure Earth had a moon once. But all that was a long time ago. Diaspar was built to maintain and care for humanity forever and it's been doing very well so far. 

Of course, there's always one bored kid who wants to poke at parts of the world and see what happens...

**1804:**

_The neurologist saw her yesterday and gave her a clean bill of health, so I’m expecting her home today._

Wooooo! That's great news. 

Migraines are strange, highlighting just how little we understand of the brain. They're also very much not fun (mine aren't, anyway) but there are ways of coping.

Something worth exploring is triggers. Reducing the number of times you get a migraine is _way_ better than reducing their severity. I used to get regular headaches that often became migraines until I changed my lifestyle to avoid the triggers. 

And a note of hope: the frequency of migraines can drop with age. Mine did: I haven't seen an aura for a couple for decades now.

**1807:**

_I suspect it's NOT just here in the U.S._

Up here one reason the Ottawa Tantrum lasted so long is that the police didn't do much to stop it until the federal government stepped in — for which Trudeau is being blamed because the Emergencies Act was an overreach and not necessary.

In a way the critics are right: the Emergencies Act wasn't necessary, because the police and the province had all the powers they needed to stop the blockades and marches (and criminal activity). Thing is, they _didn't_ use those powers until forced to by the federal government. During the Tantrum cops were quick to shut down counter-protests while sharing coffee with the protesters. One of my friends (who's an ex-cop) thinks that the reason the Ottawa police chief didn't order more stringent action is that he didn't think those orders would be obeyed and he wanted to avoid an open mutiny (which would have ended his career).

And now the head of the Toronto Police Association (union) is blaming the federal government for decisions made by the province. (Provincial government is Conservative, federal is Liberal, police lean very strongly right.)

Personal corruption ($20 with your license) seems to be pretty rare, but political interference has been part of Canadian policing for a long time. Including selective enforcement of laws: if folks blockading Ottawa had been Indigenous, or protesting deforestation or something like that, they've have been cleared out and arrested as soon as they turned off their engines, if not before.

**1813:**

On the subject of modern Republicans, I might have posted this before (I didn't write it) but it seems quite intelligent to me...

"I was at a shitty crustpunk bar once getting an after-work beer. One of those shitholes where the bartenders clearly hate you. So the bartender and I were ignoring one another when someone sits next to me and he immediately says, "no. get out."

And the dude next to me says, "hey i'm not doing anything, i'm a paying customer." and the bartender reaches under the counter for a bat or something and says, "out. now." and the dude leaves, kind of yelling. And he was dressed in a punk uniform, I noticed

Anyway, I asked what that was about and the bartender was like, "you didn't see his vest but it was all nazi shit. Iron crosses and stuff. You get to recognize them."

And i was like, ohok a.

"you have to nip it in the bud immediately. These guys come in and it's always a nice, polite one. And you serve them because you don't want to cause a scene. And then they become a regular and after awhile they bring a friend. And that dude is cool too.

And then THEY bring friends and the friends bring friends and they stop being cool and then you realize, oh shit, this is a Nazi bar now. And it's too late because they're entrenched and if you try to kick them out, they cause a PROBLEM. So you have to shut them down.

And i was like, 'oh damn.' and he said "yeah, you have to ignore their reasonable arguments because their end goal is to be terrible, awful people."

And then he went back to ignoring me. But I haven't forgotten that at all."

The original: <https://www.reddit.com/r/TalesFromYourServer/comments/hsiisw/kicking_a_nazi_out_as_soon_as_they_walk_in/>

**1818:**

Other than the police budget Chow has very little control. Pretty hard to blame a mayor who can't even have a bike lane without Ford (provincial Conservative premier) deciding that a provincial law is necessary to ban them. Ford has repeatedly demonstrated that Ontario municipalities have only the power that provincial laws give them, and if they make the wrong decisions the province will pass new laws to overrule them.

So Chow is powerless and Ford is Conservative, which leaves only Trudeau to blame.

The non-CBC media* has been doing that for years, and it seems to be working. Despite things like the province underfunding the justice system (despite law-and-order being a Conservative talking point) to the extent that non-violent offenders _have_ to be released because there's literally no room to imprison them even for a few weeks, it's all apparently Trudeau's fault.

The _really_ crazy thing is that the federal government has given the provinces extra funding for law enforcement, which in Ontario at least seems to have vanished. The York Regional Police actually contacted my MP about getting funding directly because targeted grants for things like car theft seem to have vanished into general revenue.

(The same thing also happens with funding for health care and damn-near anything else. It's apparently very hard for the federal government to control where money gets spent. There's a reason a chunk of the funding for mental health bypassed the provinces and was given straight to community organizations, and it's that many provinces have a record of not spending the money on its intended purpose, then blaming the feds for the lack of programs they took money for but redirected elsewhere.)

Oddly enough, this behaviour is not exhibited by provinces with NDP governments.

*Which gives at least one reason the CPC (non-Communist) wants to eliminate the CBC. With no CBC, all significant Canadian media will be either American or privately-owned (which up here has meant pro-Conservative). 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

**1824:**

SFReader @ 1816:

> _"Re: 'My experience was ethnicity of enlistees reflected the U.S. as a whole, skewed towards lower income families. Those whose family incomes were at or below the mean.'"_

_Apologies about that re: you're not into cat videos. And thanks for the links ... haven't checked them all out yet but a quick scan seems to indicate that their content is pretty similar to some other stuff I've looked at. (I really, really don't want to go rabbitholing down gov't policy docs ... their looping is worse than 'user agreements'.)_

No apology necessary.

_My impression is that the skew toward lower income families parallels race/ethnicity. One report I looked at mentioned that enlistees from below median neighborhoods are less likely to work in higher rank (pay) positions mostly because of education level attained. I was somewhat surprised because I'd assumed (incorrectly - probably from movies/TV) that all enlisteds were treated equally/started from the same 'zero' baseline regardless of their civilian background._

Anecdotally, based on my personal experience, the military was primarily made up of those from the second, middle and fourth [income] Quintiles - the bottom fifth can't get in and the top fifth won't serve. Most enlistees have a high-school diploma or some college. African-Americans/Latinos were slightly over-represented, at least among those on their first enlistment. 

I don't understand the _"one and done"_ mentality or attitude because most of the benefits don't really start to accrue until after you re-up at least once. 

AFAIK, all enlisted personnel ARE _"treated equally/start from the same 'zero" baseline"_**1** ... at least they were when I was coming along. 

In the National Guard it was a lot more common for sons to join because their fathers had been in the Guard - or even Fathers, Sons & Grandsons all in the Guard at the same time.

In the last 20 years or so I've also seen a fair number of daughters joining to carry on family traditions ... and it seems like those with family traditions of service tended to stay in longer 

... and the longer you stay in the better your chances of achieving rank. 

There are MANY opportunities to pursue education while serving. The military strongly encourages it. I don't know if there's some kind of ethnic bias against taking advantage of those opportunities, but I know you won't be denied them because of race/ethnicity.

A young man or woman who stays in 20 years is obviously going to have more opportunities to achieve higher rank than someone who only serves a single enlistment ... 

**1** My Basic Training Company was mixed - Regular Army, Army Reserve & National Guard and there was a hierarchy, but it was NOT racial/ethnic.

The U.S. military has for a long time been one of the few places where anyone can excel on their own merit, regardless of race/ethnicity/religion/creed or gender.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6134)

[JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093) replied to [this comment from David L](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201371) | [November 22, 2024 02:38](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201383)

**1827:**

David L @ 1819:

> _"that all enlisteds were treated equally/started from the same 'zero' baseline regardless of their civilian background."_

_Once you are out of boot camp, having a year or two of community college or higher compared to not finishing high school (for whatever reason) makes a big difference in what your options are going forward. Just in being qualified to attended a school to learn to operate high tech equipment. You can climb up from way down but it takes a while. And most of those starting low tend to not be in it for the long haul._

_JohnS can add to this or correct me here._

They don't take high school dropouts. You've got to have at least a [_GED_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Educational_Development) cetrtificate. And with a GED you have to score higher on the [_**ASVAB/AFQT**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Armed_Services_Vocational_Aptitude_Batteryl) than you have to with a high school diploma. 

Fifteen credit hours for 100 level college courses + a GED certificate is considered equivalent to a high school diploma.

Also, educational standards are higher than they were when I enlisted (1975). Back then they would take high school dropouts, but you had to obtain your GED certificate within 6 months of completing Basic Training or they'd kick you out.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=44)
