**1837:**

I'll let JohnS answer properly. My take, living in a military town (San Diego has more bases than any other city in the country, albeit they're primarily Navy and Marines), is that one of the better ways to deal with poverty is to enlist. With Iraq, Afghanistan, and military bases in over 100 countries (I'm not going to stop writing and look it up), there's a constant need for warfighters that's actually not being filled.

I'd be willing to guess that's where the diversity is coming from: need. If we had a national draft, I suspect the bigotry would percolate back in.

Anyway if you've got the skill set but not the opportunity, joining up is one way to pay for an education without student loans. When I was in grad school, I knew a woman who'd gone ROTC to get her DVM. Five years caring for the military's animals, and she could start were own practice without the hundreds of thousands in student loans she'd otherwise have to pay off. 

It's still a hard life, though. The pay for low level enlisted is so low that I suspect a fair amount of the money I give to the food bank goes to their families. PTSD, other mental health issues, and widespread sexual assault (not all against women) also make the news. And having this as the area's major industry warps politics in any number of ways, although right now I'd guess that the Navy politically is to the left of the Republican Party. Not that they're political, of course. But we get seemingly weird things like MCAS Miramar being an endangered species haven. Reason is, they prefer to have undeveloped open spaces around the runways, because having a jet crash and wipe out a vernal pool full of endangered flowers is better, PR-wise, than having it crash into a subdivision. And when the jets aren't crashing (which is almost all the time), they can rightfully brag about their environmental stewardship.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=1028)

**1839:**

Robert Prior @ 1833:

> _"The U.S. military has for a long time been one of the few places where anyone can excel on their own merit, regardless of race/ethnicity/religion/creed or gender._

_When did that happen? And how?_

Started in 1947.

When the Secretary of War became the Secretary of Defense, the Air Force became a separate service, the CIA came into being & the National Security Council was established ... President Truman went against the advice of his cabinet & the generals and ordered the military be desegregated, which started the ball rolling.

There were more changes during the 50s & 60s and then in the wake of the Vietnam war (and in the wake of the 1967/1968 urban riots that devastated cities here) ... when the draft was eliminated and we went to the _"all volunteer Army"_ the DoD had to make more changes to the military in order to attract recruits.

_I know that during WWII units were still segregated by race, and during the Vietnam War being black was a disadvantage._

_Given how many equity initiatives have been quietly sidelined, how/why did the change work in the military and not (as well) in the civilian world?_

U.S. society takes two steps forward and one step back while the military takes a hesitant step forward ...

Every time the U.S. has made any kind of progressive advance toward equality the U.S. military has made similar advances (even if somewhat reluctantly), but when the backlashes have come the DoD has held firm and not allowed similar backlashes. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537)

_Every time the U.S. has made any kind of progressive advance toward equality the U.S. military has made similar advances (even if somewhat reluctantly), but when the backlashes have come the DoD has held firm and not allowed similar backlashes._

**1848:**

[

> _"In the United States, a federal statute known as the**Posse Comitatus Act** , enacted in 1878, forbids the use of the US Army (and, as amended, the US Air Force, Navy, Marine Corps, and Space Force), as a posse comitatus or for law enforcement purposes without the approval of Congress.[17] The act originally did not mention branches of the military other than the Army (and subsequently the Air Force after its establishment), leading the US Department of the Navy and the US Secretary of Defense to prescribe equivalent regulations prohibiting the use of other branches for domestic law enforcement.[18][19] In 2021, the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2022 amended the Act to formally apply the same restrictions to the domestic use of the Navy, Marine Corps, and Space Force.[20] The limitation does not apply to the National Guard of the United States when activated by a state's governor and operating under Title 32 of the US Code, such as deployments by state governors in response to Hurricane Katrina.[21]"_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Posse_comitatus)

_We swore our oaths to the Constitution. I don't know what's going to happen if an administration comes to power & tries to trash that Constitution. I think it's going to get ugly._

I expect it will be the same thing that happened when Bitch McConnell stole 3 Supreme Court appointments. The Constitution does not allow this. The military's response: sweet fuck-all and a ringing silence.

Or like when the USSC threw out stare decisis, decided that the traitor could run again, or threw out _Roe_ , all on transparently specious "legal" reasoning. Military response: see above.

I'm too tired right now to list out unconstitutional acts of the "other" Trump admin, but I could go all the way back to Reagan. Did the military step in when Reagan was funding terrorists with drug money? For sure at least some military folk knew about it. What did they do? They aided, and they abetted.

The US military has involved itself with many unconstitutional activities. In Haiti, in Lebanon, here in the US. Remember the Bonus Army?

Militaries are hierarchical. There is little room for individual conscience to act. The tops of the hierarchies are filled by the same sorts of scumbags who run for high office, and indeed many military muckymucks go on to become political panjandrums. Bosses don't believe in oaths (maybe for other people)- they believe in bossing.

I'm not in Trumpy's head but from his own words I get that he does not think his Presidential power flowed from the Constitution. He thinks is comes from his own personal power and abilities. I think it comes from the weakness, cowardice and cupidity of those around him.

But hey, it's cool. Just another election, right? Not a national emergency or anything. The election infra is just fine, nothing to see here, move along. No, they say he's a traitor but that doesn't mean he's an "enemy, foreign or domestic"... and your paycheck is in jeopardy if you disagree. If you act, you face stockade or execution. 

So I don't think the military will be a factor in this coup, other than to join in the oppression of American civilians if there turns out to be a shortage of cops.

[Robert Prior](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5537) replied to [this comment from Greg Tingey](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201460) | [November 24, 2024 17:28](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201467)

**1864:**

_fast-tracking the 413 - UH? Explain, please - means nothing to a non-Canuck_

The 413 is a new highway through good farmland and ecologically sensitive areas, which is being pushed ostensibly because it will save commuters 30 minutes a day if they travel the full length. 

This is, of course, assuming that no more development happens and populations near the highway don't increase. (Which never happens, but it gives better numbers.)

The provincial government has already short-circuited many of the environmental assessments required for a highway to expedite construction. 

Oddly enough, none of the communities supposedly being served by the highway support it —indeed, many oppose it — but a number of developers who were at Ford's daughters wedding have acquired land along the route that will be much more valuable if there's a major highway conveniently close for commuting. (See above about estimates of time saved.) And the route jigs around a golf course owned by a major Conservative donor.

Bill 212, which supposedly is about bike lanes taking road space away from cars, contains buried provisions that would allow the provincial government to build the 413 without going through the usual expropriation procedures: basically "we're taking your land, here's the money we think it's worth". 

If you look at the route (Milton to Vaughan) there's not a lot of commuter traffic along it. And there _is_ a highway: the 407, which was built as a public-private partnership to be paid for by tolls and then free. It's currently the most expensive toll highway in North America, because Harris (Conservative) sold it off to balance a budget while giving tax cuts years ago. So there's also suspicion that the trucking industry is backing it because a public highway will save them money. But even without that, connected developers buying cheap farmland in advance of a major government project is pretty much par for the course for Ford's government. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=8141)

**1868:**

DeMarquis @ 1863:

_The key constraint on what Trump can do isn't the military, it's American public opinion. If he tries to, say, suspend part of the constitution to suppress dissent, will his voters care? What if he sends immigrants into concentration camps? If he engages in even more blatant gerrymandering and voter suppression? What if the economy goes into a decline?_

_Ahh, that last one will get their attention. Guess it's a race to see what happens first._

The key constraint on Trump is whether Congress & the courts will go along with him? ... and what he'll do if they don't? ... and who will enforce a court order against him if there is one?

Certainly the military is going to have to decide whether it's a lawful order if he orders them to start rounding up his POLITICAL opponents to put them in concentration camps (as he's said he's going to do). 

WILL the military follow his orders to round up Biden, Harris, Pelosi ... and put THEM into concentration camps? 

Will they follow orders for Gen Mark Milley, Gen John Kelly, Gen James Mattis to be rounded up and put into the brig? 

What about if he orders New York state judges Merchan & Engoron - NY Attorney General James be rounded up?

What will the Supreme Court say about that? 

And **IF** they say NO and he orders the military to start rounding up supreme court justices ... what then?

**1874:**

Re: 'Certainly the military is going to have to decide whether it's a lawful order ...'

DT's already mentioned that he's going to 're-org' the military which probably translates to: get rid of anyone who won't blindly follow his orders. And if also chooses to sue/courtmartial such people maybe he can also cut off all their pensions and whatever current and retirement health benefits they'd otherwise be entitled to and then boast: 'look how much money I've just saved taxpayers!' I doubt that the US military would lead a rebellion against the POTUS - not practical: too many weapons are already owned by civilians so it'd turn into a very messy civil war. OOC - who would the various state, county and city police forces align with? 

SCOTUS has already shown its willingness to trash human rights, specifically, a woman's right to make decisions about her own body. No great leap to wonder whether this is the first move in systematically scraping away other humans' rights to their bodies, minds, etc. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5685)

[DeMarquis](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=5685) replied to [this comment from JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201477) | [November 25, 2024 20:57](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201498)

**1875:**

"Certainly the military is going to have to decide whether it's a lawful order if he orders them to start rounding up his POLITICAL opponents to put them in concentration camps (as he's said he's going to do)."

And I'm asserting that what the military ends up doing will depend on what the majority of Americans want them to do (or is willing to tolerate them doing). 

Nitpick--I doubt it would be the military doing this, the institutional barriers are greater for them than for, say, a politicized FBI. Trump seems like the kind of guy who goes for the path of least resistance. Still, I guess it doesn't much matter which specific federal agency actually carries out the policy. 

If Trump orders Joe Biden and Kamala Harris to be arrested, I think step 2 is resistance from within the bureaucracy. But which way will public opinion go? If a majority of people are ok with this, then I expect the resistance to fall. If not, then it won't, and I expect we will end up with a constitutional crisis. 

But that involves a lot of hard work and dedication, and I actually expect Trump to engage in a lot of rhetorical signaling without actually doing anything substantive. 

I'm more worried about the inevitable protest movement, whatever the spiritual successor to BLM turns out to be. If "militia members" show up and shoot some people, will they be prosecuted? If not, what happens then? More violence probably, and then what? A state of emergency? I could see public opinion go either way. 

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=6643)

**1878:**

Starting off to be a `_**"great day"**_` today ...

Took the dogs out to do their business. I **SAW** them do their business. Brought them back in and fed them; putting her out on the back deck after so she won't gobble hers & push him off his (she can't yet get off the back deck & I plan to add 1/4" hardware cloth along the inside before she figures it out).

Meanwhile I forgot I had already filled the coffee maker with water and tried to fill it again ...

And just as soon as I let her back in she runs into the living room and shits on the rug.

It's not even Monday.

[JohnS](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093) | [November 26, 2024 23:35](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2024/10/conceptual-models-of-space-col.html#comment-2201587)

**1882:**

Dramlin @ 1881:

_Starting to think she doesn't actually like your new rug..._

I'm not blaming her. While it's annoying, it's not her fault that I don't have the skill set to house-train her properly.

I didn't house-train Prince. Someone competent had already done that before he came to live with me.

It's not a new rug. It's a kind of tattered old 9x11 Persian carpet I sent home from Iraq. 

We were about 20 miles from the border & "Persian" means it's a factory made carpet from Iran, smuggled over the border. We didn't really mind that kind of smuggling as long as it wasn't weapons or IEDs. In fact, our FOB had a kind of mini flea-market where vendors could sell stuff like that.

Cost me about $10 to mail it home.

When she IS finally trained I'll take it out & have it professionally cleaned again. Meanwhile I'll kvetch about it.

[](http://www.antipope.org/mt/mt-cp.cgi?__mode=view&blog_id=1&id=7093)

### Specials

  * **[Common Misconceptions About Publishing](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2010/04/common-misconceptions-about-pu-1.html)** —a series of essays about the industry I work in.
  * **[How I Got Here In The End](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2009/07/how-i-got-here-in-the-end-my-n.html) **—my non-writing autobiography, or what I did _before_ becoming a full-time writer.
  * **[Unwirer](http://craphound.com/unwirer/)** —an experiment in weblog mediated collaborative fiction.
  * **[Shaping the Future](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2007/05/shaping-the-future.html)** —a talk I gave on the social implications of Moore's Law.
  * **[Japan: first impressions](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static//2007/11/japan-some-impressions.html)** — or, what I did on my holidays
  * **[Inside the MIT Media Lab](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/rants/inside-the-mit-media-lab.html)** —what it’s like to spend a day wandering around the Media Lab.
  * **[The High Frontier, Redux](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/2007/06/the-high-frontier-redux.html)** — space colonization: feasible or futile?
  * **[“Nothing like this will be built again”](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/blog-static/rants/nothing-like-this-will-be-buil.html)** —inside a nuclear reactor complex.

###  About this Entry 

This page contains a single entry by [Charlie Stross](http://www.antipope.org/charlie/) published on _October 13, 2024 4:19 PM_.
